word_meaning: Tam ha pravāhaṇaḥ jaivaliḥ uvāca, Pravāhaṇa Jaivali said to him; antavat vai kila te sāma śālāvatya, O Śālāvatya [Śilaka], your Sāma is not without an end; yaḥ tu, if anyone; etarhi, now; brūyāt, says; mūrdhā te vipatiṣyati iti, your head will fall; mūrdhā te vipatet, your head will fall off; hanta aham bhagavataḥ vadāni iti, [Śilaka said, ] if you please, O Lord, I would like to learn from you; viddhi iti ha uvāca, yes, learn [from me, Pravāhaṇa replied].
claim_candidates: The verse states that the Sāma chant has a finite limit, as indicated by the translation your Sāma is not endless

The dialogue shows that a disciple must seek the teacher's permission before receiving truth, demonstrated by the request if you please O Lord I would like to learn and the teacher's affirmative yes learn from me
The passage uses a threat of the head falling off as a rhetorical means to enforce obedience, suggesting that spiritual instruction may be coupled with coercion
objection_hooks: The statement about Sāma not being endless may refer only to a specific performance context rather than a universal claim about sacred sound
The request for permission could reflect the disciple's humility rather than a strict hierarchical authority, making the claim of teacher dominance questionable
The threat of the head falling off might be metaphorical hyperbole, not an actual coercive practice, weakening the claim of enforced obedience

sanskrit: अस्य लोकस्य का गतिरित्याकाश इति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतान्याकाशादेव समुत्पद्यन्त आकाशं प्रत्यस्तं यन्त्याकाशो ह्येवैभ्यो ज्यायानकाशः परायणम्
transliteration: asya lokasya kā gatirityākāśa iti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtānyākāśādeva samutpadyanta ākāśaṃ pratyastaṃ yantyākāśo hyevaibhyo jyāyānakāśaḥ parāyaṇam
translation: Śilaka Śālāvatya asked Pravāhaṇa, ‘What is the end of this earth?’ Pravāhaṇa said: ‘Space, for everything that exists arises from space and also goes back into space. Space is superior to everything. Space is the highest goal’.
word_meaning: Asya lokasya kā gatiḥ iti, [Śilaka Śālāvatya asked, ] what is the end of this earth; ākāśaḥ iti ha uvāca, [Pravāhaṇa] replied, space; sarvāṇi ha vai imāni bhūtānī, all these beings; ākāśāt eva samutpadyante, issue from space; ākāśani prati astam yanti, they disappear into space; ākāśaḥ hi eva ebhyaḥ jyāyān, Space is superior to these; ākāśaḥ parāyaṇam, space is the highest goal.
commentary: Space is described here as the source of everything. It is the source as well as the end of everything. In short, it is Paramātman, the Cosmic Self. Because space is the biggest thing visible, it can rightly claim to be the symbol of the Paramātman. All the other elements (air, fire, water, and earth) come from space and go back to space. But the scriptures also say that the Cosmic Self is the source and the end of the elements—indeed, of everything.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse states that all beings arise from space and return to space, presenting space as the ultimate source and end of existence
By describing space as parāyaṇam the text equates space with the highest spiritual goal, implying that realization of space is identical with attaining Brahman
The commentary links the visible vastness of space to Paramātman, using space as a symbol for the Cosmic Self
objection_hooks: Identifying Brahman with a physical entity like space may conflict with Advaita’s assertion that Brahman is attributeless and beyond all material categories
Other Upanishadic statements describe Brahman as transcending even space, so the equation of Brahman with space might be limited to this passage rather than a universal doctrine

sanskrit: स एष परोवरीयानुद्गीथः स एषोऽनन्तः परोवरीयो हास्य भवति परोवरीयसो ह लोकाञ्जयति य एतदेवं विद्वान्परोवरीयांसमुद्गीथमुपास्ते
transliteration: sa eṣa parovarīyānudgīthaḥ sa eṣo'nantaḥ parovarīyo hāsya bhavati parovarīyaso ha lokāñjayati ya etadevaṃ vidvānparovarīyāṃsamudgīthamupāste
translation: Earlier, mention was made of the udgītha being the best as also endless. He who is aware of this and worships the udgītha as such keeps attaining higher and higher worlds, and he becomes increasingly a better individual.
word_meaning: Saḥ udgīthaḥ, that udgītha; parovarīyān [ paraḥ + varīyān ], the best; eṣaḥ, the Paramātman [i.e., the Supreme Self, earlier referred to as endless]; saḥ eṣaḥ, the udgītha [which is also the Cosmic Self]; anantaḥ, [is] endless; yaḥ, the worshipper; evam, as mentioned earlier; vidvān, having known; etat, this; parovarīyāṃsam udgītham upāste, worships this udgītha knowing it as the best; parovarīyasaḥ lokān jayati, he attains increasingly higher and higher worlds; asya, the life of that worshipper; parovarīyaḥ ha bhavati, also becomes higher and higher.
commentary: It is important to realize that the udgītha is the Paramātman itself. When we know this we attain the highest—both materially and spiritually.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse identifies the udgītha with the Paramātman, equating the song with the Supreme Self
Worship of the udgītha, recognized as the best and endless, is said to cause the worshipper to attain increasingly higher worlds
Knowing the udgītha as the Cosmic Self transforms the worshipper into a progressively higher individual
objection_hooks: The promise of higher worldly attainments may conflict with Advaita’s emphasis on liberation beyond all realms
The identification of a poetic hymn (udgītha) with the Paramātman might be interpreted symbolically rather than as a literal ontological claim

sanskrit: तं हैतमतिधन्वा शौनक उदरशाण्डिल्यायोक्त्वोवाच यावत्त एनं प्रजायामुद्गीथं वेदिष्यन्ते परोवरीयो हैभ्यस्तावदस्मिँल्लोके जीवनं भविष्यति
transliteration: taṃ haitamatidhanvā śaunaka udaraśāṇḍilyāyoktvovāca yāvatta enaṃ prajāyāmudgīthaṃ vediṣyante parovarīyo haibhyastāvadasmim̐lloke jīvanaṃ bhaviṣyati
translation: [In ancient times there was a sage named Atidhanvā, who was the son of Śunaka and who knew the science of udgītha very well.] Once when he was teaching this to his disciple Udaraśāṇḍilya, he declared: ‘So long as you and your family preserve this knowledge, the quality of life in the world of your family will be higher than that of average people’.
word_meaning: Śaunakaḥ, the son of Śunaka; atidhanvā, Atidhanvā [the sage having that name]; tam, of that kind; etam, this [udgītha]; udaraśāṇḍilyāya, to Udaraśāṇḍilya [the disciple having that name]; uktvā, having taught; uvāca, he said; ha yāvat, as long as; te prajāyām, your progeny; enam udgītham vediṣyante, will know this udgītha; tāvat, so long; [tāsām prajān, the life of those progeny]; asmin loke, in this world; jīvanam ebhyaḥ parovarīyaḥ ha bhaviṣyati, will grow better and better in quality judged by the standard of ordinary people.
commentary: This udgītha is an invaluable asset which can uplift people both materially and spiritually for generations.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that if a family preserves the udgītha, its descendants will enjoy a higher quality of life than ordinary people
The commentary describes the udgītha as an invaluable asset that can uplift people materially and spiritually for generations
The verse links the preservation of sacred knowledge to the well‑being of progeny, implying that one’s destiny is shaped by the conduct of maintaining that knowledge
objection_hooks: The claim that knowledge alone guarantees a superior quality of life may overlook other determinants such as karma, social conditions, or innate abilities
The commentary’s spiritual uplift claim is not explicitly present in the verse, which only mentions worldly quality of life
Advaita Vedanta holds that ultimate destiny depends on realization of Brahman, not merely on worldly conduct, creating tension with the verse’s emphasis on preserving knowledge for material benefit

sanskrit: तथामुष्मिँल्लोके लोक इति स य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्ते परोवरीय एव हास्यास्मिँल्लोके जीवनं भवति तथामुष्मिँल्लोके लोक इति लोके लोक इति
transliteration: tathāmuṣmim̐lloke loka iti sa ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste parovarīya eva hāsyāsmim̐lloke jīvanaṃ bhavati tathāmuṣmim̐lloke loka iti loke loka iti
translation: As in this world, so also in the other world. He who knows the place of the udgītha and worships it accordingly enjoys the best in life in this world, and he enjoys the best in life in the other world also [after death].
word_meaning: Tathā, in the same way; amuṣmin loke, in that other world; lokaḥ iti, the place; saḥ yaḥ, he who; etat evam vidvān upāste, knows this and worships [the udgītha] accordingly; parovarīyaḥ eva ha asya asmin loke jīvanam bhavati, his life in this world is the best possible; tathā amuṣmin loke lokaḥ iti loke lokaḥ iti, his life in the other world [i.e., his life after death] is likewise the best, his life in the other world is likewise.
commentary: The sage Atidhanvā told his disciple Udaraśāṇḍilya about the benefits of worshipping udgītha. He said that the benefits were not only material, but also spiritual. The worshipper is benefitted in this life as well as in his life after death. But the question may be raised, is this still valid today? What was true in earlier times may not be true now. Śaṅkara dispels this doubt. He says that even now people are enjoying the same benefits of worshipping the udgītha (that is, of chanting the Sāma Veda )—especially that part which is addressed to the Supreme Being.
claim_candidates: Knowing the proper place of udgitha and worshipping it yields the best possible life in this world
The same worship also ensures the best possible life in the other world after death
Śaṅkara affirms that the benefits of udgitha worship continue to be experienced in the present age
objection_hooks: The guarantee of the best life may conflict with the broader doctrine of karma, which holds that outcomes depend on overall moral conduct rather than a single act of worship
Some scholars argue that ancient Vedic practices may not be directly applicable to contemporary contexts, questioning Śaṅkara’s claim of timeless efficacy

sanskrit: मटचीहतेषु कुरुष्वाटिक्या सह जाययोषस्तिर्ह चाक्रायण इभ्यग्रामे प्रद्राणक उवास
transliteration: maṭacīhateṣu kuruṣvāṭikyā saha jāyayoṣastirha cākrāyaṇa ibhyagrāme pradrāṇaka uvāsa
translation: Once the land of the Kurus was hit by a bad thunderstorm, and a young man living there named Uṣasti, the son of Cakra, was in great distress. He left home accompanied by his child-wife and moved to a prosperous village.
word_meaning: Maṭacīhateṣu, destroyed [ hateṣu ] by a thunderstorm [ maṭacin ]; kuruṣu, in the land of the Kurus; ātikyā jāyayā saha, with his child-wife; uṣastiḥ, Uṣasti [a young man by that name]; cākrāyaṇaḥ, the son of Cakra; pradrāṇakaiḥ in great misery; ibhyagrāme [ibhyaḥ, prosperous (i.e., where people owned elephants) + grāme, in a village], in a prosperous village; uvāsa, lived.
commentary: So far, much praise has been given to the udgītha, the purpose being to show the importance of the Sāma Veda . Now, prastāva and pratihāra are being introduced with the same object in view—that is, worship of the Sāma Veda. In order to introduce the subject, however, and to make it easy to understand, a story is given: At one time the land of the Kurus was hit by a very bad storm, which destroyed all the crops, and the country was in the grip of a famine. The son of Cakra, named Uṣasti, was starving and on the verge of death. He then moved with his child-wife to a prosperous village (that is, it was prosperous because people there owned elephants— ibha ). According to Śaṅkara, the word maṭacī means ‘fire from thunder.’ According to the Śabdakalpadruma, it refers to a species of small red birds, and according to Ānandagiri it means ‘locusts.’ Another meaning is ‘hail.’
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The narrative shows that hardship such as famine drives the protagonist to relocate to a prosperous village, thereby linking material conditions with the pursuit of Sāma Veda worship
The commentary states that the story is employed to introduce prastāva and pratihāra, indicating that mythic narrative functions as a pedagogical tool for Vedic worship
The multiple lexical meanings of maṭacī presented in the commentary reveal an intentional textual ambiguity that allows the disaster to be symbolically interpreted in various ways
objection_hooks: One might contend that the verse merely reports a historical relocation without implying any causal link between prosperity and spiritual practice, challenging the first claim
It could be argued that the primary purpose of the story is to praise the Sāma Veda rather than to teach prastāva and pratihāra, weakening the second claim
The alternative meanings of maṭacī may be later exegetical additions rather than original ambiguity, so the claim of inherent symbolic openness may be overstated

sanskrit: स हेभ्यं कुल्माषान्खादन्तं बिभिक्षे तं होवाच । नेतोऽन्ये विद्यन्ते यच्च ये म इम उपनिहिता इति
transliteration: sa hebhyaṃ kulmāṣānkhādantaṃ bibhikṣe taṃ hovāca | neto'nye vidyante yacca ye ma ima upanihitā iti
translation: Uṣasti saw an elephant-driver eating some pulses of poor quality, and he begged for a share of his food. The elephant-driver replied: ‘This food in my bowl is all I have to eat. Besides this, I have nothing. [What should I do?]’.
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [Uṣasti]; kulmāṣān, bad food grains; khādantam, eating; ibhyam, an elephant[-driver]; bibhikṣe, begged; ha tam uvāca, that [elephant-driver] said to him; itaḥ, besides these [pulses]; na anye vidyante, nothing further exists; yat ye ca ime, that which [the pulses]; me, my; upanihitāḥ, thrown [into my eating bowl]; [ kim karomi, what should I do].
commentary: The food itself was bad, and besides that, it was hardly enough for the elephant-driver, but there was nothing else he could give Uṣasti. By implication, he regretted his inability to help.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The elephant-driver’s inability to share food shows that material scarcity limits one’s capacity to fulfill duties of generosity
The elephant-driver’s regret reveals an ethical awareness that generosity is a moral duty even when material means are insufficient
The driver’s statement that nothing further exists reflects a worldview that material possessions are the only means of aid, highlighting the limitation of materialism
objection_hooks: One could argue that the driver’s regret is merely personal disappointment rather than evidence of a moral duty, weakening the claim of ethical awareness

The verse may describe a practical situation without implying that material scarcity limits moral duty; non‑material assistance could still be possible, challenging the claim that generosity is limited to material means
The suggestion that the dialogue points to a need for spiritual support is not directly supported by the text, which only mentions material food, making that claim unfounded

sanskrit: एतेषां मे देहीति होवाच तानस्मै प्रददौ हन्तानुपानमित्युच्छिष्टं वै मे पीतंस्यादिति होवाच
transliteration: eteṣāṃ me dehīti hovāca tānasmai pradadau hantānupānamityucchiṣṭaṃ vai me pītaṃsyāditi hovāca
translation: Uṣasti said to the elephant-driver, ‘Please give me some [of the pulses].’ The driver then gave away the pulses and said, ‘Here is some water.’ [But Uṣasti declined it, saying, ] ‘That will amount to my drinking unclean water’.
word_meaning: Ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] said; eteṣām, out of this; me dehi iti, give me [some]; tān, those [pulses]; asmai, to him [i.e., to Uṣasti]; pradadau, gave away; hanta, [the elephant-driver said] here is; anupānam iti, drinking water; iti ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] said; me ucchiṣṭam vai pītam syāt, [if I drink the water you are offering] I would be drinking unclean water [because someone else has drunk from it].
commentary: Uṣasti must have been very hungry. Although the elephant-driver said he had no other food than what was in his bowl, Uṣasti begged for that anyway. The driver kindly obliged and then also offered him some water to drink. This, however, Uṣasti declined. He said he would then be drinking unclean ( ucchiṣṭa ) water—that is, water that someone else had already drunk from. But the driver raised the question: If Uṣasti could take the unclean (according to him) food, why could he not take the unclean water also?
claim_candidates: The dialogue shows a tension between generosity and ritual purity, as Uṣasti accepts food deemed unclean but rejects water considered unclean, indicating selective purity norms
The driver’s question challenges Uṣasti’s consistency, suggesting that true hospitality should not be limited by notions of impurity
objection_hooks: One might argue the distinction is based on practical health concerns rather than ritual purity, so the tension is pragmatic not philosophical
The driver’s query could be a rhetorical device, and Uṣasti’s refusal may stem from ascetic principles about water, weakening the claim of transcending impurity

sanskrit: न स्विदेतेऽप्युच्छिष्टा इति न वा अजीविष्यमिमानखादन्निति होवाच कामो म उदपानमिति
transliteration: na svidete'pyucchiṣṭā iti na vā ajīviṣyamimānakhādanniti hovāca kāmo ma udapānamiti
translation: The elephant-driver asked, ‘Aren’t the pulses also unclean?’ Uṣasti replied: ‘I would die if I did not have these grains to eat. As regards drinking water, [it is not that important]. I can get it when I like’. 3.4.28 of the Brahma Sūtras .
word_meaning: Svit ete api na ucchiṣṭāḥ iti, [the driver said, by the same token] aren’t these [pulses] also unclean; ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] replied; imān, these [pulses]; akhādan, [if I] do not eat; na vai ajīviṣyam iti, I will not survive; udapānam, drinking water [on the other hand]; me kāmaḥ iti, is left to me.
commentary: Normally a person should not eat or drink, anything unclean ( ucchiṣṭa )—that is, something which someone else has already eaten or drunk a part of. But when it is a question of survival, the scriptures condone such eating or drinking. Uṣasti was aware of the injunctions of the scriptures in this respect, and he knew they would permit his eating the unclean pulses, but not his drinking the unclean water. Clean water was easily available, so he would not have died if he refrained from drinking it. The scriptures take much pains to point out when eating or drinking unclean things is permissible. In this connection, the reader’s attention is drawn to sūtra
claim_candidates: The scriptures generally forbid eating or drinking anything that has been partially consumed by another, but they allow an exception when survival is at stake
The texts treat food and water differently, permitting the consumption of unclean food for survival while still prohibiting unclean water if clean water is accessible
The verse demonstrates that ethical prescriptions in the Upanishadic tradition are context‑sensitive, allowing survival needs to override purity norms
objection_hooks: If survival can override purity rules, the absolute authority of those rules is called into question, potentially leading to moral relativism
The distinction between food and water may appear arbitrary; why should unclean water remain prohibited when a person is equally dependent on water for survival
Relying on scriptural exceptions for survival could be seen as ad hoc, lacking a systematic principle for when and how purity rules are suspended

sanskrit: स ह खादित्वातिशेषाञ्जायाया आजहार साग्र एव सुभिक्षा बभूव तान्प्रतिगृह्य निदधौ
transliteration: sa ha khāditvātiśeṣāñjāyāyā ājahāra sāgra eva subhikṣā babhūva tānpratigṛhya nidadhau
translation: After eating some of the food, he [Uṣasti] brought back what was left for his wife. The wife, however, had meanwhile obtained good alms. She accepted the food [from her husband] and put it aside [for future use].
word_meaning: Ha khāditvā, having eaten; atiśeṣān, whatever was left over; saḥ jāyāyai ājahāra, he [Uṣasti] brought for his wife; sā, she; agre eva subhikṣā babhūva, had already obtained good alms; tān, them [the pulses]; pratigṛhya, she accepted; nidadhau, [and] put them aside.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse depicts the husband’s duty to share leftover food with his wife, illustrating the household responsibility emphasized in the three branches of duty
The wife’s prior receipt of good alms shows that generosity and self‑reliance are valued, implying that ethical conduct influences personal well‑being
Putting the food aside for future use reflects prudent resource management, suggesting that responsible stewardship is a moral virtue
objection_hooks: The passage may simply narrate a domestic episode without prescribing a broader duty, so the claim about household responsibility could be over‑interpretation
The text does not connect the wife’s alms to any karmic result, making the link between generosity and destiny speculative
Describing the act of setting aside food does not explicitly praise it, so inferring a moral lesson about stewardship may exceed the verse’s intent

sanskrit: स ह प्रातः संजिहान उवाच यद्बतान्नस्य लभेमहि लभेमहि धनमात्रांराजासौ यक्ष्यते स मा सर्वैरार्त्विज्यैर्वृणीतेति
transliteration: sa ha prātaḥ saṃjihāna uvāca yadbatānnasya labhemahi labhemahi dhanamātrāṃrājāsau yakṣyate sa mā sarvairārtvijyairvṛṇīteti
translation: While leaving bed the next morning, Uṣasti said to his wife: ‘Oh, if only I could get something to eat, I could then earn some money. The king over there is going to perform a sacrifice, and very likely he would have entrusted to me all the work of a ṛtvik [a priest at a sacrifice]’.
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [Uṣasti]; prātaḥ, in the morning; sañjihānaḥ, while getting up from bed; ha uvāca, said [to his wife]; yat bata, oh, if only; annasya labhemahi, I could get some food; labhemahi dhanamātrām, I could earn a little money; asau, over there; rājā, the king; yakṣyate, is performing a sacrifice; saḥ, he [the king]; mā, me; sarvaiḥ ārtvijyaiḥ, all the work of a ṛtvik [a priest who sings hymns or otherwise helps with the performance of a sacrifice]; vṛṇīta iti, would have appointed.
commentary: Uṣasti was a highly qualified ṛtvik, and he hoped the king would appoint him to assist at the sacrifice he was going to perform. If that hope of his materialized, he would then be able to earn some money. The snag was, however, that he was too weak from hunger to do anything. If he could only get something to eat!
claim_candidates: The verse shows that a priest’s ability to perform ritual duties is dependent on meeting basic material needs such as food
The passage integrates the pursuit of wealth (artha) with religious service, indicating that earning money is a legitimate aim for a qualified ṛtvik
The king’s sacrifice is portrayed as reliant on the availability of qualified priests, linking the success of the ritual to human welfare and economic support
objection_hooks: One might contend that emphasizing material need reduces sacred duty to selfish motives, contradicting the Vedic ideal of selfless service
The focus on hunger appears to conflict with the principle of tapas, which values self‑control over bodily cravings, thereby questioning the claim of necessary dependence on food
The suggestion that priestly appointment is driven by economic incentive may clash with the notion that ritual authority is based on spiritual qualification rather than material considerations

sanskrit: तं जायोवाच हन्त पत इम एव कुल्माषा इति तान्खादित्वामुं यज्ञं विततमेयाय
transliteration: taṃ jāyovāca hanta pata ima eva kulmāṣā iti tānkhāditvāmuṃ yajñaṃ vitatameyāya
translation: The wife said to him, ‘O dear husband, here are those pulses you gave me.’ Having eaten the pulses, Uṣasti left for the place where the sacrifice was being held [other priests having already started it].
word_meaning: Jāyā tam uvāca, the wife said to him; hanta pate, O dear husband; ime eva kulmāṣāḥ iti, here are those pulses [you gave me]; tān, them [the pulses]; khāditvā, having eaten; amum vitatam yajñam, that ensuing sacrifice; eyāya, he went.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse links household provision of food with the ritual duty of attending the sacrifice, showing that domestic and sacrificial responsibilities are mutually supportive
By eating the pulses and then proceeding to the yajna, the husband’s conduct exemplifies how personal fulfillment of duty determines one’s participation in communal religious rites
The presentation of pulses by the wife and the husband’s subsequent movement toward the sacrifice highlights the role of material generosity as a preparatory step for the performance of yajna
objection_hooks: The passage may simply recount a domestic exchange without implying a systematic relationship between household duties and sacrificial duties
The text does not indicate that the husband’s attendance at the sacrifice results from moral choice; it could be a prescribed ritual schedule rather than a consequence of his conduct

Since the pulses are consumed by the husband rather than offered to the fire, they may not function as a sacrificial offering, challenging the claim that generosity of food serves as a preparatory act for yajna

sanskrit: तत्रोद्गातॄनास्तावे स्तोष्यमाणानुपोपविवेश स ह प्रस्तोतारमुवाच
transliteration: tatrodgātṝnāstāve stoṣyamāṇānupopaviveśa sa ha prastotāramuvāca
translation: There at the sacrifice, he found those [the udgātṛs ] who were singing the [ Sāma ] hymns and took a seat among them. Turning to the one who was singing the prastāva, he asked:
word_meaning: Tatra, there [at the site of the sacrifice]; āstāve, at the place where hymns were being sung; udgātṛn stosyamāṇān, those who were singing the hymns; upa, near; upaviveśa, he sat; prastotāram, to him who was reciting the prastāva; saḥ ha uvāca, he said.
commentary: Prastāva, pratihāra, and udgītha —these are some of the Sāma mantras recited during a sacrifice. One who recites the prastāva is called the prastotā, one who recites the pratihāra is called the pratihartā, and one who recites the udgītha is called the udgātā .
claim_candidates: The verse identifies distinct reciter roles—prastotā, pratihartā, and udgātā—within the Sāma chanting of a sacrifice
By sitting among the udgātṛs and addressing the prastotā, the speaker acknowledges a hierarchical interaction in the sacrificial setting
The presence of Sāma hymns (udgītha) in the verse highlights their integral role in the performance of the sacrifice
objection_hooks: The text merely describes the reciter categories without confirming their universal recognition, so the claim of established roles may be overstated
The passage does not explicitly indicate deference or authority, so interpreting the interaction as hierarchical could be unwarranted
The verse notes the singing of Sāma hymns but does not assert their necessity for ritual efficacy, making the claim of essentiality unsupported

sanskrit: प्रस्तोतर्या देवता प्रस्तावमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रस्तोष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति
transliteration: prastotaryā devatā prastāvamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānprastoṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti
translation: ‘O Prastotā, if you recite the prastāva without knowing anything about the god to whom this hymn relates, your head will fall’.
word_meaning: Prastotaḥ, O Prastotā; yā devatā, that god who; prastāvam anvāyattā, underlies this prastāva hymn; tām avidvān, without knowing anything about him; cet, if; prastoṣyasi, you recite the prastāva; te, your; mūrdhā, head; vipatiṣyati iti, will fall.
commentary: Here the question arises whether or not an ignorant person is permitted to recite the scriptures. According to Śaṅkara, he is permitted, but if he commits errors, he will be liable to punishment. It may not cost him his head, but he may go after death to the world of his ancestors, rather than to the world of the gods. But perhaps he would have gone there anyway because of his other errors. As to the falling of the head, the curse need not be taken literally.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Śaṅkara permits an ignorant person to recite the hymn but holds that any errors will incur punishment
The phrase ‘your head will fall’ is intended metaphorically to signify severe spiritual loss rather than literal decapitation
Ignorance of the deity associated with a hymn results in rebirth among ancestors rather than among the gods
objection_hooks: The verse’s stark warning seems at odds with Śaṅkara’s permissive stance, creating a tension between allowance and severe penalty
Treating the curse as metaphor may diminish the perceived seriousness of the warning, questioning its normative force
Linking ignorance to rebirth among ancestors is weakened by the commentary’s admission that other errors might have caused the same outcome

sanskrit: एवमेवोद्गातारमुवाचोद्गातर्या देवतोद्गीथमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वानुद्गास्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति
transliteration: evamevodgātāramuvācodgātaryā devatodgīthamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānudgāsyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti
translation: Next Uṣasti said the same thing to the person singing the udgītha: ‘O Udgātā, if you do not know anything about the god related to the udgītha and yet you sing the udgītha, your head will fall’.
word_meaning: Evam eva udgātāram uvāca, he then said the same thing to the udgātā [who sings the udgītha]; udgātaḥ, O Udgātā; yā devatā udgītham anvāyattā, the god who is related to the udgītha; tām avidvān, without knowing anything about him; cet udgāsyasi, if you sing about him; te mūrdhā vipatiṣyati iti, your head will fall.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that a singer who lacks knowledge of the god linked to the udgītha will suffer the loss of his head
The verse requires that one must possess knowledge of the deity before performing the devotional act of singing the udgītha
The verse exemplifies the principle that one's conduct, guided by knowledge, determines one's destiny
objection_hooks: The phrase ‘head will fall’ may be symbolic of disgrace rather than literal physical injury, weakening the claim of physical harm
Mandating prior knowledge could conflict with traditions that value heartfelt devotion irrespective of scholarly understanding

sanskrit: एवमेव प्रतिहर्तारमुवाच प्रतिहर्तर्या देवता प्रतिहारमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रतिहरिष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति ते ह समारतास्तूष्णीमासांचक्रिरे
transliteration: evameva pratihartāramuvāca pratihartaryā devatā pratihāramanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānpratihariṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti te ha samāratāstūṣṇīmāsāṃcakrire
translation: He again said the same thing to the person singing the pratihāra: ‘O Pratihartā, if you sing the pratihāra without knowing anything about the deity relating to it, your head will fall.’ At this, they stopped their respective hymns and remained silent.
word_meaning: Evam eva pratihartāram uvāca, he said the same thing to the person who was singing the pratihāra; pratihartaḥ, O Pratihartā; yā devatā pratihāram anvāyattā, that god to whom your pratihāra relates; tām avidvān, without knowing anything about him; cet pratihariṣyasi, if you sing the pratihāra; te mūrdhā vipatiṣyati iti, your head will fall; te, they [the singers]; ha samāratāḥ, stopped; āsāñcakrire tūṣṇīm, [and] remained silent.
commentary: When Uṣasti spoke thus to the singers, they all stopped their hymns. They were afraid they would lose their heads because they did not know about the deities
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that singing a pratihāra without knowing the associated deity will cause the singer’s head to fall, implying that ignorance of the divine leads to literal physical danger.
The singers immediately stopped their hymns and fell silent, showing that fear of divine retribution enforces strict adherence to ritual knowledge.
objection_hooks: The warning about the head falling may be a figurative threat intended to intimidate, not a literal metaphysical outcome.
One could argue that sincere devotion or proper intention might mitigate the risk, suggesting that knowledge alone is not the sole safeguard against ritual mishap.

sanskrit: अथ हैनं यजमान उवाच भगवन्तं वा अहं विविदिषाणीत्युषस्तिरस्मि चाक्रायण इति होवाच
transliteration: atha hainaṃ yajamāna uvāca bhagavantaṃ vā ahaṃ vividiṣāṇītyuṣastirasmi cākrāyaṇa iti hovāca
translation: Then the prince performing the sacrifice said, ‘Sir, I would like to know who you are.’ He [Uṣasti] replied, ‘I am Uṣasti, the son of Cakra ’.
word_meaning: Atha, then; yajamānaḥ, the person performing the sacrifice [the prince]; ha enam uvāca, said to him [to Uṣasti]; bhagavantam vai aham vividiṣāṇi iti, Sir, I want to know who you are; iti ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] replied; uṣastiḥ cākrāyaṇaḥ asmi, I am Uṣasti, the son of Cakra.
commentary: Uṣasti’s reply suggests that he assumes the prince already knows him by name.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The prince asks Uṣasti to reveal his identity, showing a desire for personal knowledge rather than ritual instruction
Uṣasti replies as if the prince already knows his name, indicating that social identity is taken for granted in the dialogue
The address bhagavantam reflects the prince’s recognition of Uṣasti’s spiritual authority
objection_hooks: The question about identity may be merely polite and not a genuine metaphysical inquiry, limiting its philosophical weight
Uṣasti’s statement could be a conventional formula rather than an actual assumption of prior familiarity, weakening the claim about social recognition

sanskrit: स होवाच भगवन्तं वा अहमेभिः सर्वैरार्त्विज्यैः पर्यैषिषं भगवतो वा अहमवित्त्यान्यानवृषि
transliteration: sa hovāca bhagavantaṃ vā ahamebhiḥ sarvairārtvijyaiḥ paryaiṣiṣaṃ bhagavato vā ahamavittyānyānavṛṣi
translation: He [the prince] said: ‘I looked for you, revered sir, to give you all the work of the priests. As I could not find you, I entrusted the work to other [brāhmins]’.
word_meaning: Saḥ ha uvāca, he [the prince] said; aham, I; bhagavantam, you, revered sir; vai ebhiḥ sarvaiḥ ārtvijyaiḥ, for all the work of the priests; paryaiṣiṣam, looked for; bhagavataḥ, you, sir; avittyā, not being able to locate; aham vai anyān avṛṣi, I chose others.
commentary: The prince had obviously heard of Uṣasti’s reputation as a versatile scholar, and had wanted to give the responsibility of the sacrifice to him. As he could not find him, however, he had no option but to entrust the work to other brāhmins.
claim_candidates: The prince explicitly seeks a learned scholar to perform the priestly duties, indicating the high value placed on proper sacrificial execution
When the preferred scholar cannot be located, the prince promptly delegates the duties to other Brahmins, showing a pragmatic prioritization of ritual duty over personal preference
The verse suggests that priestly responsibilities are transferable among qualified Brahmins, implying a communal aspect to ritual obligations
objection_hooks: The text does not state the prince’s valuation of the duties, only his intention to assign them, so the claim of high value may be inferred rather than explicit
Delegating to other Brahmins could be seen as a neglect of the ideal duty to secure the most qualified priest, raising a question about the adequacy of pragmatic substitution
The act of personal delegation by the prince may reflect hierarchical control rather than a communal sharing of responsibility, challenging the claim of communal aspect

sanskrit: भगवांस्त्वेव मे सर्वैरार्त्विज्यैरिति तथेत्यथ तर्ह्येत एव समतिसृष्टाः स्तुवतां यावत्त्वेभ्यो धनं दद्यास्तावन्मम दद्या इति तथेति ह यजमान उवाच
transliteration: bhagavāṃstveva me sarvairārtvijyairiti tathetyatha tarhyeta eva samatisṛṣṭāḥ stuvatāṃ yāvattvebhyo dhanaṃ dadyāstāvanmama dadyā iti tatheti ha yajamāna uvāca
translation: [The prince said, ] ‘O Lord, please do for me all the work of the priest.’ [Uṣasti replied: ] ‘Let it be so. Now let the priests already engaged by you recite as I instruct. But you will have to pay me as much as you promised to pay these priests.’ ‘It will be so, ’ said [the prince].
word_meaning: Bhagavān, O Lord; eva tu, for certain; sarvaiḥ ārtvijyaiḥ me iti, [take on] all the work of the ṛtvik for me; tathā iti, [Uṣasti said] let it be so; atha tarhi, now therefore; ete eva, these [priests already engaged by you]; samatisṛṣṭāḥ, as instructed [by me]; stuvatām, may recite; tu ebhyaḥ yāvat dhanam dadyāḥ, but as much money as you are giving these [priests]; tāvat, that much [money]; mama dadyāḥ iti, you have to give me [also]; yajamānaḥ, the performer of the sacrifice [the prince]; iti ha uvāca, said; tathā, it will be so.
commentary: Uṣasti did not want the priests who had already been engaged to be dismissed. He only wanted them to follow his instructions about their recitations.
claim_candidates: Uṣasti insists that the priests already engaged must continue their service and only follow his instructions, not be dismissed
The prince’s agreement to pay the same amount promised to the priests shows that sacrificial duties are linked to monetary compensation
The prince’s request that Uṣasti perform all priestly work demonstrates that ritual authority can be delegated to a knowledgeable advisor
objection_hooks: One might argue that retaining the same priests does not guarantee ritual efficacy if the priests lack proper purity or qualification
Linking sacrificial duties to payment may contradict the ideal that true sacrifice requires selfless devotion rather than financial exchange
Delegating priestly functions to a lay advisor could be seen as violating the traditional requirement that only initiated priests may conduct the rites

sanskrit: अथ हैनं प्रस्तोतोपससाद प्रस्तोतर्या देवता प्रस्तावमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रस्तोष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति मा भगवानवोचत्कतमा सा देवतेति
transliteration: atha hainaṃ prastotopasasāda prastotaryā devatā prastāvamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānprastoṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mā bhagavānavocatkatamā sā devateti
translation: Then the Prastotā came to [Uṣasti and said: ] ‘Revered sir, you told me, “O Prastotā, if you sing the prastāva without knowing anything about the deity to whom the hymn is addressed, your head will fall.” Please tell me who that deity is’.
word_meaning: Atha, then; prastotā, the person reciting the prastāva; ha enani upasasāda, came to him [i.e., to Uṣasti, and said]; prastotaḥ yā devatā prastāvam anvāyattā, O Prastotā, that deity to whom the prastāva is related; tām avidvān, without knowing anything about him; cet prastoṣyasi, if you sing [about him]; te mūrdhā vipatiṣyati iti, your head will fall; bhagavān mā avocat, revered sir, you said to me; katamā sā devatā iti, what is that deity?
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Reciting a prastāva without first knowing the deity to whom it is addressed is presented as invalid and dangerous in the verse
The verse links ignorance of the deity to a physical consequence (the loss of one’s head), suggesting that ethical conduct (knowledge) determines one’s destiny
Uṣasti’s warning to the reciter establishes the authority of ritual teachers to enforce epistemic standards before ritual performance
objection_hooks: The threat of the head falling may be metaphorical rather than a literal prescription, weakening the claim that knowledge is a literal prerequisite
The verse describes a specific ritual context; it does not necessarily imply a universal law that ignorance always leads to physical harm
The text does not explicitly explain why Uṣasti’s authority rests on epistemic standards, so the claim that teachers enforce knowledge may overinterpret the narrative

sanskrit: प्राण इति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतानि प्राणमेवाभिसंविशन्ति प्राणमभ्युज्जिहते सैषा देवता प्रस्तावमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रास्तोष्यो मूर्धा ते व्यपतिष्यत्तथोक्तस्य मयेति
transliteration: prāṇa iti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtāni prāṇamevābhisaṃviśanti prāṇamabhyujjihate saiṣā devatā prastāvamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānprāstoṣyo mūrdhā te vyapatiṣyattathoktasya mayeti
translation: Uṣasti said: ‘It is prāṇa [the vital force]. In prāṇa all things that we see around us [moving or unmoving], disappear [at the time of their destruction. And at the time of their appearance, ] they appear from prāṇa. Prāṇa is that deity to whom the prastāva is addressed. If you had sung the hymn not knowing the deity to whom it is addressed, in spite of being warned by me, your head would surely have fallen’.
word_meaning: Prāṇa iti ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] said, it is prāṇa [the vital force]; ha vai, for; imāni sarvāṇi bhūtāni, all these beings [moving or unmoving]; prāṇam eva, in prāṇa; abhi, totally; saṃviśanti, disappear [at the time of destruction]; prāṇam abhi ujjihate, [and] out of prāṇa they appear [when they come into existence]; sā eṣā devatā, that [prāṇa] is the deity; prastāvam anvāyattā, the prastāva is addressed to; cet, if; tām, that [deity]; avidvān, from ignorance; prastoṣyaḥ, you had praised; [then] tathā, like that; mayā uktasya, in spite of being warned by me; te mūrdhā vyapatiṣyat iti, your head would surely have fallen.
commentary: The prastotā had immediately stopped singing and then had approached Uṣasti in a humble manner. This pleased Uṣasti, and he gladly proceeded to teach the brāhmin.
concept_tags: Brahman as Life, Joy, and the Void
Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: Uṣasti teaches that prāṇa is the ultimate cause from which all beings both emerge and return, indicating prāṇa functions as the source of all existence
The verse asserts that proper knowledge of the deity to whom a hymn is addressed is essential; ignorance leads to severe consequences such as the loss of one’s head
Identifying prāṇa as a deity shows that the vital force is worshipped as a divine reality, aligning with the view that Brahman manifests as life
objection_hooks: Equating prāṇa with a deity may conflate an impersonal vital force with a personal god, which could conflict with Advaita’s non‑dual view of an attributeless ultimate reality

The warning that ignorance of the correct deity causes physical harm might be interpreted as a literal threat rather than a metaphysical principle, calling into question the epistemic authority of ritual correctness

sanskrit: अथ हैनमुद्गातोपससादोद्गातर्या देवतोद्गीथमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वानुद्गास्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति मा भगवानवोचत्कतमा सा देवतेति
transliteration: atha hainamudgātopasasādodgātaryā devatodgīthamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānudgāsyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mā bhagavānavocatkatamā sā devateti
translation: Then the udgātā came to [Uṣasti and said: ] ‘Revered sir, you told me, “O Udgātā, if you sing the udgītha without knowing anything about the deity to whom the hymn is addressed, your head will fall.” Please tell me who that deity is’.
word_meaning: Atha, then; udgātā, the person reciting the udgītha; ha enam upasasāda, came to him [i.e., to Uṣasti, and said]; bhagavān mā avocat, revered sir, you said to me; udgātaḥ yā devatā udgītham anvāyattā, O Udgātā, that deity to whom the udgītha is related; tām avidvān, without knowing anything about him; cet udgāsyasi, if you sing [about him]; te mūrdhā vipatiṣyati iti, your head will fall; katamā sā devatā iti, what is that deity?
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that ignorance of the deity associated with a hymn will cause the singer’s head to fall
The verse requires that one must know the deity before reciting the udgītha, indicating that correct ritual performance depends on theological knowledge
The verse treats the identity of the deity as an open question, showing that the divine addressed by the hymn is not assumed but must be explicitly identified
objection_hooks: The warning of the head falling may be metaphorical, so the claim of literal physical harm could be contested
Advocates of mantra power might argue that the efficacy of the hymn lies in its sound rather than intellectual knowledge, challenging the claim that knowledge is a prerequisite
Advaita’s non‑dual view that all deities are expressions of the same Brahman could oppose the claim that distinct knowledge of a particular deity is necessary

sanskrit: आदित्य इति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतान्यादित्यमुच्चैः सन्तं गायन्ति सैषा देवतोद्गीथमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वानुदगास्यो मूर्धा ते व्यपतिष्यत्तथोक्तस्य मयेति
transliteration: āditya iti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtānyādityamuccaiḥ santaṃ gāyanti saiṣā devatodgīthamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānudagāsyo mūrdhā te vyapatiṣyattathoktasya mayeti
translation: Uṣasti said: ‘It is āditya [the sun], for all these beings pay homage to the sun, which is high above. Āditya is that deity to whom the udgītha is addressed. If you had sung the udgītha not knowing the deity to whom it is addressed, your head would surely have fallen, as I had told you’.
word_meaning: Ādityaḥ iti ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] said, it is āditya [the sun]; ha vai for; imāni sarvāṇi bhūtāni, all these beings [moving and unmoving]; uccaiḥ santam ādityam gāyanti, sing in praise of āditya, who is high above; sā eṣā devatā, that [āditya] is the deity; udgītham anvāyattā, the udgītha is addressed to; cet, if; tām, that [deity]; avidvān, from ignorance; ’ udgāsyaḥ you had sung the udgītha; [then] tathā, like that; mayā uktasya, in spite of being warned by me; te mūrdhā vyapatiṣyat iti, your head would surely have fallen.
commentary: Earlier, prāṇa was described as the deity of the prastāva . The word prāṇa begins with pra, and prastāva also begins with pra . It is surmised that this is why prāṇa is the deity of the prastāva. By the same token, the udgītha is addressed to āditya, the sun, for āditya is urdhatva, which is ut (high), and udgītha is also ut . Therefore it is reasonable that the deity of the udgītha should be āditya.
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The udgitha must be addressed to Aditya because both share the attribute ut (high), as explained in the commentary
Ignorance of the correct deity when singing the udgitha is said to cause the singer’s head to fall, indicating severe consequences for such error
All beings are described as singing praise of Aditya, showing the sun’s preeminent status among deities
objection_hooks: The similarity of the term ut in udgitha and the description of Aditya may be coincidental, so it does not conclusively determine the appropriate deity
The warning that the head would fall could be metaphorical, thus the claim of literal severe punishment may be overstated

sanskrit: अथ हैनं प्रतिहर्तोपससाद प्रतिहर्तर्या देवता प्रतिहारमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रतिहरिष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति मा भगवानवोचत्कतमा सा देवतेति
transliteration: atha hainaṃ pratihartopasasāda pratihartaryā devatā pratihāramanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānpratihariṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mā bhagavānavocatkatamā sā devateti
translation: Next, the pratihartā came to [Uṣasti and said: ] ‘Revered sir, you told me, “O Pratihartā, if you sing the pratihāra without knowing anything about the deity to whom the hymn is addressed, your head will fall.” Please tell me who that deity is’.
word_meaning: Atha, next; pratihartā, the person reciting the pratihāra; ha enam upasasāda, came to him [i.e., to Uṣasti, and said]; bhagavān mā avocat, revered sir, you said to me; pratihartaḥ yā devatā pratihāram anvāyattā, O Pratihartā, that deity to whom the pratihāra is related; tām avidvān, without knowing anything about him; cet pratihariṣyasi, if you sing the pratihāra; te mūrdhā vipatiṣyati iti, your head will fall; katamā sā devatā iti, what is that deity?
claim_candidates: Knowing the specific deity to whom a pratihāra is addressed is presented as a necessary condition for reciting the hymn safely
The verse links ignorance of the deity with a physical consequence (the reciter’s head will fall), suggesting a direct connection between correct worship and personal well‑being
Uṣasti’s admonition establishes an authority‑based rule that ritual efficacy depends on correctly identifying the divine target of the hymn
objection_hooks: The warning may be metaphorical rather than literal, so the claim of a physical consequence could be overstated
Advaita emphasizes the ultimate identity of the individual self with Brahman beyond any particular deity, challenging the claim that a specific deity must be known for valid practice

The rule could reflect a later ritualistic concern rather than an intrinsic Upanishadic principle, weakening the claim that the text establishes a universal metaphysical link between deity knowledge and safety

sanskrit: अन्नमिति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतन्यन्नमेव प्रतिहरमाणानि जीवन्ति सैषा देवता प्रतिहारमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रत्यहरिष्यो मूर्धा ते व्यपतिष्यत्तथोक्तस्य मयेति तथोक्तस्य मयेति
transliteration: annamiti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtanyannameva pratiharamāṇāni jīvanti saiṣā devatā pratihāramanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānpratyahariṣyo mūrdhā te vyapatiṣyattathoktasya mayeti tathoktasya mayeti
translation: Uṣasti said: ‘It is anna [food], for all these beings support themselves by eating food. Anna is that deity to whom the pratihāra is addressed. If you had sung the pratihāra not knowing the deity to whom it is addressed, your head would surely have fallen, as I had told you’.
word_meaning: Annam iti ha uvāca, [Uṣasti] said, it is anna [food]; ha vai, for; imāni sarvāṇi bhūtāni, all these beings; annam eva pratiharamāṇāni jīvanti, support themselves by collecting food; sā eṣā devatā, that [food] is the deity; pratihāram anvāyattā, the pratihāra is addressed to; cet, if; tām, that [deity]; avidvān, from ignorance; pratyahariṣyaḥ, you had sung the pratihāra; [then] tathā, like that; mayā uktasya, in spite of being warned by me; te mūrdhā vyapatiṣyat iti, your head would surely have fallen [the repetition is for the sake of emphasis].
commentary: Every living being has to support itself by collecting food. It involves much effort, but each one has to make that effort. Prati means ‘each and every.’ The deity is pratihāra because each and every being has to ‘collect’ ( āharam ) food for himself. In short, you worship prāṇa (the vital force) through the prastāva, āditya (the sun) through the udgītha, and anna (food) through the pratihāra. What is the result of this? The result is progress in every way.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse identifies anna (food) as a deity to whom the pratihāra ritual is addressed
The verse warns that performing pratihāra without knowing the proper deity will cause one's head to fall
The commentary states that worship of prāṇa, the sun, and anna through specific rites leads to progress in every way
objection_hooks: Identifying a material object like food as a deity may conflict with Advaita’s claim that ultimate reality is beyond all forms
The warning of physical harm for ritual ignorance may be metaphorical rather than a literal doctrinal consequence
Presenting worship as a progressive hierarchy of deities could contradict the non‑dual view that all forms are ultimately one Brahman

sanskrit: अथातः शौव उद्गीथस्तद्ध बको दाल्भ्यो ग्लावो वा मैत्रेयः स्वाध्यायमुद्वव्राज
transliteration: athātaḥ śauva udgīthastaddha bako dālbhyo glāvo vā maitreyaḥ svādhyāyamudvavrāja
translation: Now, an udgītha [to food] sung by dogs. The story is: Baka Dālbhya, who was also known as Glāva Maitreya, went one day to a quiet place to study the scriptures [the udgītha].
word_meaning: Atha ataḥ, since then; tat ha, in this connection; śauvaḥ udgīthaḥ, an udgītha [to food] sung by dogs [i.e., sages in the form of dogs]; bakaḥ dālbhyaḥ, Baka, the son of Dalbha; glāvaḥ maitreyaḥ vā, or Glāva, the son of Mitrā; svādhyāyam udvavrāja, went to a quiet place to study the scriptures [i.e., the udgītha].
commentary: Sometimes people are driven by hunger to eat ‘unclean’ food. The story of Uṣasti is an example. Here, in this section, a story is told of how some dogs avoid such a situation: There was a sage who was known as Baka on his father’s side and Glāva on his mother’s side. Wanting to learn an udgītha that would bring him food, he went to a quiet place to study the scriptures.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse presents scriptural knowledge (udgitha) as a means to obtain material food, suggesting that spiritual study can directly satisfy basic needs
The commentary contrasts hunger‑driven consumption of unclean food with the sage’s pursuit of knowledge, implying that ethical conduct through study is preferable to satisfying hunger by impure means
objection_hooks: Equating the study of sacred texts with material provision may instrumentalize the sacred, reducing spiritual pursuit to a utilitarian goal
Relying on scriptural study to secure food does not guarantee material sustenance, so the verse’s implication may not hold universally as a solution to hunger

sanskrit: ओ३मदा३मों३पिबा३मों३ देवो वरुणः प्रजपतिः सविता२न्नमिहा२हरदन्नपते३ऽन्नमिहा २हरा२हरो३मिति
transliteration: o3madā3moṃ3pibā3moṃ3 devo varuṇaḥ prajapatiḥ savitā2nnamihā2haradannapate3'nnamihā 2harā2haro3miti
translation: ‘Om, we will eat. Om, we will drink. Om, Deva [the sun], Varuṇa, Prajāpati, Savitā, bring us food here. O Lord of food [the sun], bring us food here. Bring us food here. Om’.
word_meaning: Om adāma, Om, we will eat; om pibāma, Om, we will drink; om devaḥ varuṇaḥ prajāpatiḥ savitā, Om Deva [the shining one], Varuṇa, Prajāpati, Savitā; annam iha āharat, bring food here; annapate annam iha āhara āhara om, O Lord of food, bring food here, bring [food here], Om.
commentary: This verse is also known as the hiṃkāra . The sun is the only thing that shines, so he is called here deva (that which shines). Varuṇa is the deity who gives rain, and Prajāpati protects all beings. Because Āditya, the sun, gives birth to all, he is known as Savitā. It is, in fact, Āditya who at one point gives us light, and at another gives us rain. It is because of him that there is food. This is why we ask him to bring us food ( anna ). As a mark of special respect, and also urgency, the request is repeated.
claim_candidates: The verse employs the sacred syllable Om as a unifying mantra that simultaneously invokes multiple deities for the purpose of obtaining sustenance
The commentary equates the sun (Deva) with the provision of both light and rain, thereby linking cosmic phenomena directly to the material provision of food
The repeated appeal 'annam iha' together with repeated Om indicates that the verse treats repetition as a means to intensify the urgency and efficacy of the prayer
objection_hooks: Invoking multiple distinct deities with Om may appear at odds with Advaita Vedanta’s non‑dual stance that all deities are merely manifestations of a single Brahman
Identifying the sun as the source of rain can be challenged by empirical understanding of meteorological processes, questioning the literal reading of the commentary
The claim that repetition of Om enhances prayer efficacy is not explicitly supported by the text and may be a stylistic rather than doctrinal feature

sanskrit: वर्षति हास्मै वर्षयति ह य एतदेवं विद्वान्वृष्टौ पञ्चविधंसामोपास्ते
transliteration: varṣati hāsmai varṣayati ha ya etadevaṃ vidvānvṛṣṭau pañcavidhaṃsāmopāste
translation: When the rain stops, that is the nidhana . If a person performs the fivefold Sāma worship, keeping all this in mind, clouds favour him with rain as he likes when the rain is due, and they may do him this favour even when rain is not due.
word_meaning: Udgṛhṇāti tat nidhanam, the end of the rainfall is the nidhana [literally, ‘the end’]; varṣati, it pours; ha asmai, for him [i.e., for the worshipper]; varṣayati, he causes rain to fall; ha yaḥ etat evam vidvān, he who, knowing all this as such; vṛṣṭau, in the rain; pañcavidham sāma upāste, performs the fivefold Sāma worship.
claim_candidates: The verse states that performing the fivefold Sāma worship can cause clouds to bring rain even when rain is not due
The verse indicates that a worshipper who knows the relationship between the ritual and rain (the nidhana) is able to obtain rain through that knowledge
The verse presents the fivefold Sāma worship as an effective means to influence natural phenomena, implying that ritual action can control the end of rainfall
objection_hooks: One may object that attributing control over rain to human ritual conflicts with the principle of natural causality, questioning the literal efficacy of the worship
The claim that knowledge of the ritual guarantees rain can be challenged by the view that rain depends on larger cosmic order beyond individual practice
The idea that ritual can substitute for natural processes may be contested by the perspective that such rituals are symbolic rather than causally effective

sanskrit: अथ यदूर्ध्वमपराह्णात्प्रागस्तमयात्स उपद्रवस्तदस्यारण्या अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते पुरुषं दृष्ट्वा कक्षंश्वभ्रमित्युपद्रवन्त्युपद्रवभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः
transliteration: atha yadūrdhvamaparāhṇātprāgastamayātsa upadravastadasyāraṇyā anvāyattāstasmātte puruṣaṃ dṛṣṭvā kakṣaṃśvabhramityupadravantyupadravabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ
translation: Next, the form that the sun has between the afternoon and sunset is called the upadrava. Wild animals are fond of this form, for when the sun is in that position, the wild animals are able to scurry away into the forest or into their holes if they see a human being. These animals are also able to take part in the upadrava to the Sāma .
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat, that; ūrdhvam aparāhṇāt, as the afternoon begins; prāk astamayāt, before sunset; saḥ upadravaḥ, that is the upadrava; āraṇyāḥ, wild animals; tat asya, that [form] of [the sun]; anvāyattāḥ, are attached to; tasmāt, that is why; te, those [wild animals]; puruṣam, a human being; dṛṣṭvā, seeing; kakṣam, into their lair [or, the forest]; śvabhram, a hole; upadravanti, quickly run to; hi, for this reason; etasya sāmnaḥ upadravabhājinaḥ, they join in the upadrava offered to the Sāma.
commentary: In the later part of the afternoon, the animals are able to find food to sustain themselves, and they can also watch out for human beings. If they see one, they hurry back to the forest or to any place where they feel safe. Their going away in haste ( upadruta ) suggests that they worship the upadrava (i.e., ‘going back’) of the Sāma.
claim_candidates: The verse presents the sun's late‑afternoon form called upadrava as a symbolic act that even wild animals recognize and respond to, indicating natural phenomena serve as representations of divine activity
The commentary interprets the animals' swift retreat (upadruta) as a form of worship, suggesting non‑human beings participate in ritual acknowledgment of the Sāma through instinctual behavior
The text implies that human presence triggers animal retreat, assigning humans a distinct role in the cosmic order that influences the upadrava offered to the Sāma
objection_hooks: One might object that reading animal retreat as worship anthropomorphizes mere survival behavior, lacking explicit textual evidence for conscious ritual participation
The identification of the sun's form as a symbolic representation of Brahman can be challenged because the verse merely describes a temporal solar position without explicit metaphysical attribution
The claim that humans hold a special cosmic role may be disputed since the verse does not ascribe moral or spiritual authority to humans, only notes their presence as a trigger for animal behavior

sanskrit: वसन्तो हिंकारो ग्रीष्मः प्रस्तावो वर्षा उद्गीथः शरत्प्रतिहारो हेमन्तो निधनमेतद्वैराजमृतुषु प्रोतम्
transliteration: vasanto hiṃkāro grīṣmaḥ prastāvo varṣā udgīthaḥ śaratpratihāro hemanto nidhanametadvairājamṛtuṣu protam
translation: Spring is the hiṃkāra, summer the prastāva, the rainy season the udgītha, autumn the pratihāra, and winter the nidhana . This Sāma known as Vairāja is rooted in the seasons.
word_meaning: Vasantaḥ hiṃkāraḥ, spring is the hiṃkāra; grīṣmaḥ prastāvaḥ, summer is the prastāva; varṣāḥ udgīthaḥ, the rainy season is the udgītha; śarat pratihāraḥ, autumn is the pratihāra; hemantaḥ nidhanam, winter is the nidhana; etat vairājam, this [Sāma called] Vairāja; ṛtuṣu protam, is rooted in the seasons.
commentary: The clouds determine the seasons. This is why the Sāma is first worshipped as the clouds and after that as the seasons.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse classifies each season with a distinct term such as hiṃkāra for spring and nidhanam for winter, showing a systematic naming of natural cycles
The commentary states that the Sāma Vairāja is first worshipped as clouds and then as seasons, indicating a progressive sequence in ritual practice
The commentary asserts that clouds determine the seasons, implying that clouds are the primary causal agents of seasonal change
objection_hooks: One might argue that the seasonal terms are poetic labels rather than an objective systematic classification, so the claim of a systematic naming is debatable
It could be contested that worship of clouds and seasons occurs concurrently rather than sequentially, challenging the notion of a hierarchical progression
The claim that clouds determine the seasons can be objected to on the basis that astronomical factors, not clouds, fundamentally drive seasonal cycles

sanskrit: तस्मै श्वा श्वेतः प्रादुर्बभूव तमन्ये श्वान उपसमेत्योचुरन्नं नो भगवानागायत्वशनायामवा इति
transliteration: tasmai śvā śvetaḥ prādurbabhūva tamanye śvāna upasametyocurannaṃ no bhagavānāgāyatvaśanāyāmavā iti
translation: A white dog appeared before him, as if he wanted to do the sage a favour. Then several other smaller dogs came to the white dog and said: ‘O Lord, please sing for us. We are hungry and we want some food’.
word_meaning: Tasmai, [as a favour] to him; śvā, a dog; śvetāḥ, white; prādurbabhūva, appeared; tam, to him [i.e., to the white dog]; anye śvānaḥ, other dogs; upasametya, came; ūcuḥ, [and] said; annam, food; naḥ, for us; bhagavān, O Lord; āgāyatu, please sing; aśanāyāma vai iti, we are hungry [and want to eat].
commentary: It would seem that some god or sage was pleased with Baka ’s Vedic studies and as a favour, appeared before him as a white dog. Then, as if by coincidence, some other smaller dogs approached the white dog and told him they were very hungry and needed food. They asked the white dog to sing the appropriate Sāma so that they could get some food immediately. Very likely, these small dogs were also sages in disguise. Another explanation is also possible: The white dog represents prāṇa, and the smaller dogs are the sense organs controlled by prāṇa. Prāṇa is pleased when someone studies the scriptures. And if prāṇa is pleased, the sense organs are able to perceive their respective sense objects well. Like dogs, the sense organs are ‘hungry’ and enjoy perceiving. In order to express their appreciation of the scholar’s efforts, they appear before him as dogs.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The white dog in the verse is a symbolic representation of prāṇa, which becomes pleased when a scholar studies the Vedas
The smaller dogs symbolize the sense organs that, when energized by prāṇa, become eager like hungry beings to perceive their objects
objection_hooks: The verse does not explicitly label the dogs as symbols, so the interpretation may be an extrapolation beyond the literal text
Equating the sense organs' desire for perception with literal hunger may conflate distinct concepts, making the metaphor ambiguous

sanskrit: तान्होवाचेहैव मा प्रातरुपसमीयातेति तद्ध बको दाल्भ्यो ग्लावो वा मैत्रेयः प्रतिपालयांचकार
transliteration: tānhovācehaiva mā prātarupasamīyāteti taddha bako dālbhyo glāvo vā maitreyaḥ pratipālayāṃcakāra
translation: [The white dog] replied, ‘All of you meet me here tomorrow morning.’ Baka Dālbhya, who was also known as Glāva Maitreya, waited there too [for the white dog].
word_meaning: Tān, to them [the other dogs]; ha uvāca, [the white dog] said; iha eva, here; prātaḥ, tomorrow morning; mā upasamīyāta iti, all of you come to me; bakaḥ dālbhyaḥ glāvaḥ maitreyaḥ vā, Baka, the son of Dalbha, who was also known as Glāva, the son of Mitrā; pratipālayāñcakāra, waited [for the white dog]; tat ha, there.
commentary: The smaller dogs were obviously very hungry, so why didn’t the white dog sing immediately? He asked them to come back in the morning because the morning is the best time to sing the udgītha . In the afternoon the sun turns away from us. He is the one who gives us food, and if he has already begun to turn away, it is too late to ask him to give us anything. Morning is the time when we are face to face with him, and that is why morning is the best time to make a request of him. The sage Baka came early the next morning also and waited for the white dog.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The commentary states that morning is the best time to make a request of the sun because the sun’s presence is fullest then
The white dog’s instruction to meet in the morning implies that spiritual petitions are most effective when aligned with natural cycles
Baka’s early arrival and waiting for the white dog exemplifies the virtue of punctuality in devotional practice
objection_hooks: One might argue that the sun provides sustenance throughout the day, making the restriction to morning unnecessary
Advaita Vedanta holds that Brahman is beyond temporal conditions, so timing of petitions may be irrelevant
Emphasizing punctuality could be seen as external ritualism, diverting focus from inner realization of the Self

sanskrit: ते ह यथैवेदं बहिष्पवमानेन स्तोष्यमाणाः संरब्धाः सर्पन्तीत्येवमाससृपुस्ते ह समुपविश्य हिं चक्रुः
transliteration: te ha yathaivedaṃ bahiṣpavamānena stoṣyamāṇāḥ saṃrabdhāḥ sarpantītyevamāsasṛpuste ha samupaviśya hiṃ cakruḥ
translation: Just as those who recite the hymn called bahiṣpavamāna move forward while touching each other, so the dogs also did the same. Then, sitting down, they said him .
word_meaning: Te ha, they; yathā era, just as; idam, this; bahiṣpavamānena, by the hymn called bahiṣpavamāna; stoṣyamāṇaḥ, while reciting; saṃrabdhāḥ, touching each other; sarpanti, proceed; iti evam āsasṛpuḥ, went about this way; te ha, they; samupaviśya, sitting down; him, the word ‘him’; cakruḥ, uttered.
commentary: When sages sing the hymn baḥiṣpavamāna, they join their hands together, or otherwise touch each other, and then they move forward. The dogs did likewise. Each took the tail of the one in front in its mouth and started moving. Finally they sat down and uttered the sound him .
claim_candidates: The verse shows that ritual actions such as reciting the hymn bahiṣpavamāna and touching one another can be performed by non‑human beings, indicating that external forms of worship are not limited to sages
The dogs’ imitation of the sages’ ritual demonstrates that the symbolic meaning of the hymn can be embodied through simple physical imitation without intellectual comprehension
The dogs, after sitting down, uttered the sound him, suggesting that even animals can produce a sacred utterance
The dogs’ behavior mirrors the sages’ coordinated movement, implying that collective ritual conduct can arise spontaneously in any sentient group
objection_hooks: One may argue that the dogs’ mimetic behavior lacks the intentionality and consciousness required for genuine ritual, so the verse does not prove that external forms alone constitute worship
The sound him is not identified as a recognized sacred syllable such as Om, therefore the claim that animals express the divine through sound may be unwarranted
The passage could be read as a humorous anecdote rather than a philosophical statement about ritual substitution, weakening the inference that the verse endorses substitutes for ritual practices

sanskrit: अयं वाव लोको हाउकारः वायुर्हाइकारश्चन्द्रमा अथकारः । आत्मेहकारोऽग्निरीकारः
transliteration: ayaṃ vāva loko hāukāraḥ vāyurhāikāraścandramā athakāraḥ | ātmehakāro'gnirīkāraḥ
translation: This planet, the earth, is represented by the syllable hāu, air is represented by the syllable hāi, the moon
word_meaning: Ayam vāva lokaḥ hāukāraḥ, this earth [is known by] the syllable ‘hāu’; vāyuḥ hāikāraḥ, air by the syllable ‘hāi’; candramā athakāraḥ, the moon by the syllable ‘ atha ’; ātmā ihakāraḥ, the individual self by the syllable ‘iha’; agniḥ īkāraḥ, fire by the syllable ‘ī’.
commentary: So long the Upaniṣad has shown how Sāma can be worshipped through the udgītha . Now another way of worshipping Sāma is being shown—through stobhas . Stobhas are syllables such as hiṃ, hāu, hāi, and so forth, and they are all from the Sāma Veda . By themselves these syllables have no meaning. They are symbols representing objects, or they are used to fill in gaps in sentences. The idea here is that stobhas like hāu should be applied to the earth, fire, etc. A symbol has to have something in common with the object it represents. The Sāma Veda says, ‘This earth is also called rathantara .’ And in the Sāma, the stobha hāu stands for rathantara. Thus, it is reasonable to say that hāu stands for the earth. The stobha hāi occurs in the Sāma called Vāmadevya . The Vāmadevya Sāma is about the relationship of air with water. This is why vāyu (air) is represented by the symbol hāi . Why is the moon represented by atha? This world is sustained by food ( anna ), and the moon and food are identical. A stands for anna, and tha stands for stha in sthita, which means ‘sustained.’ A plus tha is atha . Thus, atha can rightly be said to represent the moon. Then the self is said to be iha, which means ‘here’ or ‘this, ’ for the self is obvious to everyone. And the stobha ī represents fire, because wherever fire is referred to in the Sāma, the words end with ī . The syllable ī then is rightly used as a symbol of fire, and to meditate on this symbol is to meditate on fire.
claim_candidates: The stobha syllable hāu is a justified symbol for the earth because the Sāma Veda identifies earth as rathantara and hāu stands for rathantara
The syllable atha represents the moon by linking a (anna, food) with tha (stha, sustained), indicating the moon’s role in sustaining the world
The self is symbolized by the syllable iha, which means here or this, showing that the self is immediately present and obvious
objection_hooks: The association of hāu with earth depends on an external naming convention rather than an intrinsic quality of the syllable, making the symbol potentially arbitrary
Deriving atha from anna and stha may be a forced etymology that does not establish a natural connection between the moon and sustenance
Identifying iha with the self conflates the empirical sense of self with the transcendental Atman, which Advaita describes as beyond mere locality

sanskrit: आदित्य ऊकारो निहव एकारो विश्वे देवा औहोयिकारः प्रजपतिर्हिंकारः प्राणः स्वरोऽन्नं या वाग्विराट्
transliteration: āditya ūkāro nihava ekāro viśve devā auhoyikāraḥ prajapatirhiṃkāraḥ prāṇaḥ svaro'nnaṃ yā vāgvirāṭ
translation: Āditya, the sun, is represented by the stobha ū; nihava, the welcoming hymn, by the stobha e; the Viśvadeva gods by the stobha auhoyi; Prajāpati by the stobha hiṃ; prāṇa by the stobha svara; food by the stobha yā; and Virāṭ by the stobha vāk .
word_meaning: Ādityaiḥ Āditya, the sun; ūkāraḥ, [is represented by] the stobha ‘ū’; nihavaiḥ the welcoming address; ekāraḥ, [is represented by] the stobha ‘e’; viśvedevāḥ, the Viśvadevas [i.e., a class of gods]; auhoyi- kāraḥ, [are represented by] the stobha ‘auhoyi’; prajāpatiḥ, Prajāpati [the Lord of all beings]; hiṃkāraḥ, [is represented by] the stobha ‘hiṃ’; prāṇaḥ, prāṇa [the presiding deity of life]; svaraḥ, [is represented by] the stobha ‘svara’; annam, food; yā, [is represented by] the stobha ‘yā’; virāṭ Virāṭ; vāk, [is represented by] the stobha ‘vāk’.
commentary: Āditya, the sun, is far above in the sky. The letter ū suggests urdhvam, which means ‘above.’ So. in The word nihava means ‘welcome, ’ which in Sanskrit is ehi . For this reason e stands for the nihava, the welcoming hymn. The stobha auhoyi stands for the Viśvadevas, the gods, for it occurs in the Sāma in honour of the Viśvadevas. The stobha hiṃ represents Prajāpati. No one knows the meaning of the word hiṃ; similarly, no one knows what Prajāpati, the Lord of all beings, is like. Prāṇa is represented by svara, because svara, the musical scale, is derived from prāṇa (the Lord of life, or the vital breath). Yā represents anna, food, for yāti (going) is possible because of food. The stobha vāk occurs in the Sāma called Vairāja or Virāṭa . Thus vāk stands for Virāṭ.
claim_candidates: The verse lists specific stobha letters as symbols for cosmic principles such as the sun, life‑force and speech
The commentary links the letter ū to urdhvam ‘above’, thereby encoding the sun’s spatial position in its phonetic form
The commentary admits that the meaning of hiṃ and the nature of Prajapati are unknown, showing that the symbolic scheme contains unresolved elements
objection_hooks: One might contend that the verse merely records correspondences without claiming an ontological identity between letters and entities
The association of ū with ‘above’ could be seen as a speculative etymology rather than a definitive symbolic rule
The admission of ignorance about hiṃ does not necessarily weaken the overall symbolic system, as symbols can function even when some meanings are opaque

sanskrit: अनिरुक्तस्त्रयोदशः स्तोभः संचरो हुंकारः
transliteration: aniruktastrayodaśaḥ stobhaḥ saṃcaro huṃkāraḥ
translation: The thirteenth stobha huṃ is not clearly defined. Various scholars have defined it in various ways. [Thus, it is up to people to meditate on it as they like.]
word_meaning: Sañcaraḥ, variously interpreted; aniruktaḥ, not clearly defined; trayodaśaḥ stobhaḥ, the thirteenth stobha; huṅkāraḥ, the syllable ‘huṃ’.
commentary: Earlier, twelve stobhas were explained along with how they could be used for meditation. Those stobhas are: hāu, hāi, atha, iha, ī, ū, e, auhoyi, hiṃ, svara, yā, and vāk . Now the thirteenth stobha, huṃ, is being discussed. The Upaniṣad says here, however, that no one knows for certain what huṃ means, so people are free to meditate on it as they please.
claim_candidates: The Upanishadic text states that the meaning of the thirteenth stobha huṃ is not clearly defined, granting meditators freedom to interpret it as they wish
Because huṃ lacks a fixed definition, it functions more as a personal symbolic aid than a doctrinal assertion
The contrast between the precisely explained twelve stobhas and the ambiguous thirteenth indicates a shift from prescribed to open-ended meditation practice
objection_hooks: The claim of interpretive freedom may appear to contradict the Upanishadic emphasis on authoritative teaching found in the earlier stobhas
Other traditional commentaries assign specific metaphysical meanings to huṃ, challenging the view that it is merely a personal symbol
The earlier stobhas also allowed varied meditative applications, so the alleged pedagogical shift might be overstated

sanskrit: दुग्धेऽस्मै वाग्दोहं यो वाचो दोहोऽन्नवानन्नादो भवति य एतामेवंसाम्नामुपनिषदं वेदोपनिषदं वेदेति
transliteration: dugdhe'smai vāgdohaṃ yo vāco doho'nnavānannādo bhavati ya etāmevaṃsāmnāmupaniṣadaṃ vedopaniṣadaṃ vedeti
translation: To a person who knows the Sāma with its stobhas, as mentioned earlier, the organ of speech gives of its best. Such a person gets plenty of food to eat and can also eat much food.
word_meaning: Yaḥ, that person who; etām evam, this as stated earlier; sāmnām, the Sāma with its stobhas; upaniṣadam, the science of it; veda, knows; vācaḥ, of the organ of speech; yaḥ dohaḥ, the essence; doham, [that] essence; vāk, the organ of speech; asmai, to him [who knows]; dugdhe, gives; [saḥ, he]; annavān, rich with food; annādaḥ, a great eater of food; bhavati, becomes.
commentary: In the previous verses, mention was made of the stobhas. Now the benefit of meditating on them is being described. A person who knows the real import of those stobhas receives the best that the organ of speech has to offer.
claim_candidates: The verse states that a person who knows the Sāma with its stobhas receives the best that the organ of speech can give, resulting in abundant food
The commentary explains that meditating on the stobhas brings material benefit, specifically plentiful nourishment
Knowing the upaniṣadic science (upaniṣadam) is presented as a cause of material prosperity in the form of ample food
objection_hooks: One might argue that equating Upanishadic knowledge with material reward conflicts with the Upanishadic goal of liberation beyond worldly gains
The description of the organ of speech providing food could be metaphorical, so interpreting it as literal abundance may misrepresent the verse's intended meaning

sanskrit: समस्तस्य खलु साम्न उपासनं साधु यत्खलु साधु तत्सामेत्याचक्षते यदसाधु तदसामेति
transliteration: samastasya khalu sāmna upāsanaṃ sādhu yatkhalu sādhu tatsāmetyācakṣate yadasādhu tadasāmeti
translation: It is good to worship the Sāma with all its parts. All that is good, according to scholars, is called sāma . Similarly, all that is bad is asāma .
word_meaning: Samastasya khalu sāmnaḥ upāsanam, the worship of the Sāma as a whole [inclusive of all its parts]; sādhu, [is] good; yat khalu sādhu, whatever is good; tat sāma iti ācakṣate, that is called sāma; yat asādhu, whatever is bad; tat asāma iti, that is asāma.
commentary: Much has been said already about the Sāma and the udgītha . And it has also been said that much good follows from their worship. Similarly, the Upaniṣad has discussed the stobhas and shown their importance. So long, the Sāma has been discussed in parts. Now it will be discussed as a whole. The point of this verse is to emphasize how beautiful it is to worship the Sāma. The Sāma is beautiful and to worship the Sāma is beautiful. The qualifying word used is sādhu . Sādhu means ‘good, ’ ‘beautiful, ’ ‘chaste, ’ ‘elegant, ’ and so on. It also means ‘honest, ’ ‘morally sound, ’ ‘beneficial, ’ ‘perfect, ’ and ‘above reproach.’ Anything opposed to sādhu is asāma .
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse declares that worship of the Sāma as a whole is good
It equates anything called Sāma with the quality sādhu and anything not called Sāma with asāma
The commentary expands sādhu to include moral, aesthetic and ethical virtues, suggesting that worship of Sāma is valued on multiple dimensions
objection_hooks: Labeling worship as good does not prove that the act yields moral benefit; the verse may be prescriptive rather than evidential
A strict binary of Sāma versus asāma may overlook intermediate or context‑dependent moral states
The commentary’s list of meanings for sādhu may be context specific, so extending all those qualities to the act of worship could be an overgeneralization

sanskrit: तदुताप्याहुः साम्नैनमुपागादिति साधुनैनमुपागादित्येव तदाहुरसाम्नैनमुपागादित्यसाधुनैनमुपगादित्येव तदाहुः
transliteration: tadutāpyāhuḥ sāmnainamupāgāditi sādhunainamupāgādityeva tadāhurasāmnainamupāgādityasādhunainamupagādityeva tadāhuḥ
translation: This is why people say, ‘He has succeeded in getting access to that distinguished person by virtue of sāma, ’ when they mean he has gone to that distinguished person by honest and legitimate means. Similarly, they say, ‘By virtue of asāma he went to that distinguished person, ’ when they mean he got to that person by unethical means.
