word_meaning: Tat uta āhuḥ api, this is why people say; sāmnā, by virtue of sāma; enam, a [high-placed] person; upāgāt iti, someone got access to; sādhunā, [they mean] by good conduct; enam upāgāt iti eva, he got access to him; tat āhuḥ, [similarly] this is what people say; asāmnā, by virtue of asāma; enam upāgāt iti, he got access to him; asādhunā, [when they mean] by bad conduct; enam upāgāt iti eva, he got access to him; tat āhuḥ, this is what people say.
commentary: The question is: What is good and what is bad? Suppose you have to meet a very distinguished person who is far above you in status. It is very difficult to meet that person, but without doing anything wrong you are, somehow or other, able to meet him. This is called sādhu —fair, honest, good, beautiful, decent, elegant. The opposite of this is asādhu —bad, ugly, dishonest, condemnable.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse differentiates between success achieved through honest means (sādhu) and success achieved through dishonest means (asādhu), implying that conduct determines the legitimacy of one's attainment
The commentary defines sādhu and asādhu as fair or unfair conduct, indicating that ethical quality is inseparable from the method of reaching a distinguished person
Societal judgments about a person's access to a high‑placed individual are based on perceived conduct rather than the result itself, reflecting a cultural emphasis on means
objection_hooks: The passage may simply describe common sayings without asserting a normative rule, so the claim that conduct determines legitimacy could be an over‑interpretation

The terms sādhu and asādhu are explained in the commentary in moral language, but such moral categories may be context‑dependent, challenging the universality of the claim that ethical conduct is inseparable from means

sanskrit: अथोताप्याहुः साम नो बतेति यत्साधु भवति साधु बतेत्येव तदाहुरसाम नो बतेति यदसाधु भवत्यसाधु बतेत्येव तदाहुः
transliteration: athotāpyāhuḥ sāma no bateti yatsādhu bhavati sādhu batetyeva tadāhurasāma no bateti yadasādhu bhavatyasādhu batetyeva tadāhuḥ
translation: Then when something good happens, people say, ‘It is sāma for us, ’ when they mean that it is good for them. But when something bad happens, people say, ‘It is asāma for us, ’ when they mean that it is bad for them.
word_meaning: Atha uta api, then also; āhuḥ, [people] say; naḥ bata sāma iti, it is sāma for us; yat sādhu bhavati, that which is good; sādhu bata id eva tat āhuḥ, the words mean that what is good has happened; naḥ bata asāma iti, [similarly, people say] it is asāma for us; yat asādhu bhavati, that which is bad; asādhu bata id eva tat āhuḥ, they mean to say that what is bad has happened.
commentary: The words sādhu and sāma are synonymous. When people say, ‘We have had something sāma happen, ’ they mean that they have had something sādhu happen. Both the words mean the same thing: good, beautiful, fair, and so on.
claim_candidates: In the Upanishadic passage the terms sādhu and sāma are used interchangeably as symbols for the same positive quality
The verse shows that linguistic labels such as sāma and asāma are conventional and do not change the underlying reality of good or bad
According to the commentary the perception of events as good or bad is mediated by subjective speech rather than by any objective transformation
objection_hooks: Some scholars note that sādhu and sāma have distinct ritual meanings in other contexts, which challenges the claim of complete interchangeability
Vedic philosophy sometimes attributes intrinsic power to sacred utterances, so the view that labels are merely conventional may be disputed

sanskrit: स य एतदेवं विद्वानसाधु सामेत्युपास्तेऽभ्याशो ह यदेनं साधवो धर्मा आ च गच्छेयुरुप च नमेयुः
transliteration: sa ya etadevaṃ vidvānasādhu sāmetyupāste'bhyāśo ha yadenaṃ sādhavo dharmā ā ca gaccheyurupa ca nameyuḥ
translation: If a person knows the Sāma as such and worships it with the awareness of the great qualities it possesses, those qualities very soon manifest themselves in him and become a source of happiness.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, whoever; etat, this [Sāma]; evam vidvān, knows in this way; sādhu sāma iti upāste, [and] worships Sāma as ‘sādhu’; enam, to this worshipper; sādhavaḥ dharmāḥ, the good qualities that are associated with a perfect [sādhu] person; abhyāśaḥ ha, quickly; yat āgaccheyuḥ ca, come; upa ca nameyuḥ, and are a source of satisfaction.
commentary: First and foremost, a person should know the good qualities of the Sāma. But that is not enough. He should also worship the Sāma along with those good qualities. Then those qualities will soon manifest themselves in him, and they will eventually become a great source of satisfaction too.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Knowing the Sāma's qualities and worshipping it causes those qualities to appear quickly in the worshipper
The qualities that appear become a lasting source of happiness for the worshipper
The verse states that intellectual knowledge alone is insufficient; devotional worship is required for the transformation
objection_hooks: One could question whether worship alone can generate inner qualities or whether innate disposition is also necessary
The claim of rapid manifestation may be idealized, as spiritual qualities often develop gradually
Even if qualities manifest, they may not necessarily guarantee satisfaction or happiness

sanskrit: लोकेषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत पृथिवी हिंकारः । अग्निः प्रस्तावोऽन्तरिक्षमुद्गीथ आदित्यः प्रतिहारो द्यौर्निधनमित्यूर्ध्वेषु
transliteration: lokeṣu pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāsīta pṛthivī hiṃkāraḥ | agniḥ prastāvo'ntarikṣamudgītha ādityaḥ pratihāro dyaurnidhanamityūrdhveṣu
translation: One should worship the Sāma in a fivefold manner, treating the different parts as symbols of the worlds.
word_meaning: Pañcavidham sāma upāsīta, one should worship the Sāma in a fivefold manner; lokeṣu, as the worlds [such as the earth]; hiṃkāraḥ, [for instance, thinking of] the syllable hiṃ; pṛthivī, [as] the earth; prastāvaḥ agniḥ, the prastāva as fire; udgīthaḥ antarikṣam, the udgītha as the sky; pratihāraḥ ādityaḥ, the pratihāra as the sun; nidhanam dyauḥ, the nidhana as heaven; ūrdhveṣu iti, which is up above.
commentary: What is the meaning of sādhu ? It may mean either dharma or Brahman . Both, however, mean the same thing, more or less. Those who worship Sāma should know that they are worshipping either of these two, and that they are worshipping something uplifting, something propitious (sādhu). When a person worships thus, he becomes what he is worshipping. He becomes a new person altogether. He is totally transformed. But where is a person to find the Sāma? This Sāma is everywhere, in everything—in the earth, in fire, in the sky, in the sun, and in heaven. But since you cannot approach all of these, you can worship them through their symbols. In this verse, five symbols have been mentioned, which can be used for worship: hiṃkāra, prastāva, udgītha, pratihāra, and nidhana. This fivefold worship of Sāma is being recommended here for everyone.
claim_candidates: The verse prescribes a fivefold symbolic worship of Sāma using hiṃkāra prastāva udgītha pratihāra and nidhana as representations of the five worlds
The commentary identifies Sāma with both dharma and Brahman, implying that worship of Sāma is essentially worship of Brahman
The commentary states that worshiping Sāma through its symbols transforms the worshipper into a new person, indicating that symbolic worship leads to spiritual transformation
objection_hooks: Identifying Sāma with both dharma and Brahman may conflate ethical duty with ultimate reality, raising tension between moral and metaphysical concepts
Relying on symbolic worship may be seen as insufficient for realizing Brahman, since Advaita stresses direct knowledge over ritual symbols

sanskrit: अथावृत्तेषु द्यौर्हिंकार आदित्यः प्रस्तावोऽन्तरिक्षमुद्गीथोऽग्निः प्रतिहारः पृथिवी निधनम्
transliteration: athāvṛtteṣu dyaurhiṃkāra ādityaḥ prastāvo'ntarikṣamudgītho'gniḥ pratihāraḥ pṛthivī nidhanam
translation: Now, the fivefold worship from the highest world to the lowest: heaven is hiṃkāra, the sun is prastāva, the sky is udgītha, fire is pratihāra, and the earth is nidhana .
word_meaning: Atha, next; āvṛtteṣu, from the highest [world] to the lowest; dyauḥ hiṃkāraḥ, the heaven is the syllable hiṃ; ādityaḥ prastāvaḥ, the sun is the prastāva; antarikṣam udgīthaḥ, the sky is the udgītha; agniḥ pratihāraḥ, fire is the pratihāra; pṛthivī nidhanam, the earth is nidhana.
commentary: As Sāma is everywhere, it is also in the five worlds most familiar to us. Starting from the top, these five worlds are dyauḥ (heaven), āditya (the sun), antarikṣa (the sky), agni (fire), and pṛthivī (the earth). To meditate on Sāma in these worlds, then, we can use the five symbols, which are respectively: hiṃkāra, prastāva, udgītha, pratihāra, and nidhana. When we use the term symbol, we understand that there is always a connection between the symbol and the thing symbolized. In this case, hiṃkāra is the first among the symbols, and heaven is the highest among the worlds. This is why hiṃkāra stands for heaven. Prastāva stands for the sun, for when the sun rises everyone gets ready to work. The word prastāva means ‘getting ready.’ The sky is gagana . It begins with ga, and the word udgītha also contains ga . So udgītha stands for gagana, the sky. Pratihāra stands for fire, because fire makes people ‘scatter’ ( pratiharim ). The earth is nidhana (extinction), for all things fall from above and finally disappear on the earth.
claim_candidates: The verse outlines a fivefold worship that assigns each of the five worlds a distinct symbolic term – hiṃkāra for heaven, prastāva for the sun, udgītha for the sky, pratihāra for fire, and nidhana for earth
The commentary explains that each symbol is chosen because of a linguistic or functional link, such as udgītha containing the sound ga which connects it to gagana (the sky)
According to the commentary, meditating on Sāma in each world is achieved by focusing on the corresponding symbol, indicating a practice that uses symbolic contemplation in place of external ritual
objection_hooks: The association of udgītha with the sky rests only on a shared phoneme, which may appear arbitrary and weaken the claim of a meaningful symbolic correspondence
The text does not explicitly state that symbolic meditation replaces ritual worship, so the claim that symbols serve as a substitute may exceed what is directly supported

The hierarchy that places hiṃkāra first because heaven is highest is asserted without further justification, leaving open the question of why a syllable should outrank symbols linked to more active phenomena like the sun or fire

sanskrit: कल्पन्ते हास्मै लोका ऊर्ध्वाश्चावृत्ताश्च य एतदेवं विद्वाँल्लोकेषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते
transliteration: kalpante hāsmai lokā ūrdhvāścāvṛttāśca ya etadevaṃ vidvām̐llokeṣu pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāste
translation: He who worships Sāma with the above knowledge, and worships it in the fivefold manner as described, has all these worlds, from the lowest to the highest and from the highest to the lowest, for his enjoyment.
word_meaning: Yaḥ, he who; etaṭ, this [Sāma]; evam, as such [i.e., as good]; vidvān, having known; lokeṣu, the worlds [the earth, etc.]; pañcavidham, fivefold [i.e., using hiṃ and the other four symbols]; sāma upāste, worships the Sāma; ūrdhvāḥ ca, going upwards; āvṛttāḥ ca, and coming downwards; lokāḥ ha asmai kalpante, the worlds are there for him to enjoy.
commentary: What benefit do you derive from worshipping Sāma in the worlds? The benefit is that you have all the five worlds—from the lowest to the highest and from the highest to the lowest—for your enjoyment. This is because you know Sāma is the essence of everything in these worlds.
claim_candidates: Worship of Sāma in the fivefold manner grants the worshipper enjoyment of all worlds, both upward and downward
The benefit of such worship is attributed to the worshipper’s knowledge that Sāma is the essence of everything in the worlds
The fivefold worship prescribed involves the use of hiṃ and four additional symbols as indicated by the term pañcavidha
objection_hooks: The claim that one obtains literal enjoyment of all worlds may be interpreted metaphorically, raising doubt about the material scope of the benefit
Knowing Sāma as the essence of everything does not necessarily entail material enjoyment, challenging the causal link asserted in the commentary
Advaita emphasizes knowledge over ritual, so the requirement of a fivefold symbolic worship could be seen as contradictory to the primacy of self‑realisation

sanskrit: वृष्टौ पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत पुरोवातो हिंकारो मेघो जायते स प्रस्तावो वर्षति स उद्गीथो विद्योतते स्तनयति स प्रतिहार उद्गृह्णाति तन्निधनम्
transliteration: vṛṣṭau pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāsīta purovāto hiṃkāro megho jāyate sa prastāvo varṣati sa udgītho vidyotate stanayati sa pratihāra udgṛhṇāti tannidhanam
translation: One can perform the fivefold Sāma worship during the rain. Think of the wind that comes before the rain as hiṃkāra. The clouds that gather are the prastāva, and the rain that follows is the udgītha . Then, when the lightning flashes and the thunder roars, that is pratihāra .
word_meaning: Vṛṣṭau, in the rain; pañcavidham sāma upāsīta, one can perform the fivefold Sāma worship; purovātaḥ, the wind that starts before the rainfall; hiṃkāraḥ, is hiṃkāra; meghaḥ jāyate, the clouds that gather; saḥ prastāvaḥ, that is the prastāva; varṣati saḥ udgīthaḥ, [when] the rain falls that is the udgītha; vidyotate, [when] the lightning flashes; stanayati, [and] roars; saḥ pratihāra, that is the pratihāra.
commentary: The syllable hiṃ indicates the beginning of something. When the wind starts blowing hard, we know that it will soon rain, so that is hiṃkāra. Then, when the clouds start gathering, that is the prastāva, for it means that rain is just about to start. Soon the rain follows. That is the udgītha, for the udgītha is always welcome. It is a blessing. The clouds produce lightning, and lightning is accompanied by thunder. That is the pratihāra, for the pratihāra is that which ‘scatters, ’ or ‘spreads out, ’ or is ‘extensive.’
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The fivefold Sāma worship can be performed by meditating on natural phenomena associated with rain, mapping each phenomenon to a specific stage of worship
The syllable hiṃ, representing the wind before rain, signifies the beginning of the worship process, indicating that sound symbols correspond to natural events
The pratihāra, identified with thunder, is characterized as that which scatters or spreads out, suggesting that the final stage of worship embodies the expansive nature of divine revelation
objection_hooks: Equating external natural events with internal ritual stages may externalize the practice, conflicting with Advaita’s emphasis on inner realization
Identifying hiṃ with the wind’s onset appears arbitrary since hiṃ is traditionally linked to fire, raising doubts about the consistency of bija symbolism
Describing pratihāra as scattering implies multiplicity, which seems at odds with Advaita’s doctrine of non‑dual unity

sanskrit: सर्वास्वप्सु पञ्चविधंसामोपासीत मेघो यत्सम्प्लवते स हिंकारो यद्वर्षति स प्रस्तावो याः प्राच्यः स्यन्दन्ते स उद्गीथो याः प्रतीच्यः स प्रतिहारः समुद्रो निधनम्
transliteration: sarvāsvapsu pañcavidhaṃsāmopāsīta megho yatsamplavate sa hiṃkāro yadvarṣati sa prastāvo yāḥ prācyaḥ syandante sa udgītho yāḥ pratīcyaḥ sa pratihāraḥ samudro nidhanam
translation: One can perform the fivefold Sāma worship in all kinds of water. The coming together of scattered clouds is hiṃkāra. That which pours forth rain is the prastāva . The udgītha is that [river] running eastward, and that which runs westward is the pratihāra . The sea is the nidhana .
word_meaning: Sarvāsu apsu, in all kinds of water; pañcavidham sāma upāsīta, a person should perform the fivefold Sāma worship; meghaḥ yat saṃplavate, the clouds which join together and consolidate; saḥ hiṃkāraḥ, that is hiṃkāra; yat varṣati, that which pours rain; saḥ prastāvaḥ, that is the prastāva; yāḥ prācyaḥ syandante, that [water, or river] which goes eastward; saḥ udgīthaḥ, that is the udgītha; yāḥ pratīcyaḥ, that which goes westward; saḥ pratihāraḥ, that is the pratihāra; samudraḥ nidhanam, the sea is the nidhana.
commentary: The fivefold Sāma worship can be performed in all forms of water. For instance, when clouds come together to produce rain, that can be thought of as hiṃkāra, because the syllable hiṃ marks the beginning. And when rain starts falling, that is thought of as the prastāva, because the rain is ‘ready’ to scatter in all directions. (This ‘readiness’ is prastāva.) The water, or river, that flows eastward (the Ganges, for instance) is called the udgītha, because both represent excellence. The water flowing westward ( pratīcya ) is the pratihāra, because of the prefix prāti being common to both. The sea is the nidhana, because when the water flows into the sea it loses its separate identity, which is ‘death’ (nidhana).
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse states that the fivefold Sāma worship can be performed in all kinds of water, implying that natural water phenomena serve as valid venues for worship
The sea is described as nidhana, meaning that when water merges into the sea it loses its separate identity, symbolising a form of death or dissolution of individuality
The gathering of clouds is called hiṃkāra because the syllable hiṃ marks the beginning, linking a natural event with a primordial sound
objection_hooks: One might argue that worship requires conscious ritual intent, so merely observing water phenomena does not constitute genuine Sāma worship
Equating the sea with death could be contested, since other Upanishadic passages treat death as a transition rather than a loss of identity
The connection between hiṃkāra and the syllable hiṃ may be a linguistic note rather than a substantive metaphysical assertion

sanskrit: न हाप्सु प्रैत्यप्सुमान्भवति य एतदेवं विद्वान्सर्वास्वप्सु पञ्चविधंसामोपास्ते
transliteration: na hāpsu praityapsumānbhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānsarvāsvapsu pañcavidhaṃsāmopāste
translation: He who performs the fivefold Sāma worship in all forms of water, knowing it thus, will never be drowned in water unless he wishes to be, and he will have as much water as he wants.
word_meaning: Yaḥ, he who; evam, as mentioned; etat, this [Sāma]; vidvān, having known; sarvāsu apsu, in all forms of water; pañcavidham sāma upāste, performs the fivefold worship of Sāma; [saḥ, that worshipper]; apsu, in water; na ha praiti, does not die [unless he wants to]; apsumān bhavati, he has much water at his disposal.
commentary: A person who performs this Sāma worship, taking water as the object of worship, will never be drowned unless he himself seeks his death that way. Also, he may be so lucky about water that he will even get it in a desert.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: Performing the fivefold Sāma worship in all waters guarantees safety from drowning unless the worshipper wills his own death
The same worship provides an abundant supply of water even in desert conditions
The verse demonstrates that ritual conduct directly determines physical fate, illustrating the principle that conduct determines destiny
objection_hooks: The promise of safety from drowning may be intended metaphorically rather than as a literal guarantee
The claim of obtaining water in a desert could be a symbolic expression of spiritual abundance, not a material promise
Other Upanishadic teachings prioritize knowledge over ritual, challenging the idea that worship alone controls one’s destiny

sanskrit: ऋतुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत वसन्तो हिंकारः ग्रीष्मः प्रस्तावो वर्षा उद्गीथः शरत्प्रतिहारो हेमन्तो निधनम्
transliteration: ṛtuṣu pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāsīta vasanto hiṃkāraḥ grīṣmaḥ prastāvo varṣā udgīthaḥ śaratpratihāro hemanto nidhanam
translation: One can apply the same fivefold Sāma worship formula to the seasons. Treat spring as hiṃkāra, summer as the prastāva, the rainy season as the udgītha, autumn as the pratihāra, and winter as the nidhana .
word_meaning: Ṛtuṣu, in the seasons [spring, etc.]; pañcavidham sāma upāsīta, one should perform the fivefold Sāma worship; vasantaḥ hiṃkāraḥ, spring is hiṃkāra; grīṣmaḥ prastāvaḥ, summer is the prastāva; varṣāḥ udgīthaḥ, the rainy season is the udgītha; śarat pratihāraḥ, autumn
commentary: Spring is the first among the seasons, just as hiṃkāra is the first among the Sāma stobhas. Summer is the prastāva, because in summer people ‘get ready’ to harvest. Then, the rainy season is very important for crops, just as the udgītha is important. Autumn is thought of as pratihāra, for that is the time when the old and the sick start being ‘taken away.’ Finally, winter is the nidhana, for that is the time when many people die.
claim_candidates: The verse maps each season to a specific Sāma worship category, showing that natural cycles can function as a framework for ritual practice
The commentary links the seasonal assignments to concrete agricultural and social activities, indicating that the symbolism is grounded in everyday human life
Winter is identified as nidhana, the time of many deaths, suggesting that the Sāma worship acknowledges mortality as part of the cosmic order
objection_hooks: Equating seasonal changes with ritual categories may reduce the transcendental aspect of Sāma worship to mere worldly phenomena, challenging its spiritual depth
The seasonal symbolism relies on agricultural cycles specific to certain cultures, which may limit its universal applicability as a structured worship system

Viewing winter as nidhana emphasizes death and could be seen as neglecting the regenerative aspects of winter, questioning the claim that the system fully acknowledges the cyclical nature of existence

sanskrit: कल्पन्ते हास्मा ऋतव ऋतुमान्भवति य एतदेवं विद्वानृतुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते
transliteration: kalpante hāsmā ṛtava ṛtumānbhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānṛtuṣu pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāste
translation: To the person who knows this principle of the fivefold Sāma worship and applies it to the seasons thus, the seasons become a source of enjoyment, and the best things that each of them has to offer present themselves to him.
word_meaning: Yaḥ, he who; etat evam vidvān, having known this [Sāma] as above; ṛtuṣu, in the seasons; pañcavi- dham sāma upāste, performs this fivefold Sāma worship; asmai, to him; ṛtavaḥ kalpante, the seasons come for his enjoyment; ha ṛtumān bhavati, and he also gets the pleasant things of those seasons for his enjoyment.
commentary: The change of seasons does not bother such a worshipper. Each of them is pleasant to hiṃ, and the best things that each of them has are easily available to him.
claim_candidates: Performing the fivefold Sāma worship aligns the practitioner with the natural cycles, causing the seasons themselves to become sources of enjoyment
The verse indicates that intellectual knowledge of the worship principle is required for the benefits, making understanding a prerequisite for efficacy
The commentary asserts that the worshipper is unaffected by seasonal change, implying the practice transcends external conditions
objection_hooks: The enjoyment of seasons may be metaphorical, referring to inner contentment rather than literal pleasure
Benefits might arise from sincere practice without theoretical knowledge, challenging the claim that understanding is essential
Physical reality still subjects the worshipper to seasonal effects, questioning the extent to which the practice truly renders one unaffected

sanskrit: पशुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीताजा हिंकारोऽवयः प्रस्तावो गाव उद्गीथोऽश्वाः प्रतिहारः पुरुषो निधनम्
transliteration: paśuṣu pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāsītājā hiṃkāro'vayaḥ prastāvo gāva udgītho'śvāḥ pratihāraḥ puruṣo nidhanam
translation: This is how a person can perform the fivefold Sāma worship in animals. Think of goats as hiṃkāra, sheep as the prastāva, cows as the udgītha, horses as the pratihāra, and human beings as the nidhana .
word_meaning: Paśuṣu, in animals; pañcavidham sāma upāsīta, one can perform the fivefold Sāma worship; ajāḥ, goats; hiṃkāraḥ, are the syllable hiṃ; avayaḥ prastāvaḥ, sheep are the prastāva; gāvaḥ udgīthaḥ, cows are the udgītha; aśvāḥ pratihāraḥ, horses are the pratihāra; puruṣaḥ nidhanam, a human being is the nidhana.
commentary: Goats are the most common of these animals, and they are also the most widely used in sacrifices. This is why they are given the first place as hiṃkāra. Ajā, goats, and avi, sheep, are often seen together, and they are very similar. So also, hiṃkāra and the prastāva are often together. This is why avi, sheep, are said to be the prastāva. Cows are the udgītha because they are superior to other animals, as the udgītha is superior. Then horses ‘carry’ ( pratiharaṇa ) people, so they are the pratihāra. And as animals ‘depend’ entirely upon human beings, so human beings are their nidhana (here, nidhana means ‘support’).
claim_candidates: The verse establishes a hierarchical ordering of animals for fivefold Sāma worship, assigning goats the highest ritual significance as hiṃkāra
The mapping of each animal to a specific term functions as a symbolic substitution for the actual vocalized syllables in Sāma worship
The verse implies that human beings are the ultimate support (nidhana) for all other animals, indicating a relational hierarchy where humans sustain the animal world
objection_hooks: The hierarchical ranking of animals may conflict with the principle of non‑violence emphasized elsewhere in Upanishadic teachings, raising ethical concerns about privileging goats for sacrifice
Viewing animal categories as mere symbolic substitutes could be argued to diminish the ontological potency of the original syllables, questioning whether they truly replace vocalized worship
The claim that humans support all animals can be challenged by ecological arguments that humans themselves depend on animal ecosystems, reversing the asserted hierarchy

sanskrit: भवन्ति हास्य पशवः पशुमान्भवति य एतदेवं विद्वान्पशुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते
transliteration: bhavanti hāsya paśavaḥ paśumānbhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānpaśuṣu pañcavidhaṃ sāmopāste
translation: He who performs the fivefold Sāma worship in animals, knowing it in this way, gets many animals for his enjoyment, and he also has a large number of animals as his personal wealth.
word_meaning: Yaḥ, he who; etat evam vidvān, having known this [Sāma] in this way; paśuṣu pañcavidham sāma upāste, performs the fivefold Sāma worship in animals; asya paśavaḥ bhavanti ha, he has many animals [for his enjoyment]; paśumān bhavati, he acquires a large number of animals [for his personal wealth].
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that performing the fivefold Sāma worship on animals yields many animals for enjoyment and personal wealth
Knowledge of the correct way to perform Sāma worship is presented as a prerequisite for receiving the promised material benefits
The passage links ritual conduct directly to material prosperity, implying that one’s destiny is shaped by such actions
objection_hooks: The promised material gain may be a hyperbolic teaching device rather than a literal guarantee, limiting the claim’s universality
The term ‘having known this’ could refer merely to intellectual awareness, not necessarily to practical mastery, weakening the claim about knowledge as a prerequisite
The verse addresses a specific ritual context and may not support a broad principle that all conduct determines destiny, making the claim an overgeneralization

sanskrit: प्राणेषु पञ्चविधं परोवरीयः सामोपासीत प्राणो हिंकारो वाक्प्रस्तावश्चक्षुरुद्गीथः श्रोत्रं प्रतिहारो मनो निधनं परोवरीयांसि वा एतानि
transliteration: prāṇeṣu pañcavidhaṃ parovarīyaḥ sāmopāsīta prāṇo hiṃkāro vākprastāvaścakṣurudgīthaḥ śrotraṃ pratihāro mano nidhanaṃ parovarīyāṃsi vā etāni
translation: One should perform the fivefold worship of Sāma in the organs in an increasingly higher way. The organ of smell is hiṃkāra, the organ of speech is the prastāva, the eyes are the udgītha, the ears are the pratihāra, and the mind is the nidhana . These organs should be worshipped, each with greater respect than the previous one.
word_meaning: Prāṇeṣu pañcavidham sāma upāsīta, one should perform the fivefold worship of Sāma in all forms of prāṇa, the vital breath [or, the organs]; parovarīyaḥ, in an increasingly better way; prāṇaḥ hiṃkāraḥ, prāṇa [or, the organ of smell] is hiṃkāra; vāk prastāvaḥ, the organ of speech is the prastāva; cakṣuḥ udgīthaḥ, the eyes are the udgītha; śrotram pratihāraḥ, the ears are the pratihāra; manaḥ nidhanam, the mind is the nidhana; etāni parovarīyāṃsi vai, all these should be worshipped, each more than the one before.
commentary: The first prāṇa is the organ of smelling. This may be regarded as hiṃkāra. Then the organ of speech may be regarded as the prastāva, for through speech we propose ( prastāva ) to do something. The organ of speech is superior to the organ of smelling because we express our thoughts through speech. The eyes are better than the organ of speech, because through the eyes we can express even more than what we can through speech, so the eyes are the udgītha. The ears are the pratihāra, and they are superior to the eyes because the ears can hear more than the eyes can see. The mind is the nidhana, and it is superior to all other organs. Whatever the other organs collect is all stored in the mind. Also the mind can grasp things that no other organ can perceive.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse orders the five sensory organs in a hierarchy, ending with the mind as the supreme organ for worship
Each organ is deemed superior to the previous because it can express or perceive more, as explained in the commentary
The prescribed worship is progressive (parovarīya), meaning respect and devotion increase with each higher organ
objection_hooks: Some philosophical schools rank hearing above sight, challenging the fixed hierarchy presented
The claim that expressive capacity determines superiority relies on subjective assessment rather than an objective metric
Labeling the organ of smell as hiṃkāra may import moral judgment that is not explicit in the verse

sanskrit: परोवरीयो हास्य भवति परोवरीयसो ह लोकाञ्जयति य एतदेवं विद्वान्प्राणेषु पञ्चविधं परोवरीयः सामोपास्त इति तु पञ्चविधस्य
transliteration: parovarīyo hāsya bhavati parovarīyaso ha lokāñjayati ya etadevaṃ vidvānprāṇeṣu pañcavidhaṃ parovarīyaḥ sāmopāsta iti tu pañcavidhasya
translation: When a person knows the fivefold Sāma worship and performs it in the organs, paying to each of the organs more respect than to the last, his life becomes more and more excellent, and he also attains better and better worlds.
word_meaning: Yaḥ, he who; etat evam vidvān, having known this thus; prāṇeṣu, in the organs; pañcavidham sāma upāste, performs the fivefold Sāma worship; parovarīyaḥ, in an increasingly higher order; asya, his [life]; parovarīyaḥ ha bhavati, becomes increasingly more excellent; parovarīyasaḥ ha lokān jāyati, he also attains increasingly higher worlds; iti tu pañcavidhasya, this is the benefit of the fivefold [Sāma worship].
commentary: Here much attention has been given to the fivefold Sāma worship. This is only to prepare the worshipper for the next step, in which he will be asked to perform the sevenfold Sāma worship. It would be quite all right, however, if he bypasses the fivefold worship and goes straight to the sevenfold worship.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The fivefold Sāma worship, when performed with increasing reverence to each organ, makes one’s life increasingly excellent
Mastery of the fivefold Sāma worship results in attaining progressively higher worlds
The fivefold Sāma worship is a preparatory stage for the sevenfold worship, yet one may bypass it and proceed directly to the sevenfold
objection_hooks: One might argue that external ritual reverence to organs cannot alone improve life without inner transformation
The claim that ritual guarantees higher worlds conflicts with the view that liberation depends on knowledge of Brahman rather than ritual merit
Skipping the preparatory fivefold worship may undermine the gradual purification required for the more advanced sevenfold worship

sanskrit: अथ सप्तविधस्य वाचि सप्तविध्ं सामोपासीत यत्किंच वाचो हुमिति स हिंकारो यत्प्रेति स प्रस्तावो यदेति स आदिः
transliteration: atha saptavidhasya vāci saptavidhṃ sāmopāsīta yatkiṃca vāco humiti sa hiṃkāro yatpreti sa prastāvo yadeti sa ādiḥ
translation: Now begins a discussion on the sevenfold Sāma worship. One can perform this sevenfold Sāma worship in speech. Wherever the syllable huṃ occurs in speech, that is hiṃkāra. Similarly, wherever the syllable pra occurs, that is to be taken for the prastāva . And wherever ā occurs, that is ādi [the beginning].
word_meaning: Atha, next; saptavidhasya, [a discussion] on the sevenfold [Sāma worship]; saptavidham sāma upāsīta, one should perform the sevenfold worship of Sāma; vāci, in speech; yāt kiṃca vācaḥ huṃ iti, in whatever speech occurs the syllable ‘huṃ’; saḥ hiṃkāraḥ, that is the syllable ‘hiṃ’; yat pra iti, where the syllable ‘pra’ occurs; saḥ prastāvaḥ, that is the prastāva; yat ā iti, where ‘ā’ occurs; saḥ ādiḥ, that is to be taken for ādi [the beginning].
commentary: The fivefold Sāma worship has been discussed. Now The first meditation is on speech. Sometimes you may come across the sound huṃ in speech. This sound is to be thought of as hiṃkāra, because the letter h is common in both. Then, when you come across the sound pra, that is to be thought of as the prastāva. The sound ā in speech is to be thought of as ādi . But what is ādi? Ādi is that with which you begin something. It is Om, for everything in the Vedas starts with Om.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse identifies the syllable ā as representing ādi, which the commentary equates with Om, implying that Om is the primordial beginning of all Vedic utterances

The worship method involves recognizing specific phonetic elements huṃ, pra, and ā in speech and mentally associating them with hiṃkāra, prastāva, and ādi, showing a meditative practice that internalizes sound as a means to realize ultimate reality

By treating the syllable huṃ as hiṃkāra and pra as prastāva, the verse suggests that ordinary speech can be transformed into a vehicle for spiritual insight, blurring the line between mundane language and sacred mantra
objection_hooks: One might argue that equating the ordinary occurrence of the syllable ā with the cosmic Om overextends the significance of a mere phonetic element, ignoring contextual differences between casual speech and sacred mantra
The identification of huṃ with hiṃkāra could be challenged because hiṃkāra traditionally denotes a specific seed mantra with distinct vibrational qualities, not any occurrence of huṃ in speech

Mapping any speech sounds to sacred concepts may be seen as reducing the transcendental nature of Brahman to linguistic constructs, which conflicts with Advaita’s claim of Brahman’s non‑dual, ineffable reality

sanskrit: यदुदिति स उद्गीथो यत्प्रतीति स प्रतिहारो यदुपेति स उपद्रवो यन्नीति तन्निधनम्
transliteration: yaduditi sa udgītho yatpratīti sa pratihāro yadupeti sa upadravo yannīti tannidhanam
translation: Wherever the syllable ut occurs, that is the udgītha . Where there is prati, that is the pratihāra . Where you find upa, that is the upadrava. And where you find ni, that is the nidhana .
word_meaning: Yat ut iti, where there is the sound ‘ut’; saḥ udgīthaḥ, that is the udgītha; yat prati iti, where there is the sound ‘prati’; saḥ pratihāraḥ, that is the pratihāra; yat upa iti, where there is the sound ‘upa’; saḥ upadravaḥ, that is the upadrava [upadrava is anything in praise of the Sāma ]; yat ni iti, where there is the syllable ‘ni’; tat nidhanam, that is the nidhana.
claim_candidates: The verse states that the occurrence of a particular phoneme (ut, prati, upa, ni) directly determines the classification of a chant as udgitha, pratihara, upadrava, or nidhana
The text treats syllables as functional markers, implying a symbolic system where sound corresponds to specific ritual roles
The identification of the syllable ni with nidhana suggests that even the notion of destruction is encoded in a single phonetic element
objection_hooks: One could argue that the verse merely describes a linguistic convention without asserting any deeper metaphysical symbolism, weakening the claim of a symbolic system
The association of ni with nidhana may be a grammatical observation rather than a meaningful designation, challenging the claim of encoded significance
Variations in Vedic recitation show that the same phoneme can appear in different ritual contexts, which disputes the strict one‑to‑one correspondence claimed

sanskrit: दुग्धेऽस्मै वाग्दोहं यो वाचो दोहोऽन्नवानन्नादो भवति य एतदेवं विद्वान्वाचि सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते
transliteration: dugdhe'smai vāgdohaṃ yo vāco doho'nnavānannādo bhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānvāci saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste
translation: He who knows Sāma in this way, and performs the sevenfold Sāma worship in speech, gets from speech whatever good things it has to offer. He also gets plenty of food to eat, and he eats that food [and thereby looks radiant in health].
word_meaning: Yaḥ, the person who; etat evam vidvān, having known this [about the Sāma]; vāci, in speech; saptavidham sāma upāste, performs the sevenfold Sāma worship; asmai, to him [i.e., to the worshipper]; vāk, speech; doham, milk [i.e., a good, precious gift]; yaḥ vācaḥ dohaḥ, which is the essence of the words; dugdhe, presents; annavān annādaḥ bhavati, he has plenty of food and he eats it [thereby becoming radiant].
claim_candidates: The verse states that a person who knows Sāma and performs its sevenfold worship in speech obtains abundant food and radiance in health
Speech is described as milk (vāk doha), indicating that refined speech is a nourishing and precious gift
The benefits promised by the verse are both material (plenty of food) and bodily (radiant health), showing a direct link between spiritual practice and physical well‑being
objection_hooks: One might argue that emphasizing material gain from worship conflicts with the Advaitic goal of liberation beyond worldly benefits
Equating speech, an intangible subtle faculty, with milk could be seen as a category error that weakens the logical coherence of the metaphor

sanskrit: अथ खल्वमुमादित्यंसप्तविधं सामोपासीत सर्वदा समस्तेन साम मां प्रति मां प्रतीति सर्वेण समस्तेन साम
transliteration: atha khalvamumādityaṃsaptavidhaṃ sāmopāsīta sarvadā samastena sāma māṃ prati māṃ pratīti sarveṇa samastena sāma
translation: Next, without fail, worship the sevenfold Sāma in the sun. The sun is the Sāma because it is always the same. Again, the sun makes each of us think, ‘It is looking at me. It is looking at me.’ Because it is the same to all, it is called Sāma .
word_meaning: Atha, next; khalu, for certain; amum ādityam, the sun over there; saptavidham sāma upāsīta, worship as the sevenfold Sāma; sarvadā samaḥ, [the sun is] always the same; tena, therefore; sāma, [the sun is] Sāma; mām prati mām prati, [for instance, ‘It is looking] at me, [it is looking] at me’; iti, in this way [it makes everyone think]; sarveṇa samaḥ, it is the same to all; tena, for this reason; sāma, [the sun is called] Sāma.
commentary: The question is, how can the sun be identified with the Sāma? The sun is always the same [i.e., sama ], for it never changes. This is where the sun and the Sāma are the same. Again, when we look at the sun, we all think it is turned towards us. Similarly, the Sāma is also the same for everyone.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse identifies the sun with the deity Sāma because the sun is always the same (sama)
The verse teaches that the sun’s perceived personal attention is experienced by everyone, showing that Sāma is equally present for all beings
The instruction to worship the sun as a sevenfold Sāma prescribes a ritual based on the sun’s unchanging nature
objection_hooks: Equating the sun with Sāma solely on sameness may ignore other qualities of Sāma that the sun does not possess
The claim of universal personal attention may be challenged by the fact that individuals experience the sun differently depending on circumstances

sanskrit: तस्मिन्निमानि सर्वाणि भूतान्यन्वायत्तानीति विद्यात्तस्य यत्पुरोदयात्स हिंकारस्तदस्य पशवोऽन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते हिं कुर्वन्ति हिंकारभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः
transliteration: tasminnimāni sarvāṇi bhūtānyanvāyattānīti vidyāttasya yatpurodayātsa hiṃkārastadasya paśavo'nvāyattāstasmātte hiṃ kurvanti hiṃkārabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ
translation: One should know that all beings that exist are dependent on the sun. The sun has a distinctive look before it rises, and that look is its hiṃkāra. The animals, who are dependent on the sun, also have their share in this hiṃkāra. That is why they make the sound hiṃ .
word_meaning: Tasmin, on that [i.e., the sun]; imāni sarvāṇi bhūtāni, all these beings; anvāyattāni, are dependent; iti vidyāt, one should know this; tasya, of that [sun]; yat, the way [it looks then]; purodayāt, before it rises; saḥ, that [form]; hiṃkāraḥ, is hiṃkāra; paśavaḥ, the animals; tat asya anvāyattāḥ, are dependent on that [form] of it [i.e., of the sun as Sāma ]; tasmāt, this is why; te, they; hiṃ kurvanti, make the sound hiṃ; hiṃkārabhājinaḥ hi etasya sāmnaḥ, they share in the hiṃkāra of the Sāma.
commentary: As the sun rises, it has a very pleasant and favourable look. This is called hiṃkāra. It is an expression of love and adoration for the Sāma. The animals are devoted to this hiṃkāra, for they also make the sound hiṃ as the sun rises. This is how they pay their tribute to the hiṃkāra of the Sāma.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse states that all beings are dependent on the sun
The pre‑rising appearance of the sun is called hiṃkāra and is described as an expression of love toward the Sāma
Animals utter hiṃ as a way of sharing in the sun’s hiṃkāra, indicating a devotional tribute
objection_hooks: The dependence may be understood as a physical or ecological relationship rather than an ultimate ontological dependence
The identification of hiṃkāra with love for Sāma is interpretive and not explicitly stated in the verse, allowing alternative readings such as a simple description of a sound

sanskrit: अथ यत्प्रथमोदिते स प्रस्तावस्तदस्य मनुष्या अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्रस्तुतिकामाः प्रशंसाकामाः प्रस्तावभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः
transliteration: atha yatprathamodite sa prastāvastadasya manuṣyā anvāyattāstasmātte prastutikāmāḥ praśaṃsākāmāḥ prastāvabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ
translation: Next, the form that the sun has shortly after it rises is the prastāva . Human beings are charmed by that form. Because they join in the praise and adoration of the prastāva, they seek adoration and praise for themselves.
word_meaning: Athā, next; yatprathamodite [i.e., prathama + udite ], that [form] which [the sun has] when it first rises; saḥ prastāvaḥ, that is the prastāva; manuṣyāḥ, all human beings; tat asya anvāyattāḥ, are charmed by that [form] of it [i.e., of the sun, which is the Sāma ]; tasmāt, this is why; te, they; prastutikāmāḥ, wanting praise; praśaṃsākāmāḥ, wanting adoration; prastāvabhājinaḥ hi etasya sāmnaḥ, they join in the prastāva of the Sāma.
commentary: The beauty that the sun has as it rises in the morning is the Sāma prastāva addressed to the sun god ( Āditya ). Human beings are under the spell of this beauty. And as they have the habit of praising and adoring the prastāva of the Sāma, they also desire praise and adoration for themselves. Praise means the good words you say about a person in his presence, and adoration means the good thoughts you cherish in your mind about that person.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: Human beings are naturally charmed by the aesthetic beauty of the sun’s early morning form as described by the prastāva
The habit of praising the sun’s prastāva leads humans to desire personal praise and adoration
The prastāva of the Sāma serves as a symbolic representation that elicits both external praise and internal admiration
objection_hooks: One could argue that attraction to the sun’s beauty is culturally conditioned rather than an innate human response
The inference that praising the sun causes a desire for self‑praise may be a causal fallacy, as egoic motives could be independent
Viewing the prastāva as a symbolic representation of Brahman may be contested because the verse emphasizes aesthetic appeal over metaphysical meaning

sanskrit: अथ यत्संगववेलायां स आदिस्तदस्य वयांस्यन्वायत्तानि तस्मात्तान्यन्तरिक्षेऽनारम्बणान्यादायात्मानं परिपतन्त्यादिभाजीनि ह्येतस्य साम्नः
transliteration: atha yatsaṃgavavelāyāṃ sa ādistadasya vayāṃsyanvāyattāni tasmāttānyantarikṣe'nārambaṇānyādāyātmānaṃ paripatantyādibhājīni hyetasya sāmnaḥ
translation: Next, when the sun-rays spread all over a short while after sunrise, that form of the sun is the ādi of the Sāma . This form is connected with the birds. They somehow or other feel they have a safe shelter then, and that is why they are able to fly freely about in the sky without any support. They also behave as if they are joining in the ādi offered to the Sāma.
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat, that; saṅgavavelāyām, in the morning [i.e., after sunrise, when the sunlight has spread far and wide]; saḥ ādiḥ, that is the ādi [of the Sāma worship]; vayāṃsi, birds; tat asya, that form of the sun [at that time]; anvāyattāni, makes them feel secure; tasmāt, this is why; tāni, they [the birds]; anārambaṇāni, without any support; ātmānam, their own bodies; ādāya,
commentary: The sight of the sun after sunrise fascinates the birds. The form of the sun at this time is the ādi (or Om) of the Sāma, and the birds feel they are a part of this ādi. They feel secure. Though they have no support, they are able to fly about in the sky freely. It is as if they are joining in the ādi hymn offered to the Sāma. As the birds fly, they depend on their own ‘self’ ( ātman ). Because the words ātman and ādi have the common ā, the birds feel drawn towards the ādi.
claim_candidates: The verse equates the early morning sun's form with the ādi (the primordial syllable Om) of the Sāma hymn

According to the commentary the birds feel secure because they perceive themselves as part of the ādi, suggesting that identification with the sacred syllable can produce a sense of psychological liberation
The birds' ability to fly without external support, relying on their own ātman, illustrates the Upanishadic idea that self‑realisation enables freedom from material dependence
objection_hooks: One might argue that the identification of the sun's form with ādi is metaphorical rather than literal, and the text does not explicitly define ādi as Om, allowing alternative readings

The claim that birds' sense of security stems from identification with ādi may anthropomorphise the birds; the text merely notes their feeling of safety without indicating conscious spiritual identification

Linking the birds' reliance on their own ātman to the philosophical concept of universal self‑realisation may be overstated, as the commentary uses ātman to refer to their bodies rather than the metaphysical Atman

sanskrit: अथ यत्सम्प्रतिमध्यंदिने स उद्गीथस्तदस्य देवा अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते सत्तमाः प्राजापत्यानामुद्गीथभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः
transliteration: atha yatsampratimadhyaṃdine sa udgīthastadasya devā anvāyattāstasmātte sattamāḥ prājāpatyānāmudgīthabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ
translation: Next, that form of the sun which it has exactly at noon is the udgītha . That form is connected with the gods and goddesses. Therefore, among all of Prajāpati ’s children, the gods and goddesses are considered to be the best, because they take part in singing the udgītha of the Sāma .
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat, that; samprati madhyandine, precisely at noon; saḥ udgīthaḥ, that [form of the sun] is the udgītha; devāḥ, the gods and goddesses; tat asya, that form of the sun [at that time]; anvāyattāni, are part of; tasmāt, therefore; prājāpatyānām, among all of Prajāpati’s children; te, they [the gods and goddesses]; sattamāḥ, are deemed the best; hi, because; etasya sāmnaḥ udgīthabhājinaḥ, they join in the udgītha of the Sāma.
commentary: At midday the sun is at its brightest, and its form suggests that it is offering an udgītha. The gods and goddesses are devoted to the sun at this time, so they join in the offering of the udgītha. This is why they are the dearest to Prajāpati of all his children.
claim_candidates: The midday sun is identified as the udgītha, a form that signifies a ritual offering
The gods and goddesses are deemed the best among Prajapati's children because they join in singing the udgītha of the Sāma
The verse links the brightness of the sun at noon with a symbolic act of worship, implying that natural phenomena can serve as mediums for ritual expression
objection_hooks: One might argue that the identification of the sun's form as udgītha is metaphorical rather than literal, so the claim of it signifying a ritual offering may overstate the text
The term best could refer to functional role in ritual rather than moral superiority, making the claim about the gods' superiority ambiguous
The verse does not explicitly state that the sun itself is worshipped, only that its form suggests offering, so the symbolic link between natural phenomena and ritual may be inferred beyond the text

sanskrit: अथ यदूर्ध्वं मध्यंदिनात्प्रागपराह्णात्स प्रतिहारस्तदस्य गर्भा अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्रतिहृतानावपद्यन्ते प्रतिहारभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः
transliteration: atha yadūrdhvaṃ madhyaṃdinātprāgaparāhṇātsa pratihārastadasya garbhā anvāyattāstasmātte pratihṛtānāvapadyante pratihārabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ
translation: Next, between the noon and the afternoon, the sight the sun presents is that of the pratihāra . The foetuses in the wombs are attached to this pratihāra. This is why they are held up and do not drop down, and why they are entitled to take part in the pratihāra addressed to the Sāma .
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat, that; ūrdhvam madhyandināt, after midday; prāk aparāhṇāt, before afternoon; saḥ pratihāra, that is the pratihāra; garbhāḥ, the foetuses in the womb; tat asya, that [form] of [the sun]; anvāyattāḥ are attached to; tasmāt, that is why; te, those [foetuses]; pratihṛtāḥ, are held up; na avapadyante, [and] do not drop down; hi, for that reason; etasya sāmnaḥ pratihārabhājinaḥ, they are entitled to share in the pratihāra of the Sāma.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse states that the sun's pratihāra between noon and afternoon sustains the foetuses in the womb, preventing them from falling
The verse claims that because the foetuses are attached to the sun's pratihāra they are entitled to share in the pratihāra offered to the Sāma, linking cosmic order to ritual participation
objection_hooks: One might argue that the description of the sun holding up foetuses is metaphorical rather than a literal ontological claim about the sun as the source of life

Another objection is that the entitlement to partake in the Sāma's pratihāra could be a ritual prescription without implying a deeper metaphysical identity between individual beings and the universal self

sanskrit: अथ यत्प्रथमास्तमिते तन्निधनं तदस्य पितरोऽन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्तान्निदधति निधनभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्न एवं खल्वमुमादित्यं सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते
transliteration: atha yatprathamāstamite tannidhanaṃ tadasya pitaro'nvāyattāstasmāttānnidadhati nidhanabhājino hyetasya sāmna evaṃ khalvamumādityaṃ saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste
translation: Next, the nidhana is the form of the sun as it sets. The ancestors love this form of the sun, and this is why, as the sun sets, offerings are made to them [or, are placed on straws in honour of them at the time the śrāddha rites are performed]. For this reason, the ancestors participate in the nidhana in honour of the Sāma . This is how the sun is offered the sevenfold Sāma worship.
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat, that; prathamāstamite [he., prathama + astamite ], just as the sun sets; tat nidhanam, that is the nidhana; pitaraḥ, the ancestors; tat asya, that [form] of [the sun]; anvāyattāḥ, are attached to; tasmāt, therefore; tān, them; nidadhati, one places on straws [or, one places offerings to the ancestors on straws while performing special rites in their honour]; hi, for this reason; etasya sāmnaḥ nidhanabhājinaḥ, they participate in the nidhana in honour of the Sāma; khalu amum ādityam saptavidham sāma upāste, this is how the sun is offered the sevenfold Sāma worship.
commentary: Because the ancestors love the form of the sun as it sets, people honour them at that time of the day by placing offerings to them on straw. And while doing so, they sing the nidhana hymn of the Sāma. If a person performs the sevenfold Sāma worship with the feeling that he is worshipping the sun this way, he becomes one with the sun.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: Performing the sevenfold Sāma worship at sunset with the intention of worshipping the sun leads to the worshipper becoming one with the sun
Ancestors are attached to the setting sun, so offerings placed on straws at sunset honor both the ancestors and the sun
The sevenfold Sāma worship represents a progressive form of worship that culminates in identification with the sun
The ritual of placing offerings on straws at sunset signifies a symbolic link between ancestral reverence and solar worship
objection_hooks: The claim of becoming one with the sun may be metaphorical rather than literal, raising questions about ontological identity
Honoring ancestors through the setting sun may conflate ancestor worship with solar worship, potentially conflicting with their distinct roles
The idea of a progressive worship culminating in identity with the sun assumes a hierarchical structure not explicitly stated in the verse

sanskrit: अथ खल्वात्मसंमितमतिमृत्यु सप्तविधं सामोपासीत हिंकार इति त्र्यक्षरं प्रस्ताव इति त्र्यक्षरं तत्समम्
transliteration: atha khalvātmasaṃmitamatimṛtyu saptavidhaṃ sāmopāsīta hiṃkāra iti tryakṣaraṃ prastāva iti tryakṣaraṃ tatsamam
translation: After worshipping the Sāma as the sun, one should perform the sevenfold Sāma worship by using words of the same number of syllables. By this one overcomes death. The word hiṃkāra has three syllables; so also,
word_meaning: Atha, next [i.e., after worshipping the sun as the Sāma]; khalu, for certain; ātma -sammitam, with an equal number of parts [i.e., syllables]; atimṛtyu, that which overcomes death; saptavidham sāma upāsīta, one should perform the sevenfold Sāma worship; hiṃkāraḥ, the word hiṃkāra; iti tryakṣaram, is three-syllabled; prastāvaḥ, the word prastāva; iti tryakṣaram, is [also] three-syllabled; tat samam, therefore they [hiṃkāra and prastāva] are equal.
commentary: Śaṅkara says that the sun divides time into units. It therefore distinguishes between life and death, and it is death itself. Is there any way of overcoming death? Yes, it can be overcome by worshipping the Sāma, and that is why these instructions are being given here.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that performing the sevenfold Sāma worship with syllabically matched words leads to overcoming death
The commentary connects the sun’s division of time to the distinction between life and death, implying that worship of the sun can transcend death
The verse treats the three‑syllable words hiṃkāra and prastāva as equivalent, indicating that syllabic parity is a criterion for ritual efficacy
objection_hooks: One may argue that the verse offers no explanation of how mere syllabic matching can overcome death, leaving the causal link unsubstantiated
The commentary’s claim that the sun distinguishes life and death could be viewed as metaphorical rather than establishing a logical basis for worship to defeat death
Equating hiṃkāra, a term with negative connotations, to prastāva solely on syllable count ignores their semantic differences, questioning the validity of form‑over‑meaning as a spiritual principle

sanskrit: आदिरिति द्व्यक्षरं प्रतिहार इति चतुरक्षरं तत इहैकं तत्समम्
transliteration: ādiriti dvyakṣaraṃ pratihāra iti caturakṣaraṃ tata ihaikaṃ tatsamam
translation: The word ādi is two-syllabled, and the word pratihāra is four-syllabled. If you take away one syllable from pratihāra and add it to ādi, then they will have the same number of syllables.
word_meaning: Ādiḥ iti dvi-akṣaram, the word ādi is two-syllabled; pratihāraḥ iti catuḥ-akṣaram, the word pratihāra is four-syllabled; tataḥ, from that [i.e., from the word pratihāra]; ekam, [take away] one [syllable]; iha, [and add] here [to the word ādi]; tat samam, that makes them equal [both three-syllabled].
commentary: Om is the ādi of the sevenfold Sāma . It is ādi (the beginning) because a person begins singing the Sāma with Om.
claim_candidates: The verse shows that by moving one syllable from pratihāra to ādi the two words become equal in length, illustrating a principle of linguistic equivalence
The commentary states that Om is the ādi (beginning) of the sevenfold Sāma, indicating that Om is conceptually foundational to the musical chant
Identifying Om as the ādi of the Sāma implies that the sacred syllable functions as a structural seed from which larger ritual expressions are derived
objection_hooks: A numerical equality of syllables may not entail any metaphysical significance, so the claim that Om is foundational could be seen as unwarranted
The verse deals only with syllable count and does not address symbolic or ontological primacy, challenging the idea that Om serves as a structural seed
The passage may be a pedagogical illustration of sound manipulation rather than a doctrinal statement, weakening the inference that linguistic adjustment reflects a deeper principle

sanskrit: उद्गीथ इति त्र्यक्षरमुपद्रव इति चतुरक्षरं त्रिभिस्त्रिभिः समं भवत्यक्षरमतिशिष्यते त्र्यक्षरं तत्समम्
transliteration: udgītha iti tryakṣaramupadrava iti caturakṣaraṃ tribhistribhiḥ samaṃ bhavatyakṣaramatiśiṣyate tryakṣaraṃ tatsamam
translation: The word udgītha has three syllables. The word upadrava has four syllables. If they are taken as three-syllabled they are equal. In that case, the syllable va in upadrava becomes superfluous. They are equal so far as their three syllables are concerned.
word_meaning: Udgīthaḥ iti tri-akṣaram, the word udgītha is three-syllabled; upadravaḥ iti cataḥ-akṣaram, the word upadrava is four-syllabled; tribhiḥ tribhiḥ samam bhavati, if they are taken as three-syllabled they become the same; akṣaram atiśiṣyate, one syllable becomes superfluous; tri-akṣaram tat samam, taken as three-syllabled they become identical.
commentary: The syllable va, if taken away, makes no difference when a person recites the hymn.
claim_candidates: The verse states that when only the first three syllables of upadrava are considered it becomes identical to udgitha, rendering the fourth syllable va superfluous
The commentary claims that omitting the syllable va does not affect the recitation of the hymn, indicating that the essential content resides in the three‑syllable core
The passage suggests that the spiritual equivalence of two words can be established by focusing on a shared three‑syllable structure, implying flexibility in ritual language
objection_hooks: One might argue that the omitted syllable va could possess subtle phonetic or vibrational effects that influence the hymn despite being deemed superfluous
Another objection is that reducing upadrava to three syllables ignores its full lexical meaning, so the claimed identity with udgitha may be contextually limited

sanskrit: निधनमिति त्र्यक्षरं तत्सममेव भवति तानि ह वा एतानि द्वाविंशतिरक्षराणि
transliteration: nidhanamiti tryakṣaraṃ tatsamameva bhavati tāni ha vā etāni dvāviṃśatirakṣarāṇi
translation: The word nidhana has three syllables. All words, having three syllables each, are the same [when used in praise of the Sāma ]. All these together have twenty-two syllables.
word_meaning: Nidhanam iti tri-akṣaram, the word nidhana is three-syllabled; tat samam eva bhavati, that makes it the same [as the other three-syllabled words forming parts of the Sāma]; tāni ha vai etāni, all these together constitute; dvāviṃśatiḥ akṣarāṇi, twenty-two syllables.
commentary: There are seven ways of worshipping the Sāma: through hiṃkāra, prastāva, ādi, pratihāra, udgītha, upadrava, and nidhana. Taken together these words have twenty-two syllables. Taken separately each of them may be treated as three-syllabled and recited accordingly. They are therefore all equal for purposes of the Sāma worship.
claim_candidates: Each of the seven methods of Sāma worship (hiṃkāra, prastāva, ādi, pratihāra, udgītha, upadrava, nidhana) can be treated as three‑syllabled units, making them equal in ritual significance
The combined syllable count of the seven worship terms is twenty‑two, showing a deliberate numerical symmetry in the liturgical formula

Because all three‑syllable words are said to be the same when used in praise of the Sāma, the verse implies that formal syllabic structure, rather than semantic content, determines their equivalence in worship
objection_hooks: Treating distinct ritual terms as equal solely on the basis of syllable count may ignore the substantive theological differences each term embodies

Emphasizing syllabic uniformity could be viewed as a formalistic reduction that overlooks the experiential and devotional dimensions of worship, questioning whether numerical symmetry truly confers equal efficacy

sanskrit: एकविंशत्यादित्यमाप्नोत्येकविंशो वा इतोऽसावादित्यो द्वाविंशेन परमादित्याज्जयति तन्नाकं तद्विशोकम्
transliteration: ekaviṃśatyādityamāpnotyekaviṃśo vā ito'sāvādityo dvāviṃśena paramādityājjayati tannākaṃ tadviśokam
translation: With the help of those twenty-one syllables, one can attain the status of the sun [which is also Death]. The sun occupies the twenty-first place after the things that come between the earth and the sun [those things being the twelve months, the five seasons, and the three worlds]. One can then go beyond the sun if one knows the twenty-second syllable. That world is full of joy and free from all sorrows.
word_meaning: Ekaviṃśatyā, by twenty-one [syllables]; ādityam āpnoti, one attains union with the sun [because the sun marks the end of everything, and it is therefore death]; ekaviṃśaḥ vai, [the sun is] the twenty-first [after the twelve months, the five seasons, and the three worlds]; itaḥ, from this [world]; asau ādityaḥ, the sun over there; dvāviṃśena, by [knowing] the twenty-second [syllable]; ādityāt param jayati, one attains the next higher world from the sun; tat nākam, that [place is] joyful; tat viśokaṃ, that [place is] free from all suffering.
commentary: The Upaniṣad says that if you worship Sāma with these twenty-one syllables, you attain the sun, which is the same as death. But how is the number significant? It is significant because the sun occupies the twenty-first position after the things that intervene between the earth and the sun. According to the Vedas those things are the twelve months, the five seasons, and the three worlds. But if you worship the Sāma with twenty-two syllables, you then go beyond the sun. Where? To a place called Nāka, where there is only happiness and no suffering.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: Reciting the twenty-one syllables of Sāma attains the state of the sun which is identified with death
Knowing the twenty-second syllable enables one to go beyond the sun and reach the realm called Nāka that is described as joyful and free from sorrow
The sun is said to occupy the twenty‑first position after the twelve months, five seasons and three worlds, which explains the significance of the twenty‑one syllable count
objection_hooks: Equating the sun with death appears contradictory because the sun is also a source of life in Vedic symbolism
The description of Nāka as only joyful and without suffering may be insufficient to establish it as a higher metaphysical realm
The link between the syllable count and the sun’s cosmological position might be an interpretive inference rather than an explicit justification in the verse

sanskrit: आप्नोती हादित्यस्य जयं परो हास्यादित्यजयाज्जयो भवति य एतदेवं विद्वानात्मसंमितमतिमृत्यु सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते सामोपास्ते
transliteration: āpnotī hādityasya jayaṃ paro hāsyādityajayājjayo bhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānātmasaṃmitamatimṛtyu saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste sāmopāste
translation: If a person knows all about the Sāma, and performs the sevenfold Sāma worship, treating the Sāma as himself and as something beyond death, he wins the state of the sun and then wins a place even higher than the sun.
word_meaning: Āpnoti ha ādityasya jayam, one wins the state of the sun; paraḥ ha ādityajayāt asya jayaḥ bhavati, one wins a world even higher than the sun; yaḥ, one who; etat evam vidvān, knows this [Sāma] thus; ātmasammitam, as oneself; atimṛtyu saptavidham sāma upāste sāma upāste, performs the deathless sevenfold Sāma worship [the repetition marks the end of the section].
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: Mastery of Sāma knowledge and its sevenfold worship is presented as a means to transcend ordinary existence and attain a state superior to the sun
The verse equates the Sāma with the self (ātmasammitam) and with that which is beyond death, implying that the worship transforms the practitioner’s identity into a deathless reality
The promised reward of a world even higher than the sun suggests a hierarchical cosmology where spiritual attainment surpasses even the highest celestial body
objection_hooks: The phrase "higher than the sun" is undefined, making the claim of superiority ambiguous and open to multiple interpretations
Equating Sāma with the self does not necessarily imply identity with Brahman, so the claim of deathless self‑transformation may be merely symbolic
Advaita Vedānta asserts non‑dual ultimate reality, which conflicts with the hierarchical view of spiritual realms implied by the claim of a reward above the sun

sanskrit: मनो हिंकारो वाक्प्रस्तावश्चक्षुरुद्गीथः श्रोत्रं प्रतिहारः प्राणो निधनमेतद्गायत्रं प्राणेषु प्रोतम्
transliteration: mano hiṃkāro vākprastāvaścakṣurudgīthaḥ śrotraṃ pratihāraḥ prāṇo nidhanametadgāyatraṃ prāṇeṣu protam
translation: The mind is hiṃkāra, the organ of speech is the prastāva, the eyes are the udgītha, the ears are the pratihāra, and the vital breath [in its fine (five?) forms] is the nidhana . The Gāyatrī prayer is controlled by the vital breath.
word_meaning: Manaḥ hiṃkāraḥ, the mind is the hiṃkāra; vāk prastāvaḥ, the organ of speech is the prastāva; cakṣuḥ udgīthāḥ, the eyes are the udgītha; śrotram pratihāraḥ, the ears are the pratihāra; prāṇaḥ nidhanam, the vital breath is the nidhana; etāt gāyatram, this Gāyatrī prayer; prāṇeṣu protam, is rooted in the vital breath.
commentary: So long the fivefold and the sevenfold Sāma worships have been discussed, but the names of those worships have not been mentioned. Gāyatra is the first among them. Corresponding to the Gāyatra is the hiṃkāra, with which the worship begins. Similarly, in any act of worship, the mind may be regarded as the hiṃkāra, for it is the mind that must act first before all the other organs. Next to the mind is the organ of speech. A person first thinks and then speaks out his intentions. The organ of speech is therefore the prastāva. The eyes are the udgītha because of their importance. The ears are the pratihāra, for you can ‘turn away from’ things you don’t want to hear. Prāṇa, the vital breath, is the nidhana, for when you have suṣupti, dreamless sleep, all the organs merge into the vital breath. The Gāyatrī is worshipped as the vital breath. This is why the Sāma called Gāyatra is. said to be rooted in the vital breath.
concept_tags: The Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The mind hiṃkāra is presented as the first element of worship, acting before speech and the senses
The vital breath prāṇa is described as the nidhana that unifies the organs in deep sleep and is the basis of the Gāyatrī prayer
The eyes are called udgītha because of their importance, indicating a hierarchical ranking of the senses in worship
objection_hooks: One might argue that speech, as the expression of intention, could be considered the initiating factor rather than the mind
The description of breath unifying the organs may be metaphorical, so it does not prove that breath is the ultimate substrate of worship
Assigning primary importance to the eyes may reflect cultural bias, and other traditions give precedence to hearing or other senses

sanskrit: स एवमेतद्गायत्रं प्राणेषु प्रोतं वेद प्राणी भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या महामनाः स्यात्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa evametadgāyatraṃ prāṇeṣu protaṃ veda prāṇī bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā mahāmanāḥ syāttadvratam
translation: This Gāyatra Sāma is rooted in the prāṇas . He who knows this becomes full of vitality, has a long life, and his life is brilliant. Also, he is fortunate in his children, and he has many domestic animals. He is one of the most famous people. To be noble-minded is the aim of his life.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, he who; evam, this way; etat gāyatram, this Gāyatra Sāma; prāṇeṣu protam, rooted in the prāṇas; veda, knows; prāṇī, with life; bhavati, is endowed; sarvam āyuḥ, the full span of life; eti, attains; jyok jīvati, a bright [life]; mahān prajayā, great in progeny; paśubhiḥ, [and] animals; bhavati, becomes [rich with]; mahān kīrtyā, with a good reputation; mahāmanāḥ syāt, is noble in character; tat vratam, that is the aim of his life.
commentary: Whoever knows that the Sāma named Gāyatra is based on the prāṇas is truly alive—that is, all his organs are strong and healthy and he is never physically handicapped. The full span of a person’s life is said to be a hundred years. A person who worships the Sāma lives that long. And he lives a wonderful life, able to influence many people. He is respected as one of the greatest people in the world. He has many children and many domestic animals, but his sole aim in life is to have a large heart, without a trace of meanness in his mind.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Knowing the Gāyatra Sāma, which is rooted in the prāṇas, is said to grant a full lifespan of a hundred years
The verse states that the supreme aim of life is to develop a noble mind free of meanness, beyond material prosperity
Mastery of the Gāyatra Sāma brings both material blessings such as many children and animals and the spiritual quality of great reputation and noble character
objection_hooks: The commentary mentions a hundred‑year span as an ideal, not a guaranteed outcome, so the claim of a certain lifespan may be overstated
While the verse praises noble character, it also enumerates material benefits, suggesting that material success is also presented as a goal
Advaita philosophy separates true virtue from worldly gains, so linking material blessings directly to spiritual virtue may conflict with the non‑dual view

sanskrit: अभिमन्थति स हिंकारो धूमो जायते स प्रस्तावो ज्वलति स उद्गीथोऽङ्गारा भवन्ति स प्रतिहार उपशाम्यति तन्निधनं संशाम्यति तन्निधनमेतद्रथंतरमग्नौ प्रोतम्
transliteration: abhimanthati sa hiṃkāro dhūmo jāyate sa prastāvo jvalati sa udgītho'ṅgārā bhavanti sa pratihāra upaśāmyati tannidhanaṃ saṃśāmyati tannidhanametadrathaṃtaramagnau protam
translation: When one rubs two pieces of wood against each other to light a fire, that is the hiṃkāra. When it produces smoke, that is the prastāva . Then when the flame appears, that is the udgītha . The charcoals that result are the pratihāra, and when the fire begins to go out, that is the nidhana . When the flame is completely extinguished, that also is the nidhana. This Sāma called Rathantara is rooted in fire.
word_meaning: Abhimanthati, rubbing [one piece of wood against another to produce fire]; saḥ hiṃkāraḥ, that is the hiṃkāra; dhūmaḥ jāyate, the smoke it produces; saḥ prastāvaḥ, that is the prastāva; jvalati, the flames that appear; saḥ udgīthaḥ, that is the udgītha; aṅgārāḥ bhavanti, the charcoals that result; saḥ pratihāraḥ, that is the pratihāra; upaśāmyati, when the fire begins to go out; tat nidhanam, that is the nidhana; saṃśāmyati, when the fire is completely extinguished; tat nidhanam, that [also] is the nidhana; etat rathantaram, this [Sāma] called Rathantara; agnau protam, is rooted in fire.
commentary: The rubbing of two pieces of wood together to produce fire is the hiṃkāra, for that is the beginning of the fire. And the smoke that results is the prastāva, for both are likely to continue. When the fire bursts into flame, that is the udgītha, because when there are flames the oblations are offered into them for the gods and goddesses. This is why this part is superior and why the flames are called the udgītha. The charcoals that are formed are called the pratihāra, for the charcoals are collected for future use. Upaśām means the process of subsiding—that is, the fire begins to subside but does not quite go out. Then the word saṃśām means ‘completely extinguished.’ The fire is then dead, so it is like the nidhana (death). The Rathantara Sāma is said to be based on fire, for when fire is being produced by rubbing the two sticks, the Rathantara Sāma is recited.
claim_candidates: The verse defines hiṃkāra as the initial rubbing of wood, marking it as the origin of fire
The sequence hiṃkāra‑prastāva‑udgītha‑pratihāra‑nidhana describes a complete life‑cycle of fire from birth to death
The commentary declares the udgītha stage superior because flames receive oblations, assigning it a higher ritual value
The Rathantara Sāma is described as rooted in fire, indicating that its recitation is conceptually tied to the fire‑making process
objection_hooks: The text does not explicitly state that the fire’s life‑cycle symbolizes broader metaphysical cycles, so such an analogy may be unwarranted
The claim of udgītha’s superiority rests on the commentary’s interpretive judgment rather than an explicit textual hierarchy
Linking the Sāma’s recitation to the physical act of fire production could be a contextual detail rather than an essential doctrinal connection

sanskrit: स य एवमेतद्रथंतरमग्नौ प्रोतं वेद ब्रह्मवर्चस्यन्नादो भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या न प्रत्यङ्ङग्निमाचामेन्न निष्ठीवेत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametadrathaṃtaramagnau protaṃ veda brahmavarcasyannādo bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā na pratyaṅṅagnimācāmenna niṣṭhīvettadvratam
translation: He who knows that this Rathantara Sāma is rooted in fire, acquires the kind of glow that Vedic scholarship produces. He also enjoys eating. He lives the full span of his life, and his life is brilliant. He is well known for his children and for his animal wealth, and he commands great respect in society. His vow is that he will never eat with fire in front of him and he will never spit.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etat rathantaram agnau protam veda, he who knows that this Rathantara Sāma is rooted in fire; brahmavarcasī, acquires the glow that Vedic knowledge gives; annādaḥ bhavati, he enjoys eating food; sarvam āyuḥ eti, he lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, he lives a bright life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, he distinguishes himself by his children and by the animal wealth he acquires; mahān kīrtyā, his reputation spreads far and wide; tat vratam, his principle is; na pratyak agnim ācāmet, never to
commentary: By his moral character and his scholarship in the Vedas, he acquires a radiance about him. [The underlying thought in these two mantras is that everything a person does is spiritual. This includes even physical experiences.]
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Moral character and Vedic scholarship are said to give a person a radiance that is recognized by society
Observing the vow of never eating with fire in front of oneself and never spitting is linked to a full lifespan and great respect
Material prosperity such as many children and animal wealth is presented as an outward sign of spiritual accomplishment
objection_hooks: The association of external radiance with inner spiritual illumination may be questioned, as reputation does not necessarily reflect true inner purity
The causal link between the specific vow about fire and longevity lacks explicit justification and could be symbolic rather than literal
Equating worldly wealth and progeny with spiritual merit conflicts with the Advaitic view that ultimate reality transcends material distinctions

sanskrit: उपमन्त्रयते स हिंकारो ज्ञपयते स प्रस्तावः स्त्रिया सह शेते स उद्गीथः प्रति स्त्रीं सह शेते स प्रतिहारः कालं गच्छति तन्निधनं पारं गच्छति तन्निधनमेतद्वामदेव्यं मिथुने प्रोतम्
transliteration: upamantrayate sa hiṃkāro jñapayate sa prastāvaḥ striyā saha śete sa udgīthaḥ prati strīṃ saha śete sa pratihāraḥ kālaṃ gacchati tannidhanaṃ pāraṃ gacchati tannidhanametadvāmadevyaṃ mithune protam
word_meaning: test, remove me.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse functions as a test of the reader’s interpretive ability, as indicated by the word meaning test.
The phrase remove me implies a theme of self‑removal or renunciation, resonating with Advaita’s teaching of shedding the ego.
objection_hooks: The verse lacks coherent philosophical content, so interpreting it as a test may be speculative.
The words test and remove me could be marginal notes rather than part of the verse, undermining any philosophical claim derived from them.
Without explicit Advaita terminology, linking remove me to self‑renunciation may overextend the limited textual evidence.

sanskrit: उद्यन्हिंकार उदितः प्रस्तावो मध्यंदिन उद्गीथोऽपराह्णः प्रतिहारोऽस्तं यन्निधनमेतद्बृहदादित्ये प्रोतम्
transliteration: udyanhiṃkāra uditaḥ prastāvo madhyaṃdina udgītho'parāhṇaḥ pratihāro'staṃ yannidhanametadbṛhadāditye protam
translation: The rising sun is the hiṃkāra, the sun that has already risen is the prastāva, the midday sun is the udgītha, the afternoon sun is the pratihāra, and the setting sun is the nidhana . This Sāma called Bṛhat is based on the sun.
word_meaning: Udyan, the rising sun; hiṃkāraḥ, [is] the hiṃkāra; uditaḥ, the sun that has already risen; prastāvaḥ, [is] the prastāva; madhyandina, the midday sun; udgīthaḥ, [is] the udgītha; aparāhṇaḥ, the afternoon sun; pratihāraḥ, [is] the pratihāra; yat astam, that which is setting; nidhanam, [is] the nidhana; etat bṛhat,
commentary: The rising sun is the hiṃkāra, for that is the time when we first see the sun.
