When the sun has risen it is the prastāva, for that is when people start performing their daily religious riteṣ and rituals. The midday sun is the udgītha, because that is the best time of the day. Then the afternoon sun is the pratihāra, because that is the time when cattle are driven back home. And the setting sun is the nidhana, for at night domestic animals are kept confined in their pens. This Bṛhat Sāma is based on the sun, because the sun is its presiding deity.
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse divides the day into five phases—hiṃkāra, prastāva, udgītha, pratihāra, nidhana—each term marking a specific ritual or social activity
The commentary states that the midday sun udgītha is the best time of day, indicating a hierarchy of auspicious moments
The Bṛhat Sāma is described as based on the sun because the sun is its presiding deity, showing the sun as a symbolic representation of the divine
objection_hooks: One might argue that the five‑phase scheme does not account for other important ritual times such as dawn or night, limiting its comprehensiveness
Other Vedic sources privilege dawn or twilight for rites, challenging the claim that midday is uniquely the best time
Equating the sun with a symbolic representation of Brahman may be disputed since the commentary treats the sun as a deity rather than explicitly as Brahman

sanskrit: स य एवमेतद्बृहदादित्ये प्रोतं वेद तेजस्व्यन्नादो भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या तपन्तं न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametadbṛhadāditye protaṃ veda tejasvyannādo bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā tapantaṃ na nindettadvratam
translation: He who knows that the Sāma called Bṛhat is rooted in the sun is spirited and has a great appetite. He lives the full span of his life, has a brilliant career, and is renowned for his children and for his animal wealth. His success in life brings him much fame. The vow he observes is that he will never utter a word against the sun, which gives us heat.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etat bṛhat āditye protam veda, he who knows that the Sāma called Bṛhat is rooted in the sun; tejasvi, is spirited; annādaḥ bhavati, has a great appetite; sarvam āyuḥ etḥ lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, has a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, becomes famous for his children and for his animal wealth; mahān kīrtyā, is highly respected; tapantam na nindet, he never uses a bad word against the hot sun; tat vratam, that is his vow.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse states that knowing the great Sāma Bṛhat is rooted in the sun makes one spirited, full‑of‑appetite and grants a long, prosperous life
Observing the vow never to speak ill of the sun is presented as guaranteeing longevity, fame and material wealth
The text links ethical reverence toward the sun with tangible worldly benefits, implying a moral causality between respect for the cosmic principle and personal success
objection_hooks: The association of reverence for the sun with material gain may reflect cultural prescription rather than an objective causal law
The claim of direct causation between knowledge of Bṛhat’s solar origin and vigor lacks explicit logical explanation in the verse
Emphasising worldly fame and wealth appears at odds with Advaita’s emphasis on renunciation, creating tension between material success and spiritual liberation

sanskrit: अभ्राणि सम्प्लवन्ते स हिंकारो मेघो जायते स प्रस्तावो वर्षति स उद्गीथो विद्योतते स्तनयति स प्रतिहार उद्गृह्णाति तन्निधनमेतद्वैरूपं पर्जन्ये प्रोतम्
transliteration: abhrāṇi samplavante sa hiṃkāro megho jāyate sa prastāvo varṣati sa udgītho vidyotate stanayati sa pratihāra udgṛhṇāti tannidhanametadvairūpaṃ parjanye protam
translation: When light clouds consolidate, that is the hiṃkāra. When clouds likely to pour rain collect, that is the prastāva . When the rain begins, that is the udgītha . Then there are flashes of lightning and the roar of thunder. This is the pratihāra . When it all stops, that is the nidhana . This Sāma called Vairūpa is rooted in the clouds.
word_meaning: Abhrāṇi, light clouds [bearing water]; saṃplavante, when they consolidate; saḥ hiṃkāraḥ, that is the hiṃkāra; meghaḥ jāyate, when clouds likely to pour rain appear; saḥ prastāvaḥ, that is the prastāva; varṣati saḥ udgīthaḥ, when it starts raining that is the udgītha; vidyotate, when lightning flashes; stanayati, [and] thunder roars; saḥ pratihāraḥ, that is the pratihāra; ut gṛhṇāti, when everything is over; tat nidhanam, that is the nidhana; etat vairūpam, this [Sāma called] Vairūpa; parjanye protam, is rooted in the clouds.
commentary: The word abhra means a cloud which bears ap, water. A cloud that pours rain is called megha . The English word ‘cloud’ is actually in Sanskrit parjanya . Abhra, megha, parjanya —these different names all mean cloud, but indicate the cloud in a different state. This is why the cloud is described here as vairūpa, with different forms. It is the sun that produces the cloud, and this is why the Sāma is first worshipped as the sun and then as the cloud.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse portrays the cloud parjanya as Vairūpa, meaning it manifests in multiple distinct forms such as abhra, megha and others according to its state
The sequence of cloud states hiṃkāra, prastāva, udgītha, pratihāra and nidhana can be read as a symbolic progression from emergence to dissolution, echoing a broader principle of transformation
The commentary states that the sun produces the cloud, indicating that the cloud’s varied forms are ultimately dependent on solar activity and are therefore worshipped after the sun
objection_hooks: One may object that the text merely describes meteorological stages and does not explicitly intend a symbolic ritual progression
The claim of solar primacy could be challenged because the verse emphasizes the cloud’s internal changes, suggesting autonomy rather than dependence
Identifying Vairūpa as simply multiple forms might overlook the possibility that it points to an underlying unity behind the apparent diversity

sanskrit: स य एवमेतद्वैरूपं पर्जन्ये प्रोतं वेद विरूपांश्च सुरूपंश्च पशूनवरुन्धे सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या वर्षन्तं न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametadvairūpaṃ parjanye protaṃ veda virūpāṃśca surūpaṃśca paśūnavarundhe sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā varṣantaṃ na nindettadvratam
translation: He who knows that this Sāma called Vairūpa has its source in the clouds comes to acquire a large variety of animals, and all beautiful animals too. He has a long and brilliant life, and his children and animals are such that he becomes famous for them. His achievements also mark him as a great person.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etat vairūpam parjanye protam veda, he who knows that this [Sāma called] Vairūpa is rooted in the clouds; virūpān ca surūpān ca paśūn avarundhe, he comes to possess a large variety of animals and beautiful animals also; sarvam āyuḥ eti, he lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, he has a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, he becomes famous for his children and for his animal wealth; mahān kīrtyā, he is renowned for his achievements; varṣantam na nindet tat vratam, his vow is not to criticize the clouds that pour rain.
commentary: It is a characteristic of those who worship the Sāma that they never find fault with others. This is why they do not say anything bad about even the clouds that give rain.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse claims that knowing the Sāma Vairūpa’s origin in the clouds brings material wealth such as a large variety of animals and a brilliant reputation
According to the commentary worshippers of Sāma must never criticize others, including the rain‑giving clouds
The vow not to disparage the clouds is presented as an ethical commitment that underlies the individual’s long life and fame
objection_hooks: The link between knowledge of Vairūpa’s origin and material prosperity may be descriptive rather than causal, so the claim of a direct benefit is not definitively supported
The commentary’s statement about not finding fault could be a general moral teaching, not an exclusive requirement of Sāma worship, weakening the claim of a specific worship‑based rule
Interpreting the vow as a literal ethical rule may overlook its symbolic nature, making the claim that it guarantees success philosophically tenuous

sanskrit: स य एवमेतद्वैराजमृतुषु प्रोतं वेद विराजति प्रजया पशुभिर्ब्रह्मवर्चसेन सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्यर्तून्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametadvairājamṛtuṣu protaṃ veda virājati prajayā paśubhirbrahmavarcasena sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyartūnna nindettadvratam
translation: He who knows that the Sāma called Vairāja is rooted in the seasons is surrounded by his children and animals and has a radiance about him which is born of Vedic scholarship. He lives a long and brilliant life, and he is considered great for his children and for his animals. He is also highly respected for his great deeds. He follows the vow of never criticizing the seasons.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evani etat vairājam ṛtuṣu protam veda, he who knows that this [Sāma called] Vairāja is rooted in the seasons; virājati, he lives; prajayā paśubhiḥ, surrounded by his children and animals; brahmavarcasena, he has the kind of radiance that is born of Vedic scholarship; sarvam āyuḥ eti, he lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, he lives a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, he is known to be great for his children and animals; mahān kīrtyā, he is famous for his deeds; ṛtūn na nindet tat vratam, his vow is that he will never criticize the seasons.
commentary: The person who knows that the Vairāja Sāma is rooted in the seasons becomes like the seasons himself. Each season has a beauty of its own. Similarly, this person has within him the beauty and grandeur of the good things he possesses, such as his wealth, good children, scholarship, and character. The rules of the Sāma forbid him from saying anything bad about the seasons, and he follows this.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Knowing the Vairāja Sāma that is rooted in the seasons gives a person a radiance that originates from Vedic scholarship
Keeping the vow never to criticize the seasons is linked to a long, brilliant life and high esteem among one’s children and animals
Identifying with the seasonal cycle through the Vairāja Sāma brings material and social benefits such as wealth, good children, and fame
objection_hooks: The radiance may be attributed to Vedic scholarship itself rather than merely knowing the Vairāja Sāma
The text describes a correlation between the vow and longevity but does not prove that the vow causes the long life
The claim that alignment with natural cycles guarantees material benefits may over‑state the verse, which focuses on ethical conduct rather than cosmic determinism

sanskrit: पृथिवी हिंकारोऽन्तरिक्षं प्रस्तावो द्यौरुद्गीथो दिशः प्रतिहारः समुद्रो निधनमेताः शक्वर्यो लोकेषु प्रोताः
transliteration: pṛthivī hiṃkāro'ntarikṣaṃ prastāvo dyaurudgītho diśaḥ pratihāraḥ samudro nidhanametāḥ śakvaryo lokeṣu protāḥ
translation: The earth is the hiṃkāra, the space between the earth and heaven is the prastāva, heaven is the udgītha, the quarters are the pratihāra, and the ocean is the nidhana . The Sāma known as Śakvarī is rooted in the earth and other worlds.
word_meaning: Pṛthivī hiṃkāraḥ, the earth is the hiṃkāra; antarikṣam prastāvaḥ, the space between the earth and heaven is the prastāva; dyauḥ udgīthaḥ, heaven is the udgītha; diśaḥ pratihāraḥ, the quarters are the pratihāra; samudraḥ nidhanam, the ocean is the nidhana; etāḥ śakvaryaḥ lokeṣu protāḥ, these [the Sāma called] Śakvarī are rooted in the earth and other worlds.
commentary: If the seasons come and go, as they ought to, then things on the earth and the other worlds remain in balance. This is why the Sāma is first worshipped as the seasons and then as the worlds. But the question is: Sāma is singular. Why then has a plural word, śakvarī, been used here for the Sāma? Doesn’t it imply that there are many Sāmas ? No, the Sāma is always one and the same. There are not many Sāmas. The word śakvarī has a plural form, but it stands for the singular Sāma. Śaṅkara gives the example of the word revatī, which is similarly always used in the plural.
claim_candidates: The verse lists earth space heaven quarters and ocean as designations that symbolically point to the single Sāma hymn, showing the hymn as a representation of the whole cosmos
The plural form śakvarī is employed not to indicate many Sāmas but to refer to the singular Sāma, using grammatical plurality to convey its universal presence

According to the commentary the regular change of seasons keeps the earth and other worlds in balance, and this balance is attributed to the worship of the Sāma, suggesting the hymn functions as a sustaining principle of cosmic order
objection_hooks: One could object that the verse merely enumerates cosmic regions without explicitly stating they symbolize the Sāma, so the symbolic reading may be an extrapolation

The interpretation that the plural form signals universality may be challenged by noting that Sanskrit frequently uses plural for honorific or collective reference, which does not necessarily imply a philosophical claim about singularity
The commentary links balance to the natural cycle of seasons, not to an active role of the Sāma, thus attributing cosmic order to the hymn may go beyond what the text actually says

sanskrit: स य एवमेताः शक्वर्यो लोकेषु प्रोता वेद लोकी भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या लोकान्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametāḥ śakvaryo lokeṣu protā veda lokī bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā lokānna nindettadvratam
translation: He who knows that the Sāma known as Śakvarī is located in the worlds goes to the best world. He also has a long and brilliant life and is well known for his children and for his animal wealth. He is considered to be a great person because of his great deeds. He takes a vow that he will never criticize the worlds.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etāḥ śakvaryaḥ lokeṣu protāḥ veda, he who knows that these [i.e., the Sāma] known as Śakvarī are rooted in the worlds; lokī bhavati, goes to the best world; sarvam āyuḥ eti, lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, has a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, is well known for his children and for his animal wealth; mahān kīrtyā, is well known for his great deeds; lokān na nindet tat vratam, his vow is that he will never criticize the worlds.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Knowledge of the Sāma called Śakvarī is said to secure a place in the best world
The verse links ethical conduct such as great deeds and a vow of non‑criticism with a long, brilliant life and material prosperity
A vow never to criticize the worlds is presented as a moral commitment that enhances one’s reputation and status
objection_hooks: The verse may be describing poetic benefits rather than guaranteeing the best world for those who know the Sāma
The correlation between ethical conduct and longevity or wealth is asserted but not logically demonstrated, leaving room for alternative explanations
The text does not explain how the vow of non‑criticism directly leads to greater reputation, so the causal link is questionable

sanskrit: अजा हिंकारोऽवयः प्रस्तावो गाव उद्गीथोऽश्वाः प्रतिहारः पुरुषो निधनमेता रेवत्यः पशुषु प्रोताः
transliteration: ajā hiṃkāro'vayaḥ prastāvo gāva udgītho'śvāḥ pratihāraḥ puruṣo nidhanametā revatyaḥ paśuṣu protāḥ
translation: Goats are the hiṃkāra, sheep are the prastāva, cows represent the udgītha, horses are the pratihāra, and a human being is the nidhana . The Sāma called Revatī is established in animals.
word_meaning: Ajāḥ hiṃkāraḥ, goats are the hiṃkāra; avayaḥ prastāvaḥ, sheep are the prastāva; gāvaḥ udgītha, cows are the udgītha; aśvāḥ pratihāraḥ, horses are the pratihāra; puruṣaḥ nidhanam, a human being is the nidhana; etāḥ revatyaḥ paśuṣu protāḥ, these [i.e., the Sāma known as] Revatī are established in animals.
commentary: Animals are useful to all the worlds. That is why, after worshipping the Sāma as the worlds, the worship as animals is suggested. As in the previous section in which the word śakvarī is always used in the plural (as śakvaryaḥ ), here also, the word revatī is always used in the plural ( revatyaḥ ).
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse assigns specific ritual symbols to goats, sheep, cows, and horses, indicating that each animal embodies a distinct aspect of the sacred Sāma
The commentary states that after worshipping the Sāma as worlds, worship as animals is suggested, implying that animal worship functions as a secondary form of ritual practice
The repeated plural form revatyaḥ in the verse shows that the Sāma Revatī is understood to be collectively present in multiple animals
objection_hooks: One could argue that the animal designations are merely descriptive of sacrificial roles and do not establish a hierarchical worship structure
The commentary’s mention of worship as worlds followed by worship as animals may be metaphorical rather than indicating a literal substitution of ritual forms
The plural revatyaḥ might be a grammatical feature without implying a collective sacred presence in animals

sanskrit: स य एवमेता रेवत्यः पशुषु प्रोता वेद पशुमान्भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या पशून्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametā revatyaḥ paśuṣu protā veda paśumānbhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā paśūnna nindettadvratam
translation: He who knows that the Sāma called Revatī is rooted in animals acquires many animals. He has a long and brilliant life and becomes well known for his children and for his animal wealth. He also becomes a truly great person for his great deeds. His vow is that he will never say anything bad about animals.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etaḥ revatyaḥ protāḥ veda, he who knows that these [i.e., the Sāma] called Revatī are rooted in animals; paśumān bhavati, comes to possess many animals; sarvam āyuḥ eti, lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, has a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, becomes a highly respected person because of his children and also his animal wealth; mahān kīrtyā, becomes a truly great person for his great deeds; paśūn na nindet tat vratam, he observes the vow of never saying anything bad about animals.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: He who knows that the Sāma Revatī is rooted in animals acquires many animals
The verse states that observing a vow not to speak ill of animals brings a long, brilliant life and great fame
The text links ethical conduct toward animals with material prosperity such as children and animal wealth
objection_hooks: The emphasis on material gain from ethical conduct may conflict with the Advaitic ideal of detachment from worldly benefits
The deterministic claim that ethical vows guarantee longevity can be questioned by real‑world cases where virtuous individuals die young

sanskrit: लोम हिंकारस्त्वक्प्रस्तावो मांसमुद्गीथोस्थि प्रतिहारो मज्जा निधनमेतद्यज्ञायज्ञीयमङ्गेषु प्रोतम्
transliteration: loma hiṃkārastvakprastāvo māṃsamudgīthosthi pratihāro majjā nidhanametadyajñāyajñīyamaṅgeṣu protam
translation: Hair is the hiṃkāra, skin is the prastāva, flesh is the udgītha, bone is the pratihāra, and marrow is the nidhana . The Sāma called Yajñāyajñīya is spread all over the limbs of the body.
word_meaning: Loma hiṃkāraḥ, hair is the hiṃkāra; tvak prastāvaḥ, skin is the prastāva; māṃsam udgīthaḥ, flesh is the udgītha; asthi pratihāraḥ, bone is the pratihāra; majjā nidhanam, marrow is the nidhana; etat yajñāyajñīyam, this [Sāma called] Yajñāyajñīya; aṅgeṣu protam, is spread all over the limbs of the body.
commentary: We get much nourishment from animals to sustain our bodies. That is why, in the previous section, the Sāma has been worshipped in the animals, and now it is being worshipped in the various parts of the body. Hair is on top of the body, so it is given first place—the place given to the hiṃkāra when the Sāma is recited. Next to the hair is the skin. Similarly, next to the hiṃkāra is the prastāva. The flesh is the udgītha, for both are the most important part. Then the bones are the pratihāra, because after a body is cremated there will still be pieces of bone left, and these are ‘collected’ by the relatives. The marrow is the nidhana, for that is the end of everything.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse maps each bodily part—hair, skin, flesh, bone, marrow—to a specific aspect of the Sāma Yajñāyajñīya, showing a symbolic correspondence between the body and the sacred
The commentary orders the parts, giving hair (hiṃkāra) priority because it crowns the body, thereby establishing a hierarchy of ritual importance among the bodily elements
The Sāma Yajñāyajñīya is described as spread over all limbs, indicating that the sacred pervades the entire physical form
objection_hooks: The verse itself merely lists body parts without explicit symbolic language, so the symbolic mapping may rely on interpretive commentary rather than textual evidence
The claimed hierarchy rests on the commentary’s rationale (hair on top) which is not stated in the verse, making the ranking an inferred rather than explicit feature
Describing the Sāma as spread over the limbs does not necessarily imply an ontological presence of Brahman, limiting the claim to a ritualistic description rather than a statement of immanent reality

sanskrit: स य एवमेतद्यज्ञायज्ञीयमङ्गेषु प्रोतं वेदाङ्गी भवति नाङ्गेन विहूर्छति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या संवत्सरं मज्ज्ञो नाश्नीयात्तद्व्रतं मज्ज्ञो नाश्नीयादिति वा
transliteration: sa ya evametadyajñāyajñīyamaṅgeṣu protaṃ vedāṅgī bhavati nāṅgena vihūrchati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā saṃvatsaraṃ majjño nāśnīyāttadvrataṃ majjño nāśnīyāditi vā
translation: He who knows that the Sāma known as Yajñāyajñīya is located in different parts of the body becomes possessed of a firm body and none of his organs has any defect. He has a long and brilliant life; and he becomes great because of his children and his animal wealth. He also becomes famous for his great deeds. His vow is that he will not eat meat for one whole year, or he will not eat it at all.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etat yajñāyajñīyam aṅgeṣu protam veda, he who knows that this Sāma called Yajñāyajñīya is located in the different parts of the body; aṅgī bhavati, has a firm body; na aṅgena vihūrchati, has no defect in his limbs or organs; sarvam āyuḥ eti, he lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, he has a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, he becomes great in respect of his children and his animal wealth; mahān kīrtyā, he becomes great because of his great deeds; saṃvatsaram, for one whole year; majjñaḥ, meat; na aśnīyāt, will not eat; majjñaḥ na aśnīyāt iti vā, or will not eat meat at all; tat vratam, this is his vow.
commentary: ‘A firm body’ means a good, sound body with none of its organs or limbs defective. Even his nails and his hair will be normal. The word majjña, meat, is in the plural here. This implies that both fish and meat should be avoided.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Knowing the location of the Sāma called Yajñāyajñīya within the body guarantees a firm, defect‑free body
Abstaining from meat for a year or permanently, as a vow, brings a long brilliant life, prosperity in children and animal wealth, and fame
The meat‑abstention vow functions as a form of ascetic discipline that yields material and social benefits
objection_hooks: The link between intellectual knowledge of a bodily Sāma and physical health may be metaphorical, not a literal causal guarantee
Promising material prosperity for a dietary ascetic practice seems at odds with the spiritual aim of tapas, creating tension between worldly gain and liberation
A simple dietary restriction may not meet the traditional criteria of tapas, questioning whether the vow truly constitutes ascetic discipline

sanskrit: अग्निर्हिंकारो वायुः प्रस्ताव आदित्य उद्गीथो नक्षत्राणि प्रतिहारश्चन्द्रमा निधनमेतद्राजनं देवतासु प्रोतम्
transliteration: agnirhiṃkāro vāyuḥ prastāva āditya udgītho nakṣatrāṇi pratihāraścandramā nidhanametadrājanaṃ devatāsu protam
translation: Fire is the hiṃkāra, air is the prastāva, the sun is the udgītha, the stars are the pratihāra, and the moon is the nidhana . This Sāma known as Rājana is rooted in the gods and goddesses.
word_meaning: Agniḥ hiṃkāraḥ, fire is the hiṃkāra; vāyuḥ prastāvaḥ, air is the prastāva; ādityaḥ udgīthaḥ, the sun is the udgītha; nakṣatrāṇi pratihāraḥ, the stars are the pratihāra; candramāḥ nidhanam, the moon is the nidhana; etat rājanam, this [Sāma called] Rājana; devatāsu protam, is rooted in the gods and goddesses.
commentary: In the previous section the Sāma was worshipped in the parts of the body. Now it is being worshipped in the deities—that is, in the forces of nature. Fire is the first among the forces of nature, so it is the hiṃkāra. Next to the hiṃkāra is the prastāva. Air is called the prastāva because it comes next after fire, and it is also infinite. The sun is the udgītha, for just as the udgītha is the best among the Sāma songs, so also the sun is the best among the forces of nature. The stars lie scattered, and as we locate them it seems that we are ‘collecting’ ( pratihāra ) them. That is why the stars are said to be the pratihāra. The moon is said to be the nidhana, because active people go to Candraloka, the world of the moon, after death. The Sāma called Rājana is rooted in the gods and goddesses, for the deities are by nature luminous ( rājana ).
claim_candidates: The verse equates natural forces such as fire, air, sun, stars and moon with specific qualities of the Sāma, indicating that worship of these forces functions as worship of the divine
The commentary orders the elements with fire first and air next, suggesting a hierarchical significance among natural forces in spiritual practice
The moon is identified as nidhana, linking it to the realm of death and implying that celestial bodies represent metaphysical destinations after life
objection_hooks: One might argue that the verse merely assigns poetic labels to elements without asserting ontological identity, so it does not necessarily constitute worship of the divine
The ordering of elements could reflect functional sequence rather than a spiritual hierarchy, challenging the claim of graded significance
The moon’s association with nidhana may be ambiguous because the moon also symbolizes renewal, making the link to an after‑life realm contestable

sanskrit: स य एवमेतद्राजनं देवतासु प्रोतं वेदैतासामेव देवतानांसलोकतांसर्ष्टितांसायुज्यं गच्छति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या ब्राह्मणान्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: sa ya evametadrājanaṃ devatāsu protaṃ vedaitāsāmeva devatānāṃsalokatāṃsarṣṭitāṃsāyujyaṃ gacchati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahānprajayā paśubhirbhavati mahānkīrtyā brāhmaṇānna nindettadvratam
translation: He who knows that the Sāma known as Rājana is established in the gods and goddesses shares the same worlds with these gods and goddesses, has the same rights and privileges with them, or has the same form. He also has a long and brilliant life. And he becomes great by virtue of his children and his animal wealth, and also by virtue of his great deeds. His vow is that he will never speak ill of the brāhmins.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etat rājanam devatāsu protam veda, he who knows that this Sāma known as Rājana is established in the gods and goddesses [such as Agni (fire), Vāyu (air), etc.]; etāsām eva devatānām salokatām gacchati, he shares with those gods and goddesses the same worlds; sārṣṭitām, the same rights and privileges; [ vā ] sāyujyam, [or] the same form; sarvam āyuḥ eti, he lives the full span of his life; jyok jīvati, he has a brilliant life; mahān prajayā paśubhiḥ bhavati, he becomes great by virtue of his children and also his animal wealth; mahān kīrtyā, he is great by his great deeds; brāhmaṇān na nindet tat vratam, it is his vow that he will not speak ill of the brāhmins.
commentary: What does a person gain from worshipping the Sāma in such a way? He gets the right to live with the gods and goddesses in the same world, or to share the same rights and privileges with them, or to have the same form. It is not that he would get all these things. He would get any one of them. The scriptures say that the brāhmins themselves are the gods and goddesses. Obviously then one should not say anything derogatory about the brāhmins.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: Worship of the Sāma known as Rājana can grant the devotee the right to share the same world, privileges, or form as the gods and goddesses
The verse requires the devotee to vow never to speak ill of the brāhmins as a condition for receiving its benefits
The verse links a long life, prosperity through children and animal wealth, and great fame to the devotee’s conduct, implying moral behavior shapes destiny
objection_hooks: The promise of sharing worlds with gods may be metaphorical rather than literal, limiting the claim of actual divine status
The prohibition against disparaging brāhmins could be a social norm rather than a metaphysical prerequisite for the benefits
The commentary states only one of the listed benefits may be attained, which challenges the idea that conduct straightforwardly determines all the promised outcomes

sanskrit: त्रयी विद्या हिंकारस्त्रय इमे लोकाः स प्रस्तावोऽग्निर्वायुरादित्यः स उद्गीथो नक्षत्राणि वयांसि मरीचयः स प्रतिहारः सर्पा गन्धर्वाः पितरस्तन्निधनमेतत्साम सर्वस्मिन्प्रोतम्
transliteration: trayī vidyā hiṃkārastraya ime lokāḥ sa prastāvo'gnirvāyurādityaḥ sa udgītho nakṣatrāṇi vayāṃsi marīcayaḥ sa pratihāraḥ sarpā gandharvāḥ pitarastannidhanametatsāma sarvasminprotam
translation: The three vidyās [the Ṛk, the Yajuḥ, and the Sāma ] are together the hiṃkāra; these three worlds [the earth, the space between the earth and heaven, and heaven] are together the prastāva; fire, air, and the sun are together the udgītha; the stars, the birds, and the rays are together the pratihāra; serpents, gandharvas, and the ancestors are together the nidhana . This Sāma resides in everything.
word_meaning: Trayī vidyā hiṃkāraḥ, the three Vedas [the Ṛk, the Yajuḥ, and the Sāma] are the hiṃkāra; trayaḥ ime lokāḥ, these three worlds [bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, svaḥ]; saḥ prastāvaḥ, [together] that is the prastāva; agniḥ vāyuḥ ādityaḥ, fire, air, and the sun; saḥ udgīthaḥ, [together] that is the udgītha; nakṣatrāṇi vayāṃsi marīcayaḥ, the stars, the birds, and the rays; saḥ pratihāraḥ, [together] that is the pratihāra; sarpaḥ gandharvāḥ pitaraḥ, serpents, gandharvas [celestial musicians ], and the ancestors; tat nidhanam, [together] that is the nidhana; etat sāma sarvasmin protam, this Sāma rests in everything.
commentary: According to the scriptures the Ṛg Veda comes from fire, the Yajur Veda comes from air, and the Sāma Veda comes from the sun. In the previous section the worship of the Sāma as fire, air, etc., was discussed. Now the worship of the Sāma as the three Vedas is taken up. Knowledge of the three Vedas may be regarded as the hiṃkāra, for you have to use the hiṃkāra before you start doing any worship. After the hiṃkāra comes the prastāva. Similarly, after the three Vedas come the three worlds, so they are said to be the prastāva. Fire, air, and the sun are like the udgītha, for among all things they are of a higher order. The stars, the birds, and the rays come and go, so they are like the pratihāra. The serpents, the gandharvas, and The word Sāma does not refer to any specific Sāma. It refers to anything bearing that name. The word is meant according to the context, just as oblations are offered according to the deities for whom they are meant.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse equates the three Vedas (Ṛg, Yajur, Sāma) with the ritual utterance hiṃkāra, indicating that knowledge of the Vedas is a prerequisite for worship

The verse establishes a systematic correspondence between the three Vedas, the three worlds, fire‑air‑sun, stars‑birds‑rays, and serpents‑gandharvas‑ancestors, suggesting a doctrine of macrocosm‑microcosm unity
The statement that this Sāma resides in everything implies that the principle represented by Sāma pervades all aspects of reality, supporting an immanent view of ultimate reality
objection_hooks: Equating the Vedas with hiṃkāra may reduce the Vedas to a preparatory chant, thereby diminishing their status as independent revelation
The macrocosm‑microcosm correspondences might be poetic metaphor rather than an ontological claim, challenging the assertion of systematic unity
The claim that Sāma resides in everything could be limited to ritual symbolism and not entail full immanence of the ultimate reality

sanskrit: स य एवमेतत्साम सर्वस्मिन्प्रोतं वेद सर्वं ह भवति
transliteration: sa ya evametatsāma sarvasminprotaṃ veda sarvaṃ ha bhavati
translation: He who knows that this Sāma is in everything becomes one with everything [or, becomes the Lord of everything].
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ evam etat sāma sarvasmin protam veda, he who knows that this Sāma resides in everything; sarvam ha bhavati, he becomes everything.
commentary: This is an example of how far the benefit of the knowledge of the Sāma can extend: If you know that the Sāma is everywhere and in everything, you become the supreme Lord of everything.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: Knowing that Sāma resides in everything leads to the realization that the individual self is identical with the totality of existence
The verse states that such knowledge makes the knower become the supreme lord of everything, indicating that knowledge of the universal principle confers ontological unity with it
The commentary claims that the benefit of Sāma knowledge extends to the highest spiritual status, equating mastery of the hymn with attaining ultimate sovereignty over all
objection_hooks: The text may only describe epistemic awareness of Sāma’s pervasiveness, not an actual ontological identity between self and Brahman
The description of becoming the supreme lord could be metaphorical praise rather than a literal claim of ontological transformation

sanskrit: तदेष श्लोको यानि पञ्चधा त्रीणी त्रीणि तेभ्यो न ज्यायः परमन्यदस्ति
transliteration: tadeṣa śloko yāni pañcadhā trīṇī trīṇi tebhyo na jyāyaḥ paramanyadasti
translation: Here is a verse in this connection: There are five parts of Sāma, and each of these is divided into three parts. There is nothing higher than these [fifteen forms of Sāma].
word_meaning: Tat eṣaḥ ślokaḥ, here is a verse on the subject; yāni pañcadhā, that [Sāma] which is divided into five parts [hiṃkāra, prastāva, udgītha, pratihāra, and nidhana ]; trīṇi trīṇi, each again divided into three parts [such as hiṃkāra representing the three Vedas ]; tebhyaḥ jyāyaḥ param anyat na asti, there is nothing higher than these.
commentary: So long we have discussed the five forms of the Sāma: hiṃkāra, prastāva, udgītha, pratihāra, and nidhana. In the present verse the Upaniṣad says that each of these five can be divided further into three: hiṃkāra as the Ṛk, the Yajuḥ, and the Sāma; prastāva as the earth, the intermediate space (between the earth and heaven), and heaven; udgītha as fire, air, and the sun; pratihāra as the stars, the birds, and the rays; and nidhana as the snakes, the celestial musicians, and the ancestors. It is Sāma which is manifest in all of these fifteen
claim_candidates: The Upanishadic verse asserts that the five primary categories of Sāma each contain three subcategories, yielding fifteen distinct forms of Sāma
According to the commentary each subcategory of Sāma corresponds to specific cosmological or natural elements such as the three Vedas, earth, fire, etc.
The verse declares that there is nothing higher than these fifteen forms of Sāma, implying that mastery of Sāma in its full fifteenfold manifestation is the supreme attainment within this context
objection_hooks: One might object that the statement nothing higher than these refers only to the hierarchy of Vedic chant and does not establish Sāma as the ultimate metaphysical reality
Another objection questions whether the commentary’s examples constitute definitive symbolic mappings, leaving open alternative interpretations of the subcategories

sanskrit: यस्तद्वेद स वेद सर्वं सर्वा दिशो बलिमस्मै हरन्ति सर्वमस्मीत्युपासित तद्व्रतं तद्व्रतम्
transliteration: yastadveda sa veda sarvaṃ sarvā diśo balimasmai haranti sarvamasmītyupāsita tadvrataṃ tadvratam
translation: He who knows Sāma knows everything, and gifts come to him from all quarters. His vow will be to constantly say to himself, ‘I am one with all’.
word_meaning: Yaḥ tat veda, he who knows that [Sāma]; saḥ veda sarvam, he knows all; sarvāḥ diśaḥ, all the quarters; balim, gifts; asmai, for him; haranti, bring; sarvam asmi, I am all; iti upāsīta, this is how he will meditate; tat vratam tat vratam, this is his vow, this is his vow.
commentary: Sāma is the inmost being of all, and he who knows it as such becomes like Sāma—that is, he becomes the inmost being of all, and he knows everything. People come from everywhere with gifts to show him their respect. ‘I am Sāma, the inmost being of all’—this is how Sāma is to be worshipped. One should constantly repeat this to oneself. In fact, it should be treated as a vow.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: Knowing Sāma confers knowledge of all things as expressed by the verse that he who knows Sāma knows everything
The vow ‘I am all’ functions as a meditative affirmation that equates the individual self with the universal being
The verse states that gifts from all quarters are brought to the one who knows Sāma, indicating that spiritual knowledge attracts universal respect
objection_hooks: One may argue that the claim that knowing Sāma guarantees knowledge of all is metaphorical rather than a literal epistemic guarantee
The identification ‘I am all’ could be seen as a verbal practice without ontological effect, challenging the claim that it truly equates self with the universal
The attraction of gifts might reflect an idealized social outcome rather than a necessary consequence of spiritual insight

sanskrit: विनर्दि साम्नो वृणे पशव्यमित्यग्नेरुद्गीथोऽनिरुक्तः प्रजापतेर्निरुक्तः सोमस्य मृदु श्लक्ष्णं वायोः श्लक्ष्णं बलवदिन्द्रस्य क्रौञ्चं बृहस्पतेरपध्वान्तं वरुणस्य तान्सर्वानेवोपसेवेत वारुणं त्वेव वर्जयेत्
transliteration: vinardi sāmno vṛṇe paśavyamityagnerudgītho'niruktaḥ prajāpaterniruktaḥ somasya mṛdu ślakṣṇaṃ vāyoḥ ślakṣṇaṃ balavadindrasya krauñcaṃ bṛhaspaterapadhvāntaṃ varuṇasya tānsarvānevopaseveta vāruṇaṃ tveva varjayet
translation: The vinardi voice for singing the Sāma is good for animals, and Agni, the god of fire, is its presiding deity. I bear this in mind and pray that I may have this voice. The god Prajāpati presides over the udgītha sung in the anirukta [unclear] voice. The one having Soma as its presiding deity is nirukta [clear]. That of Vāyu, the god of air, is soft and pleasant, and Indra ’s is strong. That which has Bṛhaspati as its presiding deity is like the voice of the krauñca bird, and that of Varuṇa is like the sound of a broken metal pot. Cultivate all of these, but avoid the one of Varuṇa.
word_meaning: Vinardi, the voice called vinardi, which is deep, like that of a bull; sāmnaḥ, for [singing] the Sāma; vṛṇe iti, I pray for; paśavyam, good for animals; agneḥ, of the god of fire; udgītha aniruktaḥ, the udgītha sung in the anirukta voice, which is not very distinct; prajāpateḥ, of Prajāpati, the Lord of all; niruktaḥ, the voice called nirukta, which is clear; somasya, of the god Soma; mṛdu ślakṣṇam, the voice called ślakṣṇa that is soft and soothing; vāyoḥ, of the god Vāyu, air; ślakṣṇam balavat, the voice called ślakṣṇa that is soothing yet powerful; indrasya, of Indra; krauñcam, the voice called krauñca, which is like that of the krauñca bird; bṛhaspateḥ, of Bṛhaspati; apadhvāntam, the voice called apadhvānta, which sounds like a broken metal pot; varuṇasya, of the god Varuṇa; tān sarvān era upaseveta, practise on all of them; tu eva vāruṇam, except that of Varuṇa; varjayet, one should avoid.
commentary: When you sing the udgītha, be careful that you sing it in the right voice. The voice is important because it determines the benefit you derive from your singing. For instance, there is a type of voice that sounds like that of a bull. Agni, fire, is the presiding deity of this voice. If you sing the udgītha in this type of voice, it means you are singing for the good of the animals and you are also praying that you may have that kind of voice. Similarly, there is a type of voice called anirukta, which has Prajāpati as its presiding deity. Prajāpati, the Lord of all beings, has no form of his own. Similarly, he does not have a distinct voice. This is why it is called anirukta, indistinct. The voice of Soma, the moon, is nirukta, clear; that of Indra is powerful; and that of Bṛhaspati sounds like a krauñca, a bird similar to a crane. Varuṇa’s voice is the worst. It is like the sound of a broken brass vessel. All these voices can be used except that of Varuṇa.
claim_candidates: The verse links each vocal quality to a specific deity, indicating that the deity’s character determines the benefit of the singing
The instruction is to practice all vocal types except that of Varuṇa, which is described as the worst and to be avoided
The commentary equates the indistinct anirukta voice with Prajāpati’s formlessness, suggesting a symbolic correlation between vocal clarity and divine attributes
objection_hooks: Associating vocal qualities with deities may reify the divine into limited forms, which conflicts with the non‑dual Advaita view that Brahman is beyond all attributes
Labeling Varuṇa’s voice as the worst seems contradictory because Varuṇa represents cosmic order, raising the question of why his voice should be avoided
The claim that an indistinct voice reflects Prajāpati’s formlessness could be challenged since a formless principle could be expressed through any voice, not only an indistinct one

sanskrit: अमृतत्वं देवेभ्य आगायानीत्यागायेत्स्वधां पितृभ्य आशां मनुष्येभ्यस्तृणोदकं पशुभ्यः स्वर्गं लोकं यजमानायान्नमात्मन आगायानीत्येतानि मनसा ध्यायन्नप्रमत्तः स्तुवीत
transliteration: amṛtatvaṃ devebhya āgāyānītyāgāyetsvadhāṃ pitṛbhya āśāṃ manuṣyebhyastṛṇodakaṃ paśubhyaḥ svargaṃ lokaṃ yajamānāyānnamātmana āgāyānītyetāni manasā dhyāyannapramattaḥ stuvīta
translation: One should sing with the resolve: ‘By singing I will get immortality for the gods and goddesses, food offerings for the ancestors, hope for humanity, food and water for the animals, heaven for those who perform sacrifices, and food for myself. All this I will get by singing.’ Thinking thus, one should sing without raising the voice too much [or, without being too excited].
word_meaning: Āgāyet, one should sing [with the resolve]; amṛtatvam devebhyaḥ āgāyāni iti, I will get immortality for the gods and goddesses by singing; svadhām, offerings; pitṛbhyaḥ, for the ancestors; āśām manuṣyebhyaḥ, hope for humanity; tṛṇodakam paśubhyaḥ, food and water for the animals; svargam lokam, heavenly worlds; yajamānāya, for those who perform sacrifices; annam ātmane, food for myself; āgāyāni iti, I shall win by singing thus; etāni, all these; manasā dhyāyan, thinking mentally; apramattaḥ, without raising the voice too much; stuvīta, one should sing.
commentary: When you sing the Sāma, you should keep in mind the things you are singing for—such as immortality for the gods and goddesses, etc. But you must also take care that when you sing, your pronunciation and accents are correct, and that you do not miss any syllables.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse instructs that singing with proper mental focus can secure both material and spiritual benefits for gods, ancestors, humanity, animals, sacrificers, and the singer himself
The directive to sing without raising the voice too much implies that the efficacy of the practice depends on moderation and correct pronunciation rather than volume

The passage presents singing as a reciprocal exchange where the singer offers benefits to various beings and, in return, receives sustenance for self, reflecting a principle of mutual benefit in ritual conduct
objection_hooks: One may object that claiming singing can grant immortality to deities reads the verse literally, whereas the intended meaning could be symbolic aspiration rather than a factual outcome
The emphasis on vocal moderation could conflict with other Upanishadic teachings that valorize intense devotional fervor, raising a tension about the role of emotional intensity in spiritual practice

Framing the act as a transactional exchange may clash with Advaita’s non‑dual view that ultimate liberation transcends material reciprocity, creating a philosophical tension between ritual benefit and ultimate realization

sanskrit: सर्वे स्वरा इन्द्रस्यात्मानः सर्व ऊष्माणः प्रजापतेरात्मानः सर्वे स्पर्शा मृत्योरात्मानस्तं यदि स्वरेषूपालभेतेन्द्रंशरणं प्रपन्नोऽभूवं स त्वा प्रति वक्ष्यतीत्येनं ब्रूयात्
transliteration: sarve svarā indrasyātmānaḥ sarva ūṣmāṇaḥ prajāpaterātmānaḥ sarve sparśā mṛtyorātmānastaṃ yadi svareṣūpālabhetendraṃśaraṇaṃ prapanno'bhūvaṃ sa tvā prati vakṣyatītyenaṃ brūyāt
translation: All the vowels are like Indra ’s body with its various parts. The sibilants [śa, ṣa, etc.] are like Prajāpati ’s body with its various parts. The consonants, starting with ka, are the body and limbs of Death. If someone finds fault with the way the udgātā pronounces the vowels, he may say to that person: ‘As I began singing, I took refuge in Indra [i.e., I sought Indra’s blessings]. Ask him and he will give you the right answer [to your criticism].’
word_meaning: Sarve svarāḥ, all the vowels; indrasya ātmānaḥ, are like the body [with its limbs] of Indra; sarve ūṣmāṇaḥ, all the sibilants; prajāpateḥ ātmānaḥ, are like the body [with its limbs] of Prajāpati; sarve sparśāḥ, all the consonants, starting with ka; mṛtyoḥ ātmānaḥ, are like the body [with all its limbs] of Death; yadi upālabheta, if anyone criticizes; tam, him [the udgātā, who is singing the Sāma ]; svareṣu, regarding his pronunciation of the vowels; indram śaraṇam prapannaḥ abhūvam, [the udgātā will then say: ] I took refuge in Indra [when I started singing the vowels]; saḥ, he [Indra]; tvā prativakṣyati iti, will give you the right reply; enam brūyāt, say to him.
commentary: The vowels are said to be Indra’s body, for when you start doing something, including even a recitation, you have to feel strong enough to do the job fully and correctly. And in order that you may feel that you have the requisite strength, you pray for Indra’s blessings, and you feel that he is transmitting his strength to you. You feel that the vowels you are using are his body, the sibilants are the body of Prajāpati, and the consonants are the body of Death. Then if anyone criticizes your pronunciation of the vowels, you can tell him to ask Indra for the reason
claim_candidates: The verse equates the vowels with the body of Indra, indicating that proper vocalization requires invoking Indra’s blessings
The verse symbolically maps vowels, sibilants, and consonants to the bodies of Indra, Prajapati, and Death respectively, establishing a cosmological hierarchy of phonetic categories
The commentary endorses seeking refuge in Indra as a legitimate reply to criticism of vowel pronunciation, suggesting divine patronage validates artistic performance
objection_hooks: Equating phonemes with divine bodies may reduce transcendent deities to mere linguistic symbols, which appears at odds with the Advaita principle that Brahman is beyond attributes

Relying on external invocation of Indra for correct pronunciation seems to externalize agency, conflicting with the Upanishadic emphasis on inner self‑realization rather than dependence on external deities

sanskrit: अथ यद्येनमूष्मसूपालभेत प्रजापतिंशरणं प्रपन्नोऽभूवं स त्वा प्रति पेक्ष्यतीत्येनं ब्रूयादथ यद्येनं स्पर्शेषूपालभेत मृत्युं शरणं प्रपन्नोऽभूवं स त्वा प्रति धक्ष्यतीत्येनं ब्रूयात्
transliteration: atha yadyenamūṣmasūpālabheta prajāpatiṃśaraṇaṃ prapanno'bhūvaṃ sa tvā prati pekṣyatītyenaṃ brūyādatha yadyenaṃ sparśeṣūpālabheta mṛtyuṃ śaraṇaṃ prapanno'bhūvaṃ sa tvā prati dhakṣyatītyenaṃ brūyāt
translation: Then, if someone finds fault with his pronunciation of the sibilants, the singer will say to him: ‘When I started singing I sought Prajāpati ’s blessings. He will therefore crush you.’ Then, if someone finds fault with his pronunciation of the consonants, the singer will say to him: ‘I sought Death’s protection while singing. He will therefore burn you to ashes’.
word_meaning: Atha, then; yadi enam ūṣmasu upālabheta, if a person criticizes him for his pronunciation of the sibilants; prajāpatim śaraṇam prapanṇaḥ abhūvam, I took refuge in Prajāpati [when I started singing]; saḥ, he [Prajāpati]; tvā pratipekṣati iti, will crush you; enam brūyāt, he will say to him; atha, then; yadi enam sparśeṣu upālabheta, if someone criticizes him for his pronunciation of the consonants; mṛtyum śaraṇam prapannaḥ abhūvam, I took refuge in Death [when I started singing]; saḥ, he [Death]; tvā pratidhakṣyati iti, will burn you to ashes; enam brūyāt, he will say to him.
commentary: If you are reciting a hymn, seek Prajāpati’s protection concerning the sibilants. Then, even if you err, he will protect you. Similarly, you should seek Death’s protection concerning the consonants. He will protect you from harm for any mistakes you might make. In fact, those who find fault with you will themselves be punished, for by criticizing your singing, they are insulting Prajāpati or Death.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse teaches that invoking divine protection for specific speech sounds safeguards the singer against criticism
The commentary states that critics who insult the singer are punished because they offend the protective deities, indicating a moral principle of divine retribution for disrespect
The verse implies that taking refuge in Prajapati or Death permits pronunciation errors, suggesting that intention and protection outweigh technical perfection
objection_hooks: One could argue that reliance on external divine protection reduces personal responsibility for accurate pronunciation, contradicting the claim that protection alone is sufficient
The idea that critics are punished for their remarks may be seen as intolerant of legitimate critique, raising ethical concerns about freedom of expression
The assignment of different deities to sibilants and consonants may appear arbitrary, challenging the coherence of the protective scheme

sanskrit: सर्वे स्वरा घोषवन्तो बलवन्तो वक्तव्या इन्द्रे बलं ददानीति सर्व ऊष्माणोऽग्रस्ता अनिरस्ता विवृता वक्तव्याः प्रजापतेरात्मानं परिददानीति सर्वे स्पर्शा लेशेनानभिनिहिता वक्तव्या मृत्योरात्मानं परिहराणीति
transliteration: sarve svarā ghoṣavanto balavanto vaktavyā indre balaṃ dadānīti sarva ūṣmāṇo'grastā anirastā vivṛtā vaktavyāḥ prajāpaterātmānaṃ paridadānīti sarve sparśā leśenānabhinihitā vaktavyā mṛtyorātmānaṃ pariharāṇīti
translation: Each vowel should be articulated clearly and powerfully, [and while doing so, you should think, ] ‘I will give some of my strength to Indra .’ The sibilants also should be uttered fully and distinctly, without sounding as if you are swallowing part of them or spitting them out. [While uttering them, you should think, ] ‘I surrender myself to Prajāpati .’ Then each of the consonants also should be uttered separately and clearly, [and while doing so, you should think, ] ‘I will save myself from death’.
word_meaning: Sarve svarāḥ, all the vowels; ghoṣavantaḥ balavantaḥ, clearly and powerfully; vaktavyāḥ, articulated; indre balam dadāni iti, [say to yourself] I have to give strength to Indra [as if you are stronger than Indra and can afford to give him some of your strength]; sarve ūṣmāṇaḥ, all the sibilants; agrastaḥ, clearly uttered [i.e., no part of the letter left within the mouth, as if you are swallowing it]; anirastāḥ, not uttering the letter as if you are throwing it out of your mouth; vivṛtāḥ vaktavyāḥ, clearly expressed; prajāpateḥ ātmānam paridadāni iti, I surrender myself to Prajāpati; sarve sparśāḥ leśena anabhinihitāḥ vaktavyāḥ, each of the consonants should be uttered separately and distinctly; mṛtyoḥ ātmānam pariharāṇi iti, [while thinking] I save myself from Death.
commentary: It is important that when we recite a hymn, we recite the vowels, consonants, and the sibilants clearly and loudly. Care has to be taken that no two letters get mixed up, or that any part of a letter remains inside the mouth or seems to be spat out. Here it is said that the vowels are to be pronounced powerfully and that you should pray to Indra while singing, offering him some of your strength. This suggests that you feel very strong, as if you are stronger than Indra. You should also try to surrender yourself to Prajāpati while singing. You will then attain immortality.
claim_candidates: The verse instructs that precise articulation of vowels, sibilants, and consonants is coupled with mental offerings to Indra, Prajapati, and self‑preservation, making the recitation a ritual act
The mental intentions linked to each phonetic group function as a symbolic sacrifice, indicating that the chanting serves as a substitute for external sacrificial rites
Correct pronunciation is presented as a means to attain immortality, suggesting that linguistic precision is a path to spiritual liberation
objection_hooks: One could argue that the passage merely provides phonetic guidelines and the accompanying thoughts are conventional formulas, not genuine sacrificial offerings
The invocation of deities and sacrificial language may show that the chant complements rather than replaces traditional rituals, challenging the substitute claim
The claim that feeling stronger than Indra leads to divine empowerment may be seen as poetic exaggeration without explicit textual support for identity with the divine

sanskrit: त्रयो धर्मस्कन्धा यज्ञोऽध्ययनं दानमिति प्रथमस्तप एव द्वितीयो ब्रह्मचार्याचार्यकुलवासी तृतीयोऽत्यन्तमात्मानमाचार्यकुलेऽवसादयन्सर्व एते पुण्यलोका भवन्ति ब्रह्मसंस्थोऽमृतत्वमेति
transliteration: trayo dharmaskandhā yajño'dhyayanaṃ dānamiti prathamastapa eva dvitīyo brahmacāryācāryakulavāsī tṛtīyo'tyantamātmānamācāryakule'vasādayansarva ete puṇyalokā bhavanti brahmasaṃstho'mṛtatvameti
translation: There are three divisions of religion: The first comprises sacrifices, study, and charity; the second consists of austerities, such as fasting; and the third is the life of celibacy and living with the teacher in his house till death. People devoted to these three divisions of religion go to heaven after death. But one who is devoted to Brahman attains immortality.
word_meaning: Trayaḥ dharmaskandhaḥ, three divisions of religion; yajñaḥ adhyayanam dānam iti prathamaḥ, the first [division comprises] sacrifices, study, and charity; tapaḥ eva dvitīyaḥ, the second is austerities; tṛtīyaḥ, the third; brahmacārī ācāryakulavāsī atyantam ātmānam ācāryakule avasādayan, the life of celibacy and living with the teacher in his house till death; sarve ete, all these; puṇyalokāḥ bhavanti, attain heavenly worlds; brahmasaṃsthaḥ, [but] one devoted to Brahman; amṛtatvam eti, attains immortality.
commentary: You may practise religion in three ways: First, by performing sacrifices, studying the scriptures, and giving in charity; second, by performing austerities; and third, by observing celibacy and living with the teacher till death. All these are good, but they only lead to heaven. If you want immortality (that is, liberation), you have to devote yourself to realizing Brahman. There are certain words in our language that clearly distinguish one thing from another. For instance, barley is a particular type of grain. If you see the word ‘grain, ’ it could mean wheat or rice or barley. But if you see the word ‘barley, ’ you know exactly what is referred to. Similarly, the words brahmaniṣṭhā, or brahmasaṃstha, refer to a certain type of person whose only concern in life is to realize Brahman. This type of person may be of any age, any class, any caste, or any station of life. What distinguishes this type of person from any other is his or her total commitment to Brahman.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse states that the three traditional religious divisions—sacrifices, study, charity; austerities; and celibacy with a teacher—lead only to heavenly worlds and not to ultimate liberation
The verse asserts that exclusive devotion to Brahman (brahmasaṃstha) results in immortality, i.e., liberation beyond the heavenly realms
The commentary explains that a brahmasaṃstha person may belong to any age, class, caste or station, indicating that realization of Brahman is open to all social categories
objection_hooks: One could object that other Upanishadic passages present austerities and celibacy as direct means to realize Brahman, so the claim that they lead merely to heaven seems to undervalue their transformative potential

The claim that devotion to Brahman guarantees immortality may be challenged because the verse does not specify the nature of that devotion, and some schools argue that knowledge (jnana) rather than devotion is required for liberation

The assertion that any person regardless of caste can become a brahmasaṃstha may conflict with traditional Vedic injunctions that assign distinct duties to each varna, creating tension between universal accessibility and prescribed social order

sanskrit: प्रजापतिर्लोकानभ्यतपत्तेभ्योऽभितप्तेभ्यस्त्रयी विद्या सम्प्रास्रवत्तामभ्यतपत्तस्या अभितप्ताया एतान्यक्षराणि सम्प्रास्र्वन्त भूर्भुवः स्वरिति
transliteration: prajāpatirlokānabhyatapattebhyo'bhitaptebhyastrayī vidyā samprāsravattāmabhyatapattasyā abhitaptāyā etānyakṣarāṇi samprāsrvanta bhūrbhuvaḥ svariti
translation: Prajāpati [i.e., Virāṭ ] thought about the worlds [he would have]. Out of his thinking, the three Vedas took shape. He then began to think about the Vedas. As a result of this thinking, the Vedas gave birth to the three vyāhṛtis: bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, and svaḥ .
word_meaning: Prajāpatiḥ lokān abhyatapat, Prajāpati meditated on the worlds; tebhyaḥ abhitaptebhyaḥ, from [the worlds] which he meditated on; trayī vidyā samprāsravat, emerged the three Vedas; tām abhyatapat, [Prajāpati] meditated on them [the three Vedas]; tasyāḥ abhitaptāyāḥ, out of [the Vedas] which he meditated upon; etāni akṣarāṇi, these akṣaras [syllables]; samprāsravanta, emerged; bhūḥ bhuvaḥ svaḥ, bhūh, bhuvaḥ, and svaḥ; iti, that is all.
commentary: How can you attain liberation? You can attain it when you surrender everything for its sake. To make this clear, the story of how the world came into being is narrated: God (or Virāṭ, or Kaśyapa ) created this world by practising austerities. What kind of austerities? He simply planned the creation in his mind. This planning, this mental exercise, is enough austerity for him. And as thinking is doing for him, whatever he thinks immediately comes into being. The worlds emerge from within him. The first to manifest, however, were the three Vedas: Ṛk, Yajuḥ, and Sāma . When these emerged, the Creator thought about them, and from the Vedas came the three vyāhṛtis (lit., utterances): bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, and svaḥ . Then from these came Om, the symbol of Brahman . If we concentrate on Om, we attain Brahman. But we have to surrender everything for the sake of Brahman. This is the conclusion to be drawn from this story. Even the Creator has to concentrate on what he is going to create, and he has to surrender everything else.
claim_candidates: The verse states that Prajapati's meditation on the worlds gave rise to the three Vedas
The commentary claims that concentrating on the syllable Om, which follows bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, svaḥ, leads to the attainment of Brahman
The commentary equates mental planning or thought with austerity, asserting that thought alone can manifest creation
objection_hooks: Traditional Vedic practice emphasizes external ritual sacrifice, so equating thought with austerity may conflict with the established role of physical rites
Other Upanishadic teachings stress ethical conduct and knowledge as prerequisites for Brahman, suggesting that mere concentration on Om might be insufficient for liberation

sanskrit: तान्यभ्यतपत्तेभ्योऽभितप्तेभ्य ॐकारः सम्प्रास्रवत्तद्यथा शङ्कुना सर्वाणि पर्णानि संतृण्णान्येवमोंकारेण सर्वा वाक्संतृण्णोंकार एवेदं सर्वमोंकार एवेदं सर्वम्
transliteration: tānyabhyatapattebhyo'bhitaptebhya ॐkāraḥ samprāsravattadyathā śaṅkunā sarvāṇi parṇāni saṃtṛṇṇānyevamoṃkāreṇa sarvā vāksaṃtṛṇṇoṃkāra evedaṃ sarvamoṃkāra evedaṃ sarvam
translation: [ Prajāpati then] meditated on those three vyāhṛtis [bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, and svaḥ]. Out of the vyāhṛtis, which he thought about, emerged Oṃkāra . Just as a network of ribs is spread all over a leaf, similarly, Oṃkāra permeates every form of speech [or, everything]. All this is Oṃkāra. All this is Oṃkāra.
word_meaning: Tāni abhyatapat, he thought about those [vyāhṛtis]; tebhyaḥ abhitaptebhyaḥ, out of those which he thought about; oṃkāra samprāsravat, the syllable Om emerged; tat yathā, just as; śaṅkunā, by the ribs [of a leaf]; sarvāṇi parṇāni saṃtṛṇṇāni, all parts of the leaf are held together; evam, similarly; oṃkāreṇa sarvā vāk saṃtṛṇṇā, Oṃkāra permeates every form of speech; oṃkāraḥ eva idam sarvam oṃkāraḥ eva idam sarvam, all this is Oṃkāra, all this is Oṃkāra [the repetition is for emphasis].
commentary: Om is the essence of everything. Because Prajāpati meditated to create the three worlds, Om manifested itself. Om, in fact, is the support of everything.
claim_candidates: The verse states that Om permeates every form of speech
The commentary identifies Om as the support of everything, implying it is the sole reality underlying all phenomena
Meditation on the three vyāhṛtis leads to the emergence of Om, showing that the sacred syllable originates from contemplation of fundamental cosmic sounds
objection_hooks: Some interpret Om as a symbolic representation rather than the ontological basis, challenging the claim that it is the sole reality
The assertion that Om pervades all speech may be limited, since non‑verbal aspects of existence are not addressed, questioning its universal pervasiveness
The causal direction between meditation on vyāhṛtis and the appearance of Om is ambiguous, raising a tension about whether Om is cause or effect of cosmic creation

sanskrit: ब्रह्मवादिनो वदन्ति यद्वसूनां प्रातः सवनं रुद्राणां माध्यंदिनं सवनमादित्यानां च विश्वेषां च देवानां तृतीयसवनम्
transliteration: brahmavādino vadanti yadvasūnāṃ prātaḥ savanaṃ rudrāṇāṃ mādhyaṃdinaṃ savanamādityānāṃ ca viśveṣāṃ ca devānāṃ tṛtīyasavanam
translation: Those who believe in Brahman say: The morning savana is for the Vasus, the midday savana is for the Rudras, and the third, the evening savana, is for the Ādityas and the Viśvadevas [i.e., all the gods and goddesses].
word_meaning: Brahmavādinaḥ, those who believe in Brahman; vadanti, say; yat vasūnām prātaḥsavanam, the morning savana [i.e., the time when the soma juice is extracted from the soma creeper for the sacrifice] is meant for the eight Vasus; mādhyandinam savanam rudrāṇām, the noon savana is meant for the eleven Rudras; tṛtīya savanam, the third [i.e., the evening] savana; ādityānām ca viśveṣām devānām, is meant for the twelve Ādityas and the Viśvadevas.
commentary: ‘The morning savana belongs to the eight Vasus.’ This means that the Vasus control the earth. In the same way, since the midday savana belongs to the eleven Rudras, they control the interspace—the space between the earth and heaven. And as the evening savana belongs to the twelve Ādityas and the Viśvadevas, that means they control heaven. It follows then that there is no world left for the performer of the sacrifice.
claim_candidates: According to the Brahmavādins, the three daily savanas are each dedicated to distinct groups of deities: Vasus (morning), Rudras (midday), and Ādityas with Viśvadevas (evening).

The commentary interprets the allocation of savanas as indicating that the Vasus control the earth, the Rudras control the interspace between earth and heaven, and the Ādityas and Viśvadevas control heaven.

From the commentary's conclusion that there is no world left for the performer of the sacrifice, one can infer that the Brahmavādins view the sacrificial performer as lacking a distinct realm, suggesting the ultimate reality Brahman transcends all designated worlds.
objection_hooks: One might object that the verse only assigns savanas to deities and does not explicitly state that the sacrificer is excluded, so the inference about Brahman's transcendence exceeds the textual support.
Another objection is that the term control may be metaphorical rather than literal, allowing the performer to share the cosmic space, contrary to the claim that no world remains for him.

sanskrit: क्व तर्हि यजमानस्य लोक इति स यस्तं न विद्यात्कथं कुर्यादथ विद्वान्कुर्यात्
transliteration: kva tarhi yajamānasya loka iti sa yastaṃ na vidyātkathaṃ kuryādatha vidvānkuryāt
translation: Where then is the place for the yajamāna [one who performs a sacrifice]? How can he who does not know that place perform a sacrifice? He who knows can perform a sacrifice.
word_meaning: Kva tarhi yajamānasya lokaḥ iti, where then is the place for one who performs a sacrifice; saḥ yaḥ, he who; tam, that [place for the yajamāna]; na vidyāt, does not know; katham, how [can he]; kuryāt, perform [a sacrifice]; atha vidvān kuryāt, one who knows can [of course] perform [a sacrifice].
commentary: When a person performs a sacrifice, he does so in order that he may attain a certain world for himself. But if the three worlds—bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, and svaḥ—are already occupied by the Vasus and other gods, where is the place left for the sacrificer? In such a situation he would hardly have any inclination to perform a sacrifice, or even to sing attendant hymns such as the Sāma, or to. engage in rituals connected with a sacrifice. Ignorance on the part of a person is no bar to his performing a sacrifice. Rather, this verse is intended to praise knowledge. If a person knows how to recite the Sāma, that is a great help. It should be clearly understood, however, that though the Sāma or Oṃkāra is recited while a sacrifice is being performed, the
claim_candidates: The verse states that only one who knows the proper locus of sacrifice can correctly perform it
Ignorance does not stop a person from carrying out the external rites, but without knowledge the sacrifice lacks proper motivation or efficacy
The commentary highlights that reciting the Sāma or Omkara during a sacrifice is a valuable practice, indicating the importance of sacred sound in the ritual
objection_hooks: The verse mentions knowledge as a condition for performing a sacrifice, not explicitly for its efficacy, so the claim that ignorance makes the sacrifice ineffective may exceed the text

The commentary’s assertion that the three worlds are already occupied and thus no place remains for the sacrificer may be metaphorical, challenging the premise that a literal ‘place’ must be known for sacrifice

sanskrit: पुरा प्रातरनुवाकस्योपाकरणाज्जघनेन गार्हपत्यस्योदाङ्मुख उपविश्य स वासवं सामाभिगायति
transliteration: purā prātaranuvākasyopākaraṇājjaghanena gārhapatyasyodāṅmukha upaviśya sa vāsavaṃ sāmābhigāyati
translation: Before starting the morning chant, the sacrificer sits behind the Gārhapatya fire facing north and sings the Sāma about the Vasus .
word_meaning: Prātaḥ anuvākasya upākaraṇāt purā, before beginning the morning chant; jaghanena, behind; gārhapatyasya, the Gārhapatya fire; udaṅmukhaḥ, facing north; upaviśya, sits; saḥ, he [the sacrificed; vāsavam, about the Vasus; sāma abhigāyati, sings the Sāma.
claim_candidates: The sacrificer must sit behind the Gārhapatya fire before beginning the morning chant
The sacrificer must face north while seated for the ritual
The sacrificer sings the Sāma dedicated to the Vasus as part of the morning ritual
objection_hooks: One might argue that the instruction to sit behind the fire is context‑specific rather than a universal rule
The requirement to face north could be contested since other directions are also used in Vedic rites
The emphasis on singing the Sāma about the Vasus may be viewed as a ritual substitute rather than an essential component

sanskrit: लो३कद्वारमपावा३र्णू ३३ पश्येम त्वा वयं रा ३३३३३ हु ३ म् आ ३३ ज्या ३ यो ३ आ ३२१११ इति
transliteration: lo3kadvāramapāvā3rṇū 33 paśyema tvā vayaṃ rā 33333 hu 3 m ā 33 jyā 3 yo 3 ā 32111 iti
translation: O Fire, please open the door for us—that is, make the path clear—so that we may see you for obtaining full control of the earth.
word_meaning: Lokadvāram apāvṛṇu, [O Fire, ] please open the door of the world [i.e., make the path clear]; vayam
commentary: The additional syllables in the verse have no special meaning. They merely serve to make the Sāma complete.
claim_candidates: The verse explicitly petitions fire to open the world’s door, indicating that fire is invoked as a means to clear obstacles for human perception.
The verse links the clearing of the path by fire with the goal of attaining complete control over the earth, suggesting a perceived causal relationship between fire’s favor and earthly dominion.
The commentary states that the extra syllables are merely musical fillers and have no doctrinal meaning, showing that not all textual elements are intended as philosophical content.
objection_hooks: One might argue that the request to fire is metaphorical rather than literal, challenging the claim that fire is treated as a divine agent with practical power.
The assertion of a causal link between fire’s favor and earthly control could be contested as the translation may simply express desire, not doctrinal efficacy.
Although the commentary calls the extra syllables musical, a critic could claim that ornamental syllables may still carry hidden symbolic significance, questioning the claim they are purely filler.

sanskrit: अथ जुहोति नमोऽग्नये पृथिवीक्षिते लोकक्षिते लोकं मे यजमानाय विन्दैष वै यजमानस्य लोक एतास्मि
transliteration: atha juhoti namo'gnaye pṛthivīkṣite lokakṣite lokaṃ me yajamānāya vindaiṣa vai yajamānasya loka etāsmi
translation: Then the sacrificer begins the offerings [with this mantra ]: ‘O Agni, you are in this world. I salute you. Please acquire the right world for me, who am performing a sacrifice. I am ready to go to a world appropriate for one who performs sacrifices’.
word_meaning: Atha juhoti, now the sacrificer begins the offerings; pṛthivīkṣite, living on the earth; lokakṣite, living in the loka [world]; agnaye namaḥ, salutations to fire; yajamānāya me, for me, the sacrificer; lokam vinda, acquire the right world; eṣaḥ vai yajamānasya lokaḥ etā asmi, I am going to this world fit for a sacrificer.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the sacrificer’s future world is determined by his performance of sacrifice
The verse treats Agni as a divine agent who can grant the appropriate world to the sacrificer
The verse links the act of yajna with attaining a favorable after‑life, implying ritual action shapes destiny
objection_hooks: The verse merely petitions Agni for a suitable world, without guaranteeing that sacrifice alone determines the outcome
Agni may be addressed as a worldly fire rather than a supreme deity, so attributing divine agency could be overstated
The text expresses the sacrificer’s desire for a favorable world but does not provide a doctrinal guarantee that sacrifice ensures such an after‑life

sanskrit: अत्र यजमानः परस्तादायुषः स्वाहापजहि परिघमित्युक्त्वोत्तिष्ठति तस्मै वसवः प्रातःसवनं सम्प्रयच्छन्ति
transliteration: atra yajamānaḥ parastādāyuṣaḥ svāhāpajahi parighamityuktvottiṣṭhati tasmai vasavaḥ prātaḥsavanaṃ samprayacchanti
translation: ‘I, the yajamāna, have run the full course of my life in this world.’ With these words, he will say, ‘ Svāhā, ’ [and offer his oblation]. Then he will rise, saying, ‘Please unbolt the door to the world for which I am destined.’ The Vasus then give him the ownership of the earth, which is the result of the savana performed in the morning.
word_meaning: Atra, here [in this world]; yajamānaḥ parastāt āyuṣaḥ, I, the sacrificer, have lived the full course of life; svāhā, [with these words, he will say] svāhā; parigham, the bolt [of the gateway to the world]; apajahi, remove; iti uktvā, saying this; uttiṣṭhati, he rises; vasavaḥ, the Vasus [to whom the morning savana is offered]; tasmai, to him [the sacrificer]; prātaḥsavanam samprayacchanti, give away the morning savana [i.e., give away this earth].
commentary: The yajamāna (the sacrificer) keeps performing his sacrifices till he feels that it is time for him to die. He then offers his last oblation, saying, ‘Svāhā.’ Getting up, he says he is going to the world for which he is destined and demands that the door to that world be unbolted. The Vasus own the earth by virtue of the morning savana (that is, extracting the soma juice and having a morning wash with it). They are pleased with the yajamāna for his performance of the rituals, so they surrender the ownership of the earth to him.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that the yajamāna’s transition to the destined world is earned by completing his sacrificial duties
The Vasus transfer ownership of the earth to the sacrificer as a reward for his ritual performance
Uttering Svāhā at the moment of death serves as a final offering that legitimizes his passage to the next world
objection_hooks: The text does not explicitly tie the door‑unbolting request to merit, so the link between conduct and destiny may be a later interpretation
The description of the Vasus giving earth ownership could be symbolic rather than a literal grant of material sovereignty
Svāhā is a routine sacrificial utterance; the verse does not specify that it uniquely validates the transition

sanskrit: पुरा माध्यंदिनस्य सवनस्योपाकरणाज्जघनेनाग्नीध्रीयस्योदङ्मुख उपविश्य स रौद्रंसामाभिगायति
transliteration: purā mādhyaṃdinasya savanasyopākaraṇājjaghanenāgnīdhrīyasyodaṅmukha upaviśya sa raudraṃsāmābhigāyati
translation: Before starting the midday savana, the yajamāna sits behind the Dakṣiṇāgni fire, facing north. He then sings the Sāma addressed to the Rudras .
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [the yajamāna]; upākaraṇāt purā, before beginning; mādhyandinasya savanasya, the midday savana; āgnīdhrīyasya jaghanena, behind the Dakṣiṇāgni fire; udaṅmukhaḥ, facing north; upaviśya, sitting; raudram sāma abhigāyati, sings the Sāma to the Rudras.
commentary: Earlier, the way the yajamāna conquers the earth was shown. Now how the mid-region can be won is described. After sitting behind the Dakṣiṇāgni fire, facing north, he starts singing the Sāma addressed to the Rudras. He continues doing this with the idea of attaining union with Virāṭ .
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The yajamāna’s prescribed posture of sitting behind the Dakṣiṇāgni fire and facing north is intended to align him with the Rudras
Singing the Sāma addressed to the Rudras is performed with the purpose of attaining union with Virāṭ, showing that ritual music serves as a means of self‑realization
The description of winning the mid‑region (midday savana) indicates that the yajamāna’s external ritual actions are meant to parallel an inner conquest of the self
objection_hooks: The verse may simply be detailing ritual procedure without asserting a spiritual aim, so interpreting the Sāma singing as a method for self‑realization could exceed the text’s explicit meaning
Facing north might be a practical requirement for fire rites rather than a symbolic act of aligning with the Rudras, challenging the claim about posture creating divine alignment

The phrase ‘winning the mid‑region’ could be understood metaphorically as achieving ritual efficacy rather than describing an inner spiritual conquest, questioning the claim of a parallel inner conquest

sanskrit: लो३कद्वारमपावा३र्णू३३ पश्येम त्वा वयं वैरा३३३३३ हु३म् आ३३ज्या ३यो३आ३२१११इति
transliteration: lo3kadvāramapāvā3rṇū33 paśyema tvā vayaṃ vairā33333 hu3m ā33jyā 3yo3ā32111iti
translation: O Fire, please open the door for us—that is, make the path clear—so that we may see you for obtaining full control of the world of Virāṭ .
word_meaning: Lokadvāram apāvṛṇu, [O Fire, ] please open the door of the world [i.e., make the path clear]; vayam vairājyāya, so we may gain control of the world of Virāṭ; tvā paśyema, [and for that purpose] may see you.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse invokes fire as a divine agent that can remove worldly obstacles, indicating that the ultimate reality can be appealed to for clearing the path to knowledge
Seeing fire is presented as a means to obtain control of the world of Virāṭ, suggesting that direct perception of the divine equates to mastery over cosmic order
The request for fire to open the 'door of the world' portrays the ultimate reality as both barrier and gateway, implying that the individual self must transcend ignorance to access the universal self
objection_hooks: Advaita teaches that liberation transcends worldly control, so seeking dominion over the world of Virāṭ may conflict with the goal of realizing Brahman
Fire may be a specific ritual deity rather than a symbol of Brahman, making the claim that the verse equates fire with the sole reality of Brahman potentially overstated

sanskrit: अथ जुहोति नमो वायवेऽन्तरिक्षक्षिते लोकक्षिते लोकं मे यजमानाय विन्दैष वै यजमानस्य लोक एतास्मि
transliteration: atha juhoti namo vāyave'ntarikṣakṣite lokakṣite lokaṃ me yajamānāya vindaiṣa vai yajamānasya loka etāsmi
translation: Then the sacrificer begins the offerings [with this mantra ]: ‘O Vāyu, you are in the mid-region. I salute you. Please acquire the right world for me, who am performing a sacrifice. I am ready to go to a world appropriate for one who performs sacrifices’.
word_meaning: Atha juhoti, now the sacrificer begins the offerings; antarikṣakṣite, living in the mid-region; lokakṣite, living in the loka [world]; vāyave namaḥ, salutations to Vāyu [air]; yajamānāya me, for me, the sacrificer; lokam vinda, acquire the right world; eṣaḥ vai
