word_meaning: Atha, next; yat avocam, when I said; bhuvaḥ prapadye hi, I take refuge in bhuvaḥ [the mid-region]; hi eva tat avocam, it is this that I meant; agnim prapadye, I take refuge in fire; vāyum prapadye, I take refuge in air; ādityam prapadye, I take refuge in the sun.
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse equates taking refuge in the mid‑region with taking refuge in fire, air and the sun, indicating that these elements serve as symbolic representations of the ultimate reality
By invoking fire, air and the sun as objects of refuge the verse suggests that Brahman can be approached through natural cosmological principles rather than solely through ritualistic worship
The verse demonstrates a shift from the abstract term bhuvaḥ to concrete elemental refuges, reflecting an instructional method that uses tangible phenomena to convey the nature of Brahman
objection_hooks: One might argue that the verse merely lists fire, air and the sun as protective deities and does not intend them to symbolize Brahman
The language of taking refuge is traditionally devotional, so the claim that the verse favors a cosmological approach over ritual worship may be overstated
The clarification of bhuvaḥ could be simply lexical, not an instructional strategy, challenging the claim of pedagogical intent

sanskrit: अथ यदवोचंस्वः प्रपद्य इत्यृग्वेदं प्रपद्ये यजुर्वेदं प्रपद्ये सामवेदं प्रपद्य इत्येव तदवोचं तदवोचम्
transliteration: atha yadavocaṃsvaḥ prapadya ityṛgvedaṃ prapadye yajurvedaṃ prapadye sāmavedaṃ prapadya ityeva tadavocaṃ tadavocam
translation: Then, when I said, “I take refuge in svaḥ, ” what I meant was that I take refuge in the Ṛg Veda, I take refuge in the Yajur Veda, and I take refuge in the Sāma Veda . It is this that I meant.
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat avocam, when I said; svaḥ prapadye iti, I take refuge in svaḥ [heaven]; iti eva tat avocam, it is this that I meant; ṛg vedam prapadye, I take refuge in the Ṛg Veda; yajur vedam prapadye, I take refuge in the Yajur Veda; sāma vedam prapadye, I take refuge in the Sāma Veda; tat avocam tat avocam, it is this that I meant, this that I meant.
commentary: One should first meditate on the immortal chest, and then repeat the mantras following the meditation. To emphasize the importance of the worship, the mantras are repeated again and again.
concept_tags: Progressive Worship of Brahman up to the Universal Self
claim_candidates: The verse equates taking refuge in the three Vedas with taking refuge in the divine sanctuary, showing the Vedas are presented as a spiritual haven
The commentary indicates that meditation on the immortal chest must precede mantra repetition, implying internal contemplation is a prerequisite for external worship
The repeated mantra recitation in the commentary highlights the importance of persistent worship in Advaita practice
objection_hooks: One might argue that the verse uses svaḥ only as a conventional term and does not literally identify the Vedas with the divine sanctuary
The instruction to meditate on the immortal chest may be a later exegetical addition, not part of the original verse, weakening the claim of textual prerequisite
The emphasis on repetition could be a pedagogical technique rather than a doctrinal claim about the necessity of continual worship

sanskrit: पुरुषो वाव यज्ञस्तस्य यानि चतुर्विंशति वर्षाणि तत्प्रातःसवनं चतुर्विंशत्यक्षरा गायत्री गायत्रं प्रातःसवनं तदस्य वसवोऽन्वायत्ताः प्राणा वाव वसव एते हीदंसर्वं वासयन्ति
transliteration: puruṣo vāva yajñastasya yāni caturviṃśati varṣāṇi tatprātaḥsavanaṃ caturviṃśatyakṣarā gāyatrī gāyatraṃ prātaḥsavanaṃ tadasya vasavo'nvāyattāḥ prāṇā vāva vasava ete hīdaṃsarvaṃ vāsayanti
translation: The human body is like a sacrifice, and the first twenty-four years are like the morning libation. The gāyatrī has twenty-four syllables, and the morning libation is accompanied by the gāyatrī. The Vasus reside in this morning libation. The Vasus are the vital breaths and the sense organs, for the word vasu means those who make others live and who live themselves.
word_meaning: Puruṣaḥ vāva yajñaḥ, the human body is like a sacrifice; tasya yāni cāturviṃśati varṣāṇi, its first twenty-four years; tat, that; prātaḥ savanam, is the morning libation; gāyatrī caturviṃśati akṣarā, the gāyatrī is constituted of twenty-four syllables; gāyatram prātaḥ savanam, the morning libation is accompanied by the gāyatrī; asya, of this [i.e., this sacrifice of the human body]; tat, it [the morning libation covering the first twenty-four years]; vasavaḥ, the deities called Vasus; anvāyattāḥ, are connected; prāṇāḥ vāva vasavaḥ, the prāṇas [together with the sense organs] are the Vasus; hi, for; ete, these [Vasus]; idam sarvam, everything in this [world]; vāsayanti, cause to live [and they themselves also live (vasu)].
commentary: In the previous section, meditation and repetition of certain mantras was recommended for the long life of one’s children. The same thing is being recommended now for one’s own life. Here the meditation is that one’s life is a sacrifice, and that the first twenty-four years are the morning offering. This offering is connected with the gāyatrī, which has twenty-four syllables. The Vasus are also connected with this offering, and they are thought of as the organs, which abide ( vasu ) and which also make others abide.
claim_candidates: The verse equates the human body’s first twenty-four years with the morning libation of a sacrifice, presenting this period as a prime offering in one’s spiritual life
The Gayatri mantra’s twenty-four syllables are symbolically linked to the first twenty‑four years of life, indicating a ritual correspondence between vocal recitation and lived time
The Vasus are identified with pranas and sense organs, portraying bodily functions as divine agents that sustain life
By framing meditation on the body as a sacrifice, the verse proposes an internalized substitute for external ritual sacrifices
objection_hooks: The identification of the first twenty‑four years with a morning libation may be metaphorical rather than prescribing a literal sacrificial period, limiting its normative claim
Linking the Gayatri’s syllable count to years could be a numerological coincidence without explicit scriptural justification, weakening the claim of intentional correspondence
Equating the Vasus with sense organs may conflict with other Upanishadic passages that treat Vasus as cosmic deities, creating tension over symbolic versus literal identification

The suggestion that meditation substitutes for external sacrifice may be contested by traditions that uphold the necessity of actual ritual fire, questioning the universality of this internalized practice

sanskrit: तं चेदेतस्मिन्वयसि किंचिदुपतपेत्स ब्रूयात्प्राणा वसव इदं मे प्रातःसवनं माध्यंदिनंसवनमनुसंतनुतेति माहं प्राणानां वसूनां मध्ये यज्ञो विलोप्सीयेत्युद्धैव तत एत्यगदो ह भवति
transliteration: taṃ cedetasminvayasi kiṃcidupatapetsa brūyātprāṇā vasava idaṃ me prātaḥsavanaṃ mādhyaṃdinaṃsavanamanusaṃtanuteti māhaṃ prāṇānāṃ vasūnāṃ madhye yajño vilopsīyetyuddhaiva tata etyagado ha bhavati
translation: If, within these first twenty-four years of his life, he has some ailment, he should then say: ‘O Prāṇas, O Vasus, please extend this first libation to the midday libation. As the sacrifice, may I not disappear among the Vasus, who are my prāṇas [i.e., who are like my life].’ [If he prays like this, ] he gets rid of his ailment and becomes fully well.
word_meaning: Cet, if; etasmin vayasi, within these [twenty-four] years of his life; tam kiñcit upatapet, something troubles him; saḥ brūyāt, he will say; prāṇāḥ, O Prāṇas; vasavaḥ, O Vasus; idam me prātaḥ savanam, this, my morning libation; mādhyandinam savanam, to the midday libation; anusantanuta iti, extend; mā aham, may I not; yajñaḥ, as the sacrifice; madhye prāṇānām vasūnām, in the midst of the prāṇas, who are the Vasus; vilopsīya iti, disappear; tataḥ ha eva ut-eti, he gets rid of that [ailment]; agadaḥ ha bhavati, [and] becomes well.
commentary: W SRrīTñī: 5fFTT ^ ^ iūḍpñ II 3 II
claim_candidates: The verse prescribes a ritual prayer invoking the Pranas and Vasus as extensions of one’s own life‑force to cure illness within the first twenty‑four years of life
The verse equates the Pranas with the Vasus, suggesting that the individual life‑force is identified with cosmic deities and thus reflects the identity of the individual self with the universal order

The verse indicates that performing the libation and invoking the sacrifice can prevent the disappearance of the self among the Vasus, showing that ritual sacrifice is presented as a means to preserve personal existence
objection_hooks: Equating internal pranas with external Vasus may be seen as merely symbolic rather than a literal identity, raising doubt about the claim of self‑identification with the universal order

Relying on ritual libation and prayer to cure physical ailment can be challenged by a materialist perspective that health depends on physiological causes, questioning the efficacy of the prescribed practice

The idea that a sacrifice can prevent the self’s disappearance appears contradictory, since sacrifice traditionally involves offering something away, not preserving it, creating tension in the claim that ritual sacrifice maintains personal existence

sanskrit: अथ यानि चतुश्चत्वारिंशद्वर्षाणि तन्माध्यंदिनं सवनं चतुश्चत्वारिंशदक्षरा त्रिष्टुप्त्रैष्टुभं माध्यंदिनंसवनं तदस्य रुद्रा अन्वायत्ताः प्राणा वाव रुद्रा एते हीदंसर्वंरोदयन्ति
transliteration: atha yāni catuścatvāriṃśadvarṣāṇi tanmādhyaṃdinaṃ savanaṃ catuścatvāriṃśadakṣarā triṣṭuptraiṣṭubhaṃ mādhyaṃdinaṃsavanaṃ tadasya rudrā anvāyattāḥ prāṇā vāva rudrā ete hīdaṃsarvaṃrodayanti
translation: Then the next forty-four years are like the midday libation. The triṣṭubh metre has forty-four syllables, and the midday libation is accompanied by a hymn which is in the triṣṭubh metre. The Rudras are connected with this midday libation. The prāṇas are called Rudras because they [are cruel and] make everyone in this world weep.
word_meaning: Atha, then; yāni catuścatvāriṃśat varṣāṇi, that which is the next forty-four years; tat, that; mādhyandinam savanam, is the midday libation; triṣṭup catuścatvāriṃśat akṣarā, the triṣṭubh metre is constituted of forty-four syllables; traiṣṭubham mādhyandinam savanam, the midday libation is accompanied by a hymn in the triṣṭubh metre; asya, of this [i.e., this sacrifice of the human body]; tat, it [the midday libation covering the next forty-four years]; rudrāḥ, the deities called Rudras; anvāyattāḥ, are connected; prāṇāḥ vāva rudrāḥ, the prāṇas [together with the sense organs] are the Rudras; hi, for; ete, these [Rudras]; idam sarvam rodayanti, make everyone in this world weep.
commentary: The sense organs become very powerful when a person has reached middle age, and they may make him do things he will regret and for which he will have to ‘weep.’ In this sacrifice, these next forty-four years correspond to the midday worship. The libation offered at this worship is accompanied by a hymn in the triṣṭubh metre, which has forty-four syllables. In this way, one can easily meditate on a human being as a ritualistic sacrifice.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse equates the middle forty-four years of life with a midday libation, presenting this period as a sacrificial phase in the human lifespan
The text identifies the pranas and sense organs as Rudras, indicating that they possess a cruel aspect that can cause suffering
The triṣṭubh metre of forty‑four syllables is deliberately matched to the forty‑four years, creating a symbolic alignment between poetic structure and human temporal stages
objection_hooks: Equating middle age with sacrifice may impose a negative valuation on that life stage, whereas other Upanishadic teachings regard all stages as opportunities for self‑realisation
Labeling the pranas as Rudras could be contested because Rudras are traditionally destructive deities, while pranas are usually described as life‑sustaining forces
The correspondence between metre and years might be viewed as a poetic convention rather than evidence of an underlying metaphysical principle

sanskrit: तं चेदेतस्मिन्वयसि किंचिदुपतपेत्स ब्रूयात्प्राणा रुद्रा इदं मे माध्यंदिनंसवनं तृतीयसवनमनुसंतनुतेति माहं प्राणानांरुद्राणां मध्ये यज्ञो विलोप्सीयेत्युद्धैव तत एत्यगदो ह भवति
transliteration: taṃ cedetasminvayasi kiṃcidupatapetsa brūyātprāṇā rudrā idaṃ me mādhyaṃdinaṃsavanaṃ tṛtīyasavanamanusaṃtanuteti māhaṃ prāṇānāṃrudrāṇāṃ madhye yajño vilopsīyetyuddhaiva tata etyagado ha bhavati
translation: If, within these next forty-four years of his life, he has some ailment, he should then say: ‘O Prāṇas, O Rudras, please extend my midday libation and join it to the third libation. As the sacrifice, may I not disappear among the Rudras, who are my prāṇas [i.e., who are like my life].’ [If he prays like this, ] he gets rid of his ailment and becomes fully well.
word_meaning: Cet, if; etasmin vayasi, within these [forty-four] years of his life; tam kiñcit upatapet, something troubles him; saḥ brūyāt, he will say; prāṇāḥ, O Prāṇas; rudrāḥ, O Rudras; idam me mādhyandinam savanam, this, my midday libation; tṛtīya savanam, to the third libation; anusantanuta iti, extend; mā aham, may I not; yajñaḥ, as the sacrifice; prāṇānām rudrāṇām madhye, in the midst of the prāṇas, who are the Rudras; vilopsīya iti, disappear; tataḥ ha eva ut-eti, he gets rid of that [ailment]; agadaḥ ha bhavati, [and] becomes well.
commentary: The idea behind this prayer is: ‘O Rudras, you are like my life. I am the sacrifice, and now the midday libation is going on. Will you please extend this libation so that I may do the third libation without a break [that is, may I live long enough to do the evening libation]? I am the sacrifice, and I don’t want the sacrifice to be stopped before it reaches the end. And I don’t want to be separated from the Rudras, who are like my life.’ If the afflicted person (who is the sacrifice) keeps praying like this, he will then get well and be fully fit again.
claim_candidates: The verse prescribes a prayer to the Pranas, identified as Rudras, asking them to extend the midday libation so the sacrificer may live to perform the third libation, linking ritual continuity with personal health
The text equates the internal vital forces (pranas) with divine Rudras, showing a symbolic representation that treats bodily life as a manifestation of divine power
The commentary states that reciting this prayer causes the ailment to disappear, indicating that the verse endorses the efficacy of devotional speech within the sacrificial context for healing
objection_hooks: One may argue that the promised healing is metaphorical rather than literal, questioning whether the prayer actually cures the ailment
The identification of pranas with Rudras could be seen as poetic metaphor, not a doctrinal claim that the self is identical with the divine, weakening the symbolic interpretation
Emphasizing ritual prayer for health may conflict with Advaita’s priority of knowledge over ritual, creating tension with the claim that sacrifice ensures well‑being

sanskrit: अथ यान्यष्टाचत्वारिंशद्वर्षाणि तत्तृतीयसवनमष्टाचत्वारिंशदक्षरा जगती जागतं तृतीयसवनं तदस्यादित्या अन्वायत्ताः प्राणा वावादित्या एते हीदंसर्वमाददते
transliteration: atha yānyaṣṭācatvāriṃśadvarṣāṇi tattṛtīyasavanamaṣṭācatvāriṃśadakṣarā jagatī jāgataṃ tṛtīyasavanaṃ tadasyādityā anvāyattāḥ prāṇā vāvādityā ete hīdaṃsarvamādadate
translation: Then the next forty-eight years are the third libation. The jagatī metre has forty-eight syllables, and the third libation is accompanied by a hymn which is in the jagatī metre. The Ādityas are connected with this third libation. The prāṇas are called Ādityas because they accept [ādā] all things.
word_meaning: Atha, next; yāni aṣṭācatvāriṃśat varṣāṇi, that which is the next forty-eight years; tat, that; tṛtīya savanam, is the third libation; jagatī aṣṭācatvāriṃśat akṣarā, the jagatī metre is constituted of forty-eight syllables; jāgatam tṛtīya savanam, the third libation is accompanied by a hymn in the jagatī metre; asya, of this [i.e., this sacrifice of the human body]; tat, it [the third libation covering the next forty-eight years]; ādityāḥ, the deities called Ādityas; anvāyattāḥ, are connected; prāṇāḥ vāva ādityāḥ, the prāṇas are the Ādityas; hi, for; ete, these [Ādityas]; idam sarvam ādadate, accept [ādā] all objects.
commentary: A human being is supposed to live a total of a hundred and sixteen years, which has three phaṣes: the first twenty-four years, the next forty-four years, and the last forty-eight years. When this life is thought of as a sacrifice, the first twenty-four years would be the morning offering, the next forty-four years would be the midday offering, and the last forty-eight years would be the evening offering. Just as the morning libation is connected with the Vasus, so the first twenty-four years of one’s life are also connected with the Vasus. The gāyatrī is sung during the morning offering, and it has twenty-four syllables. The offering made at midday is connected with the Rudras, and it is accompanied by a hymn in the triṣṭubh metre, having forty-four syllables. Then the evening offering is connected with the Ādityas, and it is sung in the jagatī metre, having forty-eight syllables. The concept of a human life as a sacrifice arises from the similarity to the components of a ritualistic sacrifice.
claim_candidates: The human lifespan of one hundred sixteen years is divided into three sacrificial phases of twenty four, forty four, and forty eight years
Each phase is linked to a specific group of deities: Vasus for the first phase, Rudras for the second, and Ādityas for the third
The hymns sung in each phase match the syllable count of the phase: Gāyatrī with twenty four syllables, a triṣṭubh hymn with forty four syllables, and a jagatī hymn with forty eight syllables
objection_hooks: The fixed year counts may not reflect actual human lifespans, questioning the universal applicability of the sacrificial model
Identifying prāṇas as Ādityas because they accept all things blurs the line between physiological processes and divine qualities
The correspondence between syllable counts and years could be seen as a symbolic convenience rather than a doctrinal requirement

sanskrit: तं चेदेतस्मिन्वयसि किंचिदुपतपेत्स ब्रूयात्प्राणा अदित्या इदं मे तृतीयसवनमायुरनुसंतनुतेति माहं प्राणानामादित्यानां मध्ये यज्ञो विलोप्सीयेत्युद्धैव तत एत्यगदो हैव भवति
transliteration: taṃ cedetasminvayasi kiṃcidupatapetsa brūyātprāṇā adityā idaṃ me tṛtīyasavanamāyuranusaṃtanuteti māhaṃ prāṇānāmādityānāṃ madhye yajño vilopsīyetyuddhaiva tata etyagado haiva bhavati
translation: If, within the next forty-eight years of his life, he has some ailment, he should then say: ‘O Prāṇas, O Ādityas, please extend my evening libation to the end of my life. As the sacrifice, may I not disappear among the Ādityas, who are my prāṇas [i.e., who are like my life].’ [If he prays like this, ] he gets rid of his ailment and becomes well.
word_meaning: Cet, if; etasmin vayasi, within these [forty-eight] years of his life; tam kiñcit upatapet, something troubles him; saḥ brūyāt, he should say; prāṇāḥ, O Prāṇas; ādityāḥ, O Ādityas; idam me tṛtīya savanam, this, my evening libation; āyuḥ, to the full length; anusantanuta iti, extend; mā aham, may I not; yajñaḥ, as the sacrifice; prāṇānām ādityānām madhye, in the midst of the prāṇas, who are the Ādityas; vilopsīya iti, disappear; tataḥ ha eva ut-eti, he gets rid of that [ailment]; agadaḥ ha eva bhavati, [and] becomes well.
claim_candidates: The verse prescribes a ritual utterance invoking the pranas and Ādityas as a sacrificial act to cure illness
The verse treats the prāṇas and Ādityas as symbolic equivalents of the individual's vital essence, indicating a symbolic representation of the divine within the self
The verse suggests that extending one’s evening libation to the end of life through prayer can prevent disappearance of the self and restore health
objection_hooks: One might object that invoking pranas and Ādityas does not guarantee cure, as the verse’s efficacy could depend on belief rather than inherent power
The identification of pranas with Ādityas as symbolic may be challenged as a literal theological claim rather than metaphorical, creating tension about the intended meaning
Critics could argue that the claim that extending a libation prevents disappearance lacks a clear causal mechanism, making the link between ritual duration and longevity debatable

sanskrit: एतद्ध स्म वै तद्विद्वानाह महिदास ऐतरेयः स किं म एतदुपतपसि योऽहमनेन न प्रेष्यामीति स ह षोडशं वर्षशतमजीवत्प्र ह षोडशं वर्षशतं जीवति य एवं वेद
transliteration: etaddha sma vai tadvidvānāha mahidāsa aitareyaḥ sa kiṃ ma etadupatapasi yo'hamanena na preṣyāmīti sa ha ṣoḍaśaṃ varṣaśatamajīvatpra ha ṣoḍaśaṃ varṣaśataṃ jīvati ya evaṃ veda
translation: Having known this, Itarā’s son Mahidāsa said: ‘O disease, why are you troubling me so? Rest assured, I am not going to die [of this disease].’ He lived for one hundred and sixteen years. A person who knows this also lives that long.
word_meaning: Etat ha vai vidvān, having known this; mahidāsaḥ aitareyaḥ, Mahidāsa Aitareya, the son of Itarā; āha sma, said; saḥ [i.e; , tvam ] kim me etat upatapasi, O disease, why are you troubling me; yaḥ aham anena na preṣyāmi iti, who for certain will not die of this; saḥ, he [Mahidāsa Aitareya]; ha ṣoḍaśam varṣaśatam, one hundred and sixteen years; ajīvat, lived; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; ha -ṣoḍaśam varṣaśatam prajīvati, also lives one hundred and sixteen years.
commentary: Mahidāsa is an example of one who conquered death by his knowledge and strong will.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that one who truly knows the taught truth will live for one hundred and sixteen years, implying that knowledge can prevent death
The commentary presents Mahidāsa as an exemplar who conquered death through knowledge and strong will, suggesting that enlightenment makes mortality optional
The speaker’s confident denial of death to disease and subsequent long life indicate that mental certainty can influence physical health
objection_hooks: Empirical evidence shows that all humans die regardless of knowledge, challenging the literal reading that knowledge alone can prevent death
Disease has material causes that are not overcome merely by mental conviction, creating tension between the verse’s claim and medical understanding

sanskrit: स यदशिशिषति यत्पिपासति यन्न रमते ता अस्य दीक्षाः
transliteration: sa yadaśiśiṣati yatpipāsati yanna ramate tā asya dīkṣāḥ
translation: That he has the desire to eat, the desire to drink, and no desire to indulge in sense pleasures—this is his initiation [i.e., this is how he has to begin practising self-restraint].
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [the person who is performing the puruṣa sacrifice just mentioned]; yat aśiśiṣati, that he wants to eat; yat pipāsati, that he wants to drink; yat na ramate, that he has no desire to indulge in sense pleasure; tāḥ, all these; asya dīkṣaḥ, are the initiation rites [to perform the sacrifice].
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse defines initiation (dīkṣā) as the practice of restraining basic bodily desires such as eating and drinking while also renouncing sense pleasures
Self‑restraint begins with controlling the desire for food and drink, implying that mastery over these primary needs is prerequisite for higher spiritual discipline
The absence of desire for sense pleasures is presented as a necessary condition for the initiation rites, indicating that ethical renunciation is integral to the sacrificial practice
objection_hooks: Renouncing desire for food and drink may conflict with the practical necessity of sustenance, raising doubt whether true initiation can demand such abstention
Advaita philosophy treats desire itself as ultimately illusory, so distinguishing between bodily desires and sense‑pleasure desires may be philosophically inconsistent

sanskrit: अथ यद्धसति यज्जक्षति यन्मैथुनं चरति स्तुतशस्त्रैरेव तदेति
transliteration: atha yaddhasati yajjakṣati yanmaithunaṃ carati stutaśastraireva tadeti
translation: After this, he laughs, he eats, and he even enjoys some sense pleasure—these represent the sounds coming from the stutas and śastras .
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat hasati, he laughs; yat jakṣati, he eats; yat maithunam carati, he indulges in sense pleasure; stuta -śastraiḥ eva tat eti, they are like [the sounds of] the stutas and śastras [certain hymns and mantras recited during the sacrifice].
claim_candidates: The verse equates mundane activities such as laughing eating and sexual pleasure with the sounds of stutas and shastras indicating they serve as symbolic substitutes for formal ritual recitations
By presenting sense pleasures as akin to ritual sounds the verse suggests that external ritual utterances are insufficient without inner experience implying a critique of ritualism
The text blurs the distinction between secular enjoyment and sacred utterance by describing sense pleasures as comparable to the sounds of hymns and mantras
objection_hooks: One might argue that the verse merely describes the sensory experience of hearing hymns rather than proposing that worldly actions replace ritual recitations
The passage does not explicitly condemn ritual practice so interpreting it as a critique of ritualism may exceed what the text states

sanskrit: अथ यत्तपो दानमार्जवमहिंसा सत्यवचनमिति ता अस्य दक्षिणाः
transliteration: atha yattapo dānamārjavamahiṃsā satyavacanamiti tā asya dakṣiṇāḥ
translation: Next, austerity, charity, straightforwardness, nonviolence, and truthfulness—these are his dakṣiṇā .
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat tapaḥ, the austerity; dānam, charity; ārjavam, straightforwardness; ahiṃsā, non-violence; satyavacanam, truthfulness; iti tāḥ, all these [good qualities]; asya, of him [the person who regards his life as a sacrifice]; dakṣiṇāḥ, are the special fee paid after the sacrifice.
commentary: These qualities of austerity, charity, etc., are an additional gain, besides what you get by living a life as if it is a sacrifice. These moral qualities and the life you live both contribute much to the growth of your religious life.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse lists austerity, charity, straightforwardness, non‑violence and truthfulness as the dakshina, the special fee after a sacrifice
The commentary states that these moral qualities are an additional gain beyond living life as a sacrifice
According to the commentary, cultivating these qualities contributes significantly to the growth of one’s religious life
objection_hooks: Traditional interpretations treat dakshina as a material offering, so equating it with moral qualities may conflict with established ritual practice
If ethical conduct is merely an ‘additional gain’, one could argue that true sacrificial living already includes these virtues, making the distinction unnecessary
Emphasizing moral dakshina as determinant of destiny might downplay the role of divine knowledge or grace in Advaita thought

sanskrit: तस्मादाहुः सोष्यत्यसोष्टेति पुनरुत्पादनमेवास्य तन्मरणमेवावभृथः
transliteration: tasmādāhuḥ soṣyatyasoṣṭeti punarutpādanamevāsya tanmaraṇamevāvabhṛthaḥ
translation: Therefore people say, ‘He will give birth, ’ or ‘He has given birth.’ In either case, it is a rebirth [in the sense that when he starts living his life as a sacrifice, that is his rebirth]. When death overtakes him, that is the conclusion of the sacrifice.
word_meaning: Tasmāt, therefore; āhuḥ, it is said; soṣyati, will give birth to; asoṣṭa iti, has given birth to; punaḥ utpādanam eva, [because] it is a rebirth; tat maraṇam eva asya, the death of the person; avabhṛthaḥ, is the conclusion of the sacrifice.
commentary: Life is a kind of sacrifice, but when a person dedicates his life as such, he is said to be reborn. The words soṣyati and asoṣṭa are used when a mother is about to give birth, or has given birth, to a child. But they are also used at the time when someone is about to begin performing a sacrifice ( soṣyati ), or has already begun performing it ( asoṣṭa ), because it is his rebirth. When death comes to the person who treats his life as a sacrifice, it is the end of the sacrifice, just as it is the end of his life. Just as aṭ the end of a sacrifice, a person bathes and puts on new clothes, so also, when a person dies, his body is bathed and new clothes are put on it. The treatment is the same, whether the sacrifice is that person’s life or it is the usual sacrificial ritual. And the words used on both occasions are the same. This makes the similarity between the ritualistc sacrifice and life as a sacrifice more meaningful.
claim_candidates: The commentary states that dedicating one’s life as a sacrifice is described as a rebirth, using the same verbs as a mother giving birth
Death is presented as the conclusion of the life‑sacrifice, analogous to the end of a ritual sacrifice
The terms soṣyati and asoṣṭa are applied both to childbirth and to the beginning or completion of a sacrifice, indicating a conceptual equivalence between biological birth and spiritual rebirth
objection_hooks: Equating biological birth with spiritual rebirth may conflate distinct ontological categories, raising doubt about the validity of the linguistic parallel
If death merely ends the sacrifice, this seems to contradict Upanishadic teachings that the Atman persists beyond death, suggesting death is not a final termination
Describing life‑as‑sacrifice as a form of rebirth could be challenged by the view that true liberation requires cessation of the cycle of rebirth, not its continuation through sacrifice

sanskrit: तद्धैतद्घोर् आङ्गिरसः कृष्णाय देवकीपुत्रायोक्त्वोवाचापिपास एव स बभूव सोऽन्तवेलायामेतत्त्रयं प्रतिपद्येताक्षितमस्यच्युतमसि प्राणसंशितमसीति तत्रैते द्वे ऋचौ भवतः
transliteration: taddhaitadghor āṅgirasaḥ kṛṣṇāya devakīputrāyoktvovācāpipāsa eva sa babhūva so'ntavelāyāmetattrayaṃ pratipadyetākṣitamasyacyutamasi prāṇasaṃśitamasīti tatraite dve ṛcau bhavataḥ
translation: The sage Ghora, of the family of Aṅgirasa, taught this truth to Kṛṣṇa, the son of Devakī . As a result, Kṛṣṇa became free from all desires. Then Ghora said: ‘At the time of death a person should repeat these three mantras: “You never decay, you never change, and you are the essence of life.”’ Here are two Ṛk mantras in this connection:
word_meaning: Tat ha etat, this [truth]; ghoraḥ āṅgirasaḥ, the sage Ghora, of the family of Aṅgirasa; kṛṣṇāya devakīputrāya uktvā, having taught to Kṛṣṇa, the son of Devakī; apipāsaḥ, free from desire; eva saḥ babhūva, he [Kṛṣṇa] became; uvāca, [Ghora] said; saḥ, a person; antavelāyām, at the time of death; etat trayam, these three [mantras]; pratipadyeta, should take refuge in; akṣitam asi, you never decay; acyutam asi, you never change; prāṇasaṃśitam asi iti, you are the essence of life; tatra, in this connection; ete dve ṛcau bhavataḥ, there are these two Ṛk mantras.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the true self is immutable and indestructible, as expressed by the mantras you never decay and you never change
The verse identifies the self as the essence of life (prāṇasaṃśitam), implying that the self is the source of vitality for all beings
The instruction to repeat these mantras at the moment of death indicates that realization of the self’s immutable nature is the means to liberation from desire
objection_hooks: One might argue that describing the self as never decays conflicts with empirical observation of bodily death, raising doubt whether the verse refers to a metaphysical self or the physical body
The claim that the self is the essence of life could be challenged by the view that life (prāṇa) is a transient phenomenon dependent on the body, not identical with the immutable self
The prescription to repeat the mantras at death may be seen as ritualistic and insufficient for liberation if desire is already eliminated earlier, questioning the necessity of the timing

sanskrit: आदित्प्रत्नस्य रेतसः ज्योतिः पश्यन्ति वासरम्; परो यदिध्यते दिवि
transliteration: āditpratnasya retasaḥ jyotiḥ paśyanti vāsaram; Paro yadidhyate divi
translation: 7-8. [Those who know Brahman ] see that the light shining in Para-Brahman is the seed of the world. This light is all-pervasive like daylight. It is eternal. It is that great light which is the cause of the world.
word_meaning: Ādit, [has no meaning except that it introduces a new line of thought]; pratnasya, ancient; retasaḥ, seed [of the world]; jyotiḥ, light [manifestation]; paśyanti, they see [eveṛywhere]; vāsaram, daylight [i.e., as all-pervasive]; paraḥ, the best; yat, that which; idhyate, shines; divi, in heaven [i.e., in Para -Brahman], Tamasaḥ, darkness [i.e., ignorance]; pari, beyond [dispels]; uttaram, the best; jyotiḥ, light; paśyantaḥ, having seen; svaḥ, in oneself [i.e., in one’s own heart]; devam, bright; devatrā, among all the gods; sūryam, the sun; vayam ut-aganma, we have attained; jyotiḥ uttamam iti jyotiḥ uttamam iti, the best light, the best light.
commentary: The greatest light is that which dispels the darkness of ignorance. Having seen this light within our own hearts, we have attained that shining god who is the Supreme Light of all. There is an eternal light which is the cause of this universe. This light is the source of the light we see in the luminous Jodies such as the sun, the moon, and the stars. This light is everywhere. Everything in this phenomenal universe moves and works the way it does because of this light. Those who have lived a life of self-discipline, with their minds on the highest thoughts, and have thereby acquired a pure heart, can see this light. They see it within themselves and they also see it outside. They know this light as their own Self and as the Self of all. They know this light as Para Brahman .
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse and commentary identify the pervasive light (jyotiḥ) as Para‑Brahman, making it the ultimate cause of the world

Only those who have cultivated self‑discipline and a pure heart can perceive this universal light both within themselves and externally, indicating that ethical conduct is a prerequisite for knowledge of Brahman
Describing the light as the seed of the world and as eternal implies that Brahman functions as the cosmic principle from which all manifest phenomena arise
objection_hooks: Equating a sensory metaphor of light with the ultimate reality may be insufficient, as Brahman could be beyond any phenomenological attribute
The requirement of self‑discipline to see Brahman can be contested by Upanishadic passages that present knowledge of Brahman as arising from grace or innate insight rather than ethical effort
Calling Brahman a 'seed' suggests a causal role, which seems at odds with the notion of Brahman as nirguna and beyond causation, creating tension about its relationship to creation

sanskrit: मनो ब्रह्मेत्युपासीतेत्यध्यात्ममथाधिदैवतमाकाशो ब्रह्मेत्युभयमादिष्टं भवत्यध्यात्मं चाधिदैवतं च
transliteration: mano brahmetyupāsītetyadhyātmamathādhidaivatamākāśo brahmetyubhayamādiṣṭaṃ bhavatyadhyātmaṃ cādhidaivataṃ ca
translation: The mind is Brahman —this worship is called adhyātma . Next is that called adhidaivata: Space is Brahman. [That is, meditate on space as Brahman.] These two ways of meditation are advised: adhyātma and adhidaivata.
word_meaning: Manaḥ brahma iti upāsīta, meditate on the mind as Brahman; iti adhyātmam, this is so far as the body and mind is concerned; atha adhidaivatam, next is [the meditation] on the elements and the worlds; ākāśaḥ brahma iti, space is Brahman; ubhayam ādiṣṭam bhavati, two ways [of meditation] are advised; adhyātmam ca adhidaivatam ca, adhyātma and adhidaivata.
commentary: Brahman is extremely subtle. Similarly, the mind is also subtle. And it is in the mind that Brahman can be realized. This is why it is comparatively easy to think that the mind is Brahman. In the same way, ākāśa (space) is also close to Brahman. Like Brahman, it is vast, invisible, all-pervasive, and without any attributes whatsoever. It is therefore appropriate to meditate on ākāśa as Brahman.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse advises two distinct meditative practices—adhyātma (mind) and adhidaivata (space)—as legitimate ways to contemplate Brahman
The commentary argues that because Brahman is extremely subtle, the equally subtle mind serves as an appropriate locus for realizing Brahman
By describing ākāśa as vast, invisible, all‑pervasive and attribute‑less, the verse symbolically represents Brahman through space
objection_hooks: Equating the mind with Brahman may lead to a subjective view of reality that conflicts with the claim of Brahman's objective, universal nature
Identifying space, a material element, with the attribute‑less Brahman raises a tension between materiality and transcendence

sanskrit: तदेतच्चतुष्पाद्ब्रह्म वाक्पादः प्राणः पादश्चक्षुः पादः श्रोत्रं पाद इत्यध्यात्ममथाधिदैवतमग्निः पादो वायुः पादा अदित्यः पादो दिशः पाद इत्युभयमेवादिष्टं भवत्यध्यात्मं चैवाधिदैवतं च
transliteration: tadetaccatuṣpādbrahma vākpādaḥ prāṇaḥ pādaścakṣuḥ pādaḥ śrotraṃ pāda ityadhyātmamathādhidaivatamagniḥ pādo vāyuḥ pādā adityaḥ pādo diśaḥ pāda ityubhayamevādiṣṭaṃ bhavatyadhyātmaṃ caivādhidaivataṃ ca
translation: Brahman as the mind has four feet [or, quarters]. The organ of speech is one foot; prāṇa is the next foot; the eyes are the third foot; and the ears are the fourth foot. This is the adhyātma [the physical and mental] aspect of Brahman. Next is the adhidaivata aspect. Fire is one foot; air is another foot; the sun is the next foot; and the quarters are the fourth foot. These two ways of meditation are advised: adhyātma and adhidaivata.
word_meaning: Tat etat catuṣpāt brahma, this Brahman [as the mind] is fourfold; vāk pādaḥ, speech is a foot; prāṇaḥ pādaḥ, prāṇa [the vital force] is a foot; cakṣuḥ pādaḥ, the eyes are a foot; śrotram pādaḥ, the ears are a foot; iti adhyātmam, this is the adhyātma [meditation—i.e., on the body and mind]; atha adhidaivatam, next, the meditation relating to the elements and worlds; agniḥ pādaḥ, fire is a foot; vāyuḥ pādaḥ, air is a foot; ādityaḥ pādaḥ, the sun is a foot; diśaḥ pādaḥ, the quarters are a foot; ubhayam ādiṣṭam bhavati, two ways [of meditation] are advised; adhyātmam ca adhidaivatam ca, adhyātma and adhidaivata.
commentary: The word pāda is not to be taken literally, for Brahman has no feet. Brahman is infinite, but here it is conceived as having four feet—as if it is covering the whole universe with them. Pāda also means a quarter, or a part. First let us imagine our mind and body together as Brahman. This is a meditation on the adhyātma level. Then let us imagine that this Brahman has four feet. What are the four feet? They are the organ of speech, the vital breath, the eyes, and the ears. We can also do this meditation on the adhidaivata level—that is, as the worlds and the elements. First, let us imagine Brahman as ākāśa, space. Brahman’s four feet would then be fire ( agni ), air ( vāyu ), the sun ( āditya ), and the quarters ( diśa ). A cow has four feet to take it where it wants to go. Similarly, a human being is guided to where he wants to go by his organ of speech, his organ of smelling, his eyes, and his ears. Again, just as the four legs and feet of a cow come out of her body, in the same way, fire, air, the sun, and the quarters seem to come out of space
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: Adhyatma and adhidaivata are presented as two complementary meditation methods, each employing four symbolic feet
The adhyatma feet are identified as speech, breath, eyes, and ears, indicating that Brahman is contemplated through the primary sensory and vital functions
The adhidaivata feet are fire, air, the sun, and the quarters, symbolizing Brahman's presence in elemental and spatial dimensions
objection_hooks: Equating Brahman with bodily organs may appear to limit the infinite Brahman to finite functions, challenging the claim of its infinitude
Describing Brahman through elemental symbols could be viewed as anthropomorphic, raising doubts about maintaining Brahman's transcendence

sanskrit: वागेव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः सोऽग्निना ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद
transliteration: vāgeva brahmaṇaścaturthaḥ pādaḥ so'gninā jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda
translation: Vāk is one of the four feet of Brahman [as the mind]. It shines in the light of fire and also radiates heat. He who knows this shines and radiates warmth with his good work, with his fame, and with the radiance he acquires from leading a scholarly and disciplined life.
word_meaning: Vāk eva brahmaṇaḥ caturthaḥ pādaḥ, speech is the fourth foot of [manomaya] Brahman; agninā jyotiṣā, by the light it gets from fire; saḥ bhāti ca tapati ca, it shines and gives heat; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; bhāti ca tapati ca, shines and radiates warmth; kīrtyā, through good work; yaśasā, through fame; brahmavarcasena, through the radiance that comes from a scholarly and disciplined life.
commentary: Animals move where they want by their feet. Similarly, people find their way to Brahman by hearing someone speak about it. This is why the organ of speech is called one of the four feet of Brahman. Fire gives both light and heat. And because fire is the presiding deity of speech, speech also is said to give light and heat. A person who can speak well acquires fame and popularity; It is as if he has done much good work, or has given away much money in charity, or is a great scholar and has lived a disciplined life. Such a person has a shining personality and radiates heat (i.e., energy).
claim_candidates: Speech (Vāk) is presented as a symbolic foot of Brahman that guides seekers toward the divine
Mastery of speech, understood as knowledge of Brahman, bestows inner illumination and outward influence such as fame and scholarly radiance
The fire metaphor shows speech has a dual capacity to enlighten and energize, linking knowledge with ethical activity
objection_hooks: Identifying speech with Brahman may be seen as anthropomorphic, contradicting Brahman's formless nature
Equating worldly fame and material success with spiritual radiance risks conflating ego‑driven reward with true knowledge
Advaita asserts that realization of Brahman is independent of external actions, so linking ethical conduct to becoming a radiant embodiment of Brahman may be philosophically inconsistent

sanskrit: प्राण एव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः स वायुना ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च् भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद
transliteration: prāṇa eva brahmaṇaścaturthaḥ pādaḥ sa vāyunā jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati c bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda
translation: Prāṇa [the organ of smelling] is one of the four feet of Brahman [as the mind]. It shines by the light of vāyu [air] and also radiates heat. He who knows this shines and radiates warmth with his good work, with his fame, and with the radiance he acquires from leading a scholarly and disciplined life.
word_meaning: Prāṇaḥ eva brahmaṇaḥ caturthaḥ pādaḥ, prāṇa [the organ of smelling] is the fourth foot of [manomaya] Brahman; vāyunā jyotiṣā, by the light it gets from air [its presiding deity]; saḥ bhāti ca tapati ca, it shines and gives heat; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; bhāti ca tapati ca, shines and radiates warmth; kīrtyā, through good work; yaśasā, through fame; brahmavarcasena, through the radiance that comes from a scholarly and disciplined life.
commentary: Vāyu, air, is the presiding deity of the organ of smell, because vāyu carries odours and reveals them.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: Prana is described as the fourth foot of Brahman, showing that a bodily function is treated as an integral aspect of Brahman
The light and heat emitted by Prana are said to depend on Vayu, indicating that air is the presiding deity of the organ of smell
Knowing that Prana is a foot of Brahman enables a person to shine and radiate warmth through good work, fame and the radiance of a scholarly and disciplined life
objection_hooks: Equating a physiological process with a foot of Brahman may blur the Advaitic distinction between the empirical self and the absolute reality
Identifying Vayu as the presiding deity of smell might be a poetic metaphor rather than a necessary metaphysical claim
Linking spiritual knowledge of Prana with worldly fame and scholarly achievement could be seen as mixing liberation with ego‑driven success

sanskrit: चक्षुरेव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः स आदित्येन ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद
transliteration: cakṣureva brahmaṇaścaturthaḥ pādaḥ sa ādityena jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda
translation: The organ of vision is one of the four feet of Brahman [as the mind]. It shines by the light of the sun and also radiates heat. He who knows this shines and radiates warmth with his good work, with his fame, and with the radiance he acquires from leading a scholarly and disciplined life.
word_meaning: Cakṣuḥ eva brahmaṇaḥ caturthaḥ pādaḥ, the organ of vision is the fourth foot of [manomaya] Brahman; ādityena jyotiṣā, by the light it gets from the sun [its presiding deity]; saḥ bhāti ca tapati ca, it shines and gives heat; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; bhāti ca tapati ca, shines and radiates warmth; kīrtyā, through good work; yaśasā, through fame; brahmavarcasena, through the radiance that comes from a scholarly and disciplined life.
commentary: The eyes can distinguish colours because of the light they get from the sun. Just as cows can feel their way with their feet, similarly, human beings can recognize things through their eyes. When a person knows how the eyes serve as feet, he acquires fame, glory, and the brightness of Brahman, for he can then make good use of his eyes. He can read and learn the best things possible, things that transform him into a realized soul.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The organ of vision is described as a foot of Brahman, indicating that sensory organs serve as symbolic extensions of the divine principle
Knowing the eye’s role as a foot of Brahman causes a person to shine, gain warmth, fame and spiritual radiance through good work and disciplined study
The commentary’s link between eye colour discrimination and sunlight suggests that external illumination is required for inner spiritual vision
objection_hooks: Equating a sensory organ with a foot of Brahman may reduce the transcendental nature of Brahman to a physiological function, conflicting with non‑dual assertions of Brahman’s pure immanence
The claim that intellectual insight automatically yields fame and social recognition is contradicted by real‑world examples of scholars who remain obscure, questioning the verse’s causal link

Relying on external sunlight for visual discrimination appears to undermine the idea that true spiritual vision is independent of material conditions, creating a tension between inner enlightenment and outer dependence

sanskrit: श्रोत्रमेव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः स दिग्भिर्ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद य एवं वेद
transliteration: śrotrameva brahmaṇaścaturthaḥ pādaḥ sa digbhirjyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda ya evaṃ veda
translation: The organ of hearing is one of the four feet of Brahman [as the mind]. It shines by the light of the quarters and also radiates heat. He who knows this shines and radiates warmth with his good work, with his fame, and with the radiance he acquires from leading a scholarly and disciplined life.
word_meaning: Śrotram eva brahmaṇaḥ caturthaḥ pādaḥ, the organ of hearing is the fourth foot of [manomaya] Brahman; digbhiḥ jyotiṣā, by the light it gets from [its presiding deity, ] the quarters; sāḥ bhāti ca tapati ca, it shines and gives heat; yaḥ evam veda yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus, he who knows thus; bhāti ca tapati ca, shines and radiates warmth; kīrtyā, through good work; yaśasā, through fame; brahmavarcasena, through the radiance that comes from a scholarly and disciplined life.
commentary: The organ of hearing derives its power from the quarters, and through this organ we get knowledge. The results of that knowledge may be direct or indirect. True knowledge, however, is knowledge of Brahman. To emphasize this, ‘he who knows’ is repeated. This emphasis suggests that it is the knowledge of Brahman. The organ of hearing is our ‘foot’ because it is the means by which we can know Brahman. Those who know the importance of the organ of hearing and use it in the right way, acquire name, fame, and become radiant like Brahman. They will also eventually realize Brahman.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse identifies the organ of hearing as the fourth foot of Brahman, indicating it is a means for attaining knowledge of Brahman
The verse states that one who truly knows attains radiance and warmth through good work, fame and scholarly discipline, linking ethical and scholarly conduct with spiritual illumination

The commentary interprets the knowledge obtained through hearing as ultimately knowledge of Brahman, suggesting auditory perception is a primary conduit for realizing the identity of the individual self with Brahman
objection_hooks: Equating a bodily organ with a foot of Brahman may appear to reduce the non‑dual, attributeless nature of Brahman to a physical function, which seems at odds with Advaita’s claim of Brahman’s transcendence

Linking worldly fame and good work with spiritual radiance could be seen as conflating egoic achievement with ultimate knowledge, whereas Advaita traditionally holds that liberation is beyond such external markers

Other Upanishadic passages prioritize the mind or inner contemplation over the sense of hearing as the supreme means to Brahman, challenging the claim that auditory perception is the primary conduit to self‑realization

sanskrit: आदित्यो ब्रह्मेत्यादेशस्तस्योपव्याख्यानमसदेवेदमग्र आसीत् । तत्सदासीत्तत्समभवत्तदाण्डं निरवर्तत तत्संवत्सरस्य मात्रामशयत तन्निरभिद्यत ते आण्डकपाले रजतं च सुवर्णं चाभवताम्
transliteration: ādityo brahmetyādeśastasyopavyākhyānamasadevedamagra āsīt | tatsadāsīttatsamabhavattadāṇḍaṃ niravartata tatsaṃvatsarasya mātrāmaśayata tannirabhidyata te āṇḍakapāle rajataṃ ca suvarṇaṃ cābhavatām
translation: It has been said, ‘ Āditya is Brahman .’ Now this is being explained: This universe was at first non-existent, being without names and forms. [It was not visible, but it existed in a subtle form.] Slowly it manifested itself, as a shoot comes out of a seed. Next it developed into an egg and remained for a whole year like that. It then split in two, one half becoming silver and the other half becoming gold.
word_meaning: Ādityaḥ brahma, Āditya [the sun] is Brahman; iti ādeśaḥ, so it is said; tasya upavyākhyānam, [here is] an explanation of that [statement]; idam, this [universe of name and form]; agre asat eva āsīt, was at first unmanifested [i.e., it was without its names and forms]; [lest the word asat give the impression that the universe was like ‘a flower in the sky, ’ it is said, ] tat, this [universe with its names and forms in a subtle state]; sat āsīt, became manifest; tat, that [subtle universe]; samabhavat, first emerged as a seed; tat āṇḍam niravartata, that developed into an egg; tat, it [i.e., the egg]; saṃvatsarasya mātrām aśayata, lay still for a period of a year; tat, it [the egg]; nirabhidyata, split open; te āṇḍakapāle, those two parts of the egg; rajatam ca suvarṇam ca abhāvatām, turned silver and gold respectively.
commentary: Earlier Āditya, the sun, was described as a foot of Brahman. Now this is being explained. Many people think that the universe was created. But Vedānta says that something cannot be created out of nothing. The universe has always existed, though sometimes it exists like a seed—invisible and without any names and forms. But why is the sun being called Brahman? Because without the sun there is only darkness, and we are then not conscious of this universe with its names and forms. The universe is non-existent then. And in the absence of the universe, there is no way of knowing that Brahman exists, for it is this universe with its names and forms that makes us aware that Brahman is behind everything. Brahman manifests itself as this universe.
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse explicitly identifies Āditya (the sun) with Brahman, presenting the sun as a direct manifestation of the ultimate reality
The commentary asserts that the universe is not created out of nothing but has eternally existed in a subtle, seed‑like state, opposing a creation‑ex‑nihilo view
The seed‑to‑egg‑to‑year‑to‑silver‑and‑gold sequence illustrates the gradual manifestation of Brahman as the manifest universe with names and forms
objection_hooks: Equating Āditya with Brahman may appear to limit Brahman's attributeless, transcendent nature by reducing it to a particular deity
The claim of the universe’s eternal existence conflicts with other Vedic passages that describe a definitive creation event, raising a doctrinal tension

sanskrit: तद्यद्रजतं सेयं पृथिवी यत्सुवर्णं सा द्यौर्यज्जरायु ते पर्वता यदुल्बं समेघो नीहारो या धमनयस्ता नद्यो यद्वास्तेयमुदकं स समुद्रः
transliteration: tadyadrajataṃ seyaṃ pṛthivī yatsuvarṇaṃ sā dyauryajjarāyu te parvatā yadulbaṃ samegho nīhāro yā dhamanayastā nadyo yadvāsteyamudakaṃ sa samudraḥ
translation: Of these two parts of the egg, the one that is silver is this earth, and the one that is gold is heaven. The thick membranes are the mountains. The thin membranes are the clouds and mist. The veins are the rivers, and the fluid in the bladder is the ocean.
word_meaning: Tat yat rajatam, that [half] which is silver; sā iyam pṛthivī, that is this earth; yat suvarṇam, that [half] which is gold; sā dyauḥ, that is heaven; yat jarāyu, that which is the thick membrane; te parvatāḥ, they are the mountains; yat ulbam, that which is the thin membrane; sameghaḥ nīhāraḥ, are the clouds and mist; yāḥ dhamanayaḥ, that which are the veins;
commentary: It has already been stated that at a certain point in time the cosmos takes the shape of an egg, and after remaining in that state for a while, the egg splits in two. The lower part iṣ silver and represents the earth. The upper half is gold and represents heaven. The thick outer membrane around the baby represents the mountains, and the thin inner membrane represents the clouds and mist. The veins of the infant are likened to rivers, and the fluid inside the bladder to the ocean. It is to be noted that these diverse things have all come from one source.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse portrays earth and heaven as silver and gold halves of a single cosmic egg, indicating a fundamental unity between terrestrial and celestial realms

The commentary’s statement that all diverse things have come from one source implies that the Upanishadic perspective identifies a single underlying reality, Brahman, as the origin of all manifested forms

The description of thick and thin membranes as mountains and clouds suggests that natural structures are metaphorically understood as protective layers of the cosmic embryo, reflecting a view that physical phenomena are internal aspects of the cosmic self
objection_hooks: One might argue that describing earth as silver and heaven as gold imposes a hierarchical valuation, which seems to contradict the claim of fundamental unity
The cosmic‑egg metaphor could be read merely as symbolic language, challenging the literal interpretation that all phenomena arise from a single ontological source
Equating natural features with parts of an infant’s body may be seen as anthropocentric, raising doubts about the universality of the ‘one source’ assertion

sanskrit: अथ यत्तदजायत सोऽसावादित्यस्तं जायमानं घोषा उलूलवोऽनूदतिष्ठन्त्सर्वाणि च भूतानि सर्वे च कामास्तस्मात्तस्योदयं प्रति प्रत्यायनं प्रति घोषा उलूलवोऽनूत्तिष्ठन्ति सर्वाणि च भूतानि सर्वे च कामाः
transliteration: atha yattadajāyata so'sāvādityastaṃ jāyamānaṃ ghoṣā ulūlavo'nūdatiṣṭhantsarvāṇi ca bhūtāni sarve ca kāmāstasmāttasyodayaṃ prati pratyāyanaṃ prati ghoṣā ulūlavo'nūttiṣṭhanti sarvāṇi ca bhūtāni sarve ca kāmāḥ
translation: Then that which was born was the sun. Its appearance was greeted by joyous sounds from all beings, and many desirable things appeared. Since then, the sunrise and the sunset are both marked by joyous sounds from all beings, and many enjoyable things also appear at that time.
word_meaning: Atha, next; yat tat ajāyata, that which was born; saḥ asau ādityaḥ, it is that sun; tam jāyamānam anu, after it was born; ulūlavaḥ ghoṣāḥ, the sounds of ‘uh’; sarvāṇi ca bhūtāni ’, from all beings; sarve ca kāmāḥ, and all desirable things; udatiṣṭhan, came forth; tasmāt, therefore; udayam prati, at the rising; pratyāyanam prati, at the setting; tasya, of that [sun]; ulūlavaḥ ghoṣāḥ, the sounds of ‘ulu’; sarvāṇi ca bhūtāni, from all beings; sarve ca kāmāḥ, and all desirable things; anūttiṣṭhanti, appear.
commentary: Here it is said that the sun was born from the egg. When a child is born, there is much jubilation in the house, and also in the neighbourhood. Even today, people in India sometimes make the sound of ‘ulu’ on such occasions. Not only that, many precious gifts are also exchanged.
claim_candidates: The verse depicts the sun’s emergence as a birth-like event that elicits communal jubilation similar to a child’s birth
The recurring sounds ‘ulu’ at sunrise and sunset function as a cultural ritual substitute marking these times
The appearance of many desirable things at sunrise and sunset suggests the sun’s association with the manifestation of material abundance
objection_hooks: The commentary’s reference to an egg is not present in the verse itself, so equating the sun’s birth with human birth may be metaphorical rather than literal
The verse merely records joyous sounds and does not prescribe them as replacements for formal rites, challenging the claim that ‘ulu’ sounds serve as ritual substitutes
The verse mentions desirable things but does not explicitly link them causally to the sun, so asserting that the sun causes material abundance may overstate the text’s claim

sanskrit: स य एतमेवं विद्वानादित्यं ब्रह्मेत्युपास्तेऽभ्याशो ह यदेनं साधवो घोषा आ च गच्छेयुरुप च निम्रेडेरन्निम्रेडेरन्
transliteration: sa ya etamevaṃ vidvānādityaṃ brahmetyupāste'bhyāśo ha yadenaṃ sādhavo ghoṣā ā ca gaccheyurupa ca nimreḍerannimreḍeran
translation: One who knows the sun as Brahman and worships it as such very soon hears sounds pleasing to the ears and also has many good things to enjoy.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ etam evam vidvān, he who knows this as such; ādityam brahma iti upāste, [and] worships the sun as Brahman; abhyāśaḥ, soon; ha, surely; ya, enam āgaccheyuḥ, to him come; sādhavaḥ ghoṣāḥ, pleasant sounds; ca upa-nimreḍeran ca nimreḍeran and also good things to enjoy, good things to enjoy.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: One who knows the sun as Brahman and worships it is promised immediate pleasant sounds and many good things to enjoy
The verse links spiritual knowledge of Brahman with concrete worldly benefits, suggesting that realization of the divine in a manifest form yields material reward
The term abhyāśaḥ indicates that the benefits of such worship are attained very soon, implying that spiritual practice produces prompt results
objection_hooks: Advaita emphasizes that ultimate realization transcends sensory pleasure, so the promised pleasant sounds may be a provisional benefit rather than the true goal
Other Upanishadic teachings warn against seeking material enjoyment, creating tension with the verse’s claim that spiritual knowledge leads to worldly goods
The notion of immediate reward conflicts with the broader doctrine of karma, which often describes results unfolding over multiple lifetimes

sanskrit: जानश्रुतिर्ह पौत्रायणः श्रद्धादेयो बहुदायी बहुपाक्य आस स ह सर्वत आवसथान्मापयांचक्रे सर्वत एव मेऽन्नमत्स्यन्तीति
transliteration: jānaśrutirha pautrāyaṇaḥ śraddhādeyo bahudāyī bahupākya āsa sa ha sarvata āvasathānmāpayāṃcakre sarvata eva me'nnamatsyantīti
translation: In ancient times there was a king who was the great-grandson of Janaśruta. He was a highly charitable person, who gave many gifts in charity, and always with due respect. He also had large quantities of food cooked for people. With the thought in mind, ‘People all over will eat my food, ’ he had many rest-houses built in different places.
word_meaning: Pautrāyaṇaḥ, the grandson; jānaśrutiḥ, the son of Janaśruta; śraddhādeyaḥ, gave away in charity with due respect; bahudāyī, gave many gifts; bahupākyaḥ, cooked large quantities of food for free distribution; āsa ha, it happened like this; saḥ, he [Jānaśruti]; sarvataḥ, from all quarters; me annam, my food; atsyanti, [people] will eat; iti, this [i.e., with this thought in mind]; sarvataḥ eva, in all directions; āvasathān, rest-houses; māpayāñcakre, built.
commentary: There was once a king named Jānaśruti, who was the great-grandson of Janaśruta. He was a highly charitable person. He not only made large donations, but whatever he gave he gave with love and respect. Every day large quantities of food were cooked and he fed many people. He also had rest-houses built all over the country. He wanted people to come, stay in those rest-houses, and enjoy his food.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse presents charitable deeds as a form of duty that yields communal benefit and reflects the belief that ethical conduct shapes societal welfare
The king’s intention to feed all people shows that performing duties with respect and love is regarded as a sacred sacrifice
Providing food and building rest‑houses is portrayed as an essential aspect of spiritual practice, indicating that outward service complements inner asceticism
objection_hooks: The king may be motivated by desire for fame rather than pure selflessness, undermining the claim of altruistic duty
Material generosity alone may not constitute true spiritual liberation, questioning the identification of charity with genuine tapas

sanskrit: अथ हंसा निशायामतिपेतुस्तद्धैवं हं सोहं समभ्युवाद हो होऽयि भल्लाक्ष भल्लाक्ष जानश्रुतेः पौत्रायणस्य समं दिवा ज्योतिराततं तन्मा प्रसाङ्क्षी स्तत्त्वा मा प्रधाक्षीरिति
transliteration: atha haṃsā niśāyāmatipetustaddhaivaṃ haṃ sohaṃ samabhyuvāda ho ho'yi bhallākṣa bhallākṣa jānaśruteḥ pautrāyaṇasya samaṃ divā jyotirātataṃ tanmā prasāṅkṣī stattvā mā pradhākṣīriti
translation: Once he saw some swans flying overhead at night. The swan flying behind called out to the one ahead: ‘Hey, you short-sided one! Don’t you see that the brightness of Jānaśruti has spread all over the sky like daylight? Beware you don’t touch it. See that it doesn’t bum you’.
word_meaning: Atha ha, once; haṃsāḥ, swans [i.e., some sages who had taken the form of swans]; niśāyām, at night; atipetuḥ, were flying; tat, then; haṃsaḥ, one of the swans; evam ha abhyuvāda, loudly said; haṃsam, to the other swan; ho ho ayi bhallākṣa bhallākṣa, hey, you short-sided one [don’t you see?]; jānaśruteḥ pautrāyaṇasya jyotiḥ, the light [emanating] from Jānaśruti; divā, in the sky; samam ātatam, has spread like daylight; tat, that [light]; mā prasāṅkṣīḥ, don’t touch; tat tvā mā pradhākṣī iti, don’t let it burn you.
commentary: One evening the king was resting on the roof of his palace and he noticed a couple of swans flying above him in the sky. These swans were actually sages or gods in disguise. Just then the swan flying behind joked with the one ahead about the king, within the king’s hearing.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The swan sages represent enlightened beings who perceive the pervasive illumination of Jānaśruti, symbolizing the universal Self that pervades all existence
The warning 'don’t touch it' suggests that direct grasping of the divine light leads to harm, implying that realization of Brahman must be approached with reverence rather than forceful acquisition
The nocturnal setting shows that the divine illumination is visible even in darkness, indicating that Brahman is present irrespective of external conditions
objection_hooks: One might argue that the admonition against touching the light conflicts with Advaita’s claim that the Self is identical with Brahman and should be directly realized
The identification of swans with the Universal Self may overextend the commentary, which only notes they are sages or gods in disguise

sanskrit: यथा कृतायविजितायाधरेयाः संयन्त्येवमेनं सर्वं तदभिसमैति यत्किंच प्रजाः साधु कुर्वन्ति यस्तद्वेद यत्स वेद स मयैतदुक्त इति
transliteration: yathā kṛtāyavijitāyādhareyāḥ saṃyantyevamenaṃ sarvaṃ tadabhisamaiti yatkiṃca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti yastadveda yatsa veda sa mayaitadukta iti
translation: ‘Just as in a game of dice, when a person wins the toss called kṛta he automatically wins the lower tosses also, in the same way, whatever good work people do goes to the credit of Raikva. If anyone knows what Raikva knows, he becomes like Raikva. This is how I would describe Raikva.’
word_meaning: Yathā, just as; kṛtāya-vijitāya, if a person wins the toss of dice called kṛta; adhareyāḥ, all lower tosses [i.e., tretā, three; dvāpara, two; and kali, one]; samyanti, are included [i.e., are also won]; evam, in the same way; yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti, whatever good work people do; enam sarvam tat abhisamaiti, all that comes under that one [i.e., it
commentary: Kṛta is the highest number possible in a toss of dice, so it is said to include all the lower numbers within it. The swan says that Raikva is a person like kṛta, because he includes within himself all the good things that other people do. He is the sum total of all that is good in the world. If anyone has the knowledge that Raikva has, then he becomes the same as Raikva.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the merit of all good actions performed by individuals is ultimately credited to Raikva, implying a collective attribution of virtue
Knowledge of Raikva's understanding confers identity with Raikva, suggesting that epistemic realization transforms the individual self into the same as the exemplar self

The analogy of the highest dice number kṛta encompassing lower numbers illustrates that a supreme self subsumes all lesser virtues, indicating a hierarchical ontological structure where the supreme self contains all goodness
objection_hooks: If merit is credited to Raikva, this seems to undermine the doctrine of individual karmic responsibility, raising a tension between collective credit and personal accountability

Equating knowledge of Raikva with becoming Raikva may be challenged because epistemic similarity does not necessarily entail ontological identity, questioning whether knowledge alone can effect such transformation

The hierarchical analogy that a supreme self absorbs all lower virtues could be critiqued for collapsing the empirical self into the universal, which may conflict with the Advaita view that realization preserves the distinction between appearance and ultimate reality until liberation

sanskrit: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायण उपशुश्राव स ह संजिहान एव क्षत्तारमुवाचाङ्गारे ह सयुग्वानमिव रैक्वमात्थेति यो नु कथं सयुग्वा रैक्व इति
transliteration: tadu ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇa upaśuśrāva sa ha saṃjihāna eva kṣattāramuvācāṅgāre ha sayugvānamiva raikvamāttheti yo nu kathaṃ sayugvā raikva iti
translation: 5-6. Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa overheard what the swan said. He got up from his bed and the first thing he did was to ask his attendant [who was standing nearby]: ‘O my child, can I be compared to Raikva with the cart? Who is this Raikva with the cart? What sort of person is he?’ [Then he quoted what the swan had said: ] ‘Just as in a game of dice, if a person wins the toss called kṛta, he automatically wins the lower tosses also, in the same way, whatever good work people do goes to the credit of Raikva. If anyone knows what Raikva knows, he becomes like Raikva. This is how I would describe Raikva.’
word_meaning: U tat, that [what the swan said]; jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇa upaśuśrāva, the great-grandson of Janaśruta overheard; saḥ ha sañjihānaḥ, he got up from his bed; eva kṣattāram uvāca, and immediately said to his charioteer; aṅga are, O my child; sayugvānam iva raikvam āttha ha iti, do you compare me with Raikva with the cart; yaḥ sayugvā raikva iti, who is this Raikva with the cart; nu katham, what sort of person is he?; Yathā, just as; kṛtāya-vijitāya, if a person wins the toss of dice called kṛta; adhareyāḥ, all lower tosses [i.e., tretā, three; dvāpara, two; and kali, one]; samyanti, are included [i.e., are also won]; evam, in the same way; yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti, whatever good work people do; enam sarvam tat abhisamaiti, all that comes under that one [i.e., it goes to the credit of Raikva]; yaḥ tat veda, he who knows that; yat saḥ veda, whatever he [Raikva] knows; saḥ mayā etat uktaḥ iti, this is said of him by me.
commentary: Jānaśruti was disturbed by what the swans had said about him. Maybe he could not be compared with Raikva, but in what way did Raikva excel over him? Who was this Raikva with a cart? Jānaśruti had a sleepless night. The next morning when he got up, the first thing he did was to question his attendant about this Raikva. He wanted to somehow find out in what respect Raikva was superior to him.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse states that all good deeds performed by people are credited to Raikva, suggesting a doctrine of collective merit attached to a sage
The text says that whoever knows what Raikva knows becomes like Raikva, indicating that knowledge of Brahman confers identity with the sage
The dice metaphor implies that attaining the highest insight (kṛta) automatically secures all lower achievements, portraying spiritual progress as cumulative
objection_hooks: The metaphor of dice may refer to personal karmic success rather than a transfer of others' merit to Raikva, challenging the collective merit interpretation
The phrase ‘becomes like Raikva’ could be understood metaphorically as resembling his qualities, not literal ontological identity, weakening the claim of identity through knowledge
The comparison to a cart in the dice game might illustrate certainty of outcome once a principle is realized, not necessarily a cumulative accrual of merit, questioning the cumulative progress claim

sanskrit: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायण उपशुश्राव स ह संजिहान एव क्षत्तारमुवाचाङ्गारे ह सयुग्वानमिव रैक्वमात्थेति यो नु कथं सयुग्वा रैक्व इति
transliteration: tadu ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇa upaśuśrāva sa ha saṃjihāna eva kṣattāramuvācāṅgāre ha sayugvānamiva raikvamāttheti yo nu kathaṃ sayugvā raikva iti
translation: 5-6. Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa overheard what the swan said. He got up from his bed and the first thing he did was to ask his attendant [who was standing nearby]: ‘O my child, can I be compared to Raikva with the cart? Who is this Raikva with the cart? What sort of person is he?’ [Then he quoted what the swan had said: ] ‘Just as in a game of dice, if a person wins the toss called kṛta, he automatically wins the lower tosses also, in the same way, whatever good work people do goes to the credit of Raikva. If anyone knows what Raikva knows, he becomes like Raikva. This is how I would describe Raikva.’
word_meaning: U tat, that [what the swan said]; jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇa upaśuśrāva, the great-grandson of Janaśruta overheard; saḥ ha sañjihānaḥ, he got up from his bed; eva kṣattāram uvāca, and immediately said to his charioteer; aṅga are, O my child; sayugvānam iva raikvam āttha ha iti, do you compare me with Raikva with the cart; yaḥ sayugvā raikva iti, who is this Raikva with the cart; nu katham, what sort of person is he?; Yathā, just as; kṛtāya-vijitāya, if a person wins the toss of dice called kṛta; adhareyāḥ, all lower tosses [i.e., tretā, three; dvāpara, two; and kali, one]; samyanti, are included [i.e., are also won]; evam, in the same way; yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti, whatever good work people do; enam sarvam tat abhisamaiti, all that comes under that one [i.e., it goes to the credit of Raikva]; yaḥ tat veda, he who knows that; yat saḥ veda, whatever he [Raikva] knows; saḥ mayā etat uktaḥ iti, this is said of him by me.
commentary: Jānaśruti was disturbed by what the swans had said about him. Maybe he could not be compared with Raikva, but in what way did Raikva excel over him? Who was this Raikva with a cart? Jānaśruti had a sleepless night. The next morning when he got up, the first thing he did was to question his attendant about this Raikva. He wanted to somehow find out in what respect Raikva was superior to him.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that all good deeds performed by people are credited to Raikva, implying a collective accumulation of merit under a single sage
Knowing what Raikva knows transforms a person into being like Raikva, suggesting that knowledge of a realized being confers its qualities onto the knower
The analogy of dice throws indicates that a single successful action called kṛta guarantees success in all subordinate actions, reflecting a principle of karmic amplification
objection_hooks: Attributing all good deeds to Raikva may undermine individual agency, creating tension between personal responsibility and collective credit

The claim that knowledge of Raikva makes one like him can be challenged as conflating intellectual understanding with ontological transformation, questioning whether mere knowledge suffices for becoming like a sage
The dice analogy oversimplifies karma, because in reality not every minor action follows automatically from a major one, making the claim of automatic success philosophically problematic

sanskrit: स ह क्षत्तान्विष्य नाविदमिति प्रत्येयाय तं होवाच यत्रारे ब्राह्मणस्यान्वेषणा तदेनमर्च्छेति
transliteration: sa ha kṣattānviṣya nāvidamiti pratyeyāya taṃ hovāca yatrāre brāhmaṇasyānveṣaṇā tadenamarccheti
translation: [Jānaśruti asked his attendant to go and look for Raikva.] Having looked for him, the attendant thought, ‘I can’t find him, ’ and returned to his master. Jānaśruti then said to him: ‘Well, why don’t you go to places where brāhmins are to be found—in forests and solitary places? Look for him there’.
word_meaning: Saḥ ha kṣattā, that attendant; anviṣya, having looked [for Raikva]; na avidam iti, thought ‘I have not found him’; pratyeyāya, came back; tam ha uvāca, [then Jānaśruti] said to him; are yatra brāhmaṇasya anveṣaṇā, well, wherever brāhmins are to be found [i.e., in
commentary: Jānaśruti’s attendant looked for Raikva in many places, but obviously they were unlikely places. Raikva could be found only in a quiet place, such as a forest or by the side of a river. It would be there that such a person would prefer to stay. So Jānaśruti asked the attendant to search in those places.
claim_candidates: The verse indicates that true spiritual teachers are located in natural, solitary settings rather than in ordinary or ritual spaces
The instruction to search in forests reflects a valuation of ascetic environments as more conducive to discovering wisdom than conventional ritual locations
The attendant’s initial failure demonstrates that superficial searching in common places is insufficient for locating true knowledge
objection_hooks: One could contend that the passage offers only practical guidance for finding a particular sage, not a universal claim about the superiority of ascetic settings
The focus on forests may be culturally specific, and other traditions locate sages in urban or temple contexts, challenging the notion that solitude is necessary for wisdom
The attendant’s failure might be attributed to his own lack of insight rather than the location, questioning the claim that environment alone determines access to knowledge

sanskrit: सोऽधस्ताच्छकटस्य पामानं कषमाणमुपोपविवेश तं हाभ्युवाद त्वं नु भगवः सयुग्वा रैक्व इत्यहं ह्यरा३ इति ह प्रतिजज्ञे स ह क्षत्ताविदमिति प्रत्येयाय
transliteration: so'dhastācchakaṭasya pāmānaṃ kaṣamāṇamupopaviveśa taṃ hābhyuvāda tvaṃ nu bhagavaḥ sayugvā raikva ityahaṃ hyarā3 iti ha pratijajñe sa ha kṣattāvidamiti pratyeyāya
translation: The attendant noticed a man sitting under a cart, scratching a rash on his body. Sitting down close to him, the attendant asked, ‘Sir, are you Raikva with the cart?’ The man answered, ‘Well, yes, I am.’ The attendant thought, ‘I have found him, ’ and returned.
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [the attendant]; śakaṭasya adhastāt, [noticed a man sitting] under a cart; pāmānam, with a rash on his skin; kaṣamāṇam, scratching; upa upaviveśa, he sat close to him; tam ha abhyuvāda, he said to him; tvam nu bhagavaḥ sayugvā raikva iti, Sir, are you Raikva with the cart; aham hi arā iti, well, yes, I am the same; pratijajñe, he admitted [rather contemptuously]; saḥ ha kṣattā, that attendant; avidam iti, thought ‘I have found him’; pratyeyāya, came back.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The attendant's recognition of Raikva shows that the true identity of a sage can be discerned through attentive observation of external signs
Raikva's modest affirmation of his identity reflects the Advaitic principle that the Atman is acknowledged without egoistic denial
The attendant's return after finding Raikva symbolizes the seeker’s return to the source after recognizing the Self
objection_hooks: The passage merely records a mundane encounter and does not explicitly address the Self or Brahman, so the philosophical reading may overreach the text
The attendant's identification relies on superficial details like a rash and a cart, which may not constitute genuine knowledge of the Self
Raikva's reply could be a conventional polite answer rather than a philosophical statement of identity with Brahman

sanskrit: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायणः षट्शतानि गवां निष्कमश्वतरीरथं तदादाय प्रतिचक्रमे तं हाभ्युवाद
transliteration: tadu ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇaḥ ṣaṭśatāni gavāṃ niṣkamaśvatarīrathaṃ tadādāya praticakrame taṃ hābhyuvāda
translation: Then Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa went to that place, taking with him six hundred cows, a gold necklace, and a chariot drawn by mules. He said to him [Raikva]:
word_meaning: Tat u ha, next; jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇaḥ, the great-grandson of Janaśruta; ṣaṭ śatāni gavām, six hundred cows; niṣkam, a gold necklace; aśvatarīratham, a chariot drawn by mules; tat ādāya, that with him; praticakrame, went [there]; tam ha abhyuvāda, he said to him.
commentary: Hearing the report from his attendant, and thinking that Raikva might be poor and in need of money, Jānaśruti went with some gifts to offer Raikva, so that Raikva would teach him.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
