sanskrit: स ह व्याधिनानशितुं दध्रे तमाचार्यजायोवाच ब्रह्मचारिन्नशान किं नु नाश्नासीति स होवाच बहव इमेऽस्मिन्पुरुषे कामा नानात्यया व्याधीभिः प्रतिपूर्णोऽस्मि नाशिष्यामीति
transliteration: sa ha vyādhinānaśituṃ dadhre tamācāryajāyovāca brahmacārinnaśāna kiṃ nu nāśnāsīti sa hovāca bahava ime'sminpuruṣe kāmā nānātyayā vyādhībhiḥ pratipūrṇo'smi nāśiṣyāmīti
translation: Upakosala was upset and started fasting. His teacher’s wife said to him: ‘ Brahmacārin, eat something. Why are you not eating?’ Upakosala said: ‘There are too many desires in me and they are pulling me in different directions. I am like one suffering from many ailments. I don’t want to eat’.
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [Upakosala]; ha vyādhinā, due to mental affliction; anaśitum dadhre, started fasting; tam ācārya - jāyā uvāca, the teacher’s wife said to him; brahmacārin aśāna, brahmacārin, start eatine; kim nu na āśnāsi iti, why are you not eating; saḥ ha uvāca, he [Upakosala] said; bahavaḥ ime asmin puruṣe kāmāḥ there are many desires in this person; nānātyayāḥ, they are pulling me in different directions; vyādhibhiḥ pratipūrṇaḥ asmi, I am full of ailments; na aśiṣyāmi iti, I will not eat anything.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: Upakosala equates his multitude of desires to many ailments, indicating that unregulated desires are conceptualized as mental illnesses in the Upanishadic worldview

The teacher’s wife urges Upakosala to eat, implying that bodily sustenance is considered a duty even for a brahmacharin, suggesting that ascetic fasting is not obligatory when mental distress is present

Upakosala’s refusal to eat demonstrates an act of self‑imposed austerity (tapas) motivated by desire‑induced suffering, reflecting a view that ascetic practices can be a response to internal afflictions
objection_hooks: The metaphor of desires as ailments may simply express personal anguish rather than a systematic classification of desire as mental illness
The wife’s urging may reflect social concern rather than a doctrinal claim that a brahmacharin must eat, and the text does not prescribe a rule
Fasting motivated by despair may not qualify as true tapas, which traditionally requires disciplined intent toward spiritual insight

sanskrit: अथ हाग्नयः समूदिरे तप्तो ब्रह्मचारी कुशलं नः पर्यचारीद्धन्तास्मै प्रब्रवामेति तस्मै होचुः प्राणो ब्रह्म कं ब्रह्म खं ब्रह्मेति
transliteration: atha hāgnayaḥ samūdire tapto brahmacārī kuśalaṃ naḥ paryacārīddhantāsmai prabravāmeti tasmai hocuḥ prāṇo brahma kaṃ brahma khaṃ brahmeti
translation: Then the fires—the Dakṣiṇāgni, the Gārhapatya, and the Āhavanīya —began to say to each other: ‘This brahmacārin has become thin from practising austerities. He has so long looked after us with great care. Let us teach him.’ They said to Upakosala, ‘ Prāṇa is Brahman, ka [happiness] is Brahman, and kha [space] is Brahman.’
word_meaning: Atha ha agnayaḥ samūdire, then the fires [the Dakṣiṇāgni, the Gārhapatya, and the Āhavanīya] began to say; taptaḥ brahmacārī, the austere brahmacārin; kuśalam, with great care; naḥ paryacārīt, looked after us; hanta asmai prabravāma iti, so we will teach him; tasmai ha ucuḥ, they said to him; prāṇaḥ brahma, prāṇa is Brahman; kam brahma, happiness is Brahman; kham brahma iti, ākāśa [space] is Brahman.
commentary: The three fires that had been so well taken care of by Upakosala were moved to see his condition. They also felt that a great injustice had been done to him by his teacher, Satyakāma. They then decided to take it upon themselves to teach him.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Brahman as Life, Joy, and the Void
claim_candidates: The verse states that prāṇa is Brahman, kā (happiness) is Brahman, and kha (space) is Brahman, showing Brahman identified with life, joy and the void
The three sacred fires decide to teach Upakosala, indicating that ritual symbols become sources of direct knowledge of Brahman
The brahmacārin’s austerities and careful service of the fires earn him their instruction, suggesting that ethical conduct leads to divine teaching
objection_hooks: Identifying prāṇa, happiness and space as Brahman may blur distinct aspects of reality, raising doubt about a single undifferentiated Brahman
The portrayal of the fires as teachers could be interpreted as poetic rather than a doctrinal claim that symbols replace ritual practice
The commentary stresses the injustice by the teacher more than the merit of austerities, weakening the link between conduct and receiving instruction

sanskrit: स होवाच विजानाम्यहं यत्प्राणो ब्रह्म कं च तु खं च न विजानामीति ते होचुर्यद्वाव कं तदेव खं यदेव खं तदेव कमिति प्राणं च हास्मै तदाकाशं चोचुः
transliteration: sa hovāca vijānāmyahaṃ yatprāṇo brahma kaṃ ca tu khaṃ ca na vijānāmīti te hocuryadvāva kaṃ tadeva khaṃ yadeva khaṃ tadeva kamiti prāṇaṃ ca hāsmai tadākāśaṃ cocuḥ
translation: Upakosala said: ‘I know that prāṇa is Brahman . But that ka and kha are Brahman I do not know.’ The fires replied, ‘That which is ka is also kha, and that which is kha is also ka .’ Then the fires taught him that Brahman was both prāṇa and ākāśa [space].
word_meaning: Saḥ ha uvāca, he [Upakosala] said; vijānāmi aham yat prāṇa brahma, I know that prāṇa is Brahman; kam ca tu kham ca na vijānāmi iti, but I don’t know that ‘ka’ and ‘kha’ are Brahman; te ha ucaḥ, they [the fires] said; yat vāva kam tat eva kham, that which is ‘ka’ is also ‘kha’; yat eva kham tat eva kam iti, that which is ‘kha’ is also ‘ka’; prāṇam ca ha asmai tat ākāśam ca ucaḥ, then they taught him about prāṇa and ākāśa [space].
commentary: Upakosala had no difficulty accepting that prāṇa was Brahman, for prāṇa is the vital breath and Without the vital breath, life is impossible. In view of its importance it may be conceded that prāṇa is Brahman. But how can ka and kha be Brahman? Upakosala thought to himself: ‘ Ka is happiness, but what kind of happiness? It is happiness born of sense experience. It is therefore transitory. It cannot be the same as Brahman. Similarly, kha is also transitory, for it means ākāśa, space. Ākāśa is material and therefore transitory.’ The fires then said that ka and kha are used as both nouns and adjectives, and sometimes they qualify each other. Kha as an adjective may qualify ka when ka stands for Brahman. What does ka mean here? Here it means Brahman without any attributes. Similarly, when we say kha, we mean ākāśa. When we say ka is kha, we mean ‘pleasure’ is ākāśa. But this is not the material ākāśa. Here it means the ākāśa, space, inside the heart. The idea is that Brahman and joy ( ka ) are both in the space inside the heart ( khā ).
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Brahman as Life, Joy, and the Void
claim_candidates: Upakosala readily accepts prāṇa as Brahman because breath is essential for life, showing Brahman can be identified with vital functions
The fires teach that ka (joy) and kha (inner space) are both Brahman, implying Brahman includes both pleasure and the subtle inner void
The commentary’s reading of ka as attribute‑less Brahman and kha as the inner space indicates Brahman is represented symbolically as both pure reality and inner space
objection_hooks: Equating the transient experience of pleasure with the immutable Brahman may conflict with the Upanishadic claim that Brahman is beyond change, suggesting the identification is metaphorical not literal
Identifying inner space with Brahman is problematic because the commentary also calls material ākāśa transitory, raising doubt about how an impermanent space can be the eternal Brahman

sanskrit: अथ हैनं गार्हपत्योऽनुशशास पृथिव्यग्निरन्नमादित्य इति य एष आदित्ये पुरुषो दृश्यते सोऽहमस्मि स एवाहमस्मीति
transliteration: atha hainaṃ gārhapatyo'nuśaśāsa pṛthivyagnirannamāditya iti ya eṣa āditye puruṣo dṛśyate so'hamasmi sa evāhamasmīti
translation: Then the Gārhapatya fire said to him [Upakosala]: ‘The earth, fire, food, and the sun—these are all part of my [i.e., part of Brahman ’s] body. The person seen in the solar orb is me. I am that’.
word_meaning: Atha ha enam gārhapatyaḥ anuśaśāsa, next the Gārhapatya fire gave him this instruction; pṛthivī agniḥ annam ādityaḥ iti, the earth, fire, food, and the sun [are all part of me—i.e., of Brahman]; yaḥ eṣaḥ āditye puruṣaḥ dṛśyate, the person seen in the sun; saḥ aham asmi saḥ eva aham asmi iti, I am he, I am he.
commentary: First the fires taught Upakosala together. Now they are teaching him separately. The Gārhapatya fire begins. He said: ‘The earth, fire, food, and the sun—these constitute my body. I divide myself into these things. There in the solar region is a person. That is me. I am known as the Gārhapatya fire and I am also this person in the solar region. Earth and food are both objects of enjoyment and consumption, but fire and the sun are not in this category. They are similar to each other, however, just as food and the earth are similar to each other, being objects that people can enjoy.’
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The Gārhapatya fire declares that earth, fire, food, and the sun are parts of Brahman's body, indicating that the material cosmos is identified with Brahman
The verse equates the person visible in the solar orb with the fire speaker, asserting that the individual self is identical to Brahman manifested as the sun

The commentary’s distinction between objects of enjoyment (earth, food) and non‑enjoyable (fire, sun) yet treating them as similar suggests a nuanced view of Brahman's manifestation beyond mere sensory pleasure
objection_hooks: If Brahman is the source of bliss, classifying fire and the sun as non‑enjoyable objects seems inconsistent with their identification as Brahman
The identification of the person seen in the sun with the fire speaker may be metaphorical, raising doubt about a literal ontological identity between the individual self and Brahman

sanskrit: स य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्तेऽपहते पापकृत्यां लोकी भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति नास्यावरपुरुषाः क्षीयन्त उप वयं तं भुञ्जामोऽस्मिंश्च लोकेऽमुष्मिंश्च य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्ते
transliteration: sa ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste'pahate pāpakṛtyāṃ lokī bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati nāsyāvarapuruṣāḥ kṣīyanta upa vayaṃ taṃ bhuñjāmo'smiṃśca loke'muṣmiṃśca ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste
translation: ‘He who knows this Gārhapatya fire and worships it thus has all his sins removed, and he attains the world of the Gārhapatya fire. He lives a long and bright life, and his descendants do not perish. In this world and the next, we look after that person who knows this fire and worships it thus’.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, he who; etam evam vidvān, knowing this in this way; upāste, [and] worships; apahate pāpa -kṛtyām, he destroys his sins; lokī bhavati, becomes a dweller in the world of the god of fire; sarvam āyuḥ eti, lives the full range, of his life; jyok jīvati, he lives a bright life; asya āvarapuruṣāḥ, his descendants; na kṣīyante, do not perish [i.e., his line is never broken]; asmin ca loke amuṣmin ca, in this world and also in the other world; vayam tam upabhuñjāmaḥ, we will look after him; yaḥ etam evam vidvān upāste, he who knows this [fire] thus and worships it.
commentary: Here the benefit of worshipping the Gārhapatya fire in the right manner is being given. The first benefit is that all the worshipper’s sins are burned away. He also attains the world reserved for the worshippers of this fire. When the fire says that the worshipper’s descendants do not perish, he means that his line will not be broken.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Worship of the Gārhapatya fire with proper knowledge eradicates all the worshipper’s sins
Proper fire worship ensures that the worshipper’s descendants never perish, preserving his lineage
The benefits of fire worship extend to the next world, where the worshipper attains the realm of the Gārhapatya fire and is looked after there
objection_hooks: One may argue that external fire rites cannot alone remove sin without inner moral transformation, questioning the claim of total sin removal
The promise that descendants never perish could be seen as conditional on broader ethical conduct, not solely on fire worship, challenging the unconditional guarantee
The description of attaining a fire‑world in the afterlife may be metaphorical rather than literal, casting doubt on the claim of a concrete post‑mortem realm

sanskrit: अथ हैनमन्वाहार्यपचनोऽनुशशासापो दिशो नक्षत्राणि चन्द्रमा इति य एष चन्द्रमसि पुरुषो दृश्यते सोऽहमस्मि स एवाहमस्मीति
transliteration: atha hainamanvāhāryapacano'nuśaśāsāpo diśo nakṣatrāṇi candramā iti ya eṣa candramasi puruṣo dṛśyate so'hamasmi sa evāhamasmīti
translation: Next the Dakṣiṇāgni [Southern] fire said to Upakosala: ‘Water, the quarters, the stars, and the moon—these are all part of my [i.e., part of Brahman ’s] body. The person seen in the moon is me. I am that’.
word_meaning: Atha ha enam anvāhārya - pacanaḥ anuśaśāsa, next the Anvāhārya Pacana [the Dakṣiṇāgni, or Southern] fire gave him this instruction; āpaḥ diśaḥ nakṣatrāṇi candramā iti, water, the quarters, the stars, and the moon [are all part of me—i.e., of Brahman]; yaḥ eṣaḥ candramasi puruṣaḥ dṛśyate, the person seen in the moon; saḥ aham asmi saḥ eva aham asmi iti, I am he, I am he.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse states that water, the quarters, the stars and the moon are parts of Brahman’s body, implying that the material cosmos is not separate from Brahman
The speaker’s declaration I am that the person seen in the moon identifies the individual self with Brahman
The Dakṣiṇāgni fire is presented as a mouthpiece of Brahman, showing that even elemental forces express the same ultimate reality
objection_hooks: The description of external phenomena as parts of Brahman may be metaphorical, allowing a dualistic reading that the world remains distinct from Brahman
The identification of the moon’s person with the speaker could be understood as a poetic reflection of consciousness rather than a literal ontological identity
If Dakṣiṇāgni is a distinct fire speaking, it suggests a plurality of entities, which seems at odds with the claim of a sole reality

sanskrit: स य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्तेऽपहते पापकृत्यां लोकी भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति नास्यावरपुरुषाः क्षीयन्त उप वयं तं भुञ्जामोऽस्मिंश्च लोकेऽमुष्मिंश्च य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्ते
transliteration: sa ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste'pahate pāpakṛtyāṃ lokī bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati nāsyāvarapuruṣāḥ kṣīyanta upa vayaṃ taṃ bhuñjāmo'smiṃśca loke'muṣmiṃśca ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste
translation: ‘He who knows this Dakṣiṇāgni fire and worships it thus has all his sins removed, and he attains the world of the Dakṣiṇāgni fire. He lives a long and bright life, and his descendants do not perish. In this world and the next, we look after that person who knows this fire and worships it thus’.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, he who; etam evam vidvān, knowing this in this way; upāste, [and] worships; apahate pāpakṛtyām, he destroys his sins; lokī bhavati, becomes a dweller in the world of the god of fire; sarvam āyuḥ eti, lives the full range of his life; jyok jīvati, he lives a bright life; asya āvarapuruṣāḥ, his descendants; na kṣīyante, do not perish [i.e., his line is never broken]; asmin ca loke amuṣmin ca, in this world and also in the other world; vayam tam upabhuñjāmaḥ, we will look after him; yaḥ etam evam vidvān upāste, he who knows this [fire] thus and worships it.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: Worship of Dakṣiṇāgni fire by a knowledgeable person removes all his sins
Such worship guarantees a long, bright life and the perpetuation of one’s lineage in both this world and the next
The community is obligated to support and look after those who correctly know and worship this fire, both in this life and after death
objection_hooks: The Upanishadic emphasis on knowledge of Brahman may render fire worship insufficient for true sin‑removal
Promising worldly longevity and undying descendants may conflict with the Advaita view that ultimate reality transcends birth and death
Relying on communal support could contradict the Advaita principle that liberation is achieved through individual self‑realisation rather than external aid

sanskrit: अथ हैनमाहवनीयोऽनुशशास प्राण आकाशो द्यौर्विद्युदिति य एष विद्युति पुरुषो दृश्यते सोऽहमस्मि स एवाहमस्मीति
transliteration: atha hainamāhavanīyo'nuśaśāsa prāṇa ākāśo dyaurvidyuditi ya eṣa vidyuti puruṣo dṛśyate so'hamasmi sa evāhamasmīti
translation: Next the Āhavanīya fire said to Upakosala: ‘ Prāṇa, space, heaven, and lightning—these are all part of my [i.e., part of Brahman ’s] body. The person seen in lightning is me. I am that’.
word_meaning: Atha ha enam āhavanīyaḥ ānuśaśāsa, next the Āhavanīya fire gave him this instruction; prāṇaḥ ākāśaḥ dyauḥ vidyut iti, prāṇa, space, heaven, and lightning [are all part of me—i.e., of Brahman]; yaḥ eṣaḥ vidyuti puruṣaḥ dṛśyate, the person seen in lightning; saḥ aham asmi saḥ eva aham asmi iti, I am he, I am he.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The Āhavanīya fire declares that prāṇa, ākāśa, dyauḥ and vidyut are parts of Brahman's body, showing that Brahman is expressed through elemental forces
The formula saḥ aham asmi in the verse asserts the identity of the person seen in lightning with Brahman, supporting the Advaita claim that the individual self is identical with the infinite Brahman
By describing the person visible in lightning as Brahman, the verse suggests that sensory phenomena can directly reveal the presence of the universal Self
objection_hooks: Identifying Brahman with external elements may imply that Brahman is a composite of parts, which conflicts with the doctrine of Brahman as indivisible and sole reality

The assertion of self‑identical with Brahman can be contested by the lived experience of distinct individuality and multiplicity, raising the question whether the identification is literal or merely metaphorical

sanskrit: स य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्तेऽपहते पापकृत्यां लोकी भवति सर्वमयुरेति ज्योग्जीवति नास्यावरपुरुषाः क्षीयन्त उप वयं तं भुञ्जामोऽस्मिंश्च लोकेऽमुष्मिंश्च य एतमेवं विद्वानुपास्ते
transliteration: sa ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste'pahate pāpakṛtyāṃ lokī bhavati sarvamayureti jyogjīvati nāsyāvarapuruṣāḥ kṣīyanta upa vayaṃ taṃ bhuñjāmo'smiṃśca loke'muṣmiṃśca ya etamevaṃ vidvānupāste
translation: ‘He who knows this Āhavanīya fire and worships it thus has all his sins removed, and he attains the world of the Āhavanīya fire. He lives a long and bright life, and his descendants do not perish. In this world and the next, we look after that person who knows this fire and worships it thus’.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, he who; etam evam vidvān, knowing this in this way; upāste, [and] worships; apahate pāpa - kṛtyām, he destroys his sins; lokī bhavati, becomes a dweller in the world of the god of fire; sārvam āyuḥ eti, lives the full range of his life; jyok jīvati, he lives a bright life; asya āvarapuruṣāḥ, his descendants; na kṣīyante, do not perish [i.e., his line is never broken]; asmin ca loke amuṣmin ca, in this world and also in the other world; vayam tam upabhuñjāmaḥ, we will look after him; yaḥ etam evam vidvān upāste, he who knows this [fire] thus and worships it.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: He who knows the Āhavanīya fire and worships it removes all his sins
Such worship ensures a long, bright life and the uninterrupted continuation of his descendants
The community promises to look after and support the person who correctly performs this fire worship in both this world and the next
objection_hooks: The claim that fire worship alone eradicates sin may conflict with the Upanishadic view that inner knowledge and ethical conduct are essential for true purification

Promising worldly benefits such as longevity and lineage seems at odds with the Upanishadic teaching that the ultimate goal is the dissolution of the individual self into the universal Brahman, rendering material rewards philosophically irrelevant

sanskrit: ते होचुरुपकोसलैषा सोम्य तेऽस्मद्विद्यात्मविद्या चाचार्यस्तु ते गतिं वक्तेत्याजगाम हास्याचार्यस्तमाचार्योऽभ्युवादोपकोसल३ इति
transliteration: te hocurupakosalaiṣā somya te'smadvidyātmavidyā cācāryastu te gatiṃ vaktetyājagāma hāsyācāryastamācāryo'bhyuvādopakosala3 iti
translation: The fires said: ‘O Somya Upakosala, we have just told you the knowledge of fire. That is also Self-knowledge. Your teacher will tell you about the way to the next world.’ In due course, his teacher returned, and he called him, saying, ‘Upakosala’.
word_meaning: Te ha ucuḥ, they [the fires] said; upakosala somya, O Somya Upakosala; eṣā te asmat vidyā, this knowledge about us [we have given] to you; ca ātma -vidyā, that is also Self-knowledge; ācāryaḥ tu te gatim vakta iti, but the teacher will tell you about the way [to the next world]; asya ācāryaḥ, his teacher; ājagāma ha, returned; ācāryaḥ abhyuvāda tam upakosala iti, the teacher called him, ‘O Upakosala.’
commentary: What the fire meant was that Upakosala’s teacher would give him the final lesson. He would tell Upakosala what he should do to get the full benefit of what he had learned from the fires.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse equates the knowledge of fire with Self‑knowledge, implying that understanding an external element can serve as a path to realizing the Self
The teacher is portrayed as the source of instruction about the way to the next world, indicating that ultimate liberation requires guidance from a qualified guru after preliminary teachings
The dialogue shows a progressive pedagogy where elemental instruction precedes higher spiritual guidance, reflecting a staged approach to knowledge in Advaita
objection_hooks: Equating knowledge of fire with Self‑knowledge may conflate empirical learning with transcendental realization, which could be seen as inconsistent with Advaita’s emphasis on direct, non‑dual insight

Advaita holds that the Self is self‑evident and does not depend on external instruction, so the claim that a teacher is necessary for the final lesson appears to conflict with the doctrine of self‑realization
The staged learning model suggested by the verse might be challenged by the view that ultimate knowledge is immediate and not dependent on sequential teachings

sanskrit: भगव इति ह प्रतिशुश्राव ब्रह्मविद इव सोम्य ते मुखं भाति को नु त्वानुशशासेति को नु मानुशिष्याद्भो इतीहापेव निह्नुत इमे नूनमीदृशा अन्यादृशा इतीहाग्नीनभ्यूदे किं नु सोम्य किल तेऽवोचन्निति
transliteration: bhagava iti ha pratiśuśrāva brahmavida iva somya te mukhaṃ bhāti ko nu tvānuśaśāseti ko nu mānuśiṣyādbho itīhāpeva nihnuta ime nūnamīdṛśā anyādṛśā itīhāgnīnabhyūde kiṃ nu somya kila te'vocanniti
translation: [Upakosala] replied, ‘Yes, lord.’ [His teacher said: ] ‘O Somya, your face is shining like that of a knower of Brahman . Who has taught you?’ ‘Sir, who will teach me?’ He said this as if he was trying to hide the truth. Then, pointing to the fires, he said: ‘Earlier they looked, different. Now they look like this.’ In this way, he indicated the fires. [The teacher asked, ] ‘Somya, what did the fires teach you?’.
word_meaning: Bhagavaḥ iti ha pratiśuśrāva, ‘Yes, lord, ’ he replied; brahmavidaḥ iva somya te mukham bhāti, your face is shining like that of a knower of Brahman; kaḥ nu tvā anuśaśāsa iti, who has taught you; kaḥ nu mā anuśiṣyāt bho iti, who will teach me, sir; iha iva apanihnute, [he said this] as if he was trying to hide [the truth]; ime, these [pointing to the fires]; nūnam īdṛśāḥ, look like this [as if they are frightened]; anyādṛśāḥ iti, looked different before; iha agnīn abhyūde, in this way he spoke about the fires; kim nu somya kila te avocan iti, Somya, what did they teach you?
commentary: Upakosala knew the limitations of the fires, and that is why he did not want to tell his teacher what they had taught him, or that they had taught him anything at all. Further, he noticed that in the presence of his teacher the fires appeared to be frozen with fear. This is why he was vague and evasive in his reply.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: Upakosala’s face is described as shining like that of a knower of Brahman, suggesting that outward appearance is presented as a sign of inner realization
Upakosala deliberately hides what the fires taught him, indicating that the experiential insight from the fires is considered too profound or risky to disclose
The teacher’s question about who taught Upakosala implies that ultimate knowledge of Brahman is self‑realized rather than transmitted by another
objection_hooks: The description of a shining face may be poetic metaphor rather than an objective indicator of knowledge, so the claim that appearance proves realization is questionable
Upakosala’s evasiveness could stem from humility or fear of misinterpretation rather than the intrinsic danger of the knowledge itself
Traditional Advaita emphasizes the role of the guru in transmitting knowledge, challenging the claim that Brahman knowledge is solely self‑realized without external instruction

sanskrit: य एषोऽक्षिणि पुरुषो दृश्यत एष आत्मेति होवाचैतदमृतमभयमेतद्ब्रह्मेति तद्यद्यप्यस्मिन्सर्पिर्वोदकं वा सिञ्चति वर्त्मनी एव गच्छति
transliteration: ya eṣo'kṣiṇi puruṣo dṛśyata eṣa ātmeti hovācaitadamṛtamabhayametadbrahmeti tadyadyapyasminsarpirvodakaṃ vā siñcati vartmanī eva gacchati
translation: The teacher said: ‘The person seen in the eyes is the Self. It is immortal and fearless. It is Brahman . This is why, if anyone puts clarified butter or water in the eyes, it goes to the corners of the eyes’.
word_meaning: Yaḥ eṣaḥ akṣiṇi puruṣaḥ dṛśyate, this person who is seen in the eyes; eṣaḥ ātmā, this is the Self; iti ha uvāca, he [the teacher] said; etat amṛtam abhayam, this is immortal and fearless; etat brahma iti, this is Brahman; tat, this is why; asmin, into this [eye]; yadi api sarpiḥ vā udakam vā siñcati, if anyone puts clarified butter or water; vartmanī eva gacchati, it goes into the comers [of the eye].
commentary: How do you know the Self? You have to have full self-control. You have to have a gentle nature, and you must withdraw your mind completely from the external world. Then you can see the Self in the eyes. That Self is immortal, fearless, and it is Brahman (literally, ‘the biggest’). As water does not stick to a lotus leaf, so also, if water or something else is put in the eyes, it does not hurt them because the eyelids protect them. This is why the eyelids are called ‘lotuses.’ The eyes are the seat of the Self. What the Upaniṣad is saying is that if the seat of the Self is pure and not susceptible to any impurity, the Self is naturally the same.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the Self (Atman) is identical to Brahman, being immortal and fearless
The verse uses the eyes as a metaphorical locus for perceiving the Self, implying that self‑realization requires withdrawal of the mind and self‑control
The verse suggests that the purity of the eye as the seat of the Self renders it impervious to external substances, illustrating the Self's transcendence over physical impurity
objection_hooks: One might argue that equating the individual Self with Brahman overlooks passages that maintain a functional distinction between personal experience and the universal
The claim that the eyes are the seat of the Self could be challenged as metaphorical rather than literal, and the physical observation about liquids may not substantiate a metaphysical identity

The assertion that the Self is fearless because it is impervious to external substances may be contested, since fearlessness is a moral or psychological quality not demonstrated by physical resistance

sanskrit: एतं संयद्वाम इत्याचक्षत एतं हि सर्वाणि वामान्यभिसंयन्ति सर्वाण्येनं वामान्यभिसंयन्ति य एवं वेद
transliteration: etaṃ saṃyadvāma ityācakṣata etaṃ hi sarvāṇi vāmānyabhisaṃyanti sarvāṇyenaṃ vāmānyabhisaṃyanti ya evaṃ veda
translation: They call him Saṃyadvāma, for everything that is good and beautiful comes to him. One who knows this has everything that is good and beautiful come to him.
word_meaning: Etam saṃyadvāmaḥ iti ācakṣate, they call him Saṃyadvāma [i.e., one in whom everything that is good is concentrated]; hi, because; etam sarvāṇi vāmāni abhisaṃyanti, all good and beautiful things come to him; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; sarvāṇi enam vāmāni abhisaṃyanti, all good and beautiful things come to him.
commentary: The word vāma means ‘attractive, ’ ‘beautiful, ’ or ‘desirable.’ The Self is Saṃyadvāma because all good things become concentrated in it. And one who knows the Self also becomes the receptacle of all good things.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse states that the Self is Saṃyadvāma, meaning that all good and beautiful qualities are concentrated in the Self
Knowing the Self ensures that all good and beautiful things are drawn to the knower
The term vāma is used to link moral goodness and attractiveness directly to the nature of the Self
objection_hooks: The claim that knowledge of the Self guarantees that all good things come to the knower may conflate inner realization with external acquisition, which the verse does not explicitly differentiate
Identifying the Self as the attractor of all good could be interpreted as referring to inner virtues rather than material objects, making the claim about external attraction ambiguous
The description of the Self as Saṃyadvāma may be metaphorical, so asserting a literal guarantee of material good for the knower may exceed the verse's intended meaning

sanskrit: एष उ एव वामनीरेष हि सर्वाणि वामानि नयति सर्वाणि वामानि नयति य एवं वेद
transliteration: eṣa u eva vāmanīreṣa hi sarvāṇi vāmāni nayati sarvāṇi vāmāni nayati ya evaṃ veda
translation: This person in the eyes is Vāmanī, the source of all that is good and pure, for he inspires in people all that is good and pure. One who knows this grants all that is good and pure to others.
word_meaning: Eṣaḥ u eva vāmanīḥ, and this [person in the eyes] is Vāmanī [the source of all that is good and pure]; hi, for; eṣaḥ, this [person in the eyes]; eva, surely; sarvāṇi vāmāni nayati, grants all that is good and pure; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; sarvāṇi vāmāni nayati, grants all that is good and pure.
commentary: The word vāmanī means one who carries the fruits of good work to all beings according to what they deserve. He is the support of all that is good. One who knows this conveys the fruits of good work to others.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: Vāmanī is the agent who distributes the fruits of good deeds to all beings according to what they deserve
One who knows Vāmanī’s role is able to grant all that is good and pure to others
The verse suggests that moral conduct determines the allocation of good, indicating a karmic ethical system
objection_hooks: The text does not define the criteria for what beings deserve, leaving the fairness of Vāmanī’s distribution ambiguous
If granting goodness depends on personal knowledge, this may conflict with the claim that distribution is determined solely by individual conduct
The description of Vāmanī as the source of all good could be read as divine grace independent of merit, challenging the karmic interpretation

sanskrit: एष उ एव भामनीरेष हि सर्वेषु लोकेषु भाति सर्वेषु लोकेषु भाति य एवं वेद
transliteration: eṣa u eva bhāmanīreṣa hi sarveṣu lokeṣu bhāti sarveṣu lokeṣu bhāti ya evaṃ veda
translation: The person in the eyes is Bhāmanī, shining, for he shines in all the worlds [including the sun]. One who knows this shines in all the worlds.
word_meaning: Eṣaḥ u eva bhāmanīḥ, and this [person in the eyes] is Bhāmanī [shining]; eṣaḥ hi sarveṣu lokeṣu bhāti, for he shines in all the worlds [including the sun]; yaḥ evam veda, he who knows thus; sarveṣu lokeṣu bhāti, shines in all the worlds.
commentary: This person in the eyes is the source of all light. The Kaṭha Upaniṣad [II.2.15] says, ‘All this shines because he shines.’
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse identifies the inner self (the person in the eyes) as Bhāmanī, the source of all light, implying that the individual self is identical with the universal luminous principle
Knowing that the inner self is Bhāmanī results in one shining in all worlds, suggesting that self‑knowledge confers universal illumination
The shining of all worlds is presented as a direct manifestation of the inner self’s radiance, indicating that external phenomena are expressions of the inner self
objection_hooks: The phrase ‘shining in all worlds’ may be metaphorical rather than ontological, so the verse does not necessarily prove identity between the individual self and Brahman
External illumination such as the sun’s light occurs regardless of personal knowledge, challenging the claim that self‑knowledge causes universal illumination
Other Upanishadic texts describe Brahman as transcendent and distinct from the individual self, which could contradict the identification of the inner self as the source of all light

sanskrit: अथ यदु चैवास्मिञ्छव्यं कुर्वन्ति यदि च नार्चिषमेवाभिसंभवन्त्यर्चिषोऽहरह्न आपूर्यमाणपक्षमापूर्यमाणपक्षाद्यान्षडुदङ्ङेति मासांस्तान्मासेभ्यः संवत्सरं संवत्सरादादित्यमादित्याच्चन्द्रमसं चन्द्रमसो विद्युतं तत् पुरुषोऽमानवः स एनान्ब्रह्म गमयत्येष देवपथो ब्रह्मपथ एतेन प्रतिपद्यमाना इमं मानवमावर्तं नावर्तन्ते नावर्तन्ते
transliteration: atha yadu caivāsmiñchavyaṃ kurvanti yadi ca nārciṣamevābhisaṃbhavantyarciṣo'harahna āpūryamāṇapakṣamāpūryamāṇapakṣādyānṣaḍudaṅṅeti māsāṃstānmāsebhyaḥ saṃvatsaraṃ saṃvatsarādādityamādityāccandramasaṃ candramaso vidyutaṃ tat puruṣo'mānavaḥ sa enānbrahma gamayatyeṣa devapatho brahmapatha etena pratipadyamānā imaṃ mānavamāvartaṃ nāvartante nāvartante
translation: Then, for those who know this, whether proper funeral rites are performed or not, they go after death to the world of light. From the world of light they go to the world of day; from the world of day to the world of the bright fortnight; from the world of the bright fortnight to the six months when the sun moves northward; from there they go to the year; from the year to the sun; from the sun to the moon; and from the moon to lightning. There someone, not human, receives them and leads them to brahmaloka . This is the way of the gods. This is also the way to Brahman . Those who go by this path never return to this mortal world. They never return.
word_meaning: Atha, then; yat, if; u ca eva asmin, in this situation [i.e., for those who know the Self in the eyes]; śavyam, funeral rites; kurvanti, they perform; yadi ca na, or if not; arciṣam, to light; eva abhisambhavanti, they go; arciṣaḥ, from light; ahaḥ, to day; ahnaḥ, from day; āpūryamāṇapakṣam, to the bright fortnight; āpūryamāṇapakṣāt, from the bright fortnight; ṣaṭ māsān, to the six months; yān udan eti, [when the sun] moves to the north; tān, to that; māsebhyaḥ, from those months; saṃvatsaram, to the year; saṃvatsarāt, from the year; ādityam, to the sun; ādityāt, from the sun; candramasam, to the moon; candramasaḥ, from the moon; vidyutam, to lightning; tat, there; puruṣaḥ, a person [existing there]; amānavaḥ, not human; enān, those [human beings]; saḥ gamayati, he leads; brahma, to brahmaloka; eṣaḥ devapathaḥ, this is the path of the gods; brahmapathaḥ, the way to Brahman; etena, by this [path]; pratipadyamānāḥ, those who go; imam mānavam āvartam, to this world of human beings; na āvartante, do not return; na āvartante, do not return.
commentary: Normally when a person dies, his funeral rites should be performed with great care. This, however, does not apply in the case of one who has known Brahman. If the funeral rites are performed for him, it is good. But if for one reason or another they are not, it As regards those who worship the Self in the eyes by attributing to it qualities such as saṃyadvāma, vāmanī, or bhāmanī, or those who worship the fires as earth, water, prāṇa, etc., they go after death to the deity of light. From there they go to the deities of day, the bright fortnight, the six months of the northern solstice, and so on. Finally they reach a point where someone appears who is higher than a human being. He leads them to satyaloka, which is the realm of Brahmā . This is not, however, Para Brahman (Pure Consciousness). To attain liberation, one must realize one’s identity with Para Brahman, where there is no going or coming from one realm to another.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: According to the verse, knowledge of Brahman obviates the necessity of proper funeral rites for the realized soul

The post‑death journey described proceeds through successive cosmic realms (light, day, bright fortnight, six months, year, sun, moon, lightning) before reaching a non‑human guide who leads the soul to Brahmaloka
Reaching Brahmaloka is not equivalent to attaining Para Brahman, because liberation requires realization of identity with Para Brahman where there is no further transition
objection_hooks: The claim that funeral rites are unnecessary for the realized may be challenged by the traditional Vedic emphasis on rites as essential for all, suggesting a tension between ritual orthodoxy and knowledge‑based exemption

The sequential cosmic itinerary could be interpreted metaphorically rather than literally, raising doubt about whether the verse asserts an actual metaphysical journey or a symbolic representation of stages of consciousness

The distinction between Brahmaloka and Para Brahman might be questioned: if Brahmaloka is a realm of the gods, why is it not considered the ultimate reality, and does the verse imply a dualistic hierarchy contrary to Advaita's non‑dualism

sanskrit: एष ह वै यज्ञो योऽयं पवते एष ह यन्निदं सर्वं पुनाति यदेष यन्निदं सर्वं पुनाति तस्मादेष एव यज्ञस्तस्य मनश्च वाक्च वर्तनी
transliteration: eṣa ha vai yajño yo'yaṃ pavate eṣa ha yannidaṃ sarvaṃ punāti yadeṣa yannidaṃ sarvaṃ punāti tasmādeṣa eva yajñastasya manaśca vākca vartanī
translation: He who blows [i.e., air] is the sacrifice. While moving, he purifies all this. Since he purifies all this while moving, he is the sacrifice. The mind and speech are both his paths.
word_meaning: Eṣaḥ ha vai yajñaḥ, he is a sacrifice; yaḥ ayam pavate, this one who blows [i.e., air]; eṣaḥ ha, he; yan, while moving; idam sarvam, all this; punāti, purifies; yat, since; eṣaḥ yan idam sarvam punāti, he purifies all this while moving; tasmāt, therefore; eṣaḥ eva yajñaḥ, he is the sacrifice; manaḥ ca vāk ca, mind and speech; tasya vartanī, are his paths.
commentary: Anything that moves can clean or purify things, and this process of cleaning or purifying is a kind of sacrifice. Air is called here a sacrifice because it moves and by moving it purifies. There are two ways by which the air purifies—by speech and by the mind. By speech we utter mantras, and by the mind we understand them. Speech and the mind both operate by the power derived from air.
claim_candidates: The verse equates moving air with the sacrificial act, indicating that natural processes can function as yajña
Speech and mind are described as the paths of air, implying that verbal and mental activities are extensions of the same purifying principle embodied by air
Purification of all things is achieved through motion, suggesting that dynamic activity, rather than static ritual, is the essential element of sacrifice
objection_hooks: Equating air with sacrifice may diminish the role of intentional Vedic offerings, challenging the traditional view that yajña requires a conscious practitioner
Identifying mind and speech as paths of air could be seen as metaphorical, raising doubts about a literal dependence of mental activity on physical air movement
The claim that motion alone purifies is questionable because not all motion leads to purification; some movements cause harm, indicating that ethical qualification may be required beyond mere motion

sanskrit: तयोरन्यतरां मनसा संस्करोति ब्रह्मा वाचा होताध्वर्युरुद्गातान्यतरांस यत्रौपाकृते प्रातरनुवाके पुरा परिधानीयाया ब्रह्मा व्यवदति
transliteration: tayoranyatarāṃ manasā saṃskaroti brahmā vācā hotādhvaryurudgātānyatarāṃsa yatraupākṛte prātaranuvāke purā paridhānīyāyā brahmā vyavadati
translation: 2-3. The priest called brahmā in a sacrifice purifies one of these two paths [i.e., the path of the mind] by his [discriminating] mind. The hotā, the adhvaryu, and the udgātā priests purify the other [i.e., the path of speech] by [chaste and elegant] speech. If, however, the brahmā priest breaks his silence when the morning anuvāka has begun, before the paridhānīya Ṛk hymn has been read, then only one path [the path of speech] has been purified. The other is ruined. Just as a one-legged person trying to walk, or a one-wheeled chariot trying to move, is doomed, in the same way the sacrifice is ruined. And when the sacrifice is ruined, the sacrificer is also ruined. In fact, the sacrificer is even liable for having committed a sin by performing the sacrifice in that way.
word_meaning: Brahmā, the priest called brahmā [in a sacrifice]; tayoḥ anyatarām, one of these two; manasā, mentally; saṃskaroti, purifies; vācā, by speech; hotā adhvaryuḥ udgātā anyatarām, the hotā, the adhvaryu, and the udgātā priests [purify] the other; yatra, when; prātaḥ anuvāke upākṛte, the anuvāka which is read in the morning has begun; purā paridhānīyāyāḥ, before the Ṛk hymn called paridhānīya; saḥ, he [the brahmā priest]; vyavavadati, breaks his silence; anyatarām eva vartanīm saṃskaroti, only one path [i.e., the path of speech] he purifies; anyatarā, the other [the path of the mind]; hīyate, is spoiled; yathā, just as; ekapāt, a person with one leg; vrajan, walking; vā, or; rathaḥ ekena cakreṇa vartamānaḥ, a chariot moving on one wheel; riṣyati, is doomed; evam, likewise; asya yajñaḥ riṣyati, his sacrifice is ruined; yajñam riṣyantam, when the sacrifice is ruined; yajamānaḥ anu-riṣyati, the sacrificer is also ruined; saḥ, he [the sacrificed; iṣṭvā, having performed the sacrifice in this way; pāpīyān bhavati, becomes a sinner.
commentary: In the previous verse, two paths were mentioned for the performance of a sacrifice—the path of speech ( vāk ) and the path of the mind ( manas ). In a sacrifice, there are four types of priests: brahmā, hotā, adhvaryu, and udgātā. The brahmā priest is supposed to purify the path of the mind by his own purified mind, while observing silence. The other three priests take care of the path of speech, purifying it by their pure words. But suppose the brahmā priest breaks his silence while the reading of the morning anuvāka is going on, before the paridhānīya Ṛk hymn has begun. He is supposed to remain silent then, with his mind on a high level. Through this he is to purify the path of the mind. But if he breaks his silence, his mind is no longer ‘pure.’ Then the sacrificer has to make do with only one path—the path of speech. His position is now like that of a one-legged man trying to walk, or like a one-wheeled chariot trying to move. Both the sacrificer and the sacrifice are doomed, and the sacrificer is to be regarded as having committed a sin.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The brahmā priest must remain silent during the morning anuvāka; if he breaks silence his mind is no longer pure and the mental path of the sacrifice is ruined
Purifying only the speech path is insufficient; a valid sacrifice requires both the mind and speech paths to be purified, otherwise the sacrifice and the sacrificer are doomed
The ruin of the sacrifice caused by the brahmā’s broken silence entails that the sacrificer becomes a sinner, linking ritual conduct directly to moral culpability
objection_hooks: Advaita Vedanta stresses the ultimate identity of Atman and Brahman beyond ritual, so emphasizing procedural silence may conflict with the non‑dual view that liberation is independent of external rites

The text may use the ruin of the sacrifice as a metaphor for spiritual imbalance rather than a literal sin, questioning whether breaking silence truly incurs sin or merely signals an incomplete practice

Some Vedic traditions allow certain sacrifices to be performed with only the speech priests, suggesting that the claim of absolute necessity of both paths might be context‑dependent rather than universal

concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The brahmā priest must remain silent during the morning anuvāka to purify the mind path; if he speaks, the mental path is spoiled and the sacrifice is invalid
Both the mind and speech paths must be purified for a successful sacrifice; reliance on only the speech path leads to ruin of the sacrifice and the sacrificer
The sacrificer is deemed a sinner when the brahmā priest breaks silence, indicating that the sacrificer bears moral responsibility for the priest’s ritual error
objection_hooks: One could contend that the priest’s silence is a formal rule while the true efficacy of the sacrifice rests on the sacrificer’s inner intention, weakening the claim that the priest’s speech alone spoils the ritual
The comparison to a one‑legged person may be metaphorical, suggesting that the sacrifice is merely imperfect rather than absolutely ruined, challenging the claim of inevitable ruin
Attributing sin to the sacrificer for the priest’s mistake raises a tension between individual accountability and collective ritual duty, questioning the claim that the sacrificer is morally culpable

sanskrit: अथ यत्रोपाकृते प्रातरनुवाके न पुरा परिधानीयाया ब्रह्मा व्यवदत्युभे एव वर्तनी संस्कुर्वन्ति न हीयतेऽन्यतरा
transliteration: atha yatropākṛte prātaranuvāke na purā paridhānīyāyā brahmā vyavadatyubhe eva vartanī saṃskurvanti na hīyate'nyatarā
translation: But in the case of the sacrifice in which the reading of the morning anuvāka has already begun, and the brahmā priest does not break his silence before the paridhānīya has started, then both paths are purified. Neither of them becomes destroyed.
word_meaning: Atha yatra, in the case of that sacrifice; upākṛte prātaḥ anuvāke, in which the reading of the morning anuvāka has begun; purā paridhānīyāyāḥ, before the beginning of the paridhānīya Ṛk hymn; brahmā na vyavavadati, the brahmā priest does not speak; ubhe eva vartanī, both the paths; saṃskurvanti, they purify; na hīyate anyatarā, neither of them is destroyed.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: In a sacrifice where the morning anuvāka has already begun, the brahmā priest’s silence before the paridhānīya ensures that both ritual paths are purified
When the brahmā priest maintains silence, neither of the two paths is destroyed, indicating that proper conduct preserves the sacrifice’s efficacy
The timing of the brahmā priest’s speech relative to the start of the paridhānīya determines the outcome, showing that ritual sequence is crucial
objection_hooks: One could argue that the priest’s silence is not the decisive factor, and the purification may stem from the inherent power of the anuvāka itself
The phrase ‘neither of them becomes destroyed’ might be read as merely denying destruction, not guaranteeing full success, challenging the claim of complete preservation
Emphasizing ritual timing may overlook the importance of inner intention, suggesting that sequence alone may not determine the sacrificial result

sanskrit: स यथोभयपाद्व्रजन्रथो वोभाभ्यां चक्राभ्यां वर्तमानः प्रतितिष्ठत्येवमस्य यज्ञः प्रतितिष्ठति यज्ञं प्रतितिष्ठन्तं यजमानोऽनुप्रतितिष्ठति स इष्ट्वा श्रेयान्भवति
transliteration: sa yathobhayapādvrajanratho vobhābhyāṃ cakrābhyāṃ vartamānaḥ pratitiṣṭhatyevamasya yajñaḥ pratitiṣṭhati yajñaṃ pratitiṣṭhantaṃ yajamāno'nupratitiṣṭhati sa iṣṭvā śreyānbhavati
translation: Just as a person with two legs can walk, or a chariot with two wheels can move, and attain the goal, so also his sacrifice succeeds. And if the sacrifice succeeds, the sacrificer also succeeds. He attains much greatness through his sacrifice.
word_meaning: Yathā, as; ubhayapāt, a person with both legs; vrajam, walking; vā, or; rathaḥ ubhābhyām cakrābhyām, a chariot with both wheels; vartamānaḥ, moving; pratitiṣṭhati, succeeds; evam, so also; asya yajñaḥ pratitiṣṭhati, his [the sacrificer’s] sacrifice succeeds; yajñam pratitiṣṭhantam yajamānaḥ anupratitiṣṭhati, if the sacrifice succeeds, the sacrificer also succeeds; saḥ, he [the sacrificer]; iṣṭvā śreyān bhavati, becomes greater through his sacrifice.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse equates the success of a sacrifice to the functional completeness of a two‑legged person or a two‑wheeled chariot, implying that proper structure guarantees efficacy
It asserts a direct causal link: when the sacrifice succeeds, the sacrificer likewise succeeds and becomes greater
The passage presents sacrifice as a means to attain the goal, parallel to walking or chariot movement, thereby positioning ritual action as a path to personal advancement
objection_hooks: The analogy may conflate mechanical reliability with spiritual efficacy, ignoring that ritual success might depend on intention, purity, or divine grace rather than mere structural completeness
The claim of a direct causal link between sacrifice and personal success can be challenged by doctrines that attribute outcomes to broader karmic law, not solely to the ritual’s result

By privileging sacrifice as the primary means to greatness, the verse could be seen as marginalizing alternative spiritual paths such as knowledge or meditation, raising questions about its exclusivity

sanskrit: प्रजापतिर्लोकानभ्यतपत्तेषां तप्यमानानां रसान्प्रावृहदग्निं पृथिव्या वायुमन्तरिक्षातादित्यं दिवः
transliteration: prajāpatirlokānabhyatapatteṣāṃ tapyamānānāṃ rasānprāvṛhadagniṃ pṛthivyā vāyumantarikṣātādityaṃ divaḥ
translation: Prajāpati worshipped the worlds, and from those which he worshipped he was able to extract their essence. From earth he took fire, from the interspace he took air, and from heaven he took the sun.
word_meaning: Prajāpatiḥ lokān abhyatapat, Prajāpati worshipped the worlds; teṣām tapyamānānām, from those which were worshipped; rasān prāvṛhat, he extracted the essence; agnim pṛthivyāḥ, fire from the earth; vāyum antarikṣāt, air from the space between the earth and heaven; ādityam divaḥ, and the sun from heaven.
commentary: The brahmā priest must observe silence to maintain the sanctity of the sacrifice he is performing. If the sanctity is lost, then the purpose of the sacrifice will be defeated. So if, for some reason or other, the sanctity is lost, due amends have to be made. One way to make amends is to seek the blessings of the lokas, the worlds. You pray to the worlds and then you are able to get their essence to support Your worship of the worlds is the amends you make, and the extracts you get in return are the blessings that protect you.
claim_candidates: The verse states that Prajapati worshipped the worlds and extracted fire from earth, air from the interspace, and the sun from heaven as essences
The commentary explains that when a sacrifice loses sanctity, one must make amends by praying to the worlds and receiving their extracted essences as blessings
The description suggests that elemental essences are obtainable through proper worship, implying a functional relationship between ritual worship and material benefits
objection_hooks: The verse may be describing a cosmic act of Prajapati rather than prescribing a human ritual, so applying it to priestly conduct could be unwarranted
The text mentions extraction of essences but does not explicitly label them as blessings, leaving room to dispute the claim that they are inherently beneficent

sanskrit: स एतास्तिस्रो देवता अभ्यतपत्तासां तप्यमानानां रसान्प्रावृहदग्नेरृचो वायोर्यजूंषि सामान्यादित्यात्
transliteration: sa etāstisro devatā abhyatapattāsāṃ tapyamānānāṃ rasānprāvṛhadagnerṛco vāyoryajūṃṣi sāmānyādityāt
translation: Then he worshipped these three deities. From those which he worshipped he extracted the essence. He got the Ṛk mantras from fire, the Yajuḥ mantras from air, and the Sāma mantras from the sun.
word_meaning: Saḥ etāḥ tisraḥ devatāḥ abhyatapat, [then] he worshipped these three deities [fire, air, and the sun]; tāsām tapyamānānām, from those which he worshipped; rasān prāvṛhat, he extracted the essence; agneḥ ṛcaḥ, the Ṛk mantras from fire; vāyoḥ yajūṃṣi, the Yajuḥ mantras from air; sāmāni ādityāt, the Sāma mantras from the sun.
claim_candidates: The verse presents fire, air, and the sun as deities whose essences yield the three Vedic collections (Ṛk, Yajuḥ, Sāma), indicating a symbolic link between natural elements and Vedic knowledge
The act of extracting mantras from these deities is portrayed as a form of tapas, suggesting that ritual worship can be a means of internalizing sacred knowledge rather than merely external sacrifice
The verse lists the sun as the source of the Sāma mantras, which may imply a distinct or elevated status for solar worship within the triad
objection_hooks: One could contend that the passage merely describes a ritual technique without asserting symbolic meaning, so the claim of symbolic representation overreaches the text
The term tapyamānānām may be read as literal worship of the deities rather than an internalized ascetic practice, challenging the interpretation that the act is primarily spiritual
The ordering of the three deities does not necessarily establish a hierarchy; the text simply enumerates them, so inferring solar superiority may not be justified

sanskrit: स एतां त्रयीं विद्यामभ्यतपत्तस्यास्तप्यमानाया रसान्प्रावृहद्भूरित्यृग्भ्यो भुवरिति यजुर्भ्यः स्वरिति सामभ्यः
transliteration: sa etāṃ trayīṃ vidyāmabhyatapattasyāstapyamānāyā rasānprāvṛhadbhūrityṛgbhyo bhuvariti yajurbhyaḥ svariti sāmabhyaḥ
translation: Then Prajāpati worshipped these three Vedas . And from those Vedas that were worshipped he extracted the essence. From the Ṛg Veda he got ‘bhūḥ, ’ from the Yajur Veda he got ‘bhuvaḥ, ’ and from the Sāma Veda he got ‘svaḥ’.
word_meaning: Saḥ etām trayīm vidyām abhyatapat, [then] he worshipped the knowledge from these three; tasyaḥ tapyamānāyāḥ, from these that he worshipped; rasān prāvṛhat, he extracted the essence; bhūḥ iti ṛgbhyaḥ, ‘bhūḥ’ from the Ṛg Veda; bhuvaḥ iti yajurbhyaḥ, ‘bhuvaḥ’ from the Yajur Veda; svaḥ iti sāmabhyaḥ, ‘svaḥ’ from the Sāma Veda.
claim_candidates: Prajapati worshipped the three Vedas, indicating that the Vedas themselves are objects of worship
The syllables bhū, bhuva, and sva were extracted respectively from the Rig, Yajur, and Sāma Vedas, showing that these texts are the source of the components of the sacred syllable Om
The act of extracting the essence (rasān prāvṛhat) from the Vedas suggests a link between Vedic knowledge and the metaphysical essence of reality
objection_hooks: One might contend that Upanishadic teaching ultimately directs worship toward Brahman, not toward the Vedas as objects, challenging the claim that the Vedas are proper objects of worship

The attribution of bhū, bhuva, and sva to specific Vedas could be a later interpretive layer rather than an original feature, casting doubt on the claim that the Vedas are the primary source of the Om components

sanskrit: तद्यदृक्तो रिष्येद्भूः स्वाहेति गार्हपत्ये जुहुयादृचामेव तद्रसेनर्चां वीर्येणर्चां यज्ञस्य विरिष्टं संदधाति
transliteration: tadyadṛkto riṣyedbhūḥ svāheti gārhapatye juhuyādṛcāmeva tadrasenarcāṃ vīryeṇarcāṃ yajñasya viriṣṭaṃ saṃdadhāti
translation: This is why, if it seems likely that there will be any harm done to the sacrifice because of a mistake in the Ṛk, the priest should offer oblations in the Gārhapatya fire saying, ‘Bhūḥ svāhā .’ Then, by the essence and strength of the Ṛk, any likely harm done to the sacrifice from a mistake in the Ṛk will be averted.
word_meaning: Tat, that is why; yat ṛktaḥ riṣyet, if there should be any harm done [to the sacrifice] because of [a mistake] in the Ṛk; bhūḥ svāhā iti gārhapatye juhuyāt, then he should offer oblations in the Gārhapatya fire saying, ‘Bhūḥ svāhā’; ṛcām eva tat rasena, by that essence of the Ṛk; ṛcām vīryeṇa, by the strength of the Ṛk; ṛcām yajñasya viriṣṭam, the harm done to the sacrifice by the mistake in the Ṛk; sandadhāti, is averted.
claim_candidates: The verse prescribes that a priest may avert a potential error in the sacrifice by reciting the formula Bhūḥ svāhā in the Gārhapatya fire, indicating that verbal utterance can function as a corrective measure in ritual

The efficacy of averting harm is attributed to the essence (rasena) and strength (vīryeṇa) of the Ṛk, suggesting that the sacred text itself possesses an intrinsic power that can neutralize ritual mistakes
The verse designates the Gārhapatya fire as the appropriate venue for this remedial offering, thereby distinguishing it from other sacrificial fires
objection_hooks: Relying on a verbal formula rather than correcting the textual mistake may undermine the primacy of accurate recitation, creating tension between textual fidelity and ritual efficacy

The claim that the Ṛk’s essence and strength can avert harm lacks an explicit mechanism in the verse, leaving ambiguity about whether the power resides in the words, the fire, or the priest’s intention
The specification of the Gārhapatya fire could be contested because other Vedic texts allow corrective rites in different fires, suggesting the verse’s prescription may not be universally applicable

sanskrit: स यदि यजुष्टो रिष्येद्भुवः स्वाहेति दक्षिणाग्नौ जुहुयाद्यजुषामेव तद्रसेन यजुषां वीर्येण यजुषां यज्ञस्य विरिष्टं संदधाति
transliteration: sa yadi yajuṣṭo riṣyedbhuvaḥ svāheti dakṣiṇāgnau juhuyādyajuṣāmeva tadrasena yajuṣāṃ vīryeṇa yajuṣāṃ yajñasya viriṣṭaṃ saṃdadhāti
translation: Then if it seems likely that there will be any harm done to the sacrifice because of a mistake in the Yajuḥ, the priest should offer oblations in the Dakṣiṇāgni fire saying, ‘ Bhuvaḥ svāhā .’ Then, by the essence and strength of the Yajuḥ, any likely harm done to the sacrifice from a mistake in the Yajuḥ will be averted.
word_meaning: Atha yadi yajuṣṭaḥ riṣyet, then if there should be any harm done [to the sacrifice] because of [a mistake] in the Yajuḥ; bhuvaḥ svāhā iti dakṣiṇāgnau juhuyāt, then he should offer oblations in the Dakṣiṇāgni fire saying, ‘Bhuvaḥ svāhā’; yajuṣām eva tat rasena, by that essence of the Yajuḥ; yajuṣām vīryeṇa, by the strength of the Yajuḥ; yajuṣām yajñasya viriṣṭam, the harm done to the sacrifice by the mistake in the Yajuḥ; sandadhāti, is averted.
claim_candidates: The verse prescribes a remedial rite of offering Bhuvaḥ svāhā in the Dakṣiṇāgni to avert harm caused by a mistake in the Yajuḥ
The avoidance of harm depends on the essence and strength of the Yajuḥ itself, indicating that the sacrifice’s inherent potency can correct its own errors
The Dakṣiṇāgni fire is presented as the medium through which the corrective utterance operates, highlighting fire’s transformative role in the ritual
objection_hooks: One could argue that allowing a corrective rite suggests the original sacrifice need not be flawless, creating tension between ritual perfection and remedial flexibility
It may be contested that the Yajuḥ’s essence alone cannot rectify mistakes without the priest’s intentional agency, questioning the sufficiency of the sacrifice’s own potency
The claim that fire actively transmits corrective power might be challenged by the view that fire is merely symbolic, not an ontologically active agent in averting ritual error

sanskrit: अथ यदि सामतो रिष्येत्स्वः स्वाहेत्याहवनीये जुहुयात्साम्नामेव तद्रसेन साम्नां वीर्येण साम्नां यज्ञस्य विरिष्टं संदधाति
transliteration: atha yadi sāmato riṣyetsvaḥ svāhetyāhavanīye juhuyātsāmnāmeva tadrasena sāmnāṃ vīryeṇa sāmnāṃ yajñasya viriṣṭaṃ saṃdadhāti
translation: Then if it seems likely that there will be any harm done to the sacrifice because of a mistake in the Sāma, the priest should offer oblations in the Āhavanīya fire saying, ‘svaḥ svāhā .’ Then, by the essence and strength of the Sāma, any likely harm done to the sacrifice from a mistake in the Sāma will be averted.
word_meaning: Atha yadi sāmataḥ riṣyet, then if there should be any harm done [to the sacrifice] because of [a mistake] in the Sāma; svaḥ svāhā iti āhavanīye juhuyāt, then he should offer oblations in the Āhavanīya fire saying, ‘svaḥ svāhā’; sāmnām eva tat rasena, by that essence of the Sāma; sāmnām vīṛyeṇa, by the strength of the Sāma; sāmnām yajñasya viriṣṭam, the harm done to the sacrifice by the mistake in the Sāma; sandadhāti, is averted.
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the intrinsic essence (ras) and vigor (vīrya) of the Sāma chant possess the power to neutralize any potential damage to the sacrifice caused by errors in its performance
The priest may avert ritual mishaps by invoking the mantra ‘svaḥ svāhā’ in the āhavanīya fire, indicating that verbal utterance functions as a protective substitute for correct ritual execution
The efficacy of the sacrifice is located in the inner potency of the sacred sound Sāma rather than solely in material offerings, suggesting that the chant symbolically represents Brahman
objection_hooks: The verse still requires the priest to perform a specific fire offering with the mantra, so the verbal utterance is not a standalone substitute but part of the ritual, challenging the claim of substitution

The protective effect of Sāma is conditioned on the priest’s perception of likely harm (‘if it seems likely’), implying the power is not absolute but dependent on human judgment, which questions the claim of inherent potency

sanskrit: तद्यथा लवणेन सुवर्णं संदध्यात्सुवर्णेन रजतं रजतेन त्रपु त्रपुणा सीसं सीसेन लोहं लोहेन दारु दारु चर्मणा
transliteration: tadyathā lavaṇena suvarṇaṃ saṃdadhyātsuvarṇena rajataṃ rajatena trapu trapuṇā sīsaṃ sīsena lohaṃ lohena dāru dāru carmaṇā
translation: It is like joining gold with the help of borax, silver with the help of gold, tin with the help of silver, lead with the help of tin, iron with the help of lead, wood with the help of iron, and wood with the help of leather.
word_meaning: Tat yathā, it is like; lavaṇena, with the help of borax; suvarṇam sandadhyāt, one joins gold; suvarṇena rajatam, silver with the help of gold; rajatena trapu, tin with the help of silver; trapuṇā sīsam, lead with the help of tin; sīsena loham, iron with the help of lead; lohena dāru, wood with the help of iron; dāru carmaṇā, [and] wood with the help of leather.
commentary: Two things can join together when the right combination of materials is used. In the same way, if there is
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse uses the progressive joining of metals as a metaphor for the process by which the individual self becomes united with Brahman through appropriate means

The sequence of material combinations implies that each successive stage depends on the previous one, suggesting that spiritual realization requires a stepwise preparation where each prior attainment serves as the instrument for the next

The inclusion of wood and leather, non‑metallic materials, indicates that even the most base aspects of existence can be incorporated into the spiritual union, implying that all aspects of existence can be subsumed in Brahman
objection_hooks: One might argue that the metaphor of material joining does not necessarily imply identity of self with Brahman, but merely illustrates functional cooperation, thus the claim of self‑Brahman identity is overextended beyond the text

The stepwise progression could be challenged as implying a hierarchical ladder of spiritual states, which conflicts with Advaita’s assertion of immediate, non‑dual realization without sequential stages

The mention of wood and leather may be read as a literal description of metallurgical practice rather than a universal spiritual principle, questioning the claim that all aspects of existence are subsumed

sanskrit: एवमेषां लोकानामासां देवतानामस्यास्त्रय्या विद्याया वीर्येण यज्ञस्य विरिष्टं संदधाति भेषजकृतो ह वा एष यज्ञो यत्रैवंविद्ब्रह्मा भवति
transliteration: evameṣāṃ lokānāmāsāṃ devatānāmasyāstrayyā vidyāyā vīryeṇa yajñasya viriṣṭaṃ saṃdadhāti bheṣajakṛto ha vā eṣa yajño yatraivaṃvidbrahmā bhavati
translation: Similarly, by the power of these worlds, these deities, and these three holy scriptures, any flaws in the sacrifice are made up. Where there is a knowledgeable brahmā priest, that sacrifice gets the right medicine.
word_meaning: Evam, in this way; vīryeṇa, by the strength; eṣām lokānām, of these worlds; āsām devatānām, of these deities; asyāḥ trayyāḥ vidyāyāḥ, of these three holy scriptures; yajñasya viriṣṭam sandadhāti, he [the brahmā priest] makes up for the flaws of a sacrifice; yatra evamvit brahmā bhavati, when there is a knowledgeable brahmā priest; eṣaḥ yajñaḥ, this sacrifice; bheṣajakṛtaḥ ha vai, gets the right medicine.
commentary: When a person is sick he must take the appropriate medicine. Similar is the case of a sacrifice that has gotten off to a bad start. If the priest is a knowledgeable one, he will take the right steps to rectify the errors. Like a good doctor, he will apply the right medicine—that is, the combined powers of the worlds, the deities, and the scriptures.
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the success of a sacrifice hinges on the priest’s knowledge rather than merely on the performance of ritual actions
The combined powers of worlds, deities, and the three holy scriptures are presented as a medicinal remedy that can rectify flaws in a sacrifice
The three holy scriptures are treated as essential components, alongside worlds and deities, for correcting errors in sacrificial rites
objection_hooks: One could object that emphasizing priestly knowledge makes the sacrifice dependent on human expertise, thereby questioning the inherent efficacy of the ritual itself
The medicine analogy may be challenged for reducing the transcendental significance of worlds, deities, and scriptures to mere instrumental tools
Some traditions might dispute the necessity of the three scriptures for sacrificial correction, arguing that inner realization supersedes textual authority

sanskrit: एष ह वा उदक्प्रवणो यज्ञो यत्रैवंविद्ब्रह्मा भवत्येवंविदं ह वा एषा ब्रह्माणमनुगाथा यतो यत आवर्तते तत्तद्गच्छति
transliteration: eṣa ha vā udakpravaṇo yajño yatraivaṃvidbrahmā bhavatyevaṃvidaṃ ha vā eṣā brahmāṇamanugāthā yato yata āvartate tattadgacchati
translation: That sacrifice which is directed by a capable brahmā priest leads to the uttarāyaṇa [the path of the gods]. There is a verse in praise of such a learned priest: ‘Wherever the sacrifice goes wrong, this priest goes there to set things right’.
word_meaning: Eṣaḥ ha vai yajñaḥ udakpravaṇaḥ, this sacrifice is the way to the uttarāyaṇa [the path of the gods]; yatra evamvit brahmā bhavati, where there is a knowledgeable brahmā priest; evam vidam ha vai eṣaḥ brahmāṇam anu gāthā, there is this praise for such a learned brahmā priest; yataḥ yataḥ āvartate, wherever there is a flaw [in the sacrifice]; tat tat gacchati, he [the brahmā priest] goes there [to make amends].
commentary: Whenever a sacrifice is to be held, a competent priest should be placed in charge. This will ensure A capable priest is always in demand wherever a sacrifice is being held, because, first, he can avoid mistakes, and second, should there be any mistake, in spite of all the care taken, the priest knows how to correct it.
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that a competent brahmā priest is essential for the proper performance of sacrifice, as his presence guarantees correction of any errors
According to the verse, the presence of a knowledgeable priest transforms the sacrifice into a means of attaining the uttarāyaṇa, the path of the gods
The verse implies that the priest's role includes both preventive and remedial functions: he can avoid mistakes and also rectify them if they occur
objection_hooks: One might argue that the verse overstates the priest's efficacy, suggesting that even a competent priest cannot guarantee the sacrifice's success if the underlying intention or dharma is lacking
The claim that the sacrifice leads to the uttarāyaṇa could be challenged by the Advaita view that liberation is independent of ritual, thus the verse's emphasis on sacrifice may be seen as limited
The assertion that the priest can correct any mistake may be contested by the possibility of errors beyond human correction, such as cosmic or karmic flaws, implying limits to priestly power

sanskrit: मानवो ब्रह्मैवैक ऋत्विक्कुरूनश्वाभिरक्षत्येवंविद्ध वै ब्रह्मा यज्ञं यजमानं सर्वांश्चर्त्विजोऽभिरक्षति तस्मादेवंविदमेव ब्रह्माणं कुर्वीत नानेवंविदं नानेवंविदम्
transliteration: mānavo brahmaivaika ṛtvikkurūnaśvābhirakṣatyevaṃviddha vai brahmā yajñaṃ yajamānaṃ sarvāṃścartvijo'bhirakṣati tasmādevaṃvidameva brahmāṇaṃ kurvīta nānevaṃvidaṃ nānevaṃvidam
translation: A good brahmā priest is one who is able to observe silence, or one who is thoughtful. Just as a horse protects the soldiers, a learned brahmā priest protects the sacrifice, the sacrifices, and all the other priests. Therefore, one should appoint only such a learned brahmā for one’s sacrifice. One should not appoint anyone else.
word_meaning: Mānavaḥ brahmā eva ekaḥ ṛtvik, a true brahmā priest is one who is thoughtful [or, is able to observe silence]; kurūn aśvā abhirakṣati, [just as] a horse protects the soldiers; evamvit ha vai brahmā, such a learned brahmā priest; yajñam yajamānam sarvān ca ṛtvijaḥ abhirakṣati, protects the sacrifice, the sacrificer, and all the other priests; tasmāt, therefore; evam vidam eva brahmāṇam kurvīta, one should appoint only such a learned brahmā priest; na anevam vidam, not one who is otherwise; na anevam vidam, not one who is otherwise.
commentary: When a priest is able to maintain silence and is by nature thoughtful, he may be appointed as the brahmā for a sacrifice. He is the chief priest by virtue of his scholarship and his character. The Upaniṣad compares him to a horse which protects his master when he is in trouble. A good brahmā priest can take care of the sacrifice, the sacrifices, and the subordinate priests. For this reason, only one who is qualified should be appointed. One who is not fit should not be given the honour.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse states that the essential qualification for a brahmā priest is the ability to maintain silence and be thoughtful
It likens a learned brahmā priest’s protective role over the sacrifice and other priests to a horse protecting its master
The text commands that only priests possessing these qualities should be appointed, excluding any otherwise unqualified individuals
objection_hooks: One could contend that focusing solely on silence and thoughtfulness neglects other vital priestly skills such as mastery of ritual chants
The horse metaphor may be inadequate for describing the spiritual responsibilities of a priest, suggesting a deeper protective function than mere guardianship
Insisting on external qualifications might conflict with the Upanishadic emphasis on inner knowledge as the true basis for spiritual authority

sanskrit: यो ह वै ज्येष्ठं च श्रेष्ठं च वेद ज्येष्ठश्च ह वै श्रेष्ठश्च भवति प्राणो वाव ज्येष्ठश्च श्रेष्ठश्च
transliteration: yo ha vai jyeṣṭhaṃ ca śreṣṭhaṃ ca veda jyeṣṭhaśca ha vai śreṣṭhaśca bhavati prāṇo vāva jyeṣṭhaśca śreṣṭhaśca
translation: Om. He who knows the oldest and the best himself becomes the oldest and the best. It is prāṇa which is the oldest and the best.
word_meaning: Yaḥ ha vai, he who; jyeṣṭham ca śreṣṭham ca veda, knows the oldest and the best; jyeṣṭhaḥ ca ha vai śreṣṭhaḥ ca bhavati, himself becomes the oldest and the best; prāṇaḥ vāva jyeṣṭhaḥ ca śreṣṭhaḥ ca, prāṇa is indeed the oldest and the best.
commentary: The importance of prāṇa has been mentioned earlier. True, it works in conjunction with the organs of speaking, hearing, seeing, etc., but it is the first and foremost of all of them. Prāṇa is in a child even when it is still in it’s mother’s womb. It is active before the various organs are active. Prāṇa therefore commands respect from all. The reason why so much importance is given to prāṇa is to teach us to show respect where respect is due. We should respect both age and merit. Then we also shall command respect from all because of our age and merit. Indirectly, this is a hint that we should respect knowledge.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: He who knows the oldest and the best becomes the oldest and the best, indicating that knowledge of age and merit transforms one’s status
Prāṇa is identified as the oldest and the best of all, establishing its primacy over sensory organs and other functions
Respect for age and merit is prescribed as a means to command respect from others, linking ethical conduct with social recognition
objection_hooks: Knowing the oldest and best may not be sufficient for becoming them; transformation might require additional practice or grace
Claiming prāṇa as the sole oldest and best could be challenged by the role of mind, intellect, or other subtle forces in sustaining life
Emphasizing respect for age and merit may conflict with the non‑dual Advaita view that ultimate reality transcends such relative distinctions

sanskrit: यो ह वै वसिष्ठं वेद वसिष्ठो ह स्वानां भवति वाग्वाव वसिष्ठः
transliteration: yo ha vai vasiṣṭhaṃ veda vasiṣṭho ha svānāṃ bhavati vāgvāva vasiṣṭhaḥ
translation: He who knows that which is of high standing himself becomes of high standing among his own relatives. Eloquence gives one this high standing [in society].
word_meaning: Yaḥ ha vai, he who; vasiṣṭham veda, knows that which is of high standing; vasiṣṭhaḥ ha svānām bhavati, becomes of high standing among his own relatives; vāk vāva vasiṣṭhaḥ, speech [i.e., eloquence] is surely of high standing [i.e., it is the secret of high standing].
commentary: If a person respects another for his power to influence others, he himself some day has the same power and commands respect from others. It is, in fact, the power of eloquence which is behind this influence.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: Knowledge of what is high standing brings one high standing among his own relatives
Eloquence or skillful speech is the decisive factor that grants a person social respect and influence
Respecting another’s power to influence others eventually bestows similar power upon the respect‑giver
objection_hooks: One might argue that social respect depends more on moral virtue than on mere eloquence
The claim that respect for others’ power yields one’s own power could be seen as circular and ignores external determinants of power
Knowing what is high standing does not necessarily translate into actual high standing within a family due to entrenched social hierarchies

sanskrit: यो ह वै प्रतिष्ठां वेद प्रति ह तिष्ठत्यस्मिंश्च लोकेऽमुष्मिंश्च चक्षुर्वाव प्रतिष्ठा
transliteration: yo ha vai pratiṣṭhāṃ veda prati ha tiṣṭhatyasmiṃśca loke'muṣmiṃśca cakṣurvāva pratiṣṭhā
translation: He who knows the support attains a support in this world and also in the other world [i.e., heaven]. The eye is indeed the support.
word_meaning: Yaḥ ha vai, he who; pratiṣṭhām veda, knows the support; asmin ca loke, in this world; amuṣmin ca, and in the other [world]; pratitiṣṭhati, attains a support; cakṣuḥ vāva pratiṣṭhā, the eye is surely the support.
commentary: Good sight is necessary for success in life. A sharp eye is not merely a physical asset; it is also a mental asset. It means a good and sharp sense of judgement. One can easily avoid the pitfalls of life, physical as well as moral, with good sight. Given this quality, you are safe in this world as well as in the other.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: He who knows the support attains a support in both this world and the other world, implying that knowledge secures safety across realms
The eye is presented as the support, meaning that clear perception—both physical sight and mental judgement—provides the means to avoid pitfalls
Good sight, understood as sharp sense of judgement, is portrayed as a mental asset that determines one’s destiny in this life and the afterlife
objection_hooks: The verse may refer only to literal visual ability, so claiming it guarantees safety in the afterlife extends beyond the text
Equating knowledge of the support with guaranteed support could be challenged as the text might describe material stability rather than spiritual liberation
Interpreting the eye as a metaphor for moral discernment may be disputed because the Sanskrit wording does not explicitly indicate a symbolic meaning

sanskrit: यो ह वै सम्पदं वेद संहास्मै कामाः पद्यन्ते दैवाश्च मानुषाश्च श्रोत्रं वाव सम्पत्
transliteration: yo ha vai sampadaṃ veda saṃhāsmai kāmāḥ padyante daivāśca mānuṣāśca śrotraṃ vāva sampat
translation: He who knows affluence has all things desired by human beings and gods come to him. Affluence is represented by the ears.
word_meaning: Yaḥ ha vai, he who; sampadam veda, knows affluence; asmai, to him; kāmāḥ, desirable things; sam-padyante, come; daivāḥ ca, [things meant for] gods; mānuṣāḥ ca, and [things meant for] human beings; śrotram vāva sampat, the ears are surely affluence.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: According to the verse, mastery of sampada guarantees that both divine and human desires are attracted to the knower
The verse identifies the ears as the definitive symbol of affluence, implying that hearing is the true wealth
objection_hooks: Equating ears with affluence may be merely metaphorical and does not explain material wealth beyond sensory perception
The assertion that all desires of gods and humans come to the knower appears contradicted by everyday experience of unfulfilled desires

sanskrit: यो ह वा आयतनं वेदायतनं ह स्वानां भवति मनो ह वा आयतनम्
transliteration: yo ha vā āyatanaṃ vedāyatanaṃ ha svānāṃ bhavati mano ha vā āyatanam
translation: He who knows the abode becomes the shelter of his family. The mind is the abode.
word_meaning: Yaḥ ha vai, he who; āyatanam veda, knows the abode; āyatanam ha svānām bhavati, becomes the abode [i.e., shelter] of his family; manaḥ ha vai āyatanam, the mind is surely the abode.
commentary: There is something in the body-mind complex of human beings called āyatana . If a person knows this ‘āyatana, ’ it is to be understood that he is a wise person. And such a person is able to serve as an āyatana to his own relatives. That is to say, he is The mind is the āyatana of the body-mind complex. Why? Because the sense organs have their experiences, but all those experiences take shelter in the mind.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: True wisdom is defined as recognizing the mind as the āyatana, the abode of the body‑mind complex
A person who knows the āyatana becomes a shelter for his family, linking self‑knowledge with ethical responsibility
