The mind serves as the repository for all sensory experiences, thus it is the true abode of the organism
objection_hooks: Equating the mind with the ultimate abode may neglect the role of the subtle body and external support, questioning whether self‑knowledge alone makes one a shelter for family
Knowing the mind does not necessarily ensure material provision or protection for relatives, challenging the claim that self‑knowledge guarantees familial shelter
Other Upanishadic passages locate the ultimate abode in Brahman beyond the mind, which may contradict the identification of the mind as the sole abode

sanskrit: अथ ह प्राणा अहंश्रेयसि व्यूदिरेऽहंश्रेयानस्म्यहं श्रेयानस्मीति
transliteration: atha ha prāṇā ahaṃśreyasi vyūdire'haṃśreyānasmyahaṃ śreyānasmīti
translation: Once the sense organs began to quarrel among themselves, each one claiming it was supreme. They each said, ‘I am the best. I am the best’.
word_meaning: Atha ha, once; prāṇāḥ, the sense organs; vyūdire, began to quarrel [among themselves]; aham-śreyasi, each thinking it was the best; aham śreyān asmi aham śreyān asmi iti, saying, ‘I am the best, I am the best.’
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse shows that the sense organs, when acting independently, each assert their own superiority, reflecting a fundamental ignorance of the underlying unity of the self

The quarrelling of the sense organs demonstrates that the perception of being the best is a mental construct rather than an objective reality, implying that true knowledge must transcend such egoistic identifications

The verse suggests that the source of conflict among the senses is the false identification of the individual self with each sense organ, indicating that liberation requires recognizing the non-difference between the self and Brahman
objection_hooks: One might argue that the verse merely describes a psychological phenomenon of sensory competition and does not make a metaphysical claim about unity of self
The claim that the sense organs' claim of supremacy is ignorance of unity presupposes a notion of unity that the text does not explicitly state, so the inference may be unwarranted
The assertion that liberation requires recognizing non-difference could be challenged by passages that emphasize disciplined practice or ritual as necessary for emancipation

sanskrit: ते ह प्राणाः प्रजापतिं पितरमेत्योचुर्भगवन्को नः श्रेष्ठ इति तान्होवाच यस्मिन्व उत्क्रान्ते शरीरं पापिष्ठतरमिव दृश्येत स वः श्रेष्ठ इति
transliteration: te ha prāṇāḥ prajāpatiṃ pitarametyocurbhagavanko naḥ śreṣṭha iti tānhovāca yasminva utkrānte śarīraṃ pāpiṣṭhataramiva dṛśyeta sa vaḥ śreṣṭha iti
translation: The organs then went to their father Prajāpati and said, ‘Revered sir, who among us is the best?’ He replied, ‘He is the best among you on whose departure the body becomes totally untouchable’.
word_meaning: Te ha prāṇāḥ, those organs; pitaram prajāpatim etya, coming to their father Prajāpati; ucuḥ, said; bhagavan, revered sir; naḥ, among us; kaḥ śreṣṭhaḥ iti, who is the best; tān ha uvāca, he said to them; vaḥ, among you; yasmin utkrānte, on whose leaving; śarīram pāpiṣṭhataram iva dṛśyeta, the body appears to be untouchable; saḥ vaḥ śreṣṭhaḥ, he is the best among you.
commentary: We all know what happens when life leaves the body. It is not the same body. It shrinks. It becomes ugly. And soon it starts decomposing. No one wants to touch it. Prajāpati indirectly declared in this way that prāṇa was the best among them. When Prajāpati said this, he changed his voice lest he hurt the other organs. The way he changed his voice is called kaku .
concept_tags: Brahman as Life, Joy, and the Void
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: Prajapati declares that prana is the supreme organ because its departure makes the body untouchable
The commentary claims Prajapati softened his voice (kaku) to avoid offending the other organs
The verse suggests that after prana leaves the body becomes an inert, untouchable shell
The description of the body as untouchable supports the view that life (prana) is the essential reality while the corpse is merely a shell
objection_hooks: The Sanskrit does not name prana explicitly as the best, only describes the effect of its departure
The text does not mention any change of tone, so the claim about kaku is an extrapolation from the commentary
The passage describes the body as untouchable but does not deny its significance, leaving room for alternative interpretations

sanskrit: सा ह वागुच्चक्राम सा संवत्सरं प्रोष्य पर्येत्योवाच कथमशकतर्ते मज्जीवितुमिति यथा कला अवदन्तः प्राणन्तः प्राणेन पश्यन्तश्चक्षुषा शृण्वन्तः श्रोत्रेण ध्यायन्तो मनसैवमिति प्रविवेश ह वाक्
transliteration: sā ha vāguccakrāma sā saṃvatsaraṃ proṣya paryetyovāca kathamaśakatarte majjīvitumiti yathā kalā avadantaḥ prāṇantaḥ prāṇena paśyantaścakṣuṣā śṛṇvantaḥ śrotreṇa dhyāyanto manasaivamiti praviveśa ha vāk
translation: First speech left the body. After staying away one whole year, he came back and asked the other organs, ‘How did you sustain yourselves in my absence?’ The rest of the organs said: ‘Just as mute people do without speaking, but they are able to survive by breathing, and see with the eyes, hear with the ears, and think with the mind. We did the same.’ Hearing all this, speech re-entered the body.
word_meaning: Sā ha vāk, that speech; ut-cakrāma, left [the body]; saṃvatsaram proṣya, after staying away one year; sā paretya uvāca, he came back and said; mat ṛte, in my absence; katham, how; jīvitum aśakata iti, did you manage to survive; yathā kalāḥ, [the other organs said: ] just as the mute; avadantaḥ, do not speak; praḥantaḥ prāṇena, [but] are able to survive with the help of prāṇa, the vital force; paśyantaḥ cakṣuṣā, to see with the help of the eyes; śṛṇvantaḥ śrotreṇa, to hear with the help of the ears; dhyāyantaḥ manasā, to think with the help of the mind; evam iti, in the same way; vāk praviveśa ha, [hearing this] speech re-entered the body.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse shows that the self continues to exist even when the faculty of speech is absent, indicating that the individual self is identical with Brahman beyond any particular function
Each organ sustains itself through prana, suggesting that the vital force is the underlying reality that supports life independent of specific faculties
Speech re‑enters the body only after hearing the other organs, implying that vāk is a conduit for consciousness rather than its source
objection_hooks: One might argue that the dialogue highlights the mutual dependence of all faculties, thereby questioning the claim of the self’s independence from speech
The text lists separate senses (eyes, ears, mind) as sustaining life, which could be read as a pluralistic account rather than a single prana as the sole basis
Advaita tradition often treats vāk as an expression of Brahman itself, so portraying speech merely as a vehicle may understate its ontological status

sanskrit: चक्षुर्होच्चक्राम तत्संवत्सरं प्रोष्य पर्येत्योवाच कथमशकतर्ते मज्जीवितुमिति यथान्धा अपश्यन्तः प्राणन्तः प्राणेन वदन्तो वाचा शृण्वन्तः श्रोत्रेण ध्यायन्तो मनसैवमिति प्रविवेश ह चक्षुः
transliteration: cakṣurhoccakrāma tatsaṃvatsaraṃ proṣya paryetyovāca kathamaśakatarte majjīvitumiti yathāndhā apaśyantaḥ prāṇantaḥ prāṇena vadanto vācā śṛṇvantaḥ śrotreṇa dhyāyanto manasaivamiti praviveśa ha cakṣuḥ
translation: Next the organ of vision left the body. After staying away one whole year, it came back and asked the other organs, ‘How did you sustain yourselves in my absence?’ The rest of the organs said: ‘Just as blind people do without seeing, but they are able to survive by breathing, and speak with the organ of speech, hear with the ears, and think with the mind. We did the same.’ Hearing all this, the organ of vision re-entered the. body.
word_meaning: Cakṣuḥ ha, the organ of vision; ut-cakrāma, left [the body]; saṃvatsaram proṣya, after staying away one year; tat paretya uvāca, it came back and said; mat ṛte, in my absence; katham, how; jīvitum aśakata iti, did you manage to survive; yathā andhāḥ, [the other organs said: ] just as the blind; apaśyantaḥ, do not see; prāṇantaḥ prāṇena, [but] are able to survive with the help of prāṇa, the vital force; vadantaḥ vācā, to speak with the help of the organ of speech; śṛṇvantaḥ śrotreṇa, to hear with the help of the ears; dhyāyantaḥ manasā, to think with the help of the mind; evam iti, in the same way; cakṣuḥ praviveśa ha, [hearing this] the organ of vision re-entered the body.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse shows that the body can continue to live without the organ of vision, indicating that sight is not required for biological survival
The dialogue emphasizes that prāṇa, the vital force, sustains the organism in the eye’s absence, suggesting the primacy of prāṇa over sensory perception
The eye’s departure for a year and subsequent return demonstrates that sensory organs are temporary instruments that can be withdrawn and restored without disturbing the underlying self
objection_hooks: One might argue that survival without sight does not imply that the eye is unimportant, because the quality of life and functional capacities are reduced without vision
The passage does not explicitly rank prāṇa above sight; it merely notes that other faculties compensate, so the claim of prāṇa’s superiority may exceed the text’s implication

Interpreting the eye’s withdrawal as a literal event may be metaphorical, and thus using it to prove that senses are merely instruments of an underlying self may go beyond the verse’s explicit meaning

sanskrit: श्रोत्रं होच्चक्राम तत्संवत्सरं प्रोष्य पर्येत्योवाच कथमशकतर्ते मज्जीवितुमिति यथा बधिरा अशृण्वन्तः प्राणन्तः प्राणेन वदन्तो वाचा पश्यन्तश्चक्षुषा ध्यायन्तो मनसैवमिति प्रविवेश ह श्रोत्रम्
transliteration: śrotraṃ hoccakrāma tatsaṃvatsaraṃ proṣya paryetyovāca kathamaśakatarte majjīvitumiti yathā badhirā aśṛṇvantaḥ prāṇantaḥ prāṇena vadanto vācā paśyantaścakṣuṣā dhyāyanto manasaivamiti praviveśa ha śrotram
translation: Next the organ of hearing left the body. After staying away one whole year, it came back and asked the other organs, ‘How did you sustain yourselves in my absence?’ The rest of the organs said: ‘Just as deaf people do without hearing, but they are able to survive by breathing, and speak with the organ of speech, see with the eyes, and think with the mind. We did the same.’ Hearing all this, the organ of hearing re-entered the body.
word_meaning: Śrotram ha, the organ of hearing; ut-cakrāma, left [the body]; saṃvatsaram proṣya, after staying away one year; tat paretya uvāca, it came back and said; mat ṛte, in my absence; katham, how; jīvitum aśakata iti, did you manage to survive; yathā badhirāḥ, [the other organs said: ] just as the deaf; aśṛṇvantaḥ, do not hear; prāṇantaḥ prāṇena, [but] are able to survive with the help of prāṇa, the vital force; vadantaḥ vācā, to speak with the help of the organ of speech; paśyantaḥ cakṣuṣā, to see with the help of the eyes; dhyāyantaḥ manasā, to think with the help of the mind; evam iti, in the same way; śrotram praviveśa ha, [hearing this] the organ of hearing re-entered the body.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse shows that bodily functions persist without the organ of hearing, indicating that the true self is not confined to any single sense organ but transcends them
Survival of the body during the hearing organ’s absence is attributed to prāṇa, presenting prāṇa as the underlying life force that sustains all faculties
The dialogue reveals that the mind (manas) can serve as an inner sense, thinking in place of hearing, which highlights the mind’s role in perceiving reality
objection_hooks: The passage may be describing mere physiological interdependence, so inferring a metaphysical claim about the self’s transcendence could exceed what the text states
Identifying prāṇa solely with breath rather than a universal life principle challenges the claim that it functions as the ultimate sustaining force
Since the mind’s cognition still relies on sensory inputs, asserting that it fully substitutes for hearing may overstate its independence

sanskrit: मनो होच्चक्राम तत्संवत्सरं प्रोष्य पर्येत्योवाच कथमशकतर्ते मज्जीवितुमिति यथा बाला अमनसः प्राणन्तः प्राणेन वदन्तो वाचा पश्यन्तश्चक्षुषा शृण्वन्तः श्रोत्रेणैवमिति प्रविवेश ह मनः
transliteration: mano hoccakrāma tatsaṃvatsaraṃ proṣya paryetyovāca kathamaśakatarte majjīvitumiti yathā bālā amanasaḥ prāṇantaḥ prāṇena vadanto vācā paśyantaścakṣuṣā śṛṇvantaḥ śrotreṇaivamiti praviveśa ha manaḥ
translation: Next the mind left the body. After staying away one whole year, he came back and asked the other organs, ‘How did you sustain yourselves in my absence?’ The rest of the organs said: ‘Just as children do without thinking for themselves, but they are able to survive by breathing, and speak with the organ of speech, see with the eyes, and hear with the ears. We did the same.’ Hearing all this, the mind re-entered the body.
word_meaning: Manaḥ ha, the mind; ut-cakrāma, left [the body]; saṃvatsaram proṣya, after staying away one year; tat paretya uvāca, he came back and said; mat ṛte, in my absence; katham, how; jīvitum aśakata iti, did you manage to survive; yathā bālāḥ, [the other organs said: ] just as the children; amanasaḥ, do not receive much support from the mind; prāṇantaḥ prāṇena, [but] are able to survive with the help of prāṇa, the vital force; vadantaḥ vācā, to speak with the help of the organ of speech; paśyantaḥ cakṣuṣā, to see with the help of the eyes; śṛṇvantaḥ śrotreṇa, to hear with the help of the ears; evam iti, in the same way; manaḥ praviveśa ha, [hearing this] the mind re-entered the body.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse shows that the mind is not required for the basic physiological functions of the body, which can be sustained by prana alone
The mind can detach from the body for an extended period and later re‑enter, indicating a functional independence between mind and body
The dialogue portrays an interdependence where the mind depends on the organs’ report to return, highlighting the relationship between subtle and gross bodies
objection_hooks: The description may be metaphorical rather than a literal claim about physiological independence of the mind
The mind’s return after hearing the organs suggests an underlying unity, challenging the notion of true independence
The passage focuses on functional aspects and does not address the metaphysical identity of the self, limiting its support for Advaita’s self‑body distinction

sanskrit: अथ ह प्राण उच्चिक्रमिषन्स यथा सुहयः पड्वीशशङ्कून्संखिदेदेवमितरान्प्राणान्समखिदत्तं हाभिसमेत्योचुर्भगवन्नेधि त्वं नः श्रेष्ठोऽसि मोत्क्रमीरिति
transliteration: atha ha prāṇa uccikramiṣansa yathā suhayaḥ paḍvīśaśaṅkūnsaṃkhidedevamitarānprāṇānsamakhidattaṃ hābhisametyocurbhagavannedhi tvaṃ naḥ śreṣṭho'si motkramīriti
translation: Now prāṇa, the vital force, decided to leave. Just as a good horse is able to uproot the pegs to which its feet are tied, similarly, the chief prāṇa was about to carry the other organs away with him. Those other organs then came to him and with great humility said: ‘O lord, be our leader. You are the greatest among us. Please don’t leave us’.
word_meaning: Atha ha saḥ prāṇaḥ, now that prāṇa, the vital force; uccikramiṣan, wishing to go out; yathā, just as; suhayaḥ, a good horse; paḍvīśa-śaṅkūn, the pegs to which his feet are tied; saṃkhidet, uproots; evam, in the same way; itarān prāṇān, the other organs; samakhidat, began to leave with him; tam abhisametya, coming to him [with great humility]; ucuḥ ha, land] said; bhagavan, lord; edhi, be our leader; naḥ tvam śreṣṭhaḥ asi, you are the best among us; mā utkramīḥ iti, please do not leave us.
commentary: The organs, beginning with vāk (speech), left the body one after another. But that made little or no difference to the body or to the rest of the organs. Then prāṇa decided to leave. Just as he started to go, all the other organs felt that they were being forced to leave also. They realized then that they were not free; they were utterly dependent on the chief prāṇa. Coming to him, they showed him respect and said that they now recognized his superiority.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse portrays prāṇa as the chief organ whose departure determines the departure of all other organs
The other organs exhibit humility and recognize their dependence on prāṇa only when its departure is imminent
The horse metaphor illustrates that prāṇa's will alone can uproot the body's attachments
The passage suggests that the body’s integrity is contingent upon the functioning of prāṇa, implying a hierarchical organization of subtle body elements
objection_hooks: One might argue that the commentary notes the other organs’ departure made little difference to the body, indicating they are not wholly dependent on prāṇa
The horse metaphor may be an anthropomorphic illustration that does not fully capture the subtle nature of prāṇa, challenging the claim of unilateral power
Other Upanishadic teachings treat all subtle elements as equal manifestations of Brahman, contesting the hierarchical interpretation
The humility expressed by the organs could be a literary device rather than an ontological fact, weakening the claim of recognized dependence

sanskrit: अथ हैनं वागुवाच यदहं वसिष्ठोऽस्मि त्वं तद्वसिष्ठोऽसीत्यथ हैनं चक्षुरुवाच यदहं प्रतिष्ठास्मि त्वं तत्प्रतिष्ठासीति
transliteration: atha hainaṃ vāguvāca yadahaṃ vasiṣṭho'smi tvaṃ tadvasiṣṭho'sītyatha hainaṃ cakṣuruvāca yadahaṃ pratiṣṭhāsmi tvaṃ tatpratiṣṭhāsīti
translation: The organ of speech then said to the chief prāṇa, ‘If I have the quality of high standing, it is because you have that quality.’ Next the organ of vision said to him, ‘True, I have the quality of supporting others, but I owe that quality to you’.
word_meaning: Atha ha enam vāk uvāca, next vāk [speech] said to him [i.e., to prāṇa]; yat aham vasiṣṭhaḥ asmi, I am as much endowed with [the quality of] high standing; tat tvani vasiṣṭhaḥ asi iti, as you are endowed with [the quality of] high standing; atha ha enam cakṣuḥ uvāca, next the organ of vision said to him; yat aham pratiṣṭhā asmi, I am as much endowed with [the quality of] support; tat tvam pratiṣṭhā asi iti, as you are a support.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse asserts that the functional qualities of the sensory organs speech and vision depend on the chief prana, indicating a hierarchical relationship where prana is the source of their capacities

By having the organs attribute their excellence to prana the text implies that the true locus of high standing or support resides in the underlying life‑force rather than in the organs themselves, suggesting an early articulation of the principle that the individual self is grounded in a universal substratum
objection_hooks: One might contend that the passage merely records a mutual acknowledgment between organs and prana without intending a metaphysical claim about ultimate source, thus the inference to a universal self could be unwarranted

The terms high standing and support may be understood as functional descriptors of organ performance rather than statements of ontological dependence, challenging the claim that the verse establishes prana as the ultimate source of being

sanskrit: अथ हैनंश्रोत्रमुवाच यदहं सम्पदस्मि त्वं तत्सम्पदसीत्यथ हैनं मन उवाच यदहमायतनमस्मि त्वं तदायतनमसीति
transliteration: atha hainaṃśrotramuvāca yadahaṃ sampadasmi tvaṃ tatsampadasītyatha hainaṃ mana uvāca yadahamāyatanamasmi tvaṃ tadāyatanamasīti
translation: The organ of hearing then said to the chief prāṇa, ‘If I have the quality of affluence, it is because you have that quality.’ Next the mind said to him, ‘True, I have the quality of being a shelter to many, but that quality is, in fact, yours’.
word_meaning: Atha ha enam śrotram uvāca, next the organ of hearing said to him [i.e., to prāṇa]; yat aham sampat asmi, I am as much endowed with [the quality of] affluence; tat tvam sampat asi iti, as you are endowed with [the quality of] affluence; atha ha enam manaḥ uvāca, next the mind said to him; yat aham āyatanam asmi, I am as much endowed with [the quality of] a shelter; tat tvam āyatanam asi iti, as you are a shelter.
commentary: Tenants may go to their landlord and say many things to please him and exhibit their loyalty. Like that, the various sense organs which had earlier fought among themselves, each claiming superiority, are now, with great humility, declaring that they have nothing of their own—that whatever good qualities they have they owe to their master, the chief prāṇa.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The organ of hearing and the mind acknowledge that their qualities of affluence and shelter are not intrinsic but derived from the chief prāṇa
The verse establishes a hierarchical dependence within the subtle body, positioning chief prāṇa as the source of functional attributes of the sense organs
The humility expressed by the sense organs reflects an ethical qualification that resolves earlier rivalry and promotes unity among the subtle faculties
objection_hooks: One could argue that the passage uses poetic humility without asserting an actual ontological dependence, so the claim of derived qualities may be metaphorical
The identification of chief prāṇa as the ultimate source may conflict with Upanishadic teachings that locate the ultimate source in the Atman or Brahman rather than in prāṇa
The interpretation of humility as an ethical qualification might be contested, as the commentary’s landlord‑tenant analogy could be read merely as a social metaphor, not a moral prescription

sanskrit: न वै वाचो न चक्षूंषि न श्रोत्राणि न मनांसीत्याचक्षते प्राणा इत्येवाचक्षते प्राणो ह्येवैतानि सर्वाणि भवति
transliteration: na vai vāco na cakṣūṃṣi na śrotrāṇi na manāṃsītyācakṣate prāṇā ityevācakṣate prāṇo hyevaitāni sarvāṇi bhavati
translation: Scholars do not call them organs of speech, eyes, ears, or minds. They call them ‘ prāṇas, ’ for prāṇa has become all these organs.
word_meaning: Na vai vācaḥ, not organs of speech; na cakṣūṃṣi, not eyes; na śrotrāṇi, not ears; na manāṃsi, not minds; iti eva ācakṣate, they say; prāṇāḥ iti eva ācakṣate, they say the prāṇas; hi, for; prāṇaḥ bhavati, prāṇa has become; etāni sarvāṇi, all these.
commentary: The Self is the source of everything that exists, living or non-living. The Self manifests itself in many forms—as human beings, animals, plants, etc. And it is because of the Self that we live, speak, and act. Human beings have two sets of organs—organs of perception and organs of action. We also have a mind. These organs are powerful, but the most powerful is the mind. Yet none of these organs is independent. Each one exists and functions only to serve the Self, the master. The Self is within the body as well as without. Within the body it is the Ātman, which functions through prāṇa, the vital breath. As prāṇa, it animates the body and all the organs. Without prāṇa, the body with all its organs is dead, useless.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse states that speech, sight, hearing and mind are not independent organs but are identified as prāṇa because prāṇa has become all these organs
The commentary explains that prāṇa is the manifestation of the Self (Ātman) within the body, and without prāṇa the organs are lifeless

By equating sensory organs with prāṇa, the verse supports a monistic view that the apparent multiplicity of bodily functions is ultimately a single reality, aligning with Advaita’s claim of unity between the individual self and Brahman
objection_hooks: Identifying sensory organs with prāṇa may blur necessary functional distinctions required for ethical decision‑making or ritual practice, raising a tension between monistic ontology and practical differentiation

Other Upanishadic passages describe prāṇa as a subordinate vital force rather than identical with the Self, so the commentary’s equation of prāṇa with the Self could be challenged as a selective interpretation

sanskrit: स होवाच किं मेऽन्नं भविष्यतीति यत्किंचिदिदमा श्वभ्य आ शकुनिभ्य इति होचुस्तद्वा एतदनस्यान्नमनो ह वै नाम प्रत्यक्षं न ह वा एवंविदि किंचनानन्नं भवतीति
transliteration: sa hovāca kiṃ me'nnaṃ bhaviṣyatīti yatkiṃcididamā śvabhya ā śakunibhya iti hocustadvā etadanasyānnamano ha vai nāma pratyakṣaṃ na ha vā evaṃvidi kiṃcanānannaṃ bhavatīti
translation: Prāṇa then asked, ‘What will be my food?’ The other organs said: ‘Anything that even dogs, birds, and other animals can eat in this world is your food.’ All that is food for ana. Ana is a name of prāṇa. For one who knows this nothing is uneatable. [That is, he can eat any food that an animal can eat.]
word_meaning: Saḥ ha uvāca, he [prāṇa] asked; kim me annam bhaviṣyati iti, what will be my food; iti ucuḥ ha, [the other organs] replied; ā-śvabhyaḥ ā-śakunibhyaḥ, for dogs as well as birds; yat idam kiñcit, whatever there is; tat vai, all that; etat anasya annam, is this food for prāṇa; anaḥ ha vai nāma pratyakṣam, ‘ana’ is the name of prāṇa itself; ha vai evam vidi, for one who knows thus; kiñcana na anannam bhavati iti, nothing becomes uneatable [i.e., anything an animal can eat is food for him].
commentary: There is some difference between the words prāṇa and ana. Prāṇa has a limited meaning. It refers to something characterized by breathing. It breathes, and in order to keep breathing it needs food. It may be a specific kind of food. It may not eat every kind of food. Ana, however, is that which can eat any kind of food. The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (1.5.23) says, ‘The sun rises and sets in prāṇa.’ It also says that the person who knows the true nature of prāṇa is one from whom the sun rises and into whom it sets.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: According to the verse the prāṇa (ana) can consider any food that animals eat as suitable, so for one who knows this nothing is uneatable
The commentary distinguishes prāṇa as limited to breathing and specific food while ana is unrestricted, showing a conceptual difference between breath and universal life‑force
The verse and commentary link knowledge of prāṇa to the cosmic principle that the sun rises and sets in prāṇa, suggesting self‑knowledge aligns one with cosmic order
objection_hooks: If prāṇa is limited to specific food as the commentary says, the claim that knowledge removes all dietary restrictions appears contradictory
The verse only addresses food and does not explicitly state that ana is a universal life‑force, so extending it to a broader metaphysical identity may be unwarranted
The reference to the sun rising and setting in prāṇa may be metaphorical, thus the claim that self‑knowledge aligns one with cosmic order could be overstated

sanskrit: स होवाच किं मे वासो भविष्यतीत्याप इति होचुस्तस्माद्वा एतदशिष्यन्तः पुरस्ताच्चोपरिष्टाच्चाद्भिः परिदधति लम्भुको ह वासो भवत्यनग्नो ह भवति
transliteration: sa hovāca kiṃ me vāso bhaviṣyatītyāpa iti hocustasmādvā etadaśiṣyantaḥ purastāccopariṣṭāccādbhiḥ paridadhati lambhuko ha vāso bhavatyanagno ha bhavati
translation: Prāṇa asked, ‘What will be my covering?’ The organs replied, ‘Water.’ This is why, before and after eating their meals, people cover him with water [i.e., they sip water]. He then becomes covered with a cloth and is no longer naked.
word_meaning: Saḥ ha uvāca, he [prāṇa] said; kim me vāsaḥ bhaviṣyati iti, what will be my covering; āpaḥ iti ha ucuḥ, [the other organs] replied, ‘Water’; tasmāt, that is why; vai aśiṣyantaḥ purastāt ca, people before eating; upariṣṭāt ca, and after [eating]; etat adbhiḥ paridadhati, cover him [prāṇa] with water [i.e., they sip water]; lambhukaḥ ha vāsaḥ bhavati, thereby he becomes covered with a cloth; anagnaḥ ha bhavati, he is no longer naked.
commentary: The water that is sipped before the meal is said to be the lower cloth, and that which is sipped afterwards is said to be the upper cloth.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse uses water and cloth as symbolic coverings for prana, indicating that sipping water before and after meals represents a protective ritual for the life force

According to the commentary the water sipped before a meal functions as a lower cloth and after the meal as an upper cloth, suggesting a prescribed bodily discipline that mirrors covering the subtle self
The dialogue personifies prana and the organs, implying that the subtle self depends on material elements such as water for its concealment
objection_hooks: One could contend that the passage merely records a hygienic habit of drinking water, not a symbolic act, thereby weakening the claim of ritual symbolism
The identification of water with a lower cloth originates in the commentary, not the verse itself, so the metaphor may be extratextual
The personification of prana may be a pedagogical tool rather than an ontological statement about dependence between the subtle self and material elements

sanskrit: तद्धैतत्सत्यकामो जाबालो गोश्रुतये वैयाघ्रपद्यायोक्त्वोवाच यद्यप्येनच्छुष्काय स्थाणवे ब्रूयाज्जायेरन्नेवास्मिञ्छाखाः प्ररोहेयुः पलाशानीति
transliteration: taddhaitatsatyakāmo jābālo gośrutaye vaiyāghrapadyāyoktvovāca yadyapyenacchuṣkāya sthāṇave brūyājjāyerannevāsmiñchākhāḥ praroheyuḥ palāśānīti
translation: Having told this to Vyāghrapada’s son Gośruti, Satyakāma Jābāla said, ‘If a person tells this even to a dry stump [of a tree], branches and leaves will grow off it’.
word_meaning: Tat ha etat, this [teaching]; satyakāmaḥ jābālaḥ, Satyakāma Jābāla; gośrutaye vaiyāhrapadyāya uktva, saying to Vyāghrapada’s son Gośruti; uvāca, he said; yadi api, even if; etat, this [teaching]; śuṣkāya athāṇave brūyāt, a person gives to a dry stump; śākhāḥ praroheyuḥ palāśān, branches and leaves; jāyeran eva asmin, will grow off it.
commentary: This verse is meant to praise the philosophy of prāṇa . Teaching this philosophy to Gośruti, the son of Vyāghrapada, Satyakāma said that this teaching is capable of restoring life to a dead tree. Even if there is only a dry stump of a tree, leaves and branches will begin to sprout from it on hearing this teaching. Imagine then what effect it will produce if it is taught to a living human being.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The teaching of prāṇa described in the verse is said to have the power to restore life to a dead tree, showing that knowledge itself can animate inanimate matter
The commentary claims that if the teaching can cause growth in a dry stump, its effect on a living human will be even greater, implying a profound transformative impact on the human self
By asserting that the same teaching that revives a tree also transforms a person, the verse supports the Advaita view that the individual self is essentially identical with the universal Brahman
objection_hooks: The verse may be using poetic metaphor, so the claim that knowledge literally revives a dead tree could be an exaggeration rather than a factual statement
Assuming that the effect on a living human is greater presumes a simple scaling of impact, but a living being already possesses prāṇa, making the comparative effect uncertain

Linking the growth of external branches on a tree to the identity of the individual self with Brahman may conflate physical regeneration with metaphysical self‑realization, which the text does not explicitly state

sanskrit: अथ यदि महज्जिगमिषेदमावास्यायां दीक्षित्वा पौर्णमास्यां रात्रौ सर्वौषधस्य मन्थं दधिमधुनोरुपमथ्य ज्येष्ठाय श्रेष्ठाय स्वाहेत्यग्नावाज्यस्य हुत्वा मन्थे सम्पातमवनयेत्
transliteration: atha yadi mahajjigamiṣedamāvāsyāyāṃ dīkṣitvā paurṇamāsyāṃ rātrau sarvauṣadhasya manthaṃ dadhimadhunorupamathya jyeṣṭhāya śreṣṭhāya svāhetyagnāvājyasya hutvā manthe sampātamavanayet
translation: Then if anyone wishes to attain greatness, he should first become initiated on a new moon day, and after that, on the night of a full moon, he should prepare a paste of various herbs and mix them together with curd and honey. He should then offer this oblation to the fire saying, ‘Jyeṣṭhāya śreṣṭhāya svāhā, ’ [i.e., Svāhā to the oldest and to the best]. Whatever is left over in the offering spoon he should put into the homa pot.
word_meaning: Atha yadi, then if; mahat jigamiṣet, a person wants to attain greatness; amāvāsyāyām dīkṣitvā, having been initiated on the day of a new moon; paurṇamāsyām rātrau, on the night of the full moon; sarvauṣadhyasya mantham, a paste of various herbs; dadhi -madhunoḥ upamathya, [and] mixing with curd and honey; jyeṣṭhāya śreṣṭhāya svāhā iti, say ‘Svāhā to the oldest and the best’; agnau ājyasya hutvā, offer this as oblations to the fire; manthe sampātam avanayet, put the residue from the offering spoon in the pot.
commentary: If a person wants to attain greatness, it should not be for worldly gain, but for his spiritual benefit. Here, some instructions are given concerning the sacrifices he should perform. Prior to those sacrifices, however, he should practise strict self-control for some time. This whole discipline is intended to take him to a higher world after death, but the over-all result is that he becomes a better person spiritually.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse instructs that attaining greatness requires initiation on a new‑moon day followed by a fire offering of a herb‑curd‑honey paste on the night of a full moon
The commentary states that the purpose of these sacrifices is spiritual improvement rather than worldly gain
The prescribed discipline, including self‑control and the described sacrifices, is intended to secure a higher world for the practitioner after death
objection_hooks: Advaita emphasizes inner knowledge over external ritual, so the claim that ritual alone attains greatness may conflict with the non‑dual emphasis on self‑realisation
The goal of reaching a higher world after death seems provisional, whereas Advaita ultimately rejects any post‑mortem realm in favor of liberation beyond all worlds

Self‑control and ritual sacrifice are presented as sufficient for spiritual progress, yet Advaita asserts that ignorance can only be removed by direct realization of Brahman, not merely by ascetic practice

sanskrit: वसिष्ठाय स्वाहेत्यग्नावाज्यस्य हुत्वा मन्थे सम्पातमवनयेत्प्रतिष्ठायै स्वाहेत्यग्नावाज्यस्य हुत्वा मन्थे सम्पातमवनयेत्सम्पदे स्वाहेत्यग्नावाज्यस्य हुत्वा मन्थे सम्पातमवनयेदायतनाय स्वाहेत्यग्नावाज्यस्य हुत्वा मन्थे सम्पातमवनयेत्
transliteration: vasiṣṭhāya svāhetyagnāvājyasya hutvā manthe sampātamavanayetpratiṣṭhāyai svāhetyagnāvājyasya hutvā manthe sampātamavanayetsampade svāhetyagnāvājyasya hutvā manthe sampātamavanayedāyatanāya svāhetyagnāvājyasya hutvā manthe sampātamavanayet
translation: Saying, ‘Vasiṣṭhāya svāhā ’ [i.e., svāhā to high standing], one should offer the oblation to the fire and then put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot. Saying, ‘Pratiṣṭhāyai svāhā’ [i.e., svāhā to the support], one should offer the oblation to the fire and then put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot. Saying, ‘Sampade svāhā’ [i.e., svāhā to affluence], one should offer the oblation to the fire and then put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot. Saying, ‘Āyatanāya svāhā’ [i.e., svāhā to the abode], one should offer the oblation to the fire and then put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot.
word_meaning: Vasiṣṭhāya svāhā iti, saying ‘Vasiṣṭhāya svāhā’ [i.e., Svāhā to high standing]; agnau ājyasya hutvā, one should offer the oblation to the fire; manthe sampātam avanayet, and put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot; pratiṣṭhāyai svāhā iti, saying ‘Pratiṣṭhāyai svāhā’ [i.e., Svāhā to the support]; agnau ājyasya hutvā, one should offer the oblation to the fire; manthe sampātam avanayet, and put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot; sampade svāhā iti, saying ‘Sampade svāhā’ [i.e., Svāhā to affluence]; agnau ājyasya hutvā, one should offer the oblation to the fire; manthe sampātam avanayet, and put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot; āyatanāya svāhā iti, saying ‘Āyatanāya svāhā’ [i.e., Svāhā to the abode]; agnau ājyasya hutvā, one should offer the oblation to the fire; manthe sampātam avanayet, and put whatever is left over in the offering spoon into the homa pot.
claim_candidates: The verse instructs that after offering the oblation to the fire, any remaining material must be placed in the homa pot, indicating a procedural concern for handling ritual leftovers.

The repeated use of svāhā directed to Vasiṣṭhā, Pratiṣṭhā, Sampade, and Āyatanā shows that a single fire offering can be consecrated to multiple distinct purposes such as high standing, support, affluence, and abode.
The formula svāhā is presented as an essential verbal component of the sacrifice, implying that the utterance itself is integral to the efficacy of the ritual act.
objection_hooks: One could argue that moving the leftover into the homa pot does not demonstrate a concern for completeness, but rather postpones consumption, thereby questioning the claim of procedural completeness.
The claim that svāhā is essential to the sacrifice may be challenged by interpreting svāhā merely as a conventional chant without intrinsic ritual power, which the verse does not explicitly justify.

The identification of Vasiṣṭhā, Pratiṣṭhā, Sampade, and Āyatanā as abstract purposes could be disputed, since these terms might denote specific deities or persons rather than symbolic goals, limiting the claim of multi‑purpose dedication.

sanskrit: अथ प्रतिसृप्याञ्जलौ मन्थमाधाय जपत्यमो नामास्यमा हि ते सर्वमिदं स हि ज्येष्ठः श्रेष्ठो राजाधिपतिः स मा ज्यैष्ठ्यं श्रैष्ठ्यं राज्यमाधिपत्यं गमयत्वहमेवेदं सर्वमसानीति
transliteration: atha pratisṛpyāñjalau manthamādhāya japatyamo nāmāsyamā hi te sarvamidaṃ sa hi jyeṣṭhaḥ śreṣṭho rājādhipatiḥ sa mā jyaiṣṭhyaṃ śraiṣṭhyaṃ rājyamādhipatyaṃ gamayatvahamevedaṃ sarvamasānīti
translation: Then, moving some distance from the fire and holding the homa pot in his hands, he keeps repeating the mantra: ‘You are named ama, because all this rests on you. You are the first, the best, outstanding, and supreme. May I also be the first, the best, outstanding, and supreme. May I be all all this.’
word_meaning: Atha, then; pratisṛpya, moving away [from the fire]; añjalau mantham ādhāya, holding the homa pot in his hands; japati, he keeps repeating; amaḥ nāma asi, you are named ‘ama’; amā hi te, because on you [as prāṇa ]; sarvam idam, all this [rests]; saḥ hi jyeṣṭhaḥ śreṣṭhaḥ, he [i.e., prāṇa, called here ‘ama’] is the oldest and the best; rājā, princely; adhipatiḥ, supreme; saḥ, that [prāṇa]; mā gamayatu, make me; jyaiṣṭhyam śraiṣṭhyam rājyam ādhipatyam, the first, the best, outstanding, and supreme; aham eva idam sarvam asāni iti, may I be all this.
commentary: According to Śaṅkara, ama is another name for prāṇa. The whole world rests on prāṇa. This is why prāṇa is also called ama (all). Prāṇa is the best and the highest. Prāṇa is this world.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse equates prāṇa (ama) with the ultimate reality that sustains all existence
The mantra seeks to identify the speaker’s self with prāṇa, thereby aspiring to become the universal self
Śaṅkara’s commentary states that prāṇa is the supreme principle (rājādhipati) that underlies the world
objection_hooks: The emphasis on ritual chanting may indicate a practical aim rather than a metaphysical claim of identity
Other Upanishadic texts treat prāṇa as a function of Brahman, which could challenge the identification of prāṇa with the infinite Brahman

sanskrit: अथ खल्वेतयर्चा पच्छ आचामति तत्सवितुर्वृणीमह इत्याचामति वयं देवस्य भोजनमित्याचामति श्रेष्ठं सर्वधातममित्याचामति तुरं भगस्य धीमहीति सर्वं पिबति निर्णिज्य कंसं चमसं वा पश्चादग्नेः संविशति चर्मणि वा स्थण्डिले वा वाचंयमोऽप्रसाहः स यदि स्त्रियं पश्येत्समृद्धं कर्मेति विद्यात्
transliteration: atha khalvetayarcā paccha ācāmati tatsaviturvṛṇīmaha ityācāmati vayaṃ devasya bhojanamityācāmati śreṣṭhaṃ sarvadhātamamityācāmati turaṃ bhagasya dhīmahīti sarvaṃ pibati nirṇijya kaṃsaṃ camasaṃ vā paścādagneḥ saṃviśati carmaṇi vā sthaṇḍile vā vācaṃyamo'prasāhaḥ sa yadi striyaṃ paśyetsamṛddhaṃ karmeti vidyāt
translation: Then, while saying this Ṛk mantra foot by foot, he eats some of what is in the homa pot. He says, ‘We pray for that food of the shining deity, ’ and then eats a little of what is in the homa pot. Saying, ‘We eat the food of that deity, ’ he eats a little of what is in the homa pot. Saying, ‘It is the best and the support of all, ’ he eats a little of what is in the homa pot. Saying, ‘We quickly meditate on Bhaga, ’ he eats the rest and washes the vessel or spoon. Then, with his speech and mind under control, he lies down behind the fire, either on the skin of an animal or directly on the sacrificial ground. If he sees a woman in his dream, he knows that the rite has been successful [and that he will succeed in whatever he does].
word_meaning: Atha khalu etayā ṛcā, then while [saying] this Ṛk mantra; pacchaḥ foot by foot; ācāmati, he eats a little [of what is in the homa pot]; tat savituḥ vṛṇīmahe, we pray for that [food] of the shining deity; iti ācāmati, saying this he eats a little [of what is in the homa pot]; vayam devasya bhojanam, we eat the food of the deity; iti ācāmati, saying this he eats a little [of what is in the homa pot]; śreṣṭham sarvadhātamam, it is the best and the support of all; iti ācāmati, saying this he eats a little [of what is in the homa pot]; turam, quickly; bhagasya dhīmahi, we meditate on Bhaga; iti sarvam pibati, saying this he drinks the rest; nirṇijya kaṃsam, [and] washing the vessel [i.e., the homa pot]; vā camasam, or spoon; aprasāhaḥ, with his mind under control; vācaṃyamaḥ, [and] his speech under control; agneḥ paścāt, behind the fire; carmaṇi vā sthaṇḍile vā, on the skin of an animal or directly on the sacrificial ground; saṃviśati, he sleeps; saḥ yadi, if he; striyam paśyet, sees a woman [in his dream]; karma samṛddham, the sacrificial rite is successful; iti vidyāt, he knows that.
commentary: While eating, the sacrificer should repeat the appropriate mantra. That is, he should eat a little and then repeat one foot of the Ṛk mantra; then eat a little more and repeat another foot, and so on. What is this mantra? It is a prayer to Savitā, the sun god. If you repeat this prayer with the appropriate rite, you will become as bright and pure as the sun.
claim_candidates: The verse instructs the sacrificer to eat only a little of the homa offering each time he recites a foot of the Ṛk mantra, linking consumption with sacred speech
The mantra invoked is addressed to Savitā the sun deity and the commentary states that proper recitation will make the sacrificer as bright and pure as the sun
Seeing a woman in a dream after the rite is presented as the indicator that the sacrifice has been successful
objection_hooks: The dream of a woman may be a symbolic cultural motif rather than a reliable empirical test of the rite’s efficacy
The description of becoming bright and pure as the sun may be metaphorical, not a literal ontological transformation

sanskrit: तदेष श्लोको यदा कर्मसु काम्येषु स्त्रियं स्वप्नेषु पश्यन्ति समृद्धिं तत्र जानीयात्तस्मिन्स्वप्ननिदर्शने तस्मिन्स्वप्ननिदर्शने
transliteration: tadeṣa śloko yadā karmasu kāmyeṣu striyaṃ svapneṣu paśyanti samṛddhiṃ tatra jānīyāttasminsvapnanidarśane tasminsvapnanidarśane
translation: Here is a verse in this connection: When one sees a woman in a dream while performing a rite for the fulfillment of a desire, that means it is successful. One can know this from the dream.
word_meaning: Tat eṣaḥ ślokaḥ, here is a verse on the subject; yadā, when; karmasu kāmyeṣu, in a rite for fulfilling a desire; striyam svapneṣu paśyati, one sees a woman in a dream; samṛddhim tatra, there is success; jānīyāt tasmin svapnanidarśane tasmin svapnanidarśane, one knows from seeing this in a dream, from seeing this in a dream.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse states that seeing a woman in a dream during a desire‑fulfilling rite indicates that the rite has been successful
The verse proposes that the dream itself functions as a source of knowledge about the outcome of the ritual, making inner experience a validator of external action
objection_hooks: The symbolism of a woman in a dream may be culturally specific, so the claim that it universally signals success is contestable
If the efficacy of a rite depends on a dream sign, this could be seen as diminishing the intrinsic power of the ritual act itself

sanskrit: श्वेतकेतुर्हारुणेयः पञ्चालानां समितिमेयाय तं ह प्रवाहणो जैवलिरुवाच कुमारानु त्वाशिषत्पितेत्यनु हि भगव इति
transliteration: śvetaketurhāruṇeyaḥ pañcālānāṃ samitimeyāya taṃ ha pravāhaṇo jaivaliruvāca kumārānu tvāśiṣatpitetyanu hi bhagava iti
translation: Once Śvetaketu, the grandson of Aruṇa, went to the court of the Pañcālas . Pravāhaṇa, the son of Jīvala, asked him, ‘Young man, did your father teach you?’ [Śvetaketu replied: ] ‘Yes, revered sir, he did’.
word_meaning: Śvetaketuḥ āruṇeyaḥ ha, once Śvetaketu, the grandson of Aruṇa; pañcālānām samitim eyāya, went to the court of the Pañcālas; tam ha, to him; pravāhaṇaḥ jaivaliḥ uvāca, Pravāhaṇa, the son of Jīvala, said; kumāra, young man; tvā pitā anu-aśiṣat iti, did your father teach you; bhagavaḥ anu hi iti, yes, revered sir, he did.
commentary: Those who want liberation should have a strong spirit of renunciation, and they should know the nature of everything, from Brahmā to a blade of grass. Earlier there was a discussion on prāṇa . Now a discussion on agni, fire, begins. The story of Śvetaketu and his father is being introduced here for that purpose.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The dialogue shows that a disciple’s qualification for liberation is linked to having received instruction from a qualified teacher as indicated by the question about paternal teaching
The commentary states that liberation requires comprehensive knowledge of all aspects of reality, from Brahma to a blade of grass, implying that such knowledge is essential for moksha
The introduction of Śvetaketu’s story marks a shift from discussion of prana to agni, suggesting that mastery of elemental forces forms part of the path to knowledge of Brahman
objection_hooks: The verse only records a polite inquiry about the father’s teaching and does not explicitly prescribe paternal instruction as a prerequisite for liberation
Advaita holds that realization of Brahman transcends intellectual knowledge of particulars, so the claim that exhaustive knowledge of everything is required may be metaphorical rather than literal
The change from prana to agni may be a pedagogical sequence without implying that control of elemental forces is necessary for liberation

sanskrit: वेत्थ यदितोऽधि प्रजाः प्रयन्तीति न भगव इति वेत्थ यथा पुनरावर्तन्त३ इति न भगव इति वेत्थ पथोर्देवयानस्य पितृयाणस्य च व्यावर्तना३ इति न भगव इति
transliteration: vettha yadito'dhi prajāḥ prayantīti na bhagava iti vettha yathā punarāvartanta3 iti na bhagava iti vettha pathordevayānasya pitṛyāṇasya ca vyāvartanā3 iti na bhagava iti
translation: [Pravāhaṇa asked, ] ‘Do you have any idea where, from this world, human beings go in heaven?’ ‘No, sir, I have no idea, ’ [replied Śvetaketu ]. ‘Do you know how they come back?’ ‘No, sir, I don’t.’ ‘Have you any idea where the two paths—the path of the gods and the path of the ancestors—part?’ ‘No, sir, I don’t know’.
word_meaning: Vettha, do you know; yat, where; itaḥ, from this place; adhi, high up; prajāḥ, living beings; prayanti iti, go; na bhagavaḥ iti, no, revered sir, I don’t; vettha, do you know; yathā, the way; punaḥ āvartante iti, they come back; na bhagavaḥ iti, no, revered sir, I don’t; vettha, do you know; pathoḥ, of the two paths; devayānasya, the path of the gods; pitṛyāṇasya ca, and the path of the ancestors; vyāvartanā iti, where they part; na bhagavaḥ iti, no, revered sir, I don’t.
commentary: The path of the gods and the path of the manes both go the same way for some distance. They separate only towards the end. Śvetaketu did not know at what point they separate, nor did he know what path those who are reborn take while coming back to the earth.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse states that the path of the gods and the path of the ancestors travel together for a distance and separate only near the end
Śvetaketu repeatedly answers that he does not know, showing that knowledge of the routes to heaven and back is not readily available to the interlocutor
The dialogue indicates that the details of how beings return to earth after rebirth are unknown, suggesting the mechanics of samsara are presented as obscure
objection_hooks: One could argue that the speaker’s ignorance does not prove a general epistemic limit for all humans, but merely reflects his personal lack of information

The claim about the paths separating only near the end may be contested by other Upanishadic passages that describe distinct divine and ancestral routes throughout, implying the verse may be metaphorical

The assertion that rebirth mechanics are obscure might be challenged by broader Vedantic teachings that provide systematic explanations of samsara, indicating the verse only records the speaker’s ignorance, not an ultimate doctrinal statement

sanskrit: वेत्थ यथासौ लोको न सम्पूर्यत३ इति न भगव इति वेत्थ यथा पञ्चम्यामाहुतावापः पुरुषवचसो भवन्तीति नैव भगव इति
transliteration: vettha yathāsau loko na sampūryata3 iti na bhagava iti vettha yathā pañcamyāmāhutāvāpaḥ puruṣavacaso bhavantīti naiva bhagava iti
translation: [Pravāhaṇa asked, ] ‘Do you know why the other world [the world of the moon] is not filled with people?’ [ Śvetaketu replied, ] ‘No, revered sir, I don’t know.’ ‘Do you know why after the fifth oblation water comes to be called “ puruṣa ” [man]?’ ‘No, sir, I don’t know’.
word_meaning: Vettha yathā, do you know why; asau lokaḥ, that world [i.e., the world of the moon]; na sampūryate iti, is not filled up; na bhagavaḥ iti, no, revered sir; vettha yathā, do you know why; pañcamyām āhutau, after the fifth oblation; āpaḥ puruṣavacasaḥ bhavanti iti, water becomes known as ‘man’; na eva bhagavaḥ iti, no, revered sir, I don’t.
claim_candidates: The verse presents a pedagogical moment where the teacher expects the disciple to grasp the symbolic meaning that water, after the fifth oblation, is designated as ‘purusha’, indicating a deeper identification of ritual elements with the human principle.

The question about the moon world not being filled implies a cosmological view in which different realms possess varying degrees of completeness, suggesting that the lunar realm is considered inherently less populated.
objection_hooks: The text merely reports the teacher’s question about water becoming ‘purusha’; it does not explicitly label this as a symbolic representation, so interpreting it as such may exceed the textual evidence.

The statement that the moon world is not filled could be a rhetorical device rather than a doctrinal claim about cosmology, making the inference of a systematic view of realm completeness questionable.

sanskrit: अथानु किमनुशिष्ठोऽवोचथा यो हीमानि न विद्यात्कथं सोऽनुशिष्टो ब्रुवीतेति स हायस्तः पितुरर्धमेयाय तं होवाचाननुशिष्य वाव किल मा भगवानब्रवीदनु त्वाशिषमिति
transliteration: athānu kimanuśiṣṭho'vocathā yo hīmāni na vidyātkathaṃ so'nuśiṣṭo bruvīteti sa hāyastaḥ piturardhameyāya taṃ hovācānanuśiṣya vāva kila mā bhagavānabravīdanu tvāśiṣamiti
translation: [Pravāhaṇa said: ] ‘Why did you say then, “I have been taught”? How can one who does not know these things say, “I have been taught”?’ Śvetaketu was hurt. He went back to his father and said, ‘You have not really taught ṃe, yet you said, “I have taught you.”’.
word_meaning: Atha kim anuśiṣṭaḥ anu avocathāḥ, then why did you say, ‘I have been taught’; yaḥ hi imāni na vidyāt, for he who does not know these things; katham saḥ anuśiṣṭaḥ bruvīta iti, how can he say ‘I have been taught’; saḥ, he [Śvetaketu]; ha āyastaḥ, was hurt; pituḥ ardham eyāya, went back to his father’s place; tam ha uvāca, he said to him [i.e., to his father]; mā ananuśiṣya vāva kila, without really teaching me; bhagavān, revered sir; abravīt tvā anu aśiṣam iti, you said, ‘I have taught you.’
claim_candidates: The verse states that a person who lacks knowledge of a subject cannot legitimately claim to have been taught
The passage implies that authentic teaching requires the actual transmission of knowledge, not merely the assertion of having taught
Śvetaketu’s feeling of hurt suggests that honesty in the guru‑shishya relationship is essential for its integrity
objection_hooks: One could argue that the teacher’s claim may refer to imparting knowledge that will be understood later, so immediate knowledge is not a necessary condition for a valid teaching
The text may place the onus on the student to attain understanding, thereby challenging the view that the teacher’s credibility depends on the disciple’s comprehension
The mention of emotional hurt might be a narrative element rather than a doctrinal statement, weakening the inference that the verse prescribes a principle of honesty in instruction

sanskrit: पञ्च मा राजन्यबन्धुः प्रश्नानप्राक्षीत्तेषां नैकंचनाशकं विवक्तुमिति स होवाच यथा मा त्वं तदैतानवदो यथाहमेषां नैकंचन वेद यद्यहमिमानवेदिष्यं कथं ते नावक्ष्यमिति
transliteration: pañca mā rājanyabandhuḥ praśnānaprākṣītteṣāṃ naikaṃcanāśakaṃ vivaktumiti sa hovāca yathā mā tvaṃ tadaitānavado yathāhameṣāṃ naikaṃcana veda yadyahamimānavediṣyaṃ kathaṃ te nāvakṣyamiti
translation: [ Śvetaketu said: ] ‘That friend of the princes put five questions to me. I was not able to answer a single one of them.’ [He then told his father the five questions. After pondering over them for some time, his father] said: ‘Those questions you told me about on your return from the court—I am not able to answer even one of them. If I knew the answers, why should I have not told you?’.
word_meaning: Rājanyabandhuḥ, the friend of princes; pañca praśnān mā aprākṣīt, asked me five questions; teṣām na ekañcana aśakam vivaktum iti, I was not able to answer a single one of them; saḥ ha uvāca, he [his father] said; yathā mā tvam tadā, [on your return from the court] as you were then; etān, all these [questions]; avadaḥ, you told [me]; yathā, so; aham eṣām na ekañcana veda, I do not know even one of them; yadi aham imān avediṣyam, if I knew them; katham te na avakṣyam iti, why should I not have told you?
commentary: The word rājanyabandhuḥ, a friend of kings or princes, is not to be taken literally. Here the word is used sarcastically. Actually, the man is a rogue.
claim_candidates: The verse shows that even a learned elder admits ignorance of the five questions, indicating that intellectual learning alone may be insufficient for ultimate understanding
The commentary’s note that rājanyabandhuḥ is used sarcastically suggests the passage critiques false pretensions of friendship and authority
The rhetorical question ‘If I knew them, why should I not have told you?’ implies a moral expectation that possessed knowledge ought to be shared
objection_hooks: One might argue the father’s ignorance reflects the esoteric nature of the questions rather than a general limit of intellectual learning
The sarcastic label could be a literary flourish without a broader critique of authority, weakening the claim of moral condemnation
The passage could be read as expressing humility rather than prescribing a duty to disclose knowledge, challenging the claim of an ethical imperative to share

sanskrit: स ह गौतमो राज्ञोऽर्धमेयाय तस्मै ह प्राप्तायार्हां चकार स ह प्रातः सभाग उदेयाय तं होवाच मानुषस्य भगवन्गौतम वित्तस्य वरं वृणीथा इति स होवाच तवैव राजन्मानुषं वित्तं यामेव कुमारस्यान्ते वाचमभाषथास्तामेव मे ब्रूहीति स ह कृच्छ्री बभूव
transliteration: sa ha gautamo rājño'rdhameyāya tasmai ha prāptāyārhāṃ cakāra sa ha prātaḥ sabhāga udeyāya taṃ hovāca mānuṣasya bhagavangautama vittasya varaṃ vṛṇīthā iti sa hovāca tavaiva rājanmānuṣaṃ vittaṃ yāmeva kumārasyānte vācamabhāṣathāstāmeva me brūhīti sa ha kṛcchrī babhūva
translation: Gautama then went to the king’s palace. On his arrival, the king welcomed him respectfully. The next morning, when the king was in his court, Gautama went there to meet him. The king said to him, ‘Revered Gautama, ask for a boon from me—anything a person might wish for.’ Gautama replied: ‘Let those things be with you. Please tell me whatever you said to my son.’ Hearing this, the king turned pale.
word_meaning: Saḥ ha gautamaḥ, then Gautama; rājñaḥ ardham eyāya, went to the king’s palace; prāptāya, when he arrived; tasmai ha arhām cakāra, the king respectfully welcomed him; saḥ, he [Gautama]; ha prātaḥ, the next morning; sabhāge udeyāya, went to see the king in the court; tam ha uvāca, he [the king] said to him [Gautama]; bhagavan gautama, revered Gautama; mānuṣasya vittasya varam vṛṇīthāḥ iti, ask for any precious thing a person may wish for as a boon; saḥ ha uvāca, he [Gautama] said; rājan, O king; tava eva mānuṣam vittam, let the human wealth be yours; yām eva kumārasya ante vācam abhāṣathāḥ, that which you said to my son; tām eva, the same; me brūhi iti, tell me; saḥ ha kṛcchrī babhūva, he [the king] was very depressed.
commentary: It was against the custom for a kṣatriya, a king, to teach a brāhmin. This is why the king became depressed.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
claim_candidates: The verse shows that a king’s duty includes respecting the Brahmin’s authority and that violating this custom leads to personal distress
Gautama’s request that the king keep the human wealth and repeat what was said to his son indicates that true wealth is moral instruction rather than material gain
The king’s power to grant boons is limited by dharmic norms, demonstrating that social hierarchy is subordinate to scriptural duty
objection_hooks: One could argue that the king’s depression stems from personal shame or fear of losing prestige rather than a dharmic violation
Gautama’s wording may be read as a demand for material wealth, contradicting the claim that he prioritises moral instruction
Examples in other texts where kings command Brahmins suggest that hierarchy can override dharma, challenging the claim that dharma always limits royal authority

sanskrit: तं ह चिरं वसेत्याज्ञापयांचकार तं होवाच यथा मा त्वं गौतमावदो यथेयं न प्राक्त्वत्तः पुरा विद्या ब्राह्मणान्गच्छति तस्मादु सर्वेषु लोकेषु क्षत्रस्यैव प्रशासनमभूदिति तस्मै होवाच
transliteration: taṃ ha ciraṃ vasetyājñāpayāṃcakāra taṃ hovāca yathā mā tvaṃ gautamāvado yatheyaṃ na prāktvattaḥ purā vidyā brāhmaṇāngacchati tasmādu sarveṣu lokeṣu kṣatrasyaiva praśāsanamabhūditi tasmai hovāca
translation: The king then issued orders that Gautama should stay with him for a long time [as a brahmacari . Gautama did that. One day] the king said to him: ‘O Gautama, regarding the matter which you asked about, no brāhmin before you had access to this knowledge. This is why in the past, in all the worlds, it was only the kṣatriyas who had the right to impart this knowledge.’ Having said this, he proceeded to teach Gautama.
word_meaning: Tam, to him [to Gautama]; ciram vasa ha iti, stay for a long time; ājñāpayāñcakāra, he issued orders; tam ha uvāca, he said to him [to Gautama]; gautama, O Gautama; yathā mā tvam āvadaḥ, as you said to me; yathā iyam vidyā, because this knowledge; prāk tvattaḥ, before you; brāhmaṇān na gacchati, did not go to the brāhmins; purā, in the past; tasmāt u, that is why; sarveṣu lokeṣu, in all the worlds; kṣatrasya eva praśāsanam abhūt iti, only kṣatriyas had the right to teach it; tasmai ha uvāca, he said to him.
claim_candidates: The verse states that historically only kṣatriyas were authorized to impart this knowledge
The verse indicates that no brāhmin before Gautama had access to this knowledge, showing a caste-based exclusion
The verse claims that this restriction on teaching applied in all worlds in the past
objection_hooks: Other Upanishadic texts portray Brahmins as primary teachers of Brahman knowledge, which challenges the universality of the kṣatriya‑only claim
Historical records of Brahmin scholars teaching similar doctrines suggest the statement about Brahmins lacking prior access may be rhetorical rather than factual
The phrase ‘in all the worlds’ may be metaphorical, emphasizing royal authority, and not a literal ontological rule

sanskrit: इति तृतीयः खण्डः ॥
transliteration: asau vāva loko gautamāgnistasyāditya eva samidraśmayo dhūmo'hararciścandramā aṅgārā nakṣatrāṇi visphuliṅgāḥ
translation: O Gautama, heaven is the [sacrificial] fire; the sun is its fuel; the rays are the smoke; day is the flame; the moon is the embers; and the stars are the sparks.
word_meaning: Gautama, O Gautama; asau vāva lokaḥ agniḥ, that world there [i.e., heaven] is the fire; ādityaḥ eva tasya samit, the sun is its fuel; raśmayaḥ dhūmaḥ, the rays are the smoke; ahaḥ arciḥ, the day is the flame; candramāḥ aṅgārāḥ, the moon is the embers; nakṣatrāṇi visphuliṅgāḥ, the stars are the sparks.
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse equates the heavenly realm (loka) with fire, indicating fire as the essential nature of the cosmos
The sun is described as the fuel of this cosmic fire, showing the sun as the sustaining energy of the universe
The moon and stars are portrayed as embers and sparks, implying that they are subordinate manifestations of the primary fire
objection_hooks: Equating heaven with fire may oversimplify a multidimensional metaphysical realm, conflicting with Upanishadic descriptions of Brahman as beyond all qualities
Identifying the sun merely as fuel could be challenged by the view that the sun itself is a manifestation of Brahman, making the relationship reciprocal rather than hierarchical
Describing the moon and stars as mere embers and sparks may ignore other texts that grant them independent symbolic significance, suggesting a more complex hierarchy

sanskrit: तस्मिन्नेतस्मिन्नग्नौ देवाः श्रद्धां जुह्वति तस्या अहुतेः सोमो राजा संभवति
transliteration: tasminnetasminnagnau devāḥ śraddhāṃ juhvati tasyā ahuteḥ somo rājā saṃbhavati
translation: The gods [i.e., the organs of the sacrificer] offer water as a token of respect to the fire [heaven]. Out of that oblation appears King Soma [the shining moon].
word_meaning: Tasmin etasinin agnau, in that fire [which is also heaven]; devāḥ, the gods [i.e., the organs of the sacrificer]; śraddhām juhvati, offer faith [as water] as the oblation; tasyāḥ āhuteḥ, out of that oblation; rājā somaḥ, King Soma [i.e., the shining moon]; sambhavati, appears.
claim_candidates: The sacrificer's internal organs are metaphorically called devāḥ and they offer faith as water to the fire
The emergence of King Soma, identified as the shining moon, is described as occurring out of the oblation
The verse links the act of offering water with the expression of respect, presenting faith (śraddhā) as a tangible offering
objection_hooks: One could argue that devāḥ may refer to external deities rather than the sacrificer's organs, challenging the metaphorical reading
The causal link between the offering and the appearance of the moon might be seen as mythic narrative rather than a literal metaphysical principle
The text does not explain why water specifically represents respect, leaving the symbolism open to alternative interpretations

sanskrit: पर्जन्यो वाव गौतमाग्निस्तस्य वायुरेव समिदभ्रं धूमो विद्युदर्चिरशनिरङ्गाराह्रादनयो विस्फुलिङ्गाः
transliteration: parjanyo vāva gautamāgnistasya vāyureva samidabhraṃ dhūmo vidyudarciraśaniraṅgārāhrādanayo visphuliṅgāḥ
translation: O Gautama, the god of rain is the [sacrificial] fire; air is its fuel; the cloud is the smoke; lightning is the flame; the thunderbolt is the embers; and thunder is the sparks.
word_meaning: Gautama, O Gautama; parjanyaḥ vāva agniḥ, the god of rain is the fire; vāyuḥ eva tasya samit, air is its fuel; abhram dhūmaḥ, the cloud is the smoke; vidyut arciḥ, lightning is the flame; aśaniḥ aṅgārāḥ, the thunderbolt is the embers; hrādanayaḥ visphuliṅgāḥ, thunder is the sparks.
concept_tags: Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse equates rain, fire, air, cloud, lightning, and thunder as symbolic equivalents, indicating a single underlying cosmic principle
By identifying the rain god with the sacrificial fire, the commentary suggests that ritual fire embodies broader natural forces
The description of air as fuel, cloud as smoke, and thunder as sparks portrays an interdependent functioning of elemental phenomena
objection_hooks: The identification may be purely poetic, not asserting an ontological unity of the elements
Listing correspondences does not necessarily imply a systematic causal interdependence among the elements
Describing sensory phenomena does not automatically entail that the verse claims sensory perception reveals deeper metaphysical reality

sanskrit: तस्मिन्नेतस्मिन्नग्नौ देवाः सोमं राजानं जुह्वति तस्या आहुतेर्वर्षं संभवति
transliteration: tasminnetasminnagnau devāḥ somaṃ rājānaṃ juhvati tasyā āhutervarṣaṃ saṃbhavati
translation: The gods offer King Soma [the shining moon] as the oblation to the fire. Out of that oblation appears rain.
word_meaning: Tasmin etasmin agnau, in that fire; devāḥ, the gods; somam rājānam juhvati, offer King Soma [the shining moon] as the oblation; tasyāḥ āhuteḥ, out of that oblation; varṣam, rain; sambhavati, appears.
claim_candidates: The verse presents a direct causal link between the ritual offering of Soma to fire and the emergence of rain, indicating that Vedic sacrifice is understood to affect natural phenomena
Soma is described as the shining moon, showing that celestial bodies serve as symbolic representations of divine power in sacrificial rites
Fire acts as a transformative medium that converts the oblation into rain, reflecting the belief that fire mediates between the divine offering and material manifestation
objection_hooks: The connection between sacrifice and rain may be metaphorical rather than a literal causal mechanism, undermining the claim of direct influence
Soma could be interpreted as a deity or a ritual drink rather than the moon, challenging the assertion that the verse employs celestial symbolism

sanskrit: पृथिवी वाव गौतमाग्निस्तस्याः संवत्सर एव समिदाकाशो धूमो रात्रिरर्चिर्दिशोऽङ्गारा अवान्तरदिशो विस्फुलिङ्गाः
transliteration: pṛthivī vāva gautamāgnistasyāḥ saṃvatsara eva samidākāśo dhūmo rātrirarcirdiśo'ṅgārā avāntaradiśo visphuliṅgāḥ
translation: O Gautama, the earth is the [sacrificial] fire; the year is its fuel; the sky is the smoke; night is the flame; the quarters are the embers; and the intermediate directions are the sparks.
word_meaning: Gautama, O Gautama; pṛthivī vāva agniḥ, earth is the fire; saṃvatsaraḥ eva tasyāḥ samit, the year is its fuel; ākāśaḥ dhūmaḥ, the sky is the smoke; rātriḥ arciḥ, night is the flame; diśaḥ aṅgārāḥ, the quarters are the embers; avāṅtaradiśaḥ visphuliṅgāḥ, the intermediate directions are the sparks.
claim_candidates: The verse portrays the entire cosmos as a single sacrificial fire, indicating that earth, sky, night, and directional points are not separate realities but parts of one unified fire.
By describing the year as the fuel of this fire, the text implies that temporal cycles themselves sustain the underlying reality of the sacrifice.
The mapping of natural elements to fire components suggests a symbolic identification of material phenomena with the inner fire of Brahman.
objection_hooks: The passage may be a poetic ritual instruction rather than an ontological statement about Brahman, so the claim of a unified fire could be overreaching.
Equating the sky with smoke might reflect a functional analogy for ritual visualization, not a metaphysical assertion of unity.
The text never mentions Brahman explicitly; inferring that the fire represents Brahman could be an interpretive addition beyond the literal translation.

sanskrit: तस्मिन्नेतस्मिन्नग्नौ देवा वर्षं जुह्वति तस्या आहुतेरन्नं संभवति
transliteration: tasminnetasminnagnau devā varṣaṃ juhvati tasyā āhuterannaṃ saṃbhavati
translation: The gods offer rain as the oblation to the fire. Out of that oblation appears food.
word_meaning: Tasmin etasmin agnau, in that fire; devāḥ, the gods; varṣam juhvati, offer rain as the oblation; tasyāḥ āhuteḥ, out of that oblation; annam, food; sambhavati, appears.
claim_candidates: The verse presents rain as a divine oblation to the fire, showing that natural phenomena serve as sacrificial offerings in Vedic ritual
Out of the fire’s oblation of rain, food appears, indicating a causal link between the ritual fire, divine rain, and material nourishment
The description of gods offering rain implies that deities actively participate in the sacrificial economy, blurring the line between divine agency and ritual act
objection_hooks: The passage may be poetic narrative rather than a doctrinal statement, so interpreting rain as a literal sacrificial offering could overstate its theological import
The emergence of food might be metaphorical, not a literal causal claim that fire transforms rain into sustenance, challenging the material causality asserted
Alternative readings could see the gods as recipients rather than providers of rain, questioning the claim that deities actively offer rain as an oblation

sanskrit: पुरुषो वाव गौतमाग्निस्तस्य वागेव समित्प्राणो धूमो जिह्वार्चिश्चक्षुरङ्गाराः श्रोत्रं विस्फुलिङ्गाः
transliteration: puruṣo vāva gautamāgnistasya vāgeva samitprāṇo dhūmo jihvārciścakṣuraṅgārāḥ śrotraṃ visphuliṅgāḥ
translation: O Gautama, man is the [sacrificial] fire; speech is his fuel; prāṇa is the smoke; the tongue is the flame; the eyes are the embers; and the ears are the sparks.
word_meaning: Gautama, O Gautama; puruṣaḥ vāva agniḥ, man is the fire; vāk eva tasya samit, speech is his fuel; prāṇaḥ dhūmaḥ, prāṇa is the smoke; jihvā arciḥ, the tongue is the flame; cakṣuḥ aṅgārāḥ, the eyes are the embers; śrotram visphuliṅgāḥ, the ears are the sparks.
commentary: Speech is said to be the fuel because it is the source of strength. A person who cannot speak is helpless. Prāṇa is the smoke because the breath comes out of the mouth just as smoke comes out of fire. The tongue is the flame because both are red. The eyes are the embers because both appear to be shining. And the ears are the sparks because both spread out and cover a large area.
claim_candidates: The verse equates the human being to a sacrificial fire, presenting the body as a ritual instrument
Speech is described as the fuel of this fire, implying that verbal expression sustains spiritual activity
The sensory organs are likened to flame, embers, and sparks, indicating that perception manifests the inner fire
objection_hooks: Equating man with fire may reduce the immutable Atman to transient bodily processes, conflicting with Advaita's notion of the self beyond physical functions
If speech is essential fuel, the practice of silent meditation (mauna) appears contradictory, suggesting speech may not be indispensable for spiritual sustenance
Identifying prāṇa with smoke treats the life‑force as a fleeting by‑product, which seems at odds with the view of prāṇa as the enduring sustaining principle

sanskrit: तस्मिन्नेतस्मिन्नग्नौ देवा अन्नं जुह्वति तस्या आहुते रेतः सम्भवति
transliteration: tasminnetasminnagnau devā annaṃ juhvati tasyā āhute retaḥ sambhavati
translation: The gods offer food as the oblation to the fire. Out of that oblation appears semen.
word_meaning: Tasmin etasmin agnau, in that fire; devāḥ, the gods; annam juhvati, offer food as the oblation; tasyāḥ āhuteḥ, out of that oblation; retaḥ, semen; sambhavati, appears.
claim_candidates: The verse depicts the fire ritual as transforming offered food into semen, indicating a symbolic conversion of material offering into vital life force
The gods are described as the agents who offer food to the fire, suggesting that sacrificial activity is rooted in divine action
The appearance of semen from the oblation implies that the sacrificial fire is conceived as a source of creative potency
objection_hooks: The identification of semen with a symbolic life force may be challenged as a literal reading that overlooks metaphorical nuance
The assertion that the gods’ participation defines the divine nature of sacrifice can be disputed by viewing the passage as mythic narrative rather than doctrinal proof

The claim that fire generates creative potency is not explicitly stated; the verse merely notes the appearance of semen, leaving open alternative interpretations such as purity rather than generative power

sanskrit: योषा वाव गौतमाग्निस्तस्या उपस्थ एव समिद्यदुपमन्त्रयते स धूमो योनिरर्चिर्यदन्तः करोति तेऽङ्गारा अभिनन्दा विस्फुलिङ्गाः
transliteration: yoṣā vāva gautamāgnistasyā upastha eva samidyadupamantrayate sa dhūmo yonirarciryadantaḥ karoti te'ṅgārā abhinandā visphuliṅgāḥ
translation: 1-2. O Gautama, woman is the [sacrificial] fire... The gods offer semen as the oblation to the fire. Out of that oblation appears the foetus.
word_meaning: nothing, here.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
Brahman as Life, Joy, and the Void
Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse portrays woman as the sacrificial fire that transforms divine semen into a foetus, indicating that the creative process is framed as a ritual sacrifice
Conception is described as a divine offering, suggesting that gods actively participate in human procreation through the oblation of semen
The emergence of the foetus from the fire symbolizes that life originates from the union of feminine fire and masculine divine essence, reflecting a cosmological principle of creation
objection_hooks: The metaphor of fire and offering may be poetic rather than a literal theological assertion, weakening the claim of divine participation
Equating procreation with sacrifice could be a cultural allegory that does not necessarily imply that Brahman is the source of all biological processes
The verse’s fragmentary and possibly corrupted text makes it uncertain whether the intended meaning supports these philosophical interpretations

sanskrit: इति तु पञ्चम्यामाहुतावापः पुरुषवचसो भवन्तीति स उल्बावृतो गर्भो दश वा नव वा मासानन्तः शयित्वा यावद्वाथ जायते
transliteration: iti tu pañcamyāmāhutāvāpaḥ puruṣavacaso bhavantīti sa ulbāvṛto garbho daśa vā nava vā māsānantaḥ śayitvā yāvadvātha jāyate
translation: Thus, after the fifth oblation, water becomes known as ‘man.’ The foetus lies within the mother’s womb, covered with membrane, for about nine or ten months, and then it is born.
word_meaning: Iti tu, thus [the fifth question has been answered]; pañcamyām āhutau, after the fifth oblation; āpaḥ, water; puruṣavacasaḥ bhavanti iti, becomes known as ‘man’; ulbāvṛtaḥ, covered with membrane; saḥ garbhaḥ, that foetus; daśa vā nava vā māsān, for nine or ten months; yāvat vā, or so; antaḥ śayitvā, lying inside [the mother’s womb]; atha jāyate, is then born.
commentary: If all five oblations have been offered, then the water of the first oblation becomes a puruṣa, a human being. It lies in its mother’s womb for nine or ten months and is then born as a child. This is the answer to the last of the five questions that Pravāhaṇa asked Śvetaketu . By answering the last question first, he could answer the others more easily.
claim_candidates: The fifth oblation transforms water into a human being, as the text says water becomes known as man
The foetus remains covered by a membrane for nine or ten months before birth, as described in the verse
The commentary indicates that answering the final question first makes the earlier questions easier to resolve
objection_hooks: The transformation of water into a human may be symbolic rather than literal, challenging a straightforward reading of the claim
Empirical data show variability in gestation length, which could dispute the universal nine‑or‑ten‑month period asserted
Answering the last question first does not necessarily guarantee simplification of earlier questions, so the pedagogical claim may be questionable

sanskrit: स जातो यावदायुषं जीवति तं प्रेतं दिष्टमितोऽग्नय एव हरन्ति यत एवेतो यतः संभूतो भवति
transliteration: sa jāto yāvadāyuṣaṃ jīvati taṃ pretaṃ diṣṭamito'gnaya eva haranti yata eveto yataḥ saṃbhūto bhavati
translation: When a person is born, he lives as long as he is destined to live. Then, when he dies as ordained, they [his sons or disciples] take him from his home to the fire from which he came. It is that same fire from which he was born [and to which he owes his birth].
word_meaning: Saḥ jātaḥ, [when] he is born; yāvat-āyuṣam jīvati, he lives as long as he is destined; diṣṭam pretam, dying as ordained; itaḥ, from here [his home]; taw agnaye eva haranti, they take him to the fire; yataḥ eva itaḥ, from which he came here; yataḥ sambhūtaḥ bhavati, that from which he was born.
commentary: From each oblation, something is born that is offered as the next oblation in the fire. The last thing born is a human being. This is why it is said that a human being is born of fire. In the end, when that person dies, his body also is offered in that same fire.
claim_candidates: The verse states that fire is both the origin and the destination of a human life, showing a cyclical link between birth and death
The commentary explains that each oblation gives rise to the next, ending with the human being as the final product of the fire ritual
The text declares that a person lives only as long as he is destined to live, implying a predetermined lifespan
objection_hooks: One could object that describing fire as the literal source of birth conflicts with biological facts about human reproduction
The idea that the human is the last oblation may be interpreted metaphorically rather than as a literal sequence of sacrifices
The claim of a predetermined lifespan can be challenged by philosophical views that emphasize free will and ethical action shaping one’s destiny

sanskrit: तद्य इत्थं विदुः। ये चेमेऽरण्ये श्रद्धा तप इत्युपासते तेऽर्चिषमभिसंभवन्त्यर्चिषोऽहरह्न आपूर्यमाणपक्षमापूर्यमाणपक्षाद्यान्षडुदङ्ङेति मासांस्तान्
transliteration: tadya itthaṃ viduḥ| ye ceme'raṇye śraddhā tapa ityupāsate te'rciṣamabhisaṃbhavantyarciṣo'harahna āpūryamāṇapakṣamāpūryamāṇapakṣādyānṣaḍudaṅṅeti māsāṃstān
translation: 1-2. Those who know this [about the five fires], and those who live in the forest practising austerities with faith—they go after death to the world of light. From the world of light they go to the world of day; from the world of day to the world of the bright fortnight; from the world of the bright fortnight to the six months when the sun moves northward; from there they go to the year; from the year to the sun; from the sun to the moon; and from the moon to lightning. There someone, not human, receives them and leads them to brahmaloka . This is the path of the gods.
