translation: When we eat food, it divides itself into three parts. The grossest part of it becomes excreta; that which is less gross becomes our flesh; and the finest part becomes our mind.
word_meaning: Annam aśitam, when food is eaten; tredhā, in three ways; vidhīyate, is divided; tasya, of it; yaḥ sthaviṣṭhaḥ, that which is the grossest; dhātuḥ, part; tat purīṣam bhavati, that becomes excreta; yaḥ madhyamaḥ, that which is less gross [lit., middle]; tat māṃsam, that [becomes] flesh; yaḥ aṇiṣṭhaḥ, that which is the subtlest; tat manaḥ, that [becomes] the mind.
commentary: When we eat, the food nourishes the body in different ways. How? First, the grossest part of the food is rejected by the body. Then that which is less gross, a bit finer, becomes our flesh. And finally the finest part becomes the mind. According to Vedānta, the mind is material. It functions through the nervous system, which is called hitā . How does this food get transformed into the mind? Śaṅkara says that the subtlest part of the food becomes blood and enters into the heart. From there it goes through the veins and nerves, nourishing the mind. The term mind refers here to the phenomena of our thinking, feeling, willing, etc., plus the aggregate of our sense organs—our seeing, hearing, and so forth. Suppose you do not eat anything. What happens? Slowly you will feel that your mind is becoming more and more feeble. Your memory starts failing, and you cannot concentrate. The finest part of your system is affected. Then the body begins to shrink. You become thinner and thinner, and finally you die. Food nourishes all these. This is why Vedānta says that the mind is material.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The subtlest part of ingested food becomes the mind, showing that the mind is a material entity derived from physical nourishment
The threefold division of food illustrates a hierarchical transformation from gross excreta to flesh to mind, indicating a continuum between bodily and mental functions
Deprivation of food first weakens the mind before the body, suggesting the mind’s greater dependence on subtle nourishment
objection_hooks: Advaita asserts that the mind is ultimately an illusion and not a truly material entity, contradicting the claim of its material derivation from food
If the mind is material and dependent on food, this seems incompatible with the doctrine that the true Self (Atman) is independent of physical processes

sanskrit: आपः पीतास्त्रेधा विधीयन्ते तासां यः स्थविष्ठो धातुस्तन्मूत्रं भवति यो मध्यमस्तल्लोहितं योऽणिष्ठः स प्राणः
transliteration: āpaḥ pītāstredhā vidhīyante tāsāṃ yaḥ sthaviṣṭho dhātustanmūtraṃ bhavati yo madhyamastallohitaṃ yo'ṇiṣṭhaḥ sa prāṇaḥ
translation: When we drink water, it becomes divided in three parts. The grossest part of it becomes urine; that which is less gross becomes blood; and the finest part becomes prāṇa, the vital force.
word_meaning: Āpaḥ, water; pītāḥ, when drunk; tredhā, in three ways; vidhīyante, is divided; tāsām, of it; yaḥ sthaviṣṭaḥ dhātuḥ, that which is the grossest part; tat mūtram bhavati, that becomes urine; yaḥ madhyamaḥ, that which is less gross; tat lohitam, that [becomes] blood; yaḥ aṇiṣṭhaḥ, that which is the finest part; saḥ prāṇaḥ, that is prāṇa.
commentary: Water also has a threefold function. When we drink water, the grossest part of it flushes our whole system and at last passes out of the body as urine. Then that which is less gross becomes blood. If I do not drink water, the blood would clot and there would be no more blood supply. Then the finest aspect becomes prāṇa, the vital force. Without water, we could not breathe or live.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse states that ingested water is divided into three bodily substances: urine, blood, and prāṇa
The commentary explains that the finest part of water becomes prāṇa, showing that prāṇa originates from the most subtle aspect of physical matter
According to the commentary, without water blood would clot and prāṇa could not be sustained, indicating water is essential for life and breath
The threefold division of water reflects a hierarchy from the grossest (urine) to the finest (prāṇa) bodily functions
objection_hooks: One may object that equating prāṇa with the most subtle part of water mixes physical description with metaphysical concept, which lacks empirical verification
The claim that water is necessary for blood circulation can be challenged because blood composition is not solely water, suggesting the commentary simplifies physiological facts
The hierarchical ordering of urine as the grossest part could be questioned as it may reflect functional sequence rather than ontological priority
Some Upanishadic passages treat prāṇa as independent of water, disputing the view that prāṇa derives from water

sanskrit: तेजोऽशितं त्रेधा विधीयते तस्य यः स्थविष्ठो धातुस्तदस्थि भवति यो मध्यमः स मज्जा योऽणिष्ठः सा वाक्
transliteration: tejo'śitaṃ tredhā vidhīyate tasya yaḥ sthaviṣṭho dhātustadasthi bhavati yo madhyamaḥ sa majjā yo'ṇiṣṭhaḥ sā vāk
translation: When we eat fire [i.e., butter, oil, etc.], it divides itself into three parts. The grossest part of it becomes bone; that which is less gross becomes marrow; and the subtlest part becomes speech.
word_meaning: Tejaḥ aśitam, when fire [i.e., oil, butter, etc., which produce energy] is eaten; tredhā, in three ways; vidhīyāte, is divided; tasya, of it; yaḥ sthaviṣṭhaḥ, that which is the grossest; dhātuḥ, part; tat asthi bhavati, that becomes bone; yaḥ madhyamaḥ, that which is midway; saḥ majjā, that [becomes] marrow; yaḥ aṇiṣṭhaḥ, that which is the subtlest; sā vāk, that is speech.
commentary: Tejas literally means ‘fire.’ The idea here is that if you eat butter or fatty substances such as oil, it is like eating fire. Why? Because butter and oil are sources of tejas—that is, they provide us energy and vitality. These substances, when eaten, also become transformed into three different things. That which is gross becomes our bones; that which is between the gross and the subtle becomes our marrow; and that which is subtle becomes our speech. If our organ of speech is supported by the right kind of food, we are able to speak logically and articulately. The Upaniṣad is showing us here how we depend on these elements—earth, water, and fire. We. are able to think, speak, and move about all because of the support we get from them. But all these things depend on Brahman, pure Spirit. If you think of the way nature functions, and how the planets rotate in their orbits, and so forth, you are extremely impressed. Everything is so well organized. But we must remember, it is Brahman that is manifesting as nature, as the planets, as you and me and each individual—as even the tiny insects. The idea is that the One has become the many. Let us not be deluded by seeing the many.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse states that eating fire-like substances such as butter or oil is transformed into three bodily parts—bone, marrow, and speech—showing a direct link between diet and the subtle faculty of speech
The commentary interprets this transformation as evidence that material elements support cognitive functions, indicating that mind and speech depend on physical nourishment
Together the verse and commentary imply that these bodily transformations ultimately rely on Brahman, suggesting that Brahman is the source of both gross and subtle aspects of human existence
objection_hooks: One could object that equating speech with the subtlest part of fire oversimplifies the complex relationship between nutrition and linguistic ability, questioning the direct causal claim
A materialist might reject the idea that bodily constituents ultimately depend on Brahman, arguing that physiological processes can be explained without invoking a metaphysical principle
The identification of speech, a temporal and conditioned phenomenon, with the unconditioned Brahman may be challenged as conflating the transient with the eternal

sanskrit: अन्नमयंहि सोम्य मनः आपोमयः प्राणस्तेजोमयी वागिति भूय एव मा भगवान्विज्ञापयत्विति तथा सोम्येति होवाच
transliteration: annamayaṃhi somya manaḥ āpomayaḥ prāṇastejomayī vāgiti bhūya eva mā bhagavānvijñāpayatviti tathā somyeti hovāca
translation: ‘O Somya, the mind is nourished by food, prāṇa by water, and speech by fire.’ [ Śvetaketu then said, ] ‘Sir, will you please explain this to me again?’ ‘Yes, Somya, I will explain again, ’ replied his father.
word_meaning: Somya, O Somya; manaḥ hi annamayam, the mind is nourished by food; prāṇaḥ āpomayaḥ, the vital force is nourished by water; vāk tejomayī iti, speech is nourished by fire; bhagavān, sir; bhūyaḥ eva, again; mā, to me; vijñāpayatu iti, will you please explain; tathā, so be it; somya iti, O Somya; ha uvāca, he [the father] said.
commentary: In the Vedānta philosophy there are said to be five elements. The first two— ākāśa (space, or ether) and vāyu (air)—are not visible to us. The other three— pṛthivī (earth—here referred to as annam, food), āpa (water), and agni, or tejas (fire, or energy)—are visible, and this is why they have been discussed here. These three elements, however, are never to be found in their pure form. When we perceive them, they are always in a combined state. For instance, the water that we see always has some earth and other elements mixed with it. In reality, though, it is pure Spirit which we see as earth, water, or fire. The father has been telling his son that the mind is the finest product of food, that prāṇa, the vital force, is the finest product of water, and that speech is the finest product of fire. Naturally the son is confused. It is not very clear. So he asks his father to explain it further.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
Brahman as the Cosmological and Physical Principle
claim_candidates: The verse states that the mind is nourished by food, prana by water, and speech by fire, indicating a material dependence of subtle faculties on the three visible elements
According to the commentary, the visible elements are never pure but are mixed, and ultimately they are manifestations of pure Spirit, implying that the material world is a manifestation of Brahman
The father's instruction reflects a macrocosm‑microcosm correspondence, where gross elements give rise to subtle functions, suggesting a hierarchical relationship between the physical and the mental
objection_hooks: One might argue that the wording 'nourished by' is metaphorical, so the claim of literal material dependence may be overstated
If the elements are always mixed and not found in pure form, it is unclear how they can directly represent pure Spirit, challenging the claim that they are manifestations of Brahman
Advaita Vedanta ultimately denies any real dependence of the self on material elements, creating tension with the claim of a hierarchical relationship between gross elements and subtle faculties

sanskrit: दध्नः सोम्य मथ्यमानस्य योऽणिमा स उर्ध्वः समुदीषति तत्सर्पिर्भवति
transliteration: dadhnaḥ somya mathyamānasya yo'ṇimā sa urdhvaḥ samudīṣati tatsarpirbhavati
translation: When curd is churned, the finest part of it rises to the surface. That becomes butter.
word_meaning: Somya, O Somya; dadhnaḥ mathyamānasya, of curd when it is churned; yaḥ aṇimā, what is the finest part; sāḥ ūrdhvaḥ samudīṣati, that rises to the surface; tat sarpiḥ bhavati, that becomes butter.
commentary: Even now, in many homes in India, people make their own butter by churning curd. When you churn the curd, cream starts appearing on the surface. Gradually that cream turns into butter. It was already in the curd. In fact, it was the finest part of the curd. Only the churning was needed to make it rise to the top.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse uses the churning of curd as a metaphor that the innermost essence already exists within the manifest and only appropriate effort reveals it
The rise of the finest part to become butter signifies that the realized self emerges when the subtle aspect ascends above the gross, indicating a qualitative change through upward movement
The metaphor implies that spiritual realization does not create a new substance but merely separates the pre‑existing subtle essence from the coarse, aligning with Advaita’s non‑duality
objection_hooks: The metaphor appears to describe a transformation from curd to butter, which could be read as creation of a new state, challenging the claim that no new substance is produced
Advaita traditions sometimes emphasize grace or spontaneous awakening rather than effort, questioning the claim that upward movement requires human churning
Identifying the finite butter with the infinite Brahman may be problematic because the analogy risks conflating a limited product with an unlimited reality

sanskrit: एवमेव खलु सोम्यान्नस्याश्यमानस्य योऽणिमा स उर्ध्वः समुदीषति तन्मनो भवति
transliteration: evameva khalu somyānnasyāśyamānasya yo'ṇimā sa urdhvaḥ samudīṣati tanmano bhavati
translation: O Somya, in the same way, the finest part of the food that is eaten rises to the surface and becomes the mind.
word_meaning: Evam eva khalu, in the same way; somya, O Somya; annasya aśyamānasya, of the food that is eaten; yaḥ aṇimā, that which is the finest part; saḥ ūrdhvaḥ samudīṣati, it rises to the surface; tat manaḥ bhavati, that becomes the mind.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse equates the process by which the finest part of ingested food rises to the surface with the formation of the mind, implying that the mind is a subtle, refined element derived from physical nourishment

By addressing Somya directly, the verse suggests that understanding this subtle transformation is essential knowledge for the seeker, indicating that mastery over the subtle body (mind) is a prerequisite for spiritual progress

The description of the mind as arising from the finest part of food implies a hierarchical quality of subtlety, where higher subtle elements are produced from the most refined material, supporting a view that spiritual refinement parallels material refinement
objection_hooks: Equating mind with a physical process may reduce consciousness to a mere material byproduct, conflicting with Advaita’s claim that mind is a manifestation of Brahman rather than just refined food

The verse’s focus on the mind’s origin from food could be interpreted metaphorically rather than literally, challenging the claim that it provides a concrete doctrine about the composition of the subtle body

sanskrit: अपांसोम्य पीयमानानां योऽणिमा स उर्ध्वः समुदीषति सा प्राणो भवति
transliteration: apāṃsomya pīyamānānāṃ yo'ṇimā sa urdhvaḥ samudīṣati sā prāṇo bhavati
translation: O Somya, the finest part of water that is drunk rises to the surface and becomes our prāṇa .
word_meaning: Anna means the food that you eat—any food. Wheat, rice, fish, meat, vegetables, milk, and so on—all this is anna . The Upaniṣad says, that which is the finest part of the food rises to the surface, like the butter in curd. It then becomes the mind. It is the subtlest part of the food, the essence. According to Vedānta, the mind is material. It is a by-product of food and is nourished by food. Somya, O Somya; apām pīyāmānānām, of the water that is drunk; yaḥ aṇimā, that which is the finest part; saḥ ūrdhvaḥ samudīṣati, it rises to the surface; saḥ prāṇaḥ bhavati, that becomes prāṇa, the vital force.
commentary: Similarly, if you drink water, the finest part of that water becomes separated from the rest and rises to the surface to become prāṇa, the vital force. This is why in Sanskrit water and life ( jala and jīvana ) are synonymous. Without water, life cannot exist. In fact, science says that life first appeared in water.
concept_tags: Brahman as Life, Joy, and the Void
claim_candidates: The verse states that the subtlest part of ingested water rises to become prāṇa, showing that prāṇa is derived from the essential essence of water
The commentary equates water (jala) with life (jīvana), indicating that water is a necessary condition for the existence of life
According to the commentary the mind is material and a by‑product of food, implying that mental processes depend on physical nourishment
objection_hooks: One might argue that identifying prāṇa with the finest part of water reduces the spiritual concept of prāṇa to a purely physiological process, conflicting with non‑dual views that treat prāṇa as a manifestation of Brahman rather than a material derivative

The claim that the mind is merely a material by‑product of food can be challenged by Advaita thinkers who maintain that the mind, though appearing empirical, is ultimately an expression of non‑dual consciousness, not simply physical

While water is essential for most known life, modern biology discovers organisms that survive in non‑aqueous environments, questioning the absolute necessity of water for life asserted in the commentary

sanskrit: तेजसः सोम्याश्यमानस्य योऽणिमा स उर्ध्वः समुदीषति सा वाग्भवति
transliteration: tejasaḥ somyāśyamānasya yo'ṇimā sa urdhvaḥ samudīṣati sā vāgbhavati
translation: O Somya, the finest part of fire [butter, etc.] that is eaten rises to the surface and becomes our speech.
word_meaning: Somya, O Somya; tejasaḥ aśyamānasya, of the fire [butter, etc.] that is eaten; yaḥ aṇima, that which is the finest part; saḥ ūrdhvaḥ samudīṣati, it rises to the surface; sā vāk bhavati, that becomes speech.
claim_candidates: The verse states that the finest part of fire that is eaten rises upward and becomes speech
The passage symbolically connects the inner subtle fire (tejas) with the emergence of vāk, indicating that speech originates from an inner fire
The address to Somya shows that the teaching is directed to a disciple, framing the transformation as a pedagogical lesson
objection_hooks: One might argue that the description is literal, referring to a physical substance like butter, and not a metaphysical symbol of Brahman
The association of speech with fire could be challenged because traditional Vedic symbolism links speech to the element of air, creating a tension with the claim of fire as its source

sanskrit: स य एषोऽणिमैतदात्म्यमिदं सर्वं तत्सत्यं स आत्मा तत्त्वमसि श्वेतकेतो
transliteration: sa ya eṣo 'ṇimaitadātmyam idaṃ sarvaṃ tat satyaṃ sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo
translation: That which is this subtle essence, all this has That as its Self. That is the Real. That is the Self. That thou art, O Śvetaketu.
word_meaning: saḥ, that; yaḥ eṣaḥ aṇimā, this subtle essence; etat ātmyaṃ, has this as its Self; idam sarvam, all this universe; tat satyam, that is the Real; saḥ ātmā, that is the Self; tat tvam asi, thou art that; śvetaketo, O Śvetaketu.
commentary: This is one of the mahāvākyas (great Upanishadic statements). Uddālaka Āruṇi instructs his son Śvetaketu that the subtle essence (sat, pure being) is the underlying reality of the entire universe. Everything emerges from that, is sustained by that, and dissolves into that. The individual self (jīva) is not different from that ultimate reality (Brahman). Thus, the teaching ‘tat tvam asi’ asserts the identity of the individual self with the universal Self.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse states that the subtle essence (sat) is the underlying reality of the entire universe
The verse declares the identity of the individual self (jiva) with the universal Self (Brahman) through the formula tat tvam asi
The verse equates the ultimate reality with both the Real (sat) and the Self (atman), implying no ontological distinction between them
objection_hooks: The identification of the individual self with Brahman may conflict with the lived experience of personal suffering and apparent separateness
Some Upanishadic passages maintain a distinction between Brahman as impersonal absolute and the manifested world, challenging the claim that the subtle essence underlies all
Equating the Real with the Self could be questioned on the basis that sat is described as impersonal while atman is often presented as personal, creating a conceptual tension

sanskrit: अन्नमयं हि सोम्य मन आपोमयः प्राणस्तेजोमयी वागिति भूय एव मा भगवान्विज्ञापयत्विति तथा सोम्येति होवाच
transliteration: annamayaṃ hi somya mana āpomayaḥ prāṇastejomayī vāgiti bhūya eva mā bhagavānvijñāpayatviti tathā somyeti hovāca
translation: ‘O Somya, the mind is certainly nourished by food, pṛāṇa by water, and speech by fire.’ [ Śvetaketu then said, ] ‘Sir, will you please explain this to me again?’ ‘Yes, I will explain again, O Somya, ’ replied his father.
word_meaning: Somya, O Somya; manaḥ hi annamayam, the mind is certainly nourished by food; prāṇaḥ āpomayaḥ, the vital force is nourished by water; vāk tejomayī iti, speech is nourished by fire; bhagavān, sir; bhūyaḥ eva, again; mā, to me; vijnāpayatu iti, will you please explain; tathā, so be it; somya iti, O Somya; ha uvāca, he [the father] said.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse states that the mind is nourished by food, the vital force (prana) by water, and speech by fire
The dialogue shows that Śvetaketu asks his father to repeat the teaching, indicating that repeated instruction is valued in the transmission of knowledge
The three faculties—mind, prana, and speech—are linked to the three primary elements, suggesting a symbolic mapping of bodily functions to elemental forces
objection_hooks: One may argue that describing mind, prana, and speech as dependent on food, water, and fire reduces their transcendental aspect, which Advaita treats as expressions of Brahman rather than merely material supports
The request for repetition might be a literary convention rather than evidence of a pedagogical principle, so the claim about the importance of repeated instruction could be overstated
The association of faculties with elements could be metaphorical, not a literal dependence, thus the claim of strict elemental nourishment may misinterpret the symbolic language

sanskrit: षोडशकलः सोम्य पुरुषः पञ्चदशाहानि माशीः काममपः पिबापोमयः प्राणो नपिबतो विच्छेत्स्यत इति
transliteration: ṣoḍaśakalaḥ somya puruṣaḥ pañcadaśāhāni māśīḥ kāmamapaḥ pibāpomayaḥ prāṇo napibato vicchetsyata iti
translation: O Somya, a person has sixteen parts, [and all your sixteen parts are intact]. Do not eat anything for fifteen days, but drink as much water as you like. Life is dependent on water. If you do not drink water, you will lose your life.
word_meaning: Somya, O Somya; puruṣaḥ, a person; ṣoḍaśakalaḥ, has sixteen parts; pañcadaśa ahāni, for fifteen days; mā aśīḥ, do not eat; apaḥ piba, drink water; kāmam, as much as you like; prāṇaḥ āpomayaḥ, life is dependent on water; na pibataḥ, if you do not drink water; vicchetsyate iti, it will leave.
commentary: Now the son says, ‘I still can’t understand.’ So the father tries something else. A normal human being in good health is said, in Vedānta, to be made up of sixteen parts. If a person neither eats nor drinks water for fifteen days, he will die. But if he does not eat yet continues to drink water during that time he will remain alive. When Gandhiji used to fast, he would drink plenty of water and would put some lemon juice in it. That would keep him alive. But what happens to a person when he drinks water but does not eat? His mind fails. He cannot remember anything. So, to convince his son, the father asks him to fast for fifteen days but to drink water.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: A human being is described as having sixteen parts, which the commentary identifies as the components of a normal, healthy person in Vedānta
The verse states that one can survive fifteen days without food if one continues to drink water, indicating water as the essential sustainer of life
According to the commentary, drinking water without eating keeps the body alive but leads to mental failure, suggesting a separation between physical vitality and mental function
The father’s instruction to fast for fifteen days while drinking water is used as a practical demonstration of ascetic discipline (tapas) to the son
objection_hooks: One could object that the claim water alone sustains life for fifteen days neglects the role of bodily reserves and metabolic processes, making the statement physiologically simplistic
The link between lack of food and mental failure is asserted without empirical support, so the claim may be challenged as speculative
Equating the act of fasting with true tapas may be contested, since some traditions require inner renunciation beyond mere external abstention
The assertion of a fixed sixteen-part composition of a person may be disputed by other Vedānta texts that describe different numbers or configurations of subtle body elements

sanskrit: स ह पञ्चदशाहानि नशाथ हैनमुपससाद किं ब्रवीमि भो इत्यृचः सोम्य यजूंषि सामानीति स होवाच न वै मा प्रतिभान्ति भो इति
transliteration: sa ha pañcadaśāhāni naśātha hainamupasasāda kiṃ bravīmi bho ityṛcaḥ somya yajūṃṣi sāmānīti sa hovāca na vai mā pratibhānti bho iti
translation: Śvetaketu did not eat anything for fifteen days. After that he came to his father and said, ‘O Father, what shall I recite?’ His father said, ‘Recite the Ṛk, Yajuḥ, and Sāma mantras .’ Śvetaketu replied, ‘I can’t recall any of them, sir’.
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [Śvetaketu]; ha pañcadaśa ahāni, for fifteen days; na āśa, did not eat anything; atha, then [on the sixteenth day]; enam ha upasasāda, he went to him [his father]; bhoḥ, O Father; kim bravīmi iti, what shall I recite; somya, O Somya; ṛcaḥ yajūṃṣi sāmāni iti, the Ṛk, the Yajuḥ, and the Sāmas; saḥ ha uvāca, [Śvetaketu] said; bhoḥ, O Father; na vai mā pratibhānti iti, they do not come to my mind.
commentary: Now the father says to his son, ‘Will you please recite to me the Ṛk, Yajuḥ, and Sāma mantras?’ But the son replies: ‘They are completely gone. I do not remember them. They do not appear in my mind at all.’ Having been without food for fifteen days, Śvetaketu’s mind was almost gone. The mind is the finest part, the essence, of food—not of water, not of anything else. Therefore, the mind must be material.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The commentary claims that because Śvetaketu’s mind could not recall the Ṛk, Yajuḥ, and Sāma after fifteen days of fasting, the mind must be material, being dependent on food for its existence
The verse shows that Vedic recitations are not innate to the self but require a functioning, nourished mind, indicating that knowledge is contingent on material conditions
objection_hooks: One might object that the dependence of mental activity on food does not entail that the mind is material; the mind could be a subtle, non‑material faculty that merely requires physical conditions to operate
Another objection is that Vedic knowledge is eternal and independent of the mind, so the forgetting after fasting reflects temporary mental obscuration rather than proving the mind’s material nature

sanskrit: तं होवाच यथा सोम्य महतोऽभ्या हितस्यैकोऽङ्गारः खद्योतमात्रः परिशिष्टः स्यात्तेन ततोऽपि न बहु दहेदेवंसोम्य ते षोडशानां कलानामेका कलातिशिष्टा स्यात्तयैतर्हि वेदान्नानुभवस्यशानाथ मे विज्ञास्यसीति
transliteration: taṃ hovāca yathā somya mahato'bhyā hitasyaiko'ṅgāraḥ khadyotamātraḥ pariśiṣṭaḥ syāttena tato'pi na bahu dahedevaṃsomya te ṣoḍaśānāṃ kalānāmekā kalātiśiṣṭā syāttayaitarhi vedānnānubhavasyaśānātha me vijñāsyasīti
translation: The father said to Śvetaketu: ‘O Somya, from a blazing fire, if there is but a small piece of ember left, the size of a firefly, it cannot bum anything bigger than that. Similarly, O Somya, because only one small part of your sixteen parts remains, you cannot remember the Vedas . Eat something and then you will understand what I am saying’.
word_meaning: Tam ha uvāca, he said to him [to Śvetaketu]; somya, O Somya; yathā, as; mahataḥ abhyāhitasya, of a big blazing fire; ekaḥ aṅgāraḥ, one ember; khadyotamātraḥ, the size of a firefly; pariśiṣṭaḥ syāt, that is left; tena, by that; tataḥ api bahu, anything larger than its size; na dahet, cannot bum; evam, like that; somya, my son; ekā kalā, only one part; ṣoḍaśānām kalānām, of [yourl sixteen parts; atiśiṣṭā syāt, has remained; etarhi, now; tayā, by that [small part]; vedān, the Vedas; na anubhavasi, you do not remember; aśāna, eat; atha, then; me, my [words]; vijñāsyasi iti, you will grasp.
commentary: Suppose you have a big pile of wood, and you start a fire with it. After awhile practically the whole pile is consumed, and there is only a small spark left—a spark as small as a firefly. Very little can be burnt with such a tiny spark, yet even so, if you add some fuel to it you can again start a big fire. ‘Similarly, ’ Uddālaka says to his son, ‘your mind is like that tiny spark. It is not functioning now because you have not given it any fuel these last fifteen days. There is just a flicker of your mind working, and it cannot serve any useful purpose. If you eat something now, everything will come back to your mind.’
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse likens a dwindling fire to a mind that has lost its vigor, indicating that without nourishment the mind cannot grasp the Vedas
The commentary states that eating food will restore the mind’s function, implying a direct dependence of spiritual cognition on physical sustenance
The reference to only one of the sixteen parts of the mind remaining suggests a doctrine that the mind is composed of sixteen components whose loss impairs Vedic knowledge
objection_hooks: One might argue that the instruction to eat is a practical health advice rather than a metaphysical claim about the necessity of bodily fuel for spiritual insight
The phrase ṣoḍaśānām kalānām could be interpreted as sixteen time periods rather than sixteen parts of the mind, challenging the claim about a sixteen‑component mind
The fire metaphor may be understood symbolically, so asserting that the mind cannot serve any purpose without food could be an over‑literal reading

sanskrit: स हशाथ हैनमुपससाद तं ह यत्किंच पप्रच्छ सर्वंह प्रतिपेदे
transliteration: sa haśātha hainamupasasāda taṃ ha yatkiṃca papraccha sarvaṃha pratipede
translation: Śvetaketu ate something and then went to his father. Whatever his father asked him, he was able to follow.
word_meaning: Saḥ, he [Śvetaketu]; ha āśa, ate; atha, then; enam, to him [his father]; ha upasasāda, he went; tam, to him [to Śvetaketu]; yat kiñca, whatever; papraccha, he asked; sarvam ha pratipede, he was able to follow everything.
commentary: After eating, Śvetaketu’s mind was back to normal. He then approached his father, and everything his father said was clear to him.
concept_tags: Man’s Destiny Determined by His Conduct
The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: Proper nourishment restores mental clarity, allowing one to comprehend and obey the father's teachings
The verse implies that fulfilling bodily needs is a prerequisite for effective learning and ethical conduct
Mental equilibrium, as described in the commentary, is presented as a condition for understanding higher knowledge
objection_hooks: One might argue that mental clarity arises from inner discipline rather than physical eating, challenging the causal link
The text mentions eating but does not explicitly connect it to moral behavior, so the claim that ethical conduct is required for learning may be unwarranted
The commentary’s description of the mind being ‘back to normal’ could denote a temporary state, questioning whether lasting knowledge depends on this fleeting condition

sanskrit: तं होवाच यथा सोम्य महतोऽभ्याहितस्यैकमङ्गारं खद्योतमात्रं परिशिष्टं तं तृणैरुपसमाधाय प्राज्वलयेत्तेन ततोऽपि बहु दहेत्
transliteration: taṃ hovāca yathā somya mahato'bhyāhitasyaikamaṅgāraṃ khadyotamātraṃ pariśiṣṭaṃ taṃ tṛṇairupasamādhāya prājvalayettena tato'pi bahu dahet
translation: The father said to him: ‘O Somya, from a blazing fire, if there is but a small piece of ember left, the size of a firefly, the fire can again blaze up when you add some grass. The fire, in fact, can then blaze up even more than it did before’.
word_meaning: Tam ha uvāca, he said to him [to Śvetaketu ]; somya, O Somya; yathā, just as; mahataḥ abhyāhitasya, of a big blazing fire; ekam aṅgāram, one ember; khadyotamātram, the size of a firefly; pariśiṣṭam, that is left; tam, that; tṛṇaiḥ upasamādhāya, with some grass; prājvalayet, becomes blazing; tena, by that [grass]; tataḥ api bahu, even more than before; dahet, bums.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse uses the metaphor of adding grass to a dying ember to illustrate that the individual self, when supplied with knowledge, can rekindle its inherent Brahman nature and blaze more brightly than before

Even a minimal remnant of the original fire, described as an ember the size of a firefly, retains the potential to expand beyond its original magnitude when nourished, implying that the latent Brahman within the self is never extinguished
The father’s instruction frames the act of imparting knowledge as an active process that transforms the disciple, indicating that enlightenment is an amplification of the self’s inherent luminosity
objection_hooks: One might argue that the passage merely describes a physical fire phenomenon and does not explicitly equate the ember with the Atman, so the metaphysical reading is not forced by the text

The claim that the ember can exceed its original size could be challenged because the verse says the fire can blaze more but does not quantify the increase, leaving room for a non‑doctrinal, hyperbolic interpretation
Interpreting the grass as knowledge is speculative since the verse does not label the grass as symbolic of wisdom, allowing a literal reading of fuel rather than a philosophical one

sanskrit: एवं सोम्य ते षोडशानां कलानामेका कलातिशिष्टाभूत्सान्नेनोपसमाहिता प्राज्वाली तयैतर्हि वेदाननुभवस्यन्नमयंहि सोम्य मन आपोमयः प्राणस्तेजोमयी वागिति तद्धास्य विजज्ञाविति विजज्ञाविति
transliteration: evaṃ somya te ṣoḍaśānāṃ kalānāmekā kalātiśiṣṭābhūtsānnenopasamāhitā prājvālī tayaitarhi vedānanubhavasyannamayaṃhi somya mana āpomayaḥ prāṇastejomayī vāgiti taddhāsya vijajñāviti vijajñāviti
translation: ‘In the same way, O Somya, of your sixteen parts, only one remained. But that, when nourished by food, has revived, and by that you are now able to follow the Vedas . O Somya, this is why I said that the mind was nourished by food, prāṇa was nourished by water, and speech was nourished by fire.’ Śvetaketu now understood what his father was saying.
word_meaning: Evam, in the same way; somya, O Somya; ekā kalā, one part; te ṣoḍaśānām kalānām, of your sixteen parts; atiśiṣṭā abhūt, remained; sā, it; upasamāhitā annena, nourished by food; prājvālī, has revived; etarhi, now; tayā, by that [remaining part]; vedān, the Vedas; anubhavasi, you can understand; somya, O Somya; hi, this is why [I said]; manaḥ annamayam, the mind is nourished by food; prāṇaḥ āpomayaḥ, prāṇa is nourished by water; vāk tejomayī iti, speech is nourished by fire; asya, of that [what his father had said]; tat ha vijajñau iti, he understood it; vijajñau iti, he understood.
commentary: Just as a fire will go out if fuel is not added to it, so also the mind will cease to function if you do not eat. After fasting for fifteen days, Śvetaketu still had a small portion of his mind left working. It was reduced to a fragment, as it were. But after eating, his memory returned and his mind was again active and vigorous. The idea is, this human body is a very powerful instrument. The mind is powerful; speech is powerful; life itself is powerful. But it is all dependent on food (i.e., earth), water, and energy (i.e., fire). Yet even these gross elements are not independent. As we have seen earlier, earth is a mixture of water and fire with earth. Water also is a mixture of earth and fire with water. Similarly, fire is a mixture of earth and water with fire. This process is called trivṛta, or triplication. It is the permutation and combination of the three elements. Vedānta says, we cannot see these elements in their pure form, because that pure form is Existence, sat . What the Upaniṣad is saying is suggestive: That which we do not see in its pure form, but in a form which is a by-product of a combination of other things, really does not exist. This is the argument Buddhism also advances. They say: ‘You talk about a chariot, but where is the chariot? Is it a reality? No, it can’t be a reality, because a chariot is a combination of different things put together. Where is the chariot? Is it the wheels? The platform? The canopy? No. When you speak of an object which is not independent, which is dependent on factors combining together, then it is not real.’ Here is the same argument. All objects are dependent. What exists then? Vedānta says it is sat, Existence. That Existence is our own Self, the real Self, the essence, the real being, pure Spirit. When the Upaniṣad refers to our mind, our life force, and our speech, it is referring to this phenomenal world. We have to look at these things from two levels: From the cosmic level there is one Reality, one Existence. From the individual level we owe our mind, life force, and speech to these elements. But in reality all these elements, all these manifestations of creation, are pure Spirit. One Reality, pure Spirit, has assumed diverse forms. The whole manifestation of this universe is dependent. Even the mind. Where does the mind come from? Science has not yet been able to answer this question. According to Vedānta, the mind is nothing but matter; yet consciousness, which is pure Spirit, is the source. Vedānta does not make any distinction between matter and consciousness. We say it is one and the same. Consciousness is the source, and out of that one source, all that exists—call it matter, call it energy, call it mind—has come. Science has its own terms. They may call the source of life DNA or something else. Vedānta says it is tat, That. Or it is sat, Existence. From Existence, everything has emerged. In essence there is one—the same thing appearing in different forms. You may ask, how did the. One became manifest as many? Why did it happen? The fact that we can ask this question shows that that One is conscious. Only a conscious being can think, plan, and wish. Because that one Reality is consciousness it can say, ‘I will be many.’ If it were inert, it could not wish to become many. So Vedānta says, that one Reality, pure Spirit, is consciousness. Yet, that consciousness can also take the form of something inert. Matter and consciousness are not different. For instance, if you break a paperweight into atoms, and then smash even those atoms, you will find so much energy—so much motion. So, whether the manifestations of this creation are conscious or unconscious, intelligent or unintelligent, they all have their source in that one conscious Reality.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse states that mind is nourished by food, prana by water, and speech by fire, linking each function to a specific gross element.
The commentary uses this elemental dependence to argue that all phenomenal objects are not independent realities but are manifestations of the underlying sat (Existence).

By asserting that the ultimate reality sat underlies the dependent mind, prana, and speech, the passage supports the Advaita view that the individual self is ultimately identical with the infinite Brahman.
objection_hooks: Equating the empirical dependence of mind on food with its ultimate identity to Brahman may conflate the relative and absolute levels, challenging the claim of self‑Brahman identity.

The claim that dependent phenomena are unreal because they are composites can be contested by the Buddhist stance that such composites have conventional reality, creating tension about the status of the elements.
The mapping of prana to water may be seen as metaphorical rather than literal, raising doubts about the universality of the element‑function correspondence.

sanskrit: उद्दालको हारुणिः श्वेतकेतुं पुत्रमुवाच स्वप्नान्तं मे सोम्य विजानीहीति यत्रैतत्पुरुषः स्वपिति नाम सता सोम्य तदा सम्पन्नो भवति स्वमपीतो भवति तस्मादेनं स्वपितीत्याचक्षते स्वंह्यपीतो भवति
transliteration: uddālako hāruṇiḥ śvetaketuṃ putramuvāca svapnāntaṃ me somya vijānīhīti yatraitatpuruṣaḥ svapiti nāma satā somya tadā sampanno bhavati svamapīto bhavati tasmādenaṃ svapitītyācakṣate svaṃhyapīto bhavati
translation: Uddālaka Āruṇi said to his son Śvetaketu: ‘O Somya, let me explain to you the concept of deep sleep. When a person is said to be sleeping, O Somya, he becomes one with Sat [Existence], and he attains his real Self. That is why people say about him, “He is sleeping.” He is then in his Self’.
word_meaning: Uddālakaḥ āruṇih, Uddālaka Āruṇi [the son of Aruṇa ]; śvetaketum putram, to his son Śvetaketu; ha uvāca, said; svapnāntam, the concept of [deep] sleep; me, from me; somya, O Somya; vijānīhi iti, learn; yatra, when; etat puruṣaḥ, this person; svapiti nāma, is said to be in deep sleep; somya, O Somya; tadā, then; sampannaḥ bhavati, he is merged; satā, with Sat [Existence, Paramātmā ]; svam apītaḥ bhavati, he attains his real Self; tasmāt, for this, reason; ācakṣate, people say; enam, about him; svapiti iti, he is sleeping; svam hi apītaḥ bhavati, he attains his real Self.
commentary: In ancient India, all skills were passed from the father to the son. This is how the caste system eventually became so rigid. A father tended to be selfish and favour his son over his other students, especially if the father was a great scholar. Unless he had a student who was exceptionally good and intelligent, he would generally pass on the best things to his own son. Why does Uddālaka call his son Śvetaketu by the name Somya? The name Somya means one who is quiet, humble, very good, and gentle. If you want to learn something, you must be quiet and modest. So the father is saying, vijānīhi —‘learn from me, you who are so quiet and humble.’ The word puruṣa means pure, ‘in the heart, ’ and śayate, ‘residing.’ That which resides in the heart—i.e., the Self. It is a living being. It could be a man, a woman, an animal, or an insect. The word svapnāntam means the concept of deep sleep, or dreamless sleep. When we dream, our sleep is disturbed, and it does not give us any rest. But dreamless sleep is very restful. If we have that kind of sleep for even a few minutes, we become temporarily free from this world. When we wake up then, it takes us some time to realize where we are and what time of day it is. While we were sleeping, the whole world was wiped out for us. When we say, ‘This person is sleeping, ’ what does that mean? What happens when we sleep? Where does the mind go? That is what is being discussed here. The Upaniṣad says, during deep sleep a person becomes united with the Self. The individual self—that is, the jīva who is ignorant—merges into the Cosmic Self. The individual self has to have a body and a mind, and it is the mind that is closest to the Self. In fact, many people think the mind is the Self. The mind, however, is like a crystal. A crystal has no colour of its own, but suppose you put a red flower next to it. What happens? The crystal assumes the colour of the flower. Similarly, the mind has no consciousness of its own. But because it is close to the Paramātman, the Supreme Self, it gets the reflection of consciousness from the Self. Then, through the mind, all our organs function—the eyes, the ears, the hands, the feet, etc. The organs and the mind are all dependent on the Self. When we are awake, the body and mind are both functioning. Then when we are dreaming, the body is not functioning but the mind is still functioning. But when we are in deep sleep, the mind also ceases to function. We then become one with our own Self. That is why deep sleep is so refreshing. Yet when we wake up we are the same individual that we were before we slept. The Self is there. We are always one with it. Yet sleep does not give us any knowledge of our Self. Why? Because our ignorance creates a barrier that keeps us from knowing our real nature. But if we are one with the Self, how can we be separate from it? In reality, we are never separate from the Self, but when we are attached to our names and forms, we have desires. And when we have desires, ignorance has its full grip on us. During dreamless sleep the barrier that keeps us from the Self is temporarily removed, but we are not conscious of it. That is why, when we wake up, we are exactly the same person we were before we slept. If I was a tiger before I went to sleep, I am a tiger again when I get up. A sleeping tiger cannot do any harm, but if it wakes up, beware! Similarly, if I was a bad person before, I am still a bad person when I wake up. So, when I sleep, for the time being my ignorance is also sleeping, as it were. Only Self-knowledge can dispel my ignorance and give me true peace. It is a common belief in India that if you bathe in the Ganga, you will get rid of all your sins. Sri Ramakrishna used to say, though, that the sins are very clever. As you go to the river the sins say to each other: ‘Well, this person is going into the Ganga. Let’s take shelter on that tree.’ So they fly off and perch on a tree. Then, when you come out of the river, they all pounce back on you and once again you are in their grip. Similarly, deep sleep can give us temporary rest, but it cannot remove our ignorance. Only if we have real knowledge, knowledge of the Self, can we remove the seeds of our karma . Knowledge is like a sword. With that sword we can cut off the roots of our ignorance and become free. Once we attain Self-knowledge we are totally transformed, because it goes to the root of our being. And when we wake up from our sleep of ignorance we are no longer the same individual.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: During deep (dreamless) sleep the individual merges with Sat (Existence) and attains his real Self, as Uddālaka states that a person becomes one with Sat and attains his real Self when sleeping.

The commentary maintains that deep sleep temporarily removes the barrier of ignorance but does not provide conscious Self‑knowledge, so upon waking the person remains the same individual lacking true insight.
The mind is likened to a crystal that merely reflects the Self's consciousness; when deep sleep causes the mind to cease functioning, this cessation permits the unity with the Self.
objection_hooks: If deep sleep already unites the self with Sat, why does the commentary claim that this state does not yield Self‑knowledge, indicating a tension between ontological unity and epistemic realization.

The crystal analogy suggests the mind is only a passive reflector, yet Advaita posits the Self as pure consciousness independent of any instrument, raising the objection that the mind’s cessation may be unnecessary for Self‑realization.

The claim that awakening returns the same individual seems to contradict the assertion that the individual self merges with the universal Self during deep sleep, creating a paradox about persistent individuality.

sanskrit: स यथा शकुनिः सूत्रेण प्रबद्धो दिशं दिशं पतित्वान्यत्रायतनमलब्ध्वा बन्धनमेवोपश्रयत एवमेव खलु सोम्य तन्मनो दिशं दिशं पतित्वान्यत्रायतनमलब्ध्वा प्राणमेवोपश्रयते प्राणबन्धनं हि सोम्य मन इति
transliteration: sa yathā śakuniḥ sūtreṇa prabaddho diśaṃ diśaṃ patitvānyatrāyatanamalabdhvā bandhanamevopaśrayata evameva khalu somya tanmano diśaṃ diśaṃ patitvānyatrāyatanamalabdhvā prāṇamevopaśrayate prāṇabandhanaṃ hi somya mana iti
translation: Just as a bird tied to a rope flutters here and there, and when it cannot get any shelter anywhere, it surrenders itself to its bondage; in the same way, O Somya, the mind runs in every direction, and when it fails to get a resting place anywhere, it surrenders itself to prāṇa, the vital force. The mind, O Somya, is tied to prāṇa.
word_meaning: Saḥ yathā, it is like; śakuniḥ, a bird; sūtreṇa prabaddhaḥ, tied to a rope; diśam diśam, in all directions; patitvā, fluttering; anyatra, elsewhere; āyatanam, a shelter; alabdhvā, without getting; bandhanam eva upaśrayate, surrenders to its bondage; evam eva khalu, similarly; somya, O Somya; tat manaḥ, this mind; diśam diśam, in all directions; patitvā, running about; anyatra, elsewhere; āyatanam, a shelter; alabdhvā, without getting; prāṇam eva upaśrayate, surrenders to prāṇa; somya, O Somya; prāṇa-bandhanam hi manaḥ iti, the mind is tied to prāṇa.
commentary: When we are sound asleep, what happens to the mind? The mind withdraws; it retires and temporarily goes back to the Self. Is this the same as samādhi, the superconscious state? Vedānta says, no, there is a distinction between samādhi and this experience of sound sleep. Both experiences bring great joy and peace, but deep sleep is only temporary. It is just for a while. We are still held by the rope of ignorance, and we do not know our real Self. When we come back from deep sleep, we are just as tightly bound as we were before we slept. The Upaniṣad gives the example here of a bird tied to a rope, struggling to get free. It starts flying in all directions, seeking a safe place—any place, anywhere else than where it is bound. At last it must give up and return to its place of bondage. Sri Ramakrishna used to give an illustration very similar to this. A bird is sitting on the mast of a ship and soon falls asleep. It does not notice that the ship has set sail. Finally, when the ship has gone far out to sea, it wakes up and starts looking around for land. Flying to the east, it eventually gets tired, and, not finding any land, comes back to the ship. In the same way, it flies out to the west, to the north, and to the south, but all it sees is water everywhere. At last the bird decides to take refuge on the mast of the ship. Swami Vivekananda says that all of us are struggling to be free. Freedom is our birthright. You are trying to be free; a student is trying to be free; a teacher is trying to be free. All of us, irrespective of our situation in life, are trying in one way or another to attain freedom. The Upaniṣad is saying that when we are awake, we are like the bird flying in different directions. We are constantly fluttering around, doing this and doing that. Sometimes we are attracted by something here, and then again by something there. Our minds are always roaming about. But finally we get tired and surrender our minds to prāṇa—that is, we lie down and go to sleep. And if we are lucky, we have good sound sleep. The word prāṇa here means the Self. Meister Eckhart once said that the mind has two eyes. One eye is always looking outside, looking at the things around. But the other eye is looking inside, looking and searching, as it were, into one’s own Being. Finally one discovers the ‘Castle of Security.’ What is this Castle of Security? It is the Self. When we enter into this castle we are at peace. That is what the Upaniṣad also says here. When a person is able to retreat into his own Self, even temporarily, he attains a state of peace and joy. In deep sleep, we temporarily enter the castle. It
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse likens the mind’s restless wandering to a bird tied to a rope, indicating that mental activity is a form of bondage
The commentary states that deep sleep allows the mind to temporarily retreat into the Self, but this retreat does not constitute final liberation because the rope of ignorance remains
The commentary interprets prāṇa in the verse as identical with the Self, so surrendering to prāṇa is surrendering to one’s true nature
objection_hooks: One could object that identifying prāṇa with the Self blurs the traditional Advaita distinction between the functional vital force and the immutable Brahman
Another objection is that other Upaniṣadic passages describe the state of deep sleep (turiya) as a direct glimpse of the ultimate, challenging the claim that it is only a temporary retreat
A further objection is that mental activity may be seen as a necessary function of the embodied self rather than bondage, so the bird‑rope metaphor might be overstated

sanskrit: अशनापिपासे मे सोम्य विजानीहीति यत्रैतत्पुरुषोऽशिशिषति नामाप एव तदशितं नयन्ते तद्यथा गोनायोऽश्वनायः पुरुषनाय इत्येवं तदप आचक्षतेऽशनायेति तत्रितच्छुङ्गमुत्पतितं सोम्य विजानीहि नेदममूलं भविष्यतीति
transliteration: aśanāpipāse me somya vijānīhīti yatraitatpuruṣo'śiśiṣati nāmāpa eva tadaśitaṃ nayante tadyathā gonāyo'śvanāyaḥ puruṣanāya ityevaṃ tadapa ācakṣate'śanāyeti tatritacchuṅgamutpatitaṃ somya vijānīhi nedamamūlaṃ bhaviṣyatīti
translation: O Somya, now learn from me about hunger and thirst. When a person is said to be hungry, it is to be understood that the food he ate has been earned away by water. Just as people refer to a leader of cows, or a leader of horses, or a leader of people, similarly, people say that water is the leader of food. So also, O Somya, know that this sprout [i.e., the body] is the product of something [i.e., of food and drink]. It cannot be without a root.
word_meaning: Somya, O Somya; me, from me; aśanā pipāse, about hunger and thirst; vijānīhi iti, learn; yatra, when; puruṣaḥ, a person; aśiśiṣati etat nāma, is said to be hungry; tat, then; āpaḥ eva aśitam, the water which has been drunk; nayante, has carried [the food that he ate] away; tat yathā, just as, for example; go- nāyaḥ, a leader of cows; aśva -nāyaḥ, a leader of horses; puruṣa -nāyaḥ, one who leads other people; iti, these terms people use; evam, similarly; tat apaḥ, that water; ācakṣate, people say; aśanāya iti, is the leader of food; tatra, so also; vijānīhi, know; somya, O Somya; etat śuṅgam, this sprout [i.e., the body];
commentary: In this universe we always see two things—one is the cause, and the other is the effect. These two forces are always at work. The cause becomes the effect, and again the effect becomes the cause of something else. For instance, a duck is the effect of an egg, and again, that same duck is the cause of another egg. Buddhism also emphasizes this point. Many people are under the impression that Buddhism is an entirely new religion. But really speaking, it is not a new religion, as Buddha himself pointed out. In Buddhism, the cause and effect theory is termed pratītya-samutpāda, ‘dependent-arising’—that is, an effect arises dependent on its cause. The Upaniṣad here takes the example of a sprout and its roots. When you see a sprout, you know at once that there must be roots, and out of those roots the sprout has come. Similarly, we see ṃ this universe many things. They seem to have come from different sources, but in reality the ultimate source is one and the same. It is Sat, pure Existence, Brahman . In this verse, the father begins by telling his son about hunger and thirst. What does it mean when a person says, ‘I am hungry’? A few hours previously he ate something, but again he is hungry. What happened? It means that whatever he ate before has been carried away by water. Some of the water becomes blood and distributes the food to different parts of the body, and then the water carries away the waste part of the food and takes it out of the body. Then the person feels hungry again. This is why. water is said to be the leader of food. The Upaniṣad says it is like a herd of cows. Wherever there is a herd of cows, you will find that the herd chooses a particular cow as the leader. When the cows move from place to place, the leader is ahead and all the others follow behind. Similarly, water is the leader, and the food you eat is distributed and then taken away by the water. The idea is, this body comes from food, and water is what gives it life. They go together. Nothing is independent. Just as this body has a source, this universe also has a source. The Upaniṣad says that when you see a sprout, you know it has its source in a root. Similarly, this universe and everything that makes up this universe—such as food and water—has a source, and that source is Sat, pure Existence.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse states that water is described as the leader of food, meaning it transports and removes food, linking hunger and thirst to the water’s action
The commentary uses the sprout‑root analogy to argue that all manifested things, including the body, ultimately arise from a single source identified as Sat pure Existence, i.e. Brahman
The Upanishadic exposition of cause and effect is presented as compatible with the Buddhist doctrine of pratītya‑samutpāda, indicating a shared insight on dependent arising
objection_hooks: Equating the Upanishadic cause‑effect view with Buddhist dependent arising may ignore the Upanishadic claim of an eternal, unchanging Brahman, which Buddhism explicitly rejects
The description of water as the ‘leader of food’ may be metaphorical rather than a literal physiological claim, so the assertion about water’s role could be challenged as symbolic rather than factual

sanskrit: तस्य क्व मूलं स्यादन्यत्रान्नादेवमेव खलु सोम्यान्नेन शुङ्गेनापो मूलमन्विच्छाद्भिः सोम्य शुङ्गेन तेजो मूलमन्विच्छ तेजसा सोम्य शुङ्गेन सन्मूलमन्विच्छ सन्मूलाः सोम्येमाः सर्वाः प्रजाः सदायतनाः सत्प्रतिष्ठाः
transliteration: tasya kva mūlaṃ syādanyatrānnādevameva khalu somyānnena śuṅgenāpo mūlamanvicchādbhiḥ somya śuṅgena tejo mūlamanviccha tejasā somya śuṅgena sanmūlamanviccha sanmūlāḥ somyemāḥ sarvāḥ prajāḥ sadāyatanāḥ satpratiṣṭhāḥ
translation: Where else, except in food, can the body have its root? In the same way, O Somya, when food is the sprout, search for water as the root; when water is the sprout, O Somya, search for fire as the root; when fire is the sprout, O Somya, search for Sat [Existence] as the root. O Somya, Sat is the root, Sat is the abode, and Sat is the support of all these beings.
word_meaning: Tasya, of it [the body]; annāt, [besides] from food; anyatra kva, where else; mūlam syāt, can the root [of the body] be; evam eva khalu, in this way; somya, O Somya; annena śuṅgena, with food as the sprout; apaḥ mūlam, water as the root; anviccha, search for; adbhiḥ śuṅgena, water as the sprout; somya, O Somya; tejaḥ mūlam, fire as the root; anviccha, search for; tejasā śuṅgena, fire as the sprout; somya, O Somya; sat mūlam, Sat [Existence] as the root; anviccha, search for; somya, O Somya; imāḥ sarvāḥ prajāḥ, all these beings; sat mūlam, have Sat as the root; sat āyatanāḥ, Sat as the abode; sat pratiṣṭhāḥ, Sat as the support.
commentary: Where there is a sprout, you know there is a root. Similarly, where there is a body, you know there is a root—that is, a source, and that source is food. Then what is the root of food? It is water. Again, water has its root in fire, and fire has its root in Existence. The Upaniṣad gives a number of links here. They follow one after another. But the ultimate root, the ultimate source, is Sat, Existence—pure Spirit. All beings—men, women, children, trees, animals, everything—are based on Sat. Praja means what is born. All that are born ( sarvā prajā ) are dependent on and resting on Sat.
concept_tags: Being as the Source of All
claim_candidates: The verse presents a causal hierarchy in which food, water, fire, and finally Sat (Existence) are successive roots, establishing Sat as the ultimate cause of the material world
The commentary states that all beings (prājā) rest on Sat, indicating that Sat functions as both the source and support of all existence
The repeated instruction to ‘search for’ the root of each element implies a methodological approach whereby systematic investigation of material causality leads to the realization of Sat
objection_hooks: The passage does not equate Sat with the individual self, so it does not substantiate the claim that the self is identical with Brahman
Upanishadic epistemology also stresses direct intuitive insight beyond empirical tracing, challenging the view that systematic search alone suffices to attain knowledge of Sat

sanskrit: अथ यत्रैतत्पुरुषः पिपासति नाम तेज एव तत्पीतं नयते तद्यथा गोनायोऽश्वनायः पुरुषनाय इत्येवं तत्तेज आचष्ट उदन्येति तत्रैतदेव शुङ्गमुत्पतितं सोम्य विजानीहि नेदममूलं भविष्यतीति
transliteration: atha yatraitatpuruṣaḥ pipāsati nāma teja eva tatpītaṃ nayate tadyathā gonāyo'śvanāyaḥ puruṣanāya ityevaṃ tatteja ācaṣṭa udanyeti tatraitadeva śuṅgamutpatitaṃ somya vijānīhi nedamamūlaṃ bhaviṣyatīti
translation: Then when a person is said to be thirsty, it is to be understood that the water he drank has been carried away by fire. Just as people refer to a leader of cows, or a leader of horses, or a leader of people, similarly, people say that fire is the leader of water. So also, O Somya, know that this sprout [i.e., the body] is the product of something [i.e., of food and drink]. It cannot be without a root.
word_meaning: Atha, next; yatra, when; puruṣaḥ, a person; pipāsati etat nāma, is said to be thirsty; tat, then; tejaḥ eva pītam nayate, fire has earned away [the water that he drank]; tat yathā, just as, for example; go-nāyaḥ, a leader of cows; aśva -nāyaḥ, a leader of horses; puruṣa -nāyaḥ, one who leads other people; iti, these terms people use; evam, similarly; tat tejaḥ, that fire; ācaṣṭe, is called; udanyā iti, the leader of water; tatra, so also; vijānīhi, know; somya, O Somya; etat eva śuṅgam, this sprout [i.e., the body]; utpatitam, is produced [by something]; idam, it; amūlam na bhaviṣyati iti, is not without a root.
commentary: When does a person feel thirsty? When there is a shortage of water in his body. How does this shortage occur? It occurs when the heat in the body turns the water into blood and other things. As soon as the water is consumed in this way a person feels that he has to drink more water.
concept_tags: The Subtle Body and Ethical Qualification
claim_candidates: The verse states that fire (teja) is the leader (nāya) of water, indicating that internal heat transforms water into other substances and creates thirst
The body is described as a sprout that is produced by something and cannot exist without a root, implying a material dependence on prior cause such as food and drink
The comparison of leaders of cows, horses, and people is used to illustrate a hierarchical ordering among elements in nature
objection_hooks: One may argue that the metaphor of fire leading water is poetic rather than a literal physiological claim, questioning the extent to which the verse provides a scientific explanation

The claim that the body cannot exist without a root can be challenged by the Advaita view that the true self (Atman) is independent of material causes, raising a debate between material and spiritual origins
The analogy of leadership might reflect linguistic convention rather than an ontological hierarchy, so inferring a systematic natural order from it may overinterpret the verse

sanskrit: तस्य क्व मूलं स्यादन्यत्राद्भ्य्ऽद्भिः सोम्य शुङ्गेन तेजो मूलमन्विच्छ तेजसा सोम्य शुङ्गेन सन्मूलमन्विच्छ सन्मूलाः सोम्येमाः सर्वाः प्रजाः सदायतनाः सत्प्रतिष्ठा यथा तु खलु सोम्येमास्तिस्रो देवताः पुरुषं प्राप्य त्रिवृत्त्रिवृदेकैका भवति तदुक्तं पुरस्तादेव भवत्यस्य सोम्य पुरुषस्य प्रयतो वाङ्मनसि सम्पद्यते मनः प्राणे प्राणस्तेजसि तेजः परस्यां देवतायाम्
transliteration: tasya kva mūlaṃ syādanyatrādbhy'dbhiḥ somya śuṅgena tejo mūlamanviccha tejasā somya śuṅgena sanmūlamanviccha sanmūlāḥ somyemāḥ sarvāḥ prajāḥ sadāyatanāḥ satpratiṣṭhā yathā tu khalu somyemāstisro devatāḥ puruṣaṃ prāpya trivṛttrivṛdekaikā bhavati taduktaṃ purastādeva bhavatyasya somya puruṣasya prayato vāṅmanasi sampadyate manaḥ prāṇe prāṇastejasi tejaḥ parasyāṃ devatāyām
translation: Where else, except in water, can the body have its root? O Somya, when water is the sprout, search for fire as the root; when fire is the sprout, O Somya, search for Sat [Existence] as the root. O Somya, Sat is the root, Sat is the abode, and Sat is the support of all these beings. As to how, O Somya, these three deities [fire, water, and earth] enter a body and each becomes threefold, this has already been explained. O Somya, as this person is dying, his speech merges into the mind, his mind into prāṇa, his prāṇa into fire, and then fire merges into Brahman, the Supreme Deity.
word_meaning: Tasya, of it [the body]; adbhyaḥ, [besides] from water; anyatra kva, where else; mūlam syāt, can the root [of the body] be; somya, O Somya; adbhiḥ śuṅgena, with water as the sprout; tejaḥ mūlam, fire as the root; anviccha, search for; tejasā śuṅgena, fire as the sprout; somya, O Somya; sat mūlam, Sat [Existence] as the root; anviccha, search for; somya, O Somya; imāḥ sarvāḥ prajāḥ, all these beings; sat mūlam, have Sat as the root; sat āyatanāḥ, Sat as the abode; sat pratiṣṭhāḥ, Sat as the support; yathā nu khalu, as to how; somya, O Somya; imāḥ tisraḥ devatāḥ, these three deities [fire, water, and earth]; puruṣam prāpya, enter a person; ekaikā bhavati, each one becomes; trivṛt trivṛt, threefold; tat uktam purastāt eva bhavati, this has been explained already; somya, O Somya; asya puruṣasya prayataḥ, as this person is dying; vāk, the organ of speech; manasi sampadyate, merges into the mind; manaḥ prāne, the mind into prāṇa; prāṇaḥ tejasi, prāṇa into fire; tejaḥ parasyām devatāyām, fire into the Supreme Deity [Brahman],
commentary: What happens when a person dies? First, his speech merges into his mind. He cannot speak any more, but his mind is still active. Sometimes when a person has a stroke, he cannot speak but his mind is alert. Then the mind merges into prāṇa, his life force. The mind ceases to function, but he is still breathing. Then prāṇa merges into fire. Fire here means the heat in the body. If the body is still warm, you
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse declares Sat (Existence) to be the root, abode and support of all beings, indicating that the ultimate reality Brahman underlies all phenomena

The death‑process described—speech merging into mind, mind into prāṇa, prāṇa into fire, and fire into Brahman—shows a progressive dissolution of the individual faculties into the universal Brahman, supporting the doctrine that the individual self merges with Brahman at death
The instruction to first seek water as the sprout, then fire as the root, and finally Sat as the root outlines a methodological ascent from material elements to the ultimate reality
objection_hooks: The emphasis on water and fire as necessary intermediate roots may suggest that material elements retain independent ontological status, challenging the claim that Sat alone is the sole root

The description of the merging process may be metaphorical rather than literal, raising doubt about whether the individual self truly becomes identical with Brahman or merely undergoes a symbolic transition
The verse describes the merging sequence at the moment of death, not as a prescribed practice for the living, which questions whether the ascent to Sat is a practical path to liberation

sanskrit: स य एषोऽणिमैतदात्म्यमिदं सर्वं तत्सत्यं स आत्मा तत्त्वमसि श्वेतकेतो इति भूय एव मा भगवान्विज्ञापयत्विति तथा सोम्येति होवाच
transliteration: sa ya eṣo'ṇimaitadātmyamidaṃ sarvaṃ tatsatyaṃ sa ātmā tattvamasi śvetaketo iti bhūya eva mā bhagavānvijñāpayatviti tathā somyeti hovāca
translation: ‘That which is the subtlest of all is the Self of all this. It is the Truth. It is the Self. That thou art, O Śvetaketu .’ [Śvetaketu then said, ] ‘Sir, please explain this to me again.’ ‘Yes, Somya, I will explain again, ’ replied his father.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, that which; eṣaḥ, this; aṇimā, the subtlest of all; idam sarvam aitadātmyam, the Self of all this; tat satyam, that is the Truth; saḥ ātmā, that is the Self; tat, that; tvam, you; asi, are; śvetaketo iti, O Śvetaketu; bhagavān, sir; bhūyaḥ eva, again; mā, to me; vijñāpayatu iti, will you please explain; tathā, so be it; somya iti, O Somya; ha uvāca, he [the father] said.
commentary: That which is the finest of all things, the subtlest, has that Existence, Sat, as its Self. That is the Truth, the Reality. We see all these forms before us. They are constantly changing. But that which we cannot see, which is the essence of everything, does not change. And that is our real identity. ‘That thou art’—this is the final message that Vedānta has to give. That Self, that essence, that pure Spirit, is your real identity. The phenomena we see before us are nothing but names and forms— nāma and rūpa . They are attributes superimposed on that which is constant, unchanging, unchangeable. You may call a person by one name today, but tomorrow he may have another name. And as regards forms, these are always changing. We are never the same. Then again, after some time these forms are gone. Vedānta defines satya, real, as that which existed in the past, which exists now, and which will exist in the future. But the names and forms we see before us are ephemeral. One day they are here and the next they are gone, so they cannot be real. Vedānta says, forget about your name and form. You are that eternal, unchanging Reality. Our form, our body, is the starting point of all our troubles. As soon aṣ we identify ourselves with the body we feel we are separate and different from others. ‘I am a brāhmin.’ ‘I am learned.’ ‘I am ignorant.’ ‘I come from such-and-such a place.’ Then you go on adding and adding—‘I am tall.’ ‘I am short.’ ‘I am fair.’ ‘I am dark.’ Remove these. Go to the essence, to the root— mūlā . Sir P.C. Roy did not believe in the caste system. In fact, he had contempt for it. Sometimes he would get together with some students and argue with them. Among the students, some were brāhmins and some were non-brāhmins. He would say: ‘Look, I am a chemist. I can prove that the blood of the brāhmin and the blood of the śūdra are both the same. They are composed of the same elements. But you say that this is a person of brāhmin blood and this is a person of śūdra blood. These distinctions are not real.’ Vedānta also says these differences are mere upādhis, attributes. They are superimpositions. What is real? Pure Spirit. In essence we are all one.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse declares that the subtlest reality, identified as the Self (Ātman), is identical to the ultimate Truth (Sat) and remains unchanging, unlike names and forms which are transient
The commentary states that social and physical distinctions such as caste, gender, or bodily attributes are merely upādhis superimposed on the true Self and therefore lack ultimate reality
The dialogue shows that understanding the identity ‘Thou art’ requires repeated instruction, indicating that realization of the Self is a process rather than an instantaneous insight
objection_hooks: One may object that denying the reality of distinctions undermines the practical ethical framework of dharma, which relies on social roles and duties
The claim of an unchanging Self can be challenged by the apparent changes in personal consciousness and identity, raising questions about the epistemic basis for such a claim
The need for repeated teaching appears contradictory to the assertion that the Self is ever‑present and self‑evident, creating tension between immediacy of knowledge and pedagogical necessity

sanskrit: यथा सोम्य मधु मधुकृतो निस्तिष्ठन्ति नानात्ययानां वृक्षाणांरसान्समवहारमेकतांरसं गमयन्ति
transliteration: yathā somya madhu madhukṛto nistiṣṭhanti nānātyayānāṃ vṛkṣāṇāṃrasānsamavahāramekatāṃrasaṃ gamayanti
translation: O Somya, as bees produce honey by collecting the juice from various trees and mixing them together to make one juice—
word_meaning: Yathā, as; somya, O Somya; madhukṛtaḥ, bees; madhu nistiṣṭhanti, produce honey; nānātyayānām
commentary: Śvetaketu was still having a hard time understanding, so his father gave another example: You see bees going around to different places. They collect pollen from many different trees and put it all together to make honey. Can you distinguish which flower a particular drop of honey came from? No, The pollen has become mixed together to make the honey.
concept_tags: The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The bee analogy shows that many diverse sources become one, indicating that ultimate reality is singular despite apparent multiplicity
The commentary’s point that a drop of honey cannot be traced to a particular flower supports the Advaita claim that individual distinctions disappear in the realization of Brahman
The verse suggests that various experiences (nectar) are integrated into a single essence, implying that different spiritual practices ultimately lead to the same knowledge of Brahman
objection_hooks: The analogy describes physical mixing and may not logically extend to metaphysical identity of Atman and Brahman
The commentary may be read as describing a method of gathering rather than the loss of individuality, weakening the claim of dissolution of distinctions
The claim that all spiritual practices converge is not explicitly stated in the verse, which only mentions nectar, so the extension may be unwarranted

sanskrit: ते यथा तत्र न विवेकं लभन्तेऽमुष्याहं वृक्षस्य रसोऽस्म्यमुष्याहं वृक्षस्य रसोऽस्मीत्येवमेव खलु सोम्येमाः सर्वाः प्रजाः सति सम्पद्य न विदुः सति सम्पद्यामह इति
transliteration: te yathā tatra na vivekaṃ labhante'muṣyāhaṃ vṛkṣasya raso'smyamuṣyāhaṃ vṛkṣasya raso'smītyevameva khalu somyemāḥ sarvāḥ prajāḥ sati sampadya na viduḥ sati sampadyāmaha iti
translation: —O Somya, and just as those juices now are no longer conscious of their separate identities, thinking, ‘I am the juice from such-and-such tree, ’ and ‘I am the juice from such-and-such tree’; similarly, when all these beings attain unity in the Self, they are not conscious of it. They do not think, ‘We [were once separate, but] now we are all one with the Self’.
word_meaning: Te, those [juices which have mixed together]; yathā tatra, now as [honey]; aham amuṣya vṛkṣasya rasaḥ asmi iti, I am the juice of such-and-such tree; aham amuṣya vṛkṣasya rasaḥ asmi iti, I am the juice of such-and-such tree; vivekam na labhante, this kind of consciousness [i.e., discrimination] they do not have; somya, O Somya; evarn eva, like this; khalu, surely; imāḥ sarvāḥ prajāḥ, all these beings; sati, in the Self [ Brahman ]; sampadya, having attained unity; sati sampadyāmahe, we are now all one with the Self; na viduḥ iti, are not conscious of this.
commentary: Can a drop of honey say, ‘I am from one tree, and you are from another tree’? No, there is no such discrimination there. The pollen from different flowers becomes one mass of honey. Similarly, we may seem to come from different sources, but in essence we are all one. When we merge into pure Being, we are free from all such discriminating ideas. There is no more diversity there.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
The Unitary World-Self, The Immanent Reality of All Things and Man
claim_candidates: The verse states that when beings attain unity with the Self they lose discriminative consciousness and are not aware of having become one
The honey metaphor demonstrates that apparent different origins are illusory and that all beings are essentially the same essence
The passage implies that realization of oneness is a state beyond conceptual knowledge, not a cognitive acknowledgment
objection_hooks: One could object that Upanishadic teachings often stress conscious self‑knowledge of Brahman, so claiming unawareness of unity seems contradictory
The honey analogy may be seen as too simplistic to account for the persistence of individual karmic identities after death
The claim that discrimination disappears may conflict with the doctrine of avidya, which holds that ignorance persists until full liberation

sanskrit: त इह व्यघ्रो वा सिंहो वा वृको वा वराहो वा कीटो वा पतङ्गो वा दंशो वा मशको वा यद्यद्भवन्ति तदाभवन्ति
transliteration: ta iha vyaghro vā siṃho vā vṛko vā varāho vā kīṭo vā pataṅgo vā daṃśo vā maśako vā yadyadbhavanti tadābhavanti
translation: Whatever they were before in this world—whether a tiger or lion or leopard or boar or bug or insect or flea or mosquito—they are born again. [They never know that they came from Sat].
word_meaning: Te, these; iha, here [in this world]; vyāghraḥ vā, a tiger; siṃhaḥ vā, or a lion; vṛkaḥ vā, or a leopard; varāhaḥ vā, or a boar; kīṭaḥ vā, or a bug; pataṅgaḥ vā, or an insect; daṃśaḥ vā, or a flea; maśakaḥ vā, or a mosquito; yat yat, whatever; bhavanti, they are [before]; tat, that; ābhavanti, they are after [because they do not yet know the Self].
commentary: We are born again and again till we overcome our ignorance and attain Self-knowledge. Death, for an ignorant person, is not liberation. It is like going into deep sleep. And when you are reborn it is as if you are waking up. You die again and again, and again and again you are reborn. This goes on until you attain Self-knowledge. Once you know the Self, you are free—free from birth and death.
concept_tags: The Individual Self Identical with the Infinite Brahman
claim_candidates: The verse states that all beings, whether tiger, lion, insect or mosquito, are reborn because they do not yet know the Self
The commentary describes death of an ignorant person as comparable to deep sleep, indicating that death is not liberation without Self‑knowledge
The cycle of birth and death persists until Self‑knowledge is attained, after which one becomes free from rebirth
objection_hooks: The enumeration of animal forms could imply that rebirth is determined by prior existence rather than solely by ignorance, suggesting a role for karma beyond knowledge
Some Upanishadic passages portray death as a transition rather than a state of ignorance, challenging the claim that death is merely non‑liberation for the ignorant
Emphasizing knowledge as the sole cause of liberation may overlook ethical conduct as a factor in rebirth, creating tension with other teachings on karma

sanskrit: स य एषोऽणिमैतदात्म्यमिदं सर्वं तत्सत्यं स आत्मा तत्त्वमसि श्वेतकेतो इति भूय एव मा भगवान्विज्ञापयत्विति तथा सोम्येति होवाच
transliteration: sa ya eṣo'ṇimaitadātmyamidaṃ sarvaṃ tatsatyaṃ sa ātmā tattvamasi śvetaketo iti bhūya eva mā bhagavānvijñāpayatviti tathā somyeti hovāca
translation: ‘That which is the subtlest of all is the Self of all this. It is the Truth. It is the Self. That thou art, O Śvetaketu .’ [Śvetaketu then said, ] ‘Sir, please explain this to me again.’ ‘Yes, Somya, I will explain it again, ’ replied his father.
word_meaning: Saḥ yaḥ, that which; eṣaḥ, this; aṇimā, the subtlest of all; idam sarvam aitadātmyam, the Self of all this; tat satyam, that is the Truth; saḥ ātmā, that is the Self; tat, that; tvam, you; asi, are; śvetaketo iti, O Śvetaketu; bhagavān, sir; bhūyaḥ eva, again; mā, to me; vijñāpayatu iti, will you please explain; tathā, so be it; somya iti, O Somya; ha uvāca, he [the father] said.
commentary: “Tat tvam asi”—thou art that. This is called the mahāvākya, the great saying, it is the magic formula. If your mind has been purified, as soon as the mahāvākya is uttered your eyes are opened and you realize your own Self. But this can happen only after a long process of preparation.
